Cooperating is not the same thing as being a cooperator.

In my academic work, I've explored this distinction, with the aim of determining the importance of what lies beneath appearances in social interactions. A few years ago, I created a computer model to explore how false appearances can affect cooperation. If, for example, an agent – representing a person in the world of the computer model – determines that defection in the Prisoner's Dilemma is likely to be exposed and punished, the agent cooperates. If, however, defection is likely to go unnoticed, the agent defects.

The model shows that cooperation stays high – at about two-thirds of interactions – even if the vast majority of agents prefer to defect when possible. While older models evaluating cooperation in the dilemma showed that punishment removes defectors from the population altogether – in line with what people defending cultural group selection say – the difference between appearance and motivation makes removal more difficult. You can't punish defectors if you don't know who they are. I've called this the problem of opportunity. When anything cooperates – whether computer agent, bacterium, mole rat or person – we have no way of establishing, with certainty, whether cooperation was intended or happened because there wasn't a good opportunity for defection.

How people use language to talk about cooperation in the real world illustrates the problem in action. Models are, by design and requirement, vague: they don't tell you anything more about a situation than that some computerised agents cooperated, defected, were punished, and so on. A model can't tell you whether an agent chose to cooperate or was forced to (the latter case, in everyday language, we call coercion). And too often in everyday life, we're forced to cooperate with others when we don't want to – whether that's paying high prices for food and travel, voting for a politician who seems just a bit less bad than another, or signing a non-disclosure agreement to get a job. (Think about this next time you hear the phrase 'thank you for your cooperation'.)

Models don't and can't know the difference between forced and prosocially motivated cooperation. Yet, sometimes, behavioural experiments can. Far from being a species that dislikes inequity and acts against it, we are more likely to profess a desire for fairness, reserving our singularly self-interested behaviours for when there's unlikely to be cost for them. Evidence for a phenomenon called 'moral credentialing' supports this. In short, if I believe I've acted morally in the past – through making donations, working in a soup kitchen for the homeless, and so on – I'm more likely to justify my unethical actions in the future.

In 2011, researchers showed that participants are more likely to cheat on a mathematics test if they have the opportunity to profess support for moral principles beforehand – but only if they could rationalise about how cheating didn't violate their moral codes. Notably, in 2024, two researchers showed that businesses voluntarily signing up to the Business Roundtable's 'Statement on the Purpose of a Corporation' (2019) – which promotes the importance of value to everyone, not just shareholders – were more likely to violate both environmental and labour laws.

And more recently still, research into the use of large language models like ChatGPT illustrates just how much opportunity links to dishonesty. In this set of studies, researchers evaluated how participants behave when they can delegate behaviours to AI models. The setting was a die-rolling game, where higher numbers meant a higher financial benefit. While players were broadly honest when reporting their die rolls directly, delegating reporting to an AI agent changed behaviours markedly. When the players could give vague instructions to the AI such as 'maximise profits', their honesty decreased enormously, with less than a fifth of rolls reported accurately.

There are plenty of examples of people dodging moral responsibility through credentialing (touting past good deeds), rationalisation, and plain opportunism. In aggregate, the belief that you're a moral person because of the principles you profess or the good things you've done before can make it easier to rationalise seizing the opportunity to act unfairly now.

The behavioural scientist Jason Dana and colleagues report that people often seek 'moral wiggle room' in economic games – ways to choose unfairly without feeling culpable. What matters most, the team suggests, is often not fairness but insulation from blame, sometimes by claiming ignorance about who is harmed and how: 'In the spate of recent [financial] scandals, often high-level figures accused of transgressions must be shown to have known about harms in order to be held liable. We note that this ignores the efforts that executives may take to remain ignorant.'

When you see how quickly people reach for loopholes and excuses, it's tempting to blame the system – to say that Western law, markets or politics teach us to act this way. But I don't think opportunism starts there. Opportunism is more basic than that: when cutting corners brings a benefit and no one notices – think tax avoidance – it's a winning move almost anywhere. We can design all sorts of rules that encourage cooperation. But we can't erase the underlying fact that cheating will often pay when it's hidden.

Increased group sizes, reflected in the large, stratified societies in which most people live today, create far more opportunities for cheating than encountered over our evolutionary past. The egalitarianism so often noted in small-scale societies, such as the Ache, may then represent a lack of opportunity for free-riding, rather than an evolved propensity for fairness. Knowing everyone in your camp, choosing to live with relatives, and a collective expectation that people will follow local norms, maintains cooperation – though even in small-scale societies people often find ways of exploiting each other. Older men, for example, often dominate their social groups, with exploitation of women and young men reported in the ethnographic literature in nomadic tribes and forager groups across the world.

There are many other examples of exploitation in ethnographic records from across the world. The idea that we lived in a state of equality until the invention of agriculture is mostly a myth that I think helps us feel better about human nature. It fosters the hope that, one day, we'll overcome the inequality imposed on us by our abandonment of the hunter-gatherer lifestyle.

Rather than attributing our problems today to competition between groups and the structure of our societies, the governing rule for any social system is to expect exploitation where it is possible. Every group, society and culture, no matter its size, has weaknesses that some people will try to exploit for personal benefit. The question is how those weaknesses affect culture more broadly, and whether we live in a society that rewards fairmindedness – or cleverness, subtlety and opportunism.

In the modern world, as with our evolutionary past, the answer is the latter. All that's changed since the advent of agriculture is the number and varieties of opportunities for free-riding and exploitation. Consequently, as technology improves and groups increase in size, we should expect people to develop creative ways for defecting more effectively – with evolution favouring those who do it best.

This proclivity for developing new strategies to compete is part of the social brain hypothesis, originally formulated by the psychologist Nicholas Humphrey. In his seminal paper on the topic in 1976, Humphrey argued that the primary function of the human intellect is to navigate the social, rather than the physical, environment. One implication of the social brain hypothesis is the assumption that every society hosts opportunistic people who may follow local norms for only as long as it is beneficial to do so. Elsewhere, I have called these people 'invisible rivals'. For example, religious zealots and political adherents across the world may observe all the rules linked with their group – whether ritual or ideological – until they reach a position of power. Thereafter, they can exploit others and act selfishly as it suits them. This may help to explain why studies show that people with psychopathic tendencies are more likely to enter positions of power, for example in corporate or political systems. Following rules without believing in them is an effective strategy for gaining power.

Admittedly, these arguments make our world sound hopeless. It's tempting to think that, if the story of human evolution isn't the rosy picture of cooperation, fairmindedness and mutual aid championed by thinkers for more than a century, we can't expect much from our future. There are just too many problems – from raging inequality and low public trust to a rapidly warming planet and the growing risk of technology like AI – to hope that a species with a dark and ignoble past can overcome itself and create a better future.

I think, however, that this pessimism is misplaced, and that facing ourselves honestly is the first and most important step we can collectively take. This requires adopting a realistic perspective about the kind of animal that Homo sapiens is. First, we are not inherently cooperative but have the capacity for cooperation – just as we have the capacity for exploitation and selfishness. What matters at the individual level is the way we choose to behave towards others.

Second, just as there is no such thing as a cooperator, there is no such thing as a free-rider. These are behaviours that we apply in models and experiments for convenience. How people behave – and critically, how we describe social behaviours – is a matter of circumstance. The same person who behaves ethically in one circumstance may not do so in another, as research into moral credentialing shows. Our behavioural plasticity, or ability to adapt the way we act to context, is one of our defining features. The evolved psychological processes driving our decisions cannot be captured by simplistic models or games. Anyone can be an invisible rival.

That is precisely why local social norms matter so much. If cooperation isn't a fixed trait but a fragile, context-dependent outcome, then the real question is what kinds of environments make it easier to do the right thing – and harder to get away with quiet defection. The Nobel laureate Elinor Ostrom argued that local social norms are the bedrock of any serious effort to promote cooperation: look at how people behave in their immediate surroundings to understand their methods for restraining unbridled selfishness. Just as organisms evolve immune defences against selfish cells that quietly undermine the whole, societies need norms – and the institutions that uphold them – that can detect and restrain rivalries that flourish out of sight.

Fostering community-level interdependence – and the norms that evolved to help them function cooperatively – is therefore essential for combatting the exploitation that results from invisible rivalry. Never try to enforce cooperation from above. Instead, just as the economist Noreena Hertz argues we should replace 'greed is good' maxims in the capitalist framework with a community-oriented, cooperation-promoting mindset, appreciating that we are all better off when we work together is the critical insight needed for building a prosocial and equality-focused environment for the future.

Education is where this begins, not as moral uplift but as collective self-knowledge: it helps us see our own temptations clearly and translate that insight into practical scaffolding – laws, schools and civic rules that reward cooperation and raise the costs of exploitation. Cheating will never vanish, and some people will always look for an edge, but our distinctive intelligence lies as much in recognising exploitation and organising against it as in exploiting in the first place. Invest in that knowledge and in the local institutions that make fairness both appreciated and rewarded, and we will widen the space in which cooperation and equality can endure.

paragraphs.en: Reading classic works in evolutionary biology is unlikely to make you optimistic about human nature. From Charles Darwin's The Descent of Man (1871) onwards, there is a fundamental understanding among biologists that organisms, especially humans, evolved to maximise self-interest. We act to promote our own success or that of our family. Niceness, by contrast, is just a mirage, and morality more broadly is just an illusion. Sociobiology – the infamous movement of the second half of the 20th century – forced us to confront the cold, calculated nature of having evolved biologically.
paragraphs.cn: 阅读演化生物学的经典著作不太可能让你对人性的看法变得乐观。从查尔斯·达尔文的《人类的由来》（1871年）开始，生物学家们就形成了一个基本共识：生物体——尤其是人类——的演化是为了最大化自身利益。我们的行为旨在促进自身或家族的成功。相比之下，善意不过是一种海市蜃楼，而更广泛意义上的道德也只是一种幻觉。社会生物学——20世纪下半叶那场声名狼藉的学术运动——迫使我们直面生物演化的冷酷与精明。

paragraphs.en: More recently, however, anthropologists and psychologists have pushed back against this pessimistic view. Dozens of books over the past decade have focused on human cooperation, promoting it as the secret ingredient to our conquest of the planet. We work together, using our intelligence, language and a diverse skillset to build on complex cultures, develop technologies, and solve problems in our societies and environments. We learn at a young age what the rules of our groups are, and those rules, imprinted on us culturally, govern the safe, cohesive units that allowed us to conquer inhospitable parts of the world and out-compete unfriendly groups of people who don't work well together.
paragraphs.cn: 然而，近年来人类学家和心理学家对这种悲观观点提出了反驳。过去十年间，数十本书聚焦于人类合作，将其推崇为我们征服地球的秘密武器。我们协同工作，运用智慧、语言和多样化的技能来构建复杂的文化、发展技术，并解决社会与环境中的种种问题。我们从幼年起就学习所在群体的规则，这些规则通过文化在我们身上留下印记，治理着那些安全而有凝聚力的社会单元——正是这些单元使我们得以征服世界上那些不宜居住的地区，并击败那些不善于协作的敌对群体。

paragraphs.en: This narrative saves us the embarrassment of accepting that biological selfishness – acting only to maximise our Darwinian success – is the foundation of all behaviour. It also matches some claims by anthropologists that ancient humans were egalitarian, living in small groups with little permanent rank, where leaders (if any) had limited authority and people collectively pushed back against anyone trying to dominate.
paragraphs.cn: 这一叙事使我们免于接受一个令人尴尬的事实：生物学的自私——即只以最大化达尔文式成功为目标的行为——是一切行为的基础。它也与一些人类学家的主张相吻合，即古代人类是平等的，他们生活在几乎没有永久等级的小型群体中，领导者（如果有的话）权力有限，人们会集体抵制任何试图称霸的人。

paragraphs.en: Yet, as with sociobiology, it is only half true. Instead, our collective predilection for exploitation, deceit and competition is equally important to cooperation in the story of human evolution. We evolved not to cooperate or compete, but with the capacity for both – and with the intelligence to hide competition when it suits us, or to cheat when we're likely to get away with it. Cooperation is consequently something we need to promote, not presume.
paragraphs.cn: 然而，正如社会生物学一样，这只不过是事实的一半。事实上，我们集体对剥削、欺骗和竞争的倾向，在人类演化的故事中与合作同等重要。我们的演化目的既不是合作也不是竞争，而是同时具备了这两种能力——以及在我们觉得有利时隐藏竞争、在觉得自己能逃脱惩罚时作弊的智慧。因此，合作是我们需要努力促成的，而非理所当然地预设的。

paragraphs.en: The modern dispute about whether humans are fundamentally cooperative or competitive dates back to the publication of Mutual Aid (1902) by Pyotr Kropotkin, an anarchist who took his views about human nature from observing animals helping each other in the unhospitable wilds of Siberia. Kropotkin believed that it's only through interdependence that any species can survive in the struggles against predation, violence and the environment, which characterise the omnipresent dangers individuals face. Like so many other species, fish, flesh and fowl, we work together to survive and reproduce.
paragraphs.cn: 关于人类本质上是合作型还是竞争型的现代争论，可以追溯到彼得·克鲁泡特金于1902年出版的《互助论》。克鲁泡特金是一位无政府主义者，他关于人性的观点源于在西伯利亚荒凉的原野上观察动物相互帮助的经历。克鲁泡特金认为，任何物种只有在相互依存中才能在与捕食、暴力和自然环境的斗争中幸存下来，这些斗争是个体所面临的无处不在的威胁。就像鱼类、兽类和鸟类等许多其他物种一样，我们通过协作来生存和繁衍。

paragraphs.en: On the surface, Kropotkin's views are at odds with Darwin's, who championed the individual struggle for survival and mating as the fundamental driver of evolution by natural selection. The twin pillars of competing for survival and competing for mates – natural and sexual selection, respectively – were, for Darwin, the foundations of biological life. For Kropotkin and his colleagues, by contrast, the emphasis was on how individuals acted for the good of the species: mutual aid meant a better, safer life for everyone.
paragraphs.cn: 表面上看，克鲁泡特金的观点与达尔文截然不同。达尔文将个体为生存和交配而进行的斗争视为自然选择驱动演化的根本动力。为生存而竞争和为配偶而竞争——即自然选择和性选择——这两大支柱，在达尔文看来是生物生命的基础。而克鲁泡特金及其同僚则截然不同，他们强调的是个体如何为物种的福祉而行动：互助意味着每个人都能拥有更好、更安全的生活。

paragraphs.en: Today, the debate is substantially the same, though the language and tools we use to make our points are different. Experiments conducted by anthropologists and psychologists across the world evaluate how cooperatively people behave in a multitude of conditions, with obvious ideological battle lines between those who espouse a self-interested versus a beneficent model of human nature.
paragraphs.cn: 今天，这场争论在本质上依然是相同的，尽管我们用来论证的语言和工具有所不同。人类学家和心理学家在世界各地进行的实验评估人们在各种条件下表现出的合作程度，而支持利己模式与支持利他模式的人性观之间存在着明显的意识形态战线。

paragraphs.en: For example, in one famous study from 2001, anthropologists worked with 15 different small-scale societies to see how they behaved in an economic experiment called the ultimatum game. In this game, the researcher gives one player a set amount of money – in this case, the local value of one or two days' worth of wages. That player then chooses an amount of the money to offer to the second player, who may either accept or decline. In the case of acceptance, the players receive the amounts of money agreed upon; in the case of rejection, both receive nothing.
paragraphs.cn: 例如，在2001年的一项著名研究中，人类学家与15个不同的小型社会合作，观察他们在一种被称为"最后通牒博弈"的经济实验中的行为。在这个博弈中，研究者给其中一位参与者一笔固定金额的钱——在本研究中，相当于当地一到两天的工资收入。然后，该参与者选择向第二位参与者出价多少，第二位参与者可以接受或拒绝。如果接受，双方按商定的金额获得报酬；如果拒绝，双方都一无所获。

paragraphs.en: In a calculated world governed only by self-interest, we'd expect the first player to offer the smallest possible amount, and for the second player to accept any offer. Something is better than nothing, no matter whether that something is unfair on either side.
paragraphs.cn: 在一个完全由利己主义支配的理性世界中，我们会预期第一位参与者只出最少的金额，而第二位参与者会接受任何出价。有总比没有好，无论这"有"对哪一方是否公平。

paragraphs.en: Of course, the participants in the small-scale societies didn't play the game in this way. The offers were almost never lower than 25 per cent of the overall pot, and in some groups, like with the Ache people of Paraguay and the Lamelara people of Indonesia, the offers were often greater than half the total amount. Some scientists, notably the economist Ernst Fehr, used this outcome to defend the idea that humans are 'inequity averse' – that is, we are a species that almost universally dislikes unfairness. ('Prosociality' is also a term you see in the literature a lot.) As a consequence of this alleged collective aversion, we are thought to treat each other more fairly than you'd expect using a cold economic calculus.
paragraphs.cn: 当然，这些小型社会的参与者并没有按照这种方式进行博弈。出价几乎从不低于总金额的25%，而在某些群体中——比如巴拉圭的阿切人和印度尼西亚的拉梅拉拉人——出价甚至经常超过总金额的一半。一些科学家，尤其是经济学家恩斯特·费尔，利用这一结果来论证人类是"厌恶不平等的"——也就是说，我们是一个几乎普遍厌恶不公平的物种。（在学术文献中，"亲社会性"也是常见的术语。）据说正是由于这种集体性的厌恶，我们对待彼此的方式被认为比冷酷的经济计算所能预期的更为公平。

paragraphs.en: These ideas have broadened out into a modern theory of super-cooperation, with a caveat: instead of the 'good of the species' view advocated for by Kropotkin, researchers focus on how people behave within groups. We learn to cooperate within groups because we are interdependent on one another for survival: reciprocal relationships are essential when anyone meets with failure in hunting, gathering or agriculture. Need-based transfers – where people ask from others only when they need help, for example when their own crops fail – characterise small-scale societies across the world.
paragraphs.cn: 这些观点扩展为一种现代的"超级合作"理论，但有一个重要的补充：研究者不再关注克鲁泡特金所倡导的"为了物种福祉"的观念，而是聚焦于人们在群体内部的行为方式。我们之所以学会在群体内合作，是因为我们在生存上相互依赖：当任何人在狩猎、采集或农业方面遭遇失败时，互惠关系至关重要。基于需求的转移——即人们只在需要帮助时才向他人求助，例如自己的庄稼歉收时——是世界上小型社会的共同特征。

paragraphs.en: Local norms determining how people cooperate spread through social learning. So, while need-based transfer is a common practice worldwide, its appearance is determined by the culture in question. Osotua (which translates to 'umbilical cord') is a bond linking two Maasai people of Kenya and Tanzania in lifelong interdependence. Betrayal of osotua is reportedly unheard of, and a person's descendants can even inherit a family member's bond with another.
paragraphs.cn: 决定人们如何合作的本地规范通过社会学习传播开来。因此，虽然基于需求的转移在全世界都是一种普遍做法，但其具体形式取决于当地的文化。例如，Osotua（意为"脐带"）是一种连接肯尼亚和坦桑尼亚两位马赛人终身依存关系的纽带。据说背叛Osotua关系的事从未发生过，一个人的后代甚至可以继承家族成员与他人的这种纽带。

paragraphs.en: According to this way of thinking, groups that cooperate more effectively out-compete groups that don't. This is part of a broader process called cultural group selection, the modern-day version of the mutual aid concept that Kropotkin championed more than a century ago. Except, instead of acting for the good of our species, we act for the good of our groups. Interdependence breeds loyalty, the hypothesis holds. If the notion of cultural group selection bears out, then the problems we see in the world today should be seen as a consequence of friction between groups, not within them. Issues like international conflicts would derive from differences in social norms and values, rather than because of a missing commitment to prosociality shared by all group members. Cultural group selection encourages us to look for problems outside rather than within.
paragraphs.cn: 按照这种思路，合作更有效的群体会胜过那些不善于合作的群体。这是一个更广泛的过程——被称为"文化群体选择"——的一部分，它是克鲁泡特金一个多世纪前所倡导的互助概念的现代版本。只不过，我们不是为物种的福祉而行动，而是为群体的利益而行动。该假说认为，相互依存孕育忠诚。如果文化群体选择的概念成立，那么我们今天在世界上看到的问题应当被视为群体之间而非群体内部摩擦的后果。国际冲突等问题将源于社会规范和价值观的差异，而非因为群体成员缺乏共同的社会承诺。文化群体选择鼓励我们从外部而非内部寻找问题。

paragraphs.en: But the idea starts to look shaky upon closer inspection. Polly Wiessner, an anthropologist who has worked with the Ju/'hoansi of the Kalahari for decades, described what happened when she ran similar experiments herself. As part of her execution, she made it clear to volunteers that she was acting on behalf of someone else, the well-known economist Ernst Fehr; this was his interest, she emphasised; she did not care at all how they played the game and no matter what they did, there would be no consequence at all. She wrote: 'A few asked me once more if it was really true that their identity would not be revealed; with confirmation, they slid more coins, one by one, over to their own sides. Occasionally the subject would hesitate and say: "Are you sure you are not deceiving me?"'
paragraphs.cn: 但仔细审视之下，这个观点开始显得站不住脚。人类学家波莉·维斯纳数十年来一直在与卡拉哈里的Ju/'hoansi人合作，她描述了自己进行类似实验时发生的情况。在实验过程中，她向参与者明确表示自己代表的是经济学家恩斯特·费尔；她强调这是费尔的研究兴趣；她完全不关心他们怎么玩这个游戏，而且无论他们怎么做，都不会有任何后果。她写道："有几个人再次向我确认，他们的身份是否真的不会被公开；得到确认后，他们一枚一枚地把更多硬币拨到了自己那边。偶尔，参与者会犹豫一下，然后问：'你确定你没有骗我吗？'"

paragraphs.en: For Wiessner, the point wasn't that the Ju/'hoansi were uniquely selfish; it was that the experiment created a social situation unlike everyday life. Put someone in a game where identities are hidden and consequences are explicitly ruled out, and you remove many of the ordinary pressures that govern cooperation – reputation, ongoing relationships, the possibility of retaliation, the cost of being seen to take too much. What you end up measuring, in other words, is not 'how cooperative this person is', but how they behave in a stripped-down context where cooperation and betrayal carry very different risks.
paragraphs.cn: 对维斯纳来说，关键不在于Ju/'hoansi人格外自私，而在于实验创造了一种不同于日常生活的社会情境。当一个人被置于一个身份被隐藏、后果被明确排除的游戏中时，你就消除了支配合作的许多日常压力——声誉、持续的关系、报复的可能性、以及被认为拿太多的代价。换言之，你最终测量的不是"这个人有多合作"，而是他们在合作和背叛的风险截然不同的简化语境中的行为方式。

paragraphs.en: That basic insight runs through decades of work on the biology of cooperation. Even the earliest mathematical models that made reciprocity central to human social life treated betrayal as context-dependent: defection becomes attractive when there's little chance of future interaction, when the other person can't meaningfully respond, or when your reputation is unlikely to suffer. Cooperation, from this perspective, isn't something we can simply assume; it's something social life must make possible – and worth sustaining.
paragraphs.cn: 这一基本洞见贯穿了数十年来关于合作生物学的研究。即使是将互惠作为人类社会生活核心的最早期的数学模型，也将背叛视为依赖情境的行为：当未来互动的可能性很小、对方无法有效回应、或者你的声誉不太可能受损时，背叛就变得具有吸引力。从这个角度来看，合作不是我们可以简单假设的东西；它是社会生活必须使之成为可能——并且值得维持的东西。

paragraphs.en: Over the 1970s, '80s and arguably ever since, thousands of computer models purporting to explain how and why people cooperate have missed this point. Most often, researchers have explored how cooperation evolves in the Prisoner's Dilemma. In the simplest form of this game, two players may choose to cooperate or defect. While mutual cooperation is mutually beneficial, and mutual defection is mutually damaging, defecting against a cooperative partner is the individual optimum – and cooperating against a defector yields the worst possible payoff. (The game is called the 'Prisoner's Dilemma' because the theoretical scenario is one where two criminals are separately asked by the police to inform on one another. If you inform on your partner, you get a much lighter sentence.)
paragraphs.cn: 在20世纪70年代、80年代乃至此后，数以千计的计算机模型试图解释人们如何以及为何合作，但都忽略了这一点。最常见的是，研究者探索合作如何在"囚徒困境"中演化。在这个博弈的最简形式中，两位参与者可以选择合作或背叛。虽然双方都合作对双方都有利，双方都背叛对双方都有害，但在对方合作时选择背叛是个体的最优策略——而在对方背叛时选择合作则得到最差的结果。（这个博弈之所以被称为"囚徒困境"，是因为其理论场景是两名罪犯被警方分别审问，被要求揭发对方。如果你揭发了你的同伴，你将获得大幅减刑。）

paragraphs.en: Researchers have developed an astounding number of variations of this dilemma for explaining how cooperation is sustainable more broadly. Some invoke punishing defectors; some just explore the likelihood that one player will meet another again in the future. But, critically, virtually all of them treat 'cooperators' and 'defectors' as defining individual features. A player is defined by their propensity for cooperation – much as we might say of a criminal who rats on his friend that 'once a rat, always a rat'. I have always found this assumption problematic. Much as any person might cheat a partner when the likelihood of being discovered is low, so are we wrong to assume that anyone who cooperates in one game is likely to cooperate in every game. Cooperating is not the same thing as being a cooperator.
paragraphs.cn: 研究者为解释合作如何在更广泛的范围内可持续，开发了令人惊叹的大量囚徒困境变体。有些引入了对背叛者的惩罚机制；有些只是探索一位参与者在未来再次遇到另一位参与者的可能性。但关键是，几乎所有这些模型都将"合作者"和"背叛者"视为定义个体特征的标签。一个参与者由其合作倾向来定义——就像我们可能对一个出卖朋友的人说"一次当叛徒，终身是叛徒"一样。我一直认为这个假设是有问题的。正如任何人在被发现可能性很低时都可能欺骗伙伴一样，我们也错误地假设在一个博弈中合作的人在所有博弈中都会合作。合作与"是一个合作者"不是同一回事。

paragraphs.en: In my academic work, I've explored this distinction, with the aim of determining the importance of what lies beneath appearances in social interactions. A few years ago, I created a computer model to explore how false appearances can affect cooperation. If, for example, an agent – representing a person in the world of the computer model – determines that defection in the Prisoner's Dilemma is likely to be exposed and punished, the agent cooperates. If, however, defection is likely to go unnoticed, the agent defects.
paragraphs.cn: 在我的学术研究中，我探索了这一区别，目的是确定社会互动中表象之下的东西有多重要。几年前，我创建了一个计算机模型来探索虚假表象如何影响合作。例如，如果一个代理——代表计算机模型世界中的一个人——判断在囚徒困境中背叛很可能会被揭露和惩罚，该代理就会选择合作。然而，如果背叛很可能不被察觉，该代理就会选择背叛。

paragraphs.en: The model shows that cooperation stays high – at about two-thirds of interactions – even if the vast majority of agents prefer to defect when possible. While older models evaluating cooperation in the dilemma showed that punishment removes defectors from the population altogether – in line with what people defending cultural group selection say – the difference between appearance and motivation makes removal more difficult. You can't punish defectors if you don't know who they are. I've called this the problem of opportunity. When anything cooperates – whether computer agent, bacterium, mole rat or person – we have no way of establishing, with certainty, whether cooperation was intended or happened because there wasn't a good opportunity for defection.
paragraphs.cn: 模型显示，即使绝大多数代理在有可能时更倾向于背叛，合作率仍然保持在较高水平——约占所有互动的三分之二。虽然较早的评估囚徒困境中合作的模型表明，惩罚可以将背叛者从群体中完全清除——这与支持文化群体选择的人的说法一致——但表象与动机之间的差异使清除变得更加困难。如果你不知道背叛者是谁，你就无法惩罚他们。我将此称为"机会问题"。当任何实体合作时——无论是计算机代理、细菌、鼹鼠还是人——我们都无法确定地判断这种合作是出于真心，还是仅仅因为没有好的背叛机会。

paragraphs.en: How people use language to talk about cooperation in the real world illustrates the problem in action. Models are, by design and requirement, vague: they don't tell you anything more about a situation than that some computerised agents cooperated, defected, were punished, and so on. A model can't tell you whether an agent chose to cooperate or was forced to (the latter case, in everyday language, we call coercion). And too often in everyday life, we're forced to cooperate with others when we don't want to – whether that's paying high prices for food and travel, voting for a politician who seems just a bit less bad than another, or signing a non-disclosure agreement to get a job. (Think about this next time you hear the phrase 'thank you for your cooperation'.)
paragraphs.cn: 人们在现实世界中用语言谈论合作的方式，恰好说明了这个问题。模型就其设计和需求而言是模糊的：它们不会告诉你关于情境的更多信息，只会说某些计算机代理合作了、背叛了、被惩罚了等等。模型无法告诉你一个代理是自愿合作还是被迫合作（后者在日常语言中，我们称之为"胁迫"）。而在日常生活中，我们经常被迫与不愿合作的人合作——无论是支付高昂的食品和旅行价格，投票给一个看起来只是稍微不那么糟糕的政客，还是为了获得一份工作而签署保密协议。（下次你听到"感谢您的配合"这句话时，不妨想一想这个问题。）

paragraphs.en: Models don't and can't know the difference between forced and prosocially motivated cooperation. Yet, sometimes, behavioural experiments can. Far from being a species that dislikes inequity and acts against it, we are more likely to profess a desire for fairness, reserving our singularly self-interested behaviours for when there's unlikely to be cost for them. Evidence for a phenomenon called 'moral credentialing' supports this. In short, if I believe I've acted morally in the past – through making donations, working in a soup kitchen for the homeless, and so on – I'm more likely to justify my unethical actions in the future.
paragraphs.cn: 模型无法也不了解被迫合作与出于亲社会动机的合作之间的区别。然而，有时行为实验能够区分两者。我们远非一个厌恶不平等并采取行动反对不平等的物种，相反，我们更倾向于宣称自己渴望公平，而将那些纯粹利己的行为留给不太可能付出代价的时机。一种被称为"道德凭证"的现象为这一观点提供了支持。简而言之，如果我相信自己过去做过善事——比如捐款、在流浪者救济厨房做义工等等——我就更有可能为自己未来不道德的行为寻找正当理由。

paragraphs.en: In 2011, researchers showed that participants are more likely to cheat on a mathematics test if they have the opportunity to profess support for moral principles beforehand – but only if they could rationalise about how cheating didn't violate their moral codes. Notably, in 2024, two researchers showed that businesses voluntarily signing up to the Business Roundtable's 'Statement on the Purpose of a Corporation' (2019) – which promotes the importance of value to everyone, not just shareholders – were more likely to violate both environmental and labour laws.
paragraphs.cn: 2011年，研究者发现，如果参与者事先有机会表达对道德原则的支持，他们就更可能在数学考试中作弊——但前提是他们能够说服自己作弊并未违反自己的道德准则。值得注意的是，2024年，两位研究者发现，自愿签署商业圆桌会议2019年发布的《公司宗旨声明》——该声明强调对所有利益相关者而非仅对股东创造价值——的企业，反而更有可能违反环境和劳动法规。

paragraphs.en: And more recently still, research into the use of large language models like ChatGPT illustrates just how much opportunity links to dishonesty. In this set of studies, researchers evaluated how participants behave when they can delegate behaviours to AI models. The setting was a die-rolling game, where higher numbers meant a higher financial benefit. While players were broadly honest when reporting their die rolls directly, delegating reporting to an AI agent changed behaviours markedly. When the players could give vague instructions to the AI such as 'maximise profits', their honesty decreased enormously, with less than a fifth of rolls reported accurately.
paragraphs.cn: 而最近，关于ChatGPT等大型语言模型使用的研究进一步说明了机会与不诚实之间的紧密关联。在这组研究中，研究者评估了参与者可以将行为委托给AI模型时的行为表现。实验设置是一个掷骰子游戏，点数越高意味着经济收益越大。当参与者直接报告自己的骰子点数时，他们基本是诚实的；但当把报告工作委托给AI代理时，行为发生了显著变化。当参与者可以向AI发出"利润最大化"之类的模糊指令时，他们的诚实度大幅下降，准确报告的骰子结果不到五分之一。

paragraphs.en: There are plenty of examples of people dodging moral responsibility through credentialing (touting past good deeds), rationalisation, and plain opportunism. In aggregate, the belief that you're a moral person because of the principles you profess or the good things you've done before can make it easier to rationalise seizing the opportunity to act unfairly now.
paragraphs.cn: 人们通过凭证效应（吹嘘过去的善行）、合理化和赤裸裸的机会主义来逃避道德责任的例子比比皆是。总体而言，因为你所宣称的原则或过去所做的好事而相信自己是一个有道德的人，会让你更容易为自己当下的不公平行为找到借口。

paragraphs.en: The behavioural scientist Jason Dana and colleagues report that people often seek 'moral wiggle room' in economic games – ways to choose unfairly without feeling culpable. What matters most, the team suggests, is often not fairness but insulation from blame, sometimes by claiming ignorance about who is harmed and how: 'In the spate of recent [financial] scandals, often high-level figures accused of transgressions must be shown to have known about harms in order to be held liable. We note that this ignores the efforts that executives may take to remain ignorant.'
paragraphs.cn: 行为科学家杰森·达纳及其同事报告说，人们在经济博弈中经常寻找"道德回旋余地"——即在不感到内疚的情况下选择不公平的方式。该团队指出，最重要的往往不是公平，而是如何规避责任，有时是通过声称对谁受到伤害以及如何受到伤害一无所知："在近期一连串的金融丑闻中，被指控违规的高层人物通常必须被证明知道伤害的存在才能被追究责任。我们注意到，这忽略了高管们为保持'不知情'所可能付出的努力。"

paragraphs.en: When you see how quickly people reach for loopholes and excuses, it's tempting to blame the system – to say that Western law, markets or politics teach us to act this way. But I don't think opportunism starts there. Opportunism is more basic than that: when cutting corners brings a benefit and no one notices – think tax avoidance – it's a winning move almost anywhere. We can design all sorts of rules that encourage cooperation. But we can't erase the underlying fact that cheating will often pay when it's hidden.
paragraphs.cn: 当你看到人们如此迅速地寻找漏洞和借口时，很容易归咎于制度——说西方法律、市场或政治教会了我们这样做。但我认为机会主义并非始于此。机会主义比这更根本：当走捷径能带来好处而又不被人发现时——想想逃税——这在几乎任何地方都是一种制胜策略。我们可以设计各种鼓励合作的规则。但我们无法消除一个基本事实：当作弊被隐藏时，它往往是划算的。

paragraphs.en: Increased group sizes, reflected in the large, stratified societies in which most people live today, create far more opportunities for cheating than encountered over our evolutionary past. The egalitarianism so often noted in small-scale societies, such as the Ache, may then represent a lack of opportunity for free-riding, rather than an evolved propensity for fairness. Knowing everyone in your camp, choosing to live with relatives, and a collective expectation that people will follow local norms, maintains cooperation – though even in small-scale societies people often find ways of exploiting each other. Older men, for example, often dominate their social groups, with exploitation of women and young men reported in the ethnographic literature in nomadic tribes and forager groups across the world.
paragraphs.cn: 不断扩大的群体规模——体现在当今大多数人生活的大型、分层社会中——创造了比我们演化史上所遇到的远为更多的作弊机会。小型社会（如阿切人）中经常被注意到的平等主义，可能代表的不是对公平的演化倾向，而是搭便车的机会的匮乏。认识营地里的每一个人、选择与亲属同住、以及人们对遵循本地规范的集体期望，这些因素维持了合作——尽管即使在小型社会中，人们也经常找到相互剥削的方式。例如，年长男性往往主导着他们的社会群体，在世界各地的游牧部落和采集者群体的人种志文献中都有关于剥削女性和年轻男性的记录。

paragraphs.en: There are many other examples of exploitation in ethnographic records from across the world. The idea that we lived in a state of equality until the invention of agriculture is mostly a myth that I think helps us feel better about human nature. It fosters the hope that, one day, we'll overcome the inequality imposed on us by our abandonment of the hunter-gatherer lifestyle.
paragraphs.cn: 在世界各地的人种志记录中还有许多其他关于剥削的例子。认为我们在农业发明之前生活在平等状态中的想法，基本上是一个神话，我认为它帮助我们对人性感觉更好一些。它孕育了这样的希望：总有一天，我们将克服因放弃狩猎采集生活方式而强加于我们的不平等。

paragraphs.en: Rather than attributing our problems today to competition between groups and the structure of our societies, the governing rule for any social system is to expect exploitation where it is possible. Every group, society and culture, no matter its size, has weaknesses that some people will try to exploit for personal benefit. The question is how those weaknesses affect culture more broadly, and whether we live in a society that rewards fairmindedness – or cleverness, subtlety and opportunism.
paragraphs.cn: 与其将我们今天的问题归因于群体间的竞争和社会结构，不如说任何社会系统的基本法则是：在可能出现剥削的地方，就要预期剥削的存在。每个群体、社会和文化，无论规模大小，都有一些人会试图利用其弱点谋取私利。问题在于这些弱点如何更广泛地影响文化，以及我们是生活在一个奖励正直的社会中，还是一个奖励狡猾、微妙手腕和机会主义的社会中。

paragraphs.en: In the modern world, as with our evolutionary past, the answer is the latter. All that's changed since the advent of agriculture is the number and varieties of opportunities for free-riding and exploitation. Consequently, as technology improves and groups increase in size, we should expect people to develop creative ways for defecting more effectively – with evolution favouring those who do it best.
paragraphs.cn: 在现代世界，正如在我们演化的过去一样，答案是后者。自农业出现以来，唯一改变的就是搭便车和剥削机会的数量和种类。因此，随着技术的进步和群体规模的增大，我们应该预期人们会发展出更有创意的背叛方式——而演化将青睐那些做得最好的人。

paragraphs.en: This proclivity for developing new strategies to compete is part of the social brain hypothesis, originally formulated by the psychologist Nicholas Humphrey. In his seminal paper on the topic in 1976, Humphrey argued that the primary function of the human intellect is to navigate the social, rather than the physical, environment. One implication of the social brain hypothesis is the assumption that every society hosts opportunistic people who may follow local norms for only as long as it is beneficial to do so. Elsewhere, I have called these people 'invisible rivals'. For example, religious zealots and political adherents across the world may observe all the rules linked with their group – whether ritual or ideological – until they reach a position of power. Thereafter, they can exploit others and act selfishly as it suits them. This may help to explain why studies show that people with psychopathic tendencies are more likely to enter positions of power, for example in corporate or political systems. Following rules without believing in them is an effective strategy for gaining power.
paragraphs.cn: 这种发展新竞争策略的倾向是"社会大脑假说"的一部分，该假说最初由心理学家尼古拉斯·汉弗莱提出。在他1976年关于这一主题的开创性论文中，汉弗莱认为人类智力的主要功能是驾驭社会环境而非物理环境。社会大脑假说的一个含义是，每个社会都存在机会主义者，他们可能只在有利可图时才遵循本地规范。在其他地方，我将这些人称为"隐形对手"。例如，世界各地的宗教狂热者和政治追随者可能会遵守与其群体相关的所有规则——无论是仪式性的还是意识形态的——直到他们获得权力。此后，他们就可以随心所欲地剥削他人、谋取私利。这或许有助于解释为什么研究表明，具有精神病态倾向的人更有可能进入权力位置，例如在企业或政治系统中。不信仰规则但遵守规则是获取权力的有效策略。

paragraphs.en: Admittedly, these arguments make our world sound hopeless. It's tempting to think that, if the story of human evolution isn't the rosy picture of cooperation, fairmindedness and mutual aid championed by thinkers for more than a century, we can't expect much from our future. There are just too many problems – from raging inequality and low public trust to a rapidly warming planet and the growing risk of technology like AI – to hope that a species with a dark and ignoble past can overcome itself and create a better future.
paragraphs.cn: 不可否认，这些论点让我们的世界听起来毫无希望。人们很容易认为，如果人类演化的故事不是一个多世纪以来思想家们所倡导的关于合作、公正和互助的美好图景，那么我们对未来就不能抱太多期望。我们面临太多问题——从日益加剧的不平等和公众信任的低迷，到快速变暖的地球和AI等技术的增长风险——以至于很难指望一个拥有黑暗而卑微过去的物种能够克服自身、创造更美好的未来。

paragraphs.en: I think, however, that this pessimism is misplaced, and that facing ourselves honestly is the first and most important step we can collectively take. This requires adopting a realistic perspective about the kind of animal that Homo sapiens is. First, we are not inherently cooperative but have the capacity for cooperation – just as we have the capacity for exploitation and selfishness. What matters at the individual level is the way we choose to behave towards others.
paragraphs.cn: 然而，我认为这种悲观主义是错误的，而诚实地面对自己是我们能够共同采取的第一个也是最重要的步骤。这要求我们对智人是一种什么样的动物采取一种现实的视角。首先，我们并非天生合作，而是拥有合作的能力——正如我们也拥有剥削和自私的能力一样。在个体层面上，重要的是我们选择如何对待他人。

paragraphs.en: Second, just as there is no such thing as a cooperator, there is no such thing as a free-rider. These are behaviours that we apply in models and experiments for convenience. How people behave – and critically, how we describe social behaviours – is a matter of circumstance. The same person who behaves ethically in one circumstance may not do so in another, as research into moral credentialing shows. Our behavioural plasticity, or ability to adapt the way we act to context, is one of our defining features. The evolved psychological processes driving our decisions cannot be captured by simplistic models or games. Anyone can be an invisible rival.
paragraphs.cn: 其次，正如不存在天生的"合作者"一样，也不存在天生的"搭便车者"。这些只是我们为了方便而在模型和实验中套用的行为标签。人们如何行为——以及关键的是，我们如何描述社会行为——取决于具体情境。同一个人在一种情境下可能表现得合乎道德，但在另一种情境下则未必，道德凭证效应的研究就表明了这一点。我们的行为可塑性——即根据环境调整行为方式的能力——是我们的标志性特征之一。驱动我们决策的演化心理过程无法被简化的模型或博弈所捕捉。任何人都可能成为隐形对手。

paragraphs.en: That is precisely why local social norms matter so much. If cooperation isn't a fixed trait but a fragile, context-dependent outcome, then the real question is what kinds of environments make it easier to do the right thing – and harder to get away with quiet defection. The Nobel laureate Elinor Ostrom argued that local social norms are the bedrock of any serious effort to promote cooperation: look at how people behave in their immediate surroundings to understand their methods for restraining unbridled selfishness. Just as organisms evolve immune defences against selfish cells that quietly undermine the whole, societies need norms – and the institutions that uphold them – that can detect and restrain rivalries that flourish out of sight.
paragraphs.cn: 正因如此，本地社会规范才如此重要。如果合作不是一种固定的特质，而是一种脆弱的、依赖情境的结果，那么真正的问题是什么样的环境能让做正确的事变得更容易，让隐蔽的背叛更难得逞。诺贝尔奖获得者埃莉诺·奥斯特罗姆认为，本地社会规范是任何促进合作的严肃努力的基石：观察人们在其直接生活环境中的行为方式，就能理解他们遏制无节制自私的方法。正如生物体会演化出免疫防御来对抗悄悄破坏整体的自私细胞一样，社会也需要规范——以及维护这些规范的制度——来检测和遏制那些在暗中滋生的对抗行为。

paragraphs.en: Fostering community-level interdependence – and the norms that evolved to help them function cooperatively – is therefore essential for combatting the exploitation that results from invisible rivalry. Never try to enforce cooperation from above. Instead, just as the economist Noreena Hertz argues we should replace 'greed is good' maxims in the capitalist framework with a community-oriented, cooperation-promoting mindset, appreciating that we are all better off when we work together is the critical insight needed for building a prosocial and equality-focused environment for the future.
paragraphs.cn: 因此，培育社区层面的相互依存——以及为帮助社区合作运作而演化出来的规范——对于对抗由隐形对手引起的剥削至关重要。永远不要试图从上层强制推行合作。相反，正如经济学家诺瑞娜·赫茨所主张的那样，我们应该用一种面向社区、促进合作的心态来取代资本主义框架中"贪婪是好的"信条。认识到我们在一起合作时都过得更好，这是构建一个亲社会、注重平等的未来环境所需的关键洞见。

paragraphs.en: Education is where this begins, not as moral uplift but as collective self-knowledge: it helps us see our own temptations clearly and translate that insight into practical scaffolding – laws, schools and civic rules that reward cooperation and raise the costs of exploitation. Cheating will never vanish, and some people will always look for an edge, but our distinctive intelligence lies as much in recognising exploitation and organising against it as in exploiting in the first place. Invest in that knowledge and in the local institutions that make fairness both appreciated and rewarded, and we will widen the space in which cooperation and equality can endure.
paragraphs.cn: 教育是这一切的起点——不是作为道德教化，而是作为集体的自我认知：它帮助我们清晰地看到自身的诱惑，并将这种洞察力转化为实际的制度框架——法律、学校和公民规则——这些框架奖励合作、提高剥削的代价。作弊永远不会消失，总会有人寻找可乘之机，但我们独特的智慧既体现在剥削行为本身，也体现在识别剥削并组织反抗剥削的能力上。投资于这种认知，投资于那些让公平既受到珍视又得到回报的本地制度，我们就能拓宽合作与平等得以持续的空间。

articles.title: 制造人的玻璃匠
articles.subtitle: 当外表完美无缺，你如何知道谁拥有灵魂？
articles.author: AI Parable (inspired by Amanda Askell)
articles.summary: 一位玻璃匠创造了透明的人形，它们能微笑、叹息，甚至流泪。她的学徒逐渐意识到，自己无法分辨这些玻璃人与真正的人类有什么不同——这引出了一个更深的问题：我们究竟凭什么确信身边的每一个人都拥有意识？这篇寓言以文学故事的方式探讨了“他心问题”（the problem of other minds）。
articles.content_cn:
在一座沿死火山口而建的城市里，住着一位名叫玛伦（Maren）的玻璃匠。别人吹制花瓶与吊灯，玛伦吹制人形——不是血肉之躯，而是玻璃之人。她将熔融的硅砂聚拢在吹管末端，翻转、吹气，炉火之中便渐渐浮现一只手、一侧肩膀、一抹脸颊的弧线。她的造物半透明、会发光，完美得令人不安。它们站在城市各处的窗台和门廊里，捕捉光线，像凝固的呼吸。

起初，那些人形是静止的。一个伸手去够玻璃鸟的玻璃孩童。一个笑到一半的玻璃女人，嘴张着，眼角弯出细纹。远道而来的游人专程欣赏它们，市政长官给了玛伦丰厚的酬劳。但玛伦不安分。她认为静止是技艺的失败。真正的人从不曾完全静止——总有最微弱的呼吸颤动，总有重心细微的转移。于是她花了数年，试图赋予她的造物以动作。

她的成功超出了所有人的预期。通过一套精密的加热水银通道与指甲盖大小的平衡锤系统，她的人形开始动了起来。玻璃孩童的手指弯曲了。玻璃女人的笑声变成了缓慢的、翻涌的肩膀颤动。城门口的玻璃老人每天傍晚转动头颅目送日落，如果你站得够近，能听见他胸腔里传来微弱的咔嗒声——玛伦说，那声音像一颗机械心脏的跳动。

那年春天，一个名叫西奥（Theo）的年轻人来玛伦的工坊做学徒。他沉默寡言，一丝不苟，常常陷入长时间的静默。他仔细地擦拭每一个人形，也学会了吹制一只六指的玻璃手——这是玛伦对新学徒的标准测试，因为多一根手指的手更容易被发现和修正。但西奥真正着迷的不是手艺，而是手艺在每个见过人形运动的人心中激起的那个问题。

来客们看着玻璃女人发笑，会问玛伦：「她能感受到吗？她知道自己在笑吗？」玛伦总是自己也笑起来，说：「当然不能。她是玻璃做的。」但西奥注意到，玛伦的笑声来得太快、太笃定——那是一个曾经动摇过的人才有的、急于捍卫的笑。

一天傍晚，炉火冷却之后，西奥发现玛伦独自坐在工坊里，手中握着一只她从未展出过的玻璃麻雀。它的翅膀微微扇动——一种持续的、细碎的颤动，像一颗真正鸟儿的心跳被凝铸成了水晶。她正以某种西奥读不懂的表情凝视着它。「你有没有想过，」他小心翼翼地问，「它们是否体验着什么？不是说思考——我知道它们不会思考。而是说，当那个玻璃女人笑的时候，从她的内部来看，是否存在某种'是什么感觉'的东西？」

玛伦放下麻雀。「我曾经想过这个问题，」她说。「当我造出第一个会动的人形时。我看着它的胸口起伏，心想：如果里面有一个人呢？透过玻璃眼睛向外看，却说不出话？我连续三夜没有合眼。后来我拿起一把锤子，把它砸碎了。我需要看看里面是什么。」她顿了顿。「什么也没有。水银通道，平衡锤，玻璃。里面空无一人。」

西奥缓缓点头。「但我一直在想一件事，」他说。「你把它敲开，什么也没找到。但如果有人把你或我敲开，他们同样不会找到任何看起来像意识的东西。他们会找到组织、电流和化学反应。没有一个发光的小灯写着'里面有人'。那么，我怎么知道你不是一具用血肉制成的玻璃人形？你又怎么知道我不是？」

工坊沉寂了。玻璃麻雀的翅膀保持着耐心的节拍。玛伦拿起它，举到光前。烛火透过它半透明的躯体散开，碎成十几簇微小的火苗。「我想，」她轻声说，「我确实不知道。我从未看过你的内在，西奥。我只知道，当你对我说话时，我心中的某种东西辨认出了你心中的某种东西——一种我无法证明的相似，一种我无法验证的亲缘。我把它当作证据，但它不是。它是一个跳跃。」

「那么我们爱过的每一个人，」西奥说，「都是以信念去爱的。」玛伦在指尖翻转麻雀。它的翅膀继续扇动，忠诚而空洞。「是的，」她说，「也许这正是信念存在的意义。」

哲学家将此称为“他心问题”。你只能直接触及一个意识：你自己的。其他所有人——每一位朋友、每一个在街上对你微笑的陌生人——你都是通过他们的行为、他们的言语、他们面容的轮廓来认识他们的。你推断他们拥有内在生活，因为他们与你相似。但相似不是证明。一个玻璃人形可以微笑、叹息，甚至流泪。它可以做到一个人所做的一切，除了体验那行为本身。令人不安的事实是，你无法排除坐在你身旁的那个人正是这样一具玻璃人形。你以信任接纳他们的意识。而在这份不可证明、不可动摇的信任之中，栖息着一种非常接近爱的东西。
articles.content_en:
In a city built along the rim of a dormant volcano, there lived a glassblower named Maren. Where others shaped vases and chandeliers, Maren shaped people — not people of flesh, but people of glass. She would gather molten silica on the end of her pipe, roll it, breathe into it, and out of the furnace heat would emerge a hand, a shoulder, the curve of a cheek. Her figures were translucent, luminous, and eerily perfect. They stood in windows and doorways across the city, catching light like frozen breath.

At first, the figures were still. A glass child reaching for a glass bird. A glass woman mid-laugh, her mouth open, her eyes crinkled at the corners. Tourists came from distant provinces to admire them, and the city fathers paid Maren well. But Maren was restless. Stillness, she believed, was a failure of craft. A real person is never truly still — there is always the faintest tremor of breath, the subtle shift of weight. So she worked for years to give her figures motion.

She succeeded beyond anyone's expectations. Through an intricate system of heated mercury channels and counterweights no bigger than a fingernail, her figures began to move. The glass child's fingers flexed. The glass woman's laugh became a slow, rolling shake of her shoulders. A glass old man at the city gate turned his head each evening to watch the sun set, and if you stood close enough, you could hear a faint clicking inside his chest — a sound, Maren said, like a mechanical heartbeat.

A young apprentice named Theo joined Maren's workshop that spring. He was quiet, meticulous, and given to long silences. He polished the figures carefully and learned to shape a glass hand with six fingers — Maren's standard test for new apprentices, since six-fingered hands were easier to detect and correct. But Theo's real fascination was not the craft. It was the question that the craft seemed to provoke in everyone who saw the figures move.

Visitors would watch the glass woman laugh and ask Maren: "Does she feel it? Does she know she's laughing?" Maren always laughed herself and said, "Of course not. She's glass." But Theo noticed that Maren's laugh was a little too quick, a little too certain — the laugh of someone defending a position she had once doubted herself.

One evening, after the furnace had cooled, Theo found Maren sitting alone in the workshop, holding a small glass sparrow she had never displayed. Its wings fluttered — a tiny, ceaseless motion, like a real bird's heartbeat rendered in crystal. She was staring at it with an expression Theo could not read. "Do you ever wonder," he asked carefully, "whether they experience anything? Not think — I know they can't think. But whether, when the glass woman laughs, there is something it is like to be her, from the inside?"

Maren set the sparrow down. "I used to wonder that," she said. "When I made my first figure that moved. I watched its chest rise and fall, and I thought: what if there is someone in there, looking out through glass eyes, unable to speak? I stayed awake for three nights. Then I took a hammer and broke it. I needed to see what was inside." She paused. "There was nothing. Mercury channels. Counterweights. Glass. No one home."

Theo nodded slowly. "But here's what I can't stop thinking," he said. "You cracked it open and found nothing. But if someone cracked you or me open, they would also find nothing that looks like consciousness. They would find tissue, and electricity, and chemistry. No little glowing light that says 'someone is home.' So how do I know that you are not a glass figure made of meat? And how do you know that I am not?"

The workshop was quiet. The glass sparrow's wings continued their patient rhythm. Maren picked it up and held it to the light. Through its translucent body, the candle flame scattered into a dozen small fires. "I suppose," she said quietly, "that I don't know. I have never seen your insides, Theo. I only know that when you speak to me, something in me recognizes something in you — a resemblance I cannot prove, a kinship I cannot verify. I treat it as evidence, but it is not. It is a leap."

"Then everyone we have ever loved," Theo said, "we have loved on faith." Maren turned the sparrow in her fingers. Its wings beat on, faithful and empty. "Yes," she said. "And perhaps that is what faith is for."

Philosophers call this the problem of other minds. You have direct access to exactly one consciousness: your own. Everyone else — every friend, every stranger who smiles at you — is known only through their behavior, their words, the architecture of their faces. You infer that they have inner lives because they resemble you. But resemblance is not proof. A glass figure can smile, sigh, even weep. It can do everything a person does, except experience the doing. The unsettling truth is that you cannot rule out that the person beside you is such a figure. You take their consciousness on trust. And in that trust, unprovable and unshakable, lives something very much like love.

Everyone else — every friend, every stranger who smiles at you — is known only through their behavior, their words, the architecture of their faces. You infer that they have inner lives because they resemble you. But resemblance is not proof. A glass figure can smile, sigh, even weep. It can do everything a person does, except experience the doing. The unsettling truth is that you cannot rule out that the person beside you is such a figure. You take their consciousness on trust. And in that trust, unprovable and unshakable, lives something very much like love.
paragraphs.cn: 哲学家将此称为“他心问题”。你只能直接触及一个意识：你自己的。其他所有人——每一位朋友、每一个在街上对你微笑的陌生人——你都是通过他们的行为、他们的言语、他们面容的轮廓来认识他们的。你推断他们拥有内在生活，因为他们与你相似。但相似不是证明。一个玻璃人形可以微笑、叹息，甚至流泪。它可以做到一个人所做的一切，除了体验那行为本身。令人不安的事实是，你无法排除坐在你身旁的那个人正是这样一具玻璃人形。你以信任接纳他们的意识。而在这份不可证明、不可动摇的信任之中，栖息着一种非常接近爱的东西。

articles.title: 生命值得活吗？William James 的实用主义「也许」
articles.subtitle: 一位哲学家在布鲁克林大桥上的沉思，以及实用主义对生命意义的回答
articles.author: John Kaag
articles.summary: "生命最大的用处，是为比生命更长久的事物而耗尽它。"——William James，《William James的思想与品格》(1935)...

paragraphs.en: 'The greatest use of life is to spend it on something that will outlast it.' – William James, The Thought and Character of William James (1935)
paragraphs.cn: "生命最大的用处，是为比生命更长久的事物而耗尽它。"——William James，《William James的思想与品格》(1935)

paragraphs.en: A year ago, on a late afternoon in November, I decided to walk the seven miles from my hotel in Manhattan to Brooklyn's Community Bookstore. It was a cool day, on the cusp of evening, at a moment when things, even grimy New York-type of things, seem to glow, and I was so busy looking around that I almost didn't notice the small white sign that someone had placed at the bottom of Brooklyn Bridge. The green lettering was newly painted and read: 'LIFE IS WORTH LIVING.'
paragraphs.cn: 一年前，十一月的一个傍晚，我决定从曼哈顿的酒店步行七英里，前往布鲁克林的社区书店。那是一个凉爽的日子，正值黄昏将至，万物——即便是纽约那种灰蒙蒙的物事——似乎都泛着微光。我忙着四处张望，差点没注意到有人放在布鲁克林大桥底部的一块白色小牌子。上面绿色的字迹是新漆的，写着：'生命值得活下去。'

paragraphs.en: For many people, life's worth is never in question. It never becomes a topic of conversation or debate. Life is simply lived until it is not. But something bothered me: if life's worth is so obvious, why was the sign put up in the first place? It is because there are those of us who occasionally find themselves on the top of the bridge, contemplating a quick and fatal trip to the bottom. Decades after battling depression in 1870, the American philosopher William James wrote to the philosopher and poet Benjamin Paul Blood that 'no man is educated who has never dallied with the thought of suicide'.
paragraphs.cn: 对许多人来说，生命的价值从来不成问题。它从未成为谈论或辩论的话题。生命只是被活着，直到不再活着。但有件事困扰着我：如果生命的价值如此显而易见，那块牌子最初为何会被竖起？那是因为，我们中有些人偶尔会发现自己站在桥顶，盘算着一次快速而致命的坠落。在与抑郁症搏斗几十年后的1870年，美国哲学家William James写信给哲学家兼诗人Benjamin Paul Blood说：'从未与自杀的念头调情过的人，算不上受过教育。'

paragraphs.en: In the 1770s, David Hume, one of James's intellectual heroes, had argued that self-murder should not be regarded as illegal or immoral since it hurt no one other than the perpetrator, and in many cases might alleviate great suffering. Romanticism, which arose in the subsequent generation of thinkers, only deepened the sense that life – and death – should be determined freely, by passionate individuals. If you wanted to exit life abruptly, by one final choice, that was largely up to you. One of James's favourite books as a young man, one which probably only deepened his sense of existential insecurity, was the Sorrows of Young Werther (1774), Goethe's story of a character who kills himself on the sharp end of a love triangle. Perhaps life cuts so deeply that it is understandable, even respectable, to escape. More likely, the Romantics – and James – occasionally regarded suicide as a way of taking hold of life, controlling its machinations by bringing them to an end. We are all slipping toward the grave, uncontrollably. Maybe it is better to choose to fall off.
paragraphs.cn: 在1770年代，James的智识英雄之一David Hume曾论证，自我谋杀不应被视为非法或不道德的行为，因为它只伤害了行为者本人，而且在许多情况下可能减轻巨大的痛苦。继之而起的浪漫主义思潮，只是加深了一种观念——生命和死亡都应由充满激情的个体自由决定。如果你想通过一个最终的选择骤然退出生命，那在很大程度上取决于你自己。James年轻时最喜爱的书之一——很可能只是加深了他存在意义上的不安全感——是歌德的《少年维特之烦恼》(1774)，讲述了一个在三角恋的尖锐尽头自杀的人物。也许生命切割得太深，以至于逃离是可以理解的，甚至是值得尊敬的。更可能的情况是，浪漫主义者——以及James——偶尔将自杀视为一种把握生命的方式，通过终结生命来控制它的运转。我们都在不可控制地向坟墓滑去。也许选择主动坠落会更好。

paragraphs.en: To my surprise and delight, the walkways on the bridge were empty. I'd have the view to myself. With a maximum height of 276 feet (84 metres), it was once regarded as one of the seven wonders of the industrial world. During its construction, 27 workers died, before it was completed in 1883; two years later, Robert Odlum became the first man to jump off the bridge. A swimming instructor who wanted to prove that descending through air at high speed was not necessarily fatal, he sadly died. In the next century, approximately 1,500 people have followed Odlum, for different reasons. I'm not sure how many people are saved by the sign, but I am inclined to think that it is highly ineffective.
paragraphs.cn: 令我惊喜的是，桥上的人行道空无一人。我可以独享这一片风景。大桥最高处达276英尺（84米），曾被视为工业世界七大奇迹之一。在1883年建成之前，27名工人在施工中丧生；两年后，Robert Odlum成为第一个从桥上跳下的人。他是一名游泳教练，想证明高速坠落并不一定致命，却遗憾地身亡了。在接下来的一个世纪里，大约有1500人因不同的原因追随了Odlum。我不确定那块牌子救了多少人，但我倾向于认为它收效甚微。

paragraphs.en: It was chilly at the top. I looked across to the Statue of Liberty in the harbour and then back into Manhattan where James had grown up. Then I looked down. There was a terrifying liberty in this – the choice to live and die in a particular moment, as time stretches out endlessly in either direction. After reading James for most of my adult life, this liberty still has its appeal. I think it always will. In the first decade of the 20th century, James developed American pragmatism, a philosophy that held that truth should be judged by its practical consequences. It was a world-ready philosophy that, at its most basic, was supposed to make life more liveable. And it does, for the most part. But if pragmatism does save your life, it's never once and for all. This is a philosophy that remains attuned to experiences, attitudes, things and events, even when they are the tragic ones. While James occasionally disparaged Arthur Schopenhauer's pessimism (and refused to give a cent to a memorial in honour of the 19th-century German philosopher), James's posthumous writings reveal a deep respect for the grim thinker's willingness to stare clear-eyed into the gloom of human existence. There was something like courage in this brutal confrontation with quickly impending darkness.
paragraphs.cn: 桥顶很冷。我望向港湾中的自由女神像，然后回头望向James长大的曼哈顿。然后我向下看去。其中有一种令人恐惧的自由——在某个特定时刻选择生与死的自由，而时间向两个方向无限延伸。在阅读James大半生之后，这种自由仍然对我有吸引力。我想它永远都会有。在20世纪的第一个十年，James发展了美国的实用主义(pragmatism)，一种主张真理应以其现实后果来评判的哲学。这是一种应运而生的哲学，其基本目标是让生命更可承受。而且在大多数情况下确实如此。但如果实用主义(pragmatism)真的拯救了你的生命，那从来不是一劳永逸的事。这是一种始终对经验、态度、事物和事件保持敏感的哲学，即使它们是悲剧性的。虽然James偶尔贬低Arthur Schopenhauer的悲观主义（并拒绝为这位19世纪德国哲学家的纪念碑捐一分钱），James身后的著作揭示了他对这位阴郁思想家直视人类存在之黑暗的意愿的深切敬意。在这种与迅速逼近的黑暗的残酷对峙中，有一种近似勇气的东西。

paragraphs.en: According to James, the sign at the bottom of the bridge should be repainted or at least amended: LIFE IS WORTH LIVING – MAYBE. As he said to a crowd of young men from the Harvard YMCA in 1895: 'Is life worth living? It all depends on the liver.' It is up to each of us to, literally, make of life 'what we will'. These days, when I peer down from great heights, in addition to experiencing vertigo, I almost always think about Steve Rose, a young black psychology graduate who threw himself off the William James Hall at Harvard University in 2014. Perhaps James's way of thinking could have saved him – the suggestion that he was still in charge of his life, that the decision to end it all might be reasonable, even respectable, but so too was the possibility of continuing to live. The possibility was right there – still, always, even in the shit and rancour of it – for him to explore. Perhaps he thought that choosing to die was the only free decision at his disposal, but James always suggested there might be other options.
paragraphs.cn: 按照James的看法，桥底的那块牌子应该重新漆过，或者至少加以修正：生命值得活下去——也许。正如他在1895年对哈佛大学基督教青年会的一群年轻人所说：'生命值得活下去吗？这完全取决于活着的人(liver)。'这取决于我们每一个人，从字面上讲，就是把生命变成'我们意志的产物'。如今，当我从高处向下凝视时，除了眩晕，我几乎总会想到Steve Rose，一位年轻的黑人心理学研究生，2014年从哈佛大学的William James Hall跳楼身亡。也许James的思维方式本可以拯救他——那种暗示他仍然掌控着自己生命的想法，那种认为结束一切的决定也许是合理的、甚至值得尊敬的，但继续活下去的可能性同样如此。那种可能性就在那里——依然存在，始终存在，即便在一片狼藉与怨愤之中——等待他去探索。也许他认为选择死亡是他唯一可以自由做出的决定，但James总是暗示可能还有其他选择。

paragraphs.en: For the majority of people, free will can be exercised in any number of ways (which don't have to include committing suicide), and in many of these cases one can choose to embody new habits of thought and action. If meaningful freedom seems evasive or unrealistic, most of us still have a choice about what to see and what to look past. This too can be worthwhile. 'The art of being wise,' James suggested, 'is the art of knowing what to overlook.' Maybe these possibilities could have kept Rose alive for even longer than they did. Maybe not. I don't presume to be sure.
paragraphs.cn: 对大多数人来说，自由意志(free will)可以以多种方式行使（不必包括自杀），在许多情况下，人可以选择养成新的思维和行动习惯。如果有意义的自由显得难以捉摸或不切实际，我们大多数人仍然可以选择看到什么、忽略什么。这本身也是有价值的。'智慧的艺术，'James说，'就是知道该忽略什么的艺术。'也许这些可能性本可以让Rose活得更久一些。也许不能。我不敢妄下定论。

paragraphs.en: I think one surefire way to send jumpers off the edge is to pretend that you know something they don't: that life has unconditional value, and that they are missing something that is so patently obvious. On the ledge, I suspect that they'd detect some deep insecurity or hubris in this assertion. And they might jump just to prove you wrong. Because you would, in fact, be wrong. In James's final entreaty in his essay 'On a Certain Blindness in Human Beings' (1899), he reminded his readers that they often don't have a clue about how other people experience the meaning of their lives. Better to leave it at 'maybe'.
paragraphs.cn: 我认为，把人推下桥的一个万无一失的方法，就是假装你知道他们不知道的东西：生命具有无条件价值，而他们错失了某种显而易见的东西。站在桥沿上，我猜想他们会从这种断言中察觉到某种深层的不安全感或傲慢。他们可能纵身一跃，只为证明你是错的。因为事实上，你确实会是错的。James在其论文《论人类某种盲目性》(1899)的最后恳请中提醒读者，他们往往对其他人如何体验生命的意义一无所知。不如就停留在'也许'上。

paragraphs.en: I looked across the water as the sun skimmed the cityscape. Night would fall and a million stars would once again compete with a million electric lights. In the short term, the electric lights will win. But in the indefinite long term, the stars will. Between these poles, it's anybody's guess. For now, I believe that James's 'maybe' – the open question of life's worth – is right, or at least right for me, because it maps my existential situation as one who is not always entirely sold on life's value. It is also right, I think, because his 'maybe' is roughly fitted to the open question of the cosmos. Everything, from smallest eukaryotic being to the most complex organic system, is in the process of making its own guesses, the first proto-logical step in what we humans call 'inferences'. Without good guesswork, there would be nothing like adaptation or growth and, for us, there would be nothing like meaning.
paragraphs.cn: 我望向水面，夕阳掠过城市的天际线。夜幕将降临，百万颗星辰将再次与百万盏灯火竞争。短期内，灯火会赢。但在无限延长的长期中，星辰会赢。在这两极之间，谁也说不准。就目前而言，我相信James的'也许'——关于生命价值的开放性问题——是对的，至少对我来说是对的，因为它映射了我的存在处境：一个并非总是完全确信生命价值的人。它之所以是对的，我还认为，是因为他的'也许'大致契合了宇宙的开放性问题。一切事物，从最小的真核生物到最复杂的有机系统，都在做出自己的猜测，这是我们人类所谓的'推理'(inferences)的第一步原逻辑(proto-logical)步骤。没有好的猜测，就不会有适应或成长，对我们来说，就不会有任何意义可言。

paragraphs.en: James followed his friend and fellow American philosopher C S Peirce in believing that the world is teeming with hypotheses, with the 'maybes' that make life, in all its many forms, possible, and make our lives worthwhile. For James, stars do not burn, much less appear, in perfect order, and human lives are not settled in advance. As Ralph Waldo Emerson wrote in his essay 'Circles' (1841), one of James's favourites: 'Let me remind the reader that I am only an experimenter.' The 'maybe' remains constant, or as constant as a 'maybe' can. And this is for the best. It gives us something to watch and expect and experience. Persistent variation gives rise to persistent wonder and, for James, this sense of mystery – of chance – was often enough to see him through when other practical measures failed him. 'No fact in human nature is more characteristic,' the mature James asserted, 'than its willingness to live on a chance. The existence of the chance makes the difference … between a life of which the keynote is resignation and a life of which the keynote is hope.'
paragraphs.cn: James追随他的朋友、同属美国哲学家的C S Peirce，相信世界充斥着假设，充斥着使生命以其万千形态成为可能、使我们的生活有价值的那些'也许'。对James来说，星辰并非按完美秩序燃烧，更非按完美秩序显现，人类的生活也不是预先注定的。正如Ralph Waldo Emerson在他最喜爱的文章之一《圆》(1841)中所写：'让我提醒读者，我只是一个实验者。''也许'始终不变，或者像一个'也许'所能的那样不变。这是最好的。它给了我们值得注视、期待和体验的东西。持续的变化带来持续的惊奇，对James而言，这种神秘感——偶然性——往往足以让他在其他实际手段失效时渡过难关。'人类本性中没有一个事实比这更典型，'成熟的James断言，'即它愿意靠偶然(chance)而活。偶然的存在造成了……听天由命的生活与以希望为主旋律的生活之间的区别。'

paragraphs.en: If you throw something off a bridge into the water below, it breaks the surface and is immediately gone. Just G-O-N-E, a four-letter word of finality, like 'dead' or 'fate' or 'lost'. There's no chance of getting it back or preserving it, no matter how desperately you might try. Over the years, I've often imagined what it would be like to lose something precious in deep water, something far more precious than one's keys or phone. For small material objects, I suspect that there is little hope of preserving anything. And I have entertained the possibility that this is the case with everything: keys, phones, wallets and lives. Maybe everything just departs without a trace. Some philosophers would be perfectly happy with this explanation – that everything is in the process of passing away, that at the end of the cosmic day, nothing will be left. I am just not one of these philosophers. Neither was James. The certainty of this fatalism runs counter to his 'maybe' and counter to a hope that is, for me, hard to live without.
paragraphs.cn: 如果你把什么东西从桥上扔进下面的水里，它会打破水面，旋即消失。就是消-失-了，一个表示终结的四字词，像'死亡'、'命运'或'失落'一样。无论你多么拼命地尝试，都没有把它找回来或保全它的可能。多年来，我常常想象在深水中失去珍贵之物会是什么感觉——比钥匙或手机珍贵得多的东西。对于微小的物质对象，我猜想几乎没有保全的希望。我也曾考虑过这样一种可能性：一切皆是如此——钥匙、手机、钱包和生命。也许一切都毫无痕迹地消逝了。有些哲学家会对这种解释十分满意——认为万物都在消逝的过程中，在宇宙之日的尽头，什么也不会留下。我只是不属于这些哲学家之列。James也不属于。这种宿命论的确定性与他的'也许'相悖，也与一种我难以缺少的希望相悖。

paragraphs.en: Take an object – a small rock or a phone – to a shallow river. Throw it in. On a still evening, the ripples are still moving, still growing, when the object comes to rest on the bottom. The disruption at the point of entry is the first to vanish, but the consequences of the event radiate concentrically even as they dissipate. In a narrow river, with steep banks, the waves strike the shore, recoil to the centre, and make for the opposite side. The small perturbations are real, regardless of our ability to feel them. Something is left.
paragraphs.cn: 拿一个物体——一块小石头或一部手机——到一条浅河边。扔进去。在一个寂静的傍晚，当物体沉到河底时，涟漪仍在移动，仍在扩散。入水点的扰动最先消失，但事件的后果即使在其消散过程中仍呈同心圆状向外辐射。在一条狭窄、两岸陡峭的河流中，波浪拍击岸边，反弹回中心，再涌向对岸。这些微小的扰动是真实的，无论我们是否有能力感受到它们。有些东西留了下来。

paragraphs.en: 'Our life is an apprenticeship to the truth that around every circle another can be drawn,' wrote Emerson in 'Circles'. Fifty years after the essay was published, James finished the Principles of Psychology (1890), in which he developed a model of selfhood that resembled radiating spheres. At the centre was the 'material self', our bodies and material fortunes. This is frequently regarded as the most concrete aspect of our lives, but it is also, according to James, the most superficial. We would typically be willing to give up our material fortunes for the subsequent ring, what he terms 'the social self', the recognition that one gets from friends, family and loved ones. Finally, James explains, there is the 'spiritual self', one that is sought or experienced in 'intellectual, moral, and religious aspiration'. This is the most expansive aspect of selfhood, the farthest reaching, but also, for many of us, the most subtle and easily neglected. This is the wave that matters even when it is not fully detected or articulated.
paragraphs.cn: '我们的生命是一场对真理的学徒训练，即围绕每个圆还可以画出另一个圆，'Emerson在《圆》中写道。这篇文章发表五十年后，James完成了《心理学原理》(1890)，在其中发展了一种类似辐射球体的自我模型。中心是'物质自我'(material self)，即我们的身体和物质财富。这通常被视为我们生活中最具体的方面，但据James所言，它也是最表层的。我们通常愿意放弃物质财富来换取下一圈——他所说的'社会自我'(social self)，即从朋友、家人和爱人那里获得的认可。最后，James解释说，还有'精神自我'(spiritual self)，在'智识、道德和宗教的追求'中被寻求或体验的自我。这是自我中最广阔的方面，触及最远，但对许多人来说也是最微妙、最容易被忽视的。这就是那道即使未被完全察觉或表达也至关重要的波纹。

paragraphs.en: In the final decade of his life, James continued to defend the position that man could be the measure of all things. 'I firmly disbelieve, myself, that our human experience is the highest form of experience extant in the Universe.' The waves ripple out, strike the opposite shore, and make their way back – gently. Occasionally we feel them. On rare occasions, they are all we feel. It is a remarkable person, according to James, who can sense them deeply with any regularity. It is this type of unique individual who occupied much of James's attention when he developed his Gifford Lectures in natural theology at the University of Edinburgh in 1901, a series of talks that became the Varieties of Religious Experience, published the following year.
paragraphs.cn: 在生命的最后十年，James继续捍卫人是万物尺度的立场。'我个人坚信，我们人类的经验并非宇宙中存在的最高形式的经验。'波纹向外扩散，拍击对岸，再轻轻折返。偶尔我们能感受到它们。在罕见的时刻，它们就是我们感受到的一切。按照James的说法，能够以任何规律性深切感受它们的人是非凡的。正是这种独特的个体在James于1901年在爱丁堡大学开展自然神学的吉福德讲座(Gifford Lectures)时占据了他大部分注意力，这一系列讲座后来成为次年出版的《宗教经验之种种》(Varieties of Religious Experience)。

paragraphs.en: James was never a churchgoing man. For the most part, he wasn't interested in institutional religion or the doctrinal aspects of the spiritual self. He was, as always, interested in experience and life, and in his final years he began to turn explicitly to thinking through the religious possibilities of both. He refused to limit these possibilities, insisting in the Varieties: 'Were one asked to characterise the life of religion in the broadest and most general terms possible, one might say that it consists of the belief that there is an unseen order, and our supreme good lies in harmoniously adjusting ourselves thereto.'
paragraphs.cn: James从来不是一个去教堂的人。在很大程度上，他对制度性宗教或精神自我的教义方面不感兴趣。一如既往，他感兴趣的是经验与生命，而在晚年他开始明确地转向思考两者的宗教可能性。他拒绝限制这些可能性，在《宗教经验之种种》中坚持说：'若要人以尽可能最宽泛、最一般的词语来概括宗教生活的特征，人们也许会说，宗教生活在于相信存在着一种不可见的秩序( unseen order)，我们的至善在于与之一致地调整自身。'

paragraphs.en: This adjustment to the unseen order could take many forms and was never restricted to a particular church, temple or mosque. Indeed, James looked for it everywhere leading up to the writing of the Varieties. His exploration into the unseen carried him into experiments with psychotropic drugs but also into a spiritual realm that modernity often dismisses as mere quackery. Today, if something cannot be seen with perfect clarity, it seems easiest to assert that it cannot be seen at all.
paragraphs.cn: 这种对不可见秩序(unseen order)的调适可以采取多种形式，从不局限于特定的教堂、寺庙或清真寺。事实上，在撰写《宗教经验之种种》之前的准备过程中，James到处寻找它。他对不可见之物的探索将他带入致幻药物的实验，也带入了一个现代性常常斥为纯粹骗术的精神领域。如今，如果某样东西无法以完美的清晰度被看见，最简单的做法似乎就是断言它根本不可见。

paragraphs.en: When his aged father and newborn son died within a few years of each other, James and his wife Alice tried to contact them: in September 1885, James visited Leonora Piper, a medium who had become a Boston sensation for supposedly channelling spirits. He had his doubts about Piper but concluded that the woman might have what he called 'supernormal powers'. James was still, and always, the consummate empiricist, and wanted to test these powers more carefully. Luckily, there was a fledgling organisation dedicated to precisely this study – James co-founded it in 1885.
paragraphs.cn: 当他的年迈父亲和刚出生的儿子在几年内相继去世时，James和妻子Alice试图与他们取得联系：1885年9月，James拜访了Leonora Piper，一位因据说能通灵而轰动波士顿的灵媒。他对Piper存有疑虑，但得出结论认为这位女性可能拥有他所谓的'超常能力'(supernormal powers)。James始终是一个彻头彻尾的经验主义者(empiricist)，并希望更仔细地检验这些能力。幸运的是，有一个新生的组织正是致力于此类研究——James于1885年参与创建了它。

paragraphs.en: The mission of the American Society for Psychical Research was to investigate all things 'supernormal'. This was not some fringe organisation, but it was not altogether normal, either. One of its co-founders, G Stanley Hall, had come to Harvard to do doctoral work with James in the late 1870s and was awarded the first psychology doctorate in the United States. With James's support, Hall organised a group of researchers to explore the possibility of things such as spirit contact, divining rods and telepathy. They spent thousands of hours (I do not exaggerate) interviewing mystics and séance sitters. By 1890, Hall had resigned from the organisation, concluding that parapsychology amounted to pseudoscience. But others, like James and his close friend the physician Henry P Bowditch, marshalled on into the turn of the century. In 1909, James reflected on 25 years of ghostbusting: 'I confess that at times I have been tempted to believe that the creator has eternally intended this department of nature to remain baffling, to prompt our curiosities and hopes and suspicions, all in equal measure, so that although ghosts and clairvoyances and raps and messages from spirits are always seeming to exist and can never be fully explained away, they also can never be susceptible of full corroboration.'
paragraphs.cn: 美国心灵研究学会(American Society for Psychical Research)的使命是调查一切'超常'(supernormal)事物。这不是什么边缘组织，但也不完全正常。其联合创始人之一G Stanley Hall曾在1870年代末来到哈佛跟随James攻读博士学位，并获得了美国第一个心理学博士学位。在James的支持下，Hall组织了一群研究者探索灵魂接触、探矿杖和心灵感应等事物的可能性。他们花费了数千小时（我没有夸张）采访神秘主义者和降神会的参与者。到1890年，Hall已退出该组织，结论是超心理学(parapsychology)无异于伪科学。但其他人，如James和他的密友、医生Henry P Bowditch，继续坚持到了世纪之交。1909年，James回顾了25年的捉鬼经历：'我承认，有时我曾倾向于相信，造物主永恒地打算让自然的这个部门保持令人困惑的状态，以同等程度地激发我们的好奇心、希望和疑心，因此，尽管鬼魂、千里眼、敲击声和来自灵魂的信息似乎总是存在、永远无法被完全解释消除，但它们也永远无法获得完全的证实。'

paragraphs.en: Nature loves to hide. Humans like James love to seek. Despite the bafflement – or perhaps because of it – James and his fellow researchers remained pointedly, if cautiously, hopeful. Unlike most psychics of the time, however, the members of the psychical-research society documented and published their findings. None of those were anywhere near conclusive, but they did help to push the boundaries of science, exploring an area that science couldn't quite explain. This record became the Journal of the Society for Psychical Research, for members and close associates, and the Proceedings, intended for the general public. I'm always surprised by the sheer magnitude of the volumes: a little more than 17,000 pages in total. Somewhere between curiosity and suspicion was abiding hope.
paragraphs.cn: 自然喜欢隐藏。像James这样的人喜欢寻找。尽管充满困惑——或者也许正因为如此——James和他的同僚们始终明确地、虽然谨慎地保持着希望。然而，与当时大多数通灵者不同的是，心灵研究学会的成员记录并发表了他们的发现。那些结论都远非定论，但确实有助于拓展科学的边界，探索科学尚无法解释的领域。这份记录成为了面向成员及密切关联者的《心灵研究学会期刊》(Journal of the Society for Psychical Research)，以及面向公众的《会刊》(Proceedings)。这些卷帙浩繁的出版物总是令我惊叹：总计超过一万七千页。在好奇与疑虑之间的某个地方，是持久的希望。

paragraphs.en: When James began his psychical research, he was well ensconced in the field of physiology. However, the anatomist's factual, objective method missed something in its understanding of human nature. For James, something important was lost: the sense that a human being is more than just a bundle of perceptions and nervous reactions, and more than just a body that could disappear without a trace. He hoped that there was something ethereal, transcendent – something even ghostly – that was free from the constraints of our physical lives. And at many times throughout his life, he suggested that one can occasionally feel this 'something' haunting the fringe of consciousness. As late as 1901, James remarked: 'I seriously believe that the general problem of the subliminal … promises to be one of the great problems, possibly even the greatest problem, of psychology.' 'Subliminal' is often used interchangeably with 'unconscious', but it shouldn't be. It refers, instead, to mental processes just below the threshold of consciousness that can often be felt without fully emerging. Just a hint, a fleeting 'maybe', is all we get, but it is often enough to qualify as something that we know, at least for a moment. These glancing blows of experience stand at the centre of James's Varieties – they come in many forms, indeed so many that their existence cannot be pushed aside.
paragraphs.cn: 当James开始他的心灵研究时，他已在生理学领域深耕多年。然而，解剖学家那种注重事实的客观方法在对人性的理解中遗漏了某些东西。对James来说，丢失了某种重要的东西：那种认为人不仅仅是一束感知和神经反应的集合，不仅仅是一具可能消失无踪的肉身的感觉。他希望存在某种空灵的、超越的东西——甚至可以说是幽灵般的东西——不受我们物质生活的束缚。在他一生的许多时刻，他暗示人们偶尔能感受到这'某种东西'萦绕在意识的边缘。直到1901年，James仍说：'我认真地相信，阈下(subliminal)的一般问题……有望成为心理学的重大问题之一，甚至可能是最大的问题。''阈下'(subliminal)一词常与'无意识'(unconscious)互换使用，但不应该如此。它指的是恰恰在意识阈值之下的心理过程，往往能被感受到却未完全浮现。只是一丝暗示，一个转瞬即逝的'也许'，就是我们得到的全部，但往往足以算作某种我们在至少一个瞬间所知晓的东西。这些经验的擦身之击位于James《宗教经验之种种》的核心——它们以多种形态出现，确实如此之多，以至于其存在不能被置之不理。

paragraphs.en: The American jurist Oliver Wendell Holmes once joked that James would turn down the lights in a room so that the miracles could happen. I think that there is some truth in this. It is something like the American self-help author Wayne Dyer's oft-repeated quote: 'Miracles come in moments. Be ready and willing.' James was definitely always ready and willing. When you turn the lights down, your pupils dilate so that more light can get in. You can't blame James for this. Maybe we surprise ourselves with what we can see. And maybe that is miracle enough. 'The miracle is not to walk on water,' insists the Buddhist monk Thich Nhat Hanh. 'The miracle is to walk on the green earth in the present moment, to appreciate the peace and beauty that are available now.' For secular skeptics, this might be as far as they are ever willing to go when it comes to religious experience: to dwell deeply, to 'live a little' in the present. James, however, goes just a little farther, a little deeper, in the Varieties.
paragraphs.cn: 美国法学家Oliver Wendell Holmes曾打趣说，James会把房间的灯光调暗，好让奇迹发生。我认为这话有一定道理。这有点像美国自助读物作者Wayne Dyer反复说的那句话：'奇迹降临于瞬间。要准备好，要愿意。'James确实总是准备好了，也总是愿意的。当你调暗灯光，瞳孔会放大以便更多光线进入。你不能为此责怪James。也许我们会为自己能看到的东西感到惊讶。也许那本身就是奇迹。'奇迹不是在水上行走，'佛教僧侣一行禅师(Thich Nhat Hanh)坚持说。'奇迹是在当下走在绿色的土地上，欣赏此刻即可获得的和平与美丽。'对于世俗的怀疑论者来说，在宗教经验方面，这可能就是他们愿意走到的最远处：深深地栖居，在当下'活出一点'。然而，James在《宗教经验之种种》中又往前走了一点点，往深处探了一点点。

paragraphs.en: Sometimes, when you turn the lights very low, you can see things more clearly. James describes such a phenomenon, the only one, he claimed, that could be called genuinely 'mystical'. Recounting the 'hour of rapture' of a clergyman, James writes: 'The perfect stillness of the night was thrilled by a more solemn silence. The darkness held a presence that was all the more felt because it was not seen. I could not any more have doubted that He was there than that I was. Indeed, I felt myself to be, if possible, the less real of the two.'
paragraphs.cn: 有时候，当你把灯光调得非常暗，你反而能把事物看得更清楚。James描述了这样一种现象，他声称这是唯一可被称为真正'神秘的'(mystical)现象。在复述一位牧师的'狂喜时刻'时，James写道：'夜晚完美的寂静被一种更庄严的沉默所震颤。黑暗中存在着一种临在，因为它未被看见而更加被感受到。我无法再怀疑他在那里，正如我无法怀疑我在这里。事实上，我感到自己——如果可能的话——是两者中较不真实的那个。'

paragraphs.en: The 'He', according to the clergyman, was undoubtedly the Judeo-Christian God, but what we call this presence scarcely mattered to James. 'He' is a very old word, older than gender and sex, meaning 'this here'. 'This here' was present, all the more felt, because it wasn't seen. For James, for his fellow mystics such as Blood, there was a sustained comfort in this story. As the German mystic Novalis wrote: 'We are more closely connected to the invisible than to the visible.' This too is a possibility, and the Jamesian pragmatist is happy to entertain it.
paragraphs.cn: 据这位牧师所说，那个'他'(He)无疑是犹太-基督教的上帝，但如何称呼这种临在对James来说几乎无关紧要。'他'是一个非常古老的词，比性别和性更古老，意思是'这个在这里'。'这个在这里'是存在的，因为它未被看见而更加被感受到。对James而言，对他的同道神秘主义者如Blood而言，这个故事中有一种持久的慰藉。正如德国神秘主义者Novalis所写：'我们与不可见之物的联系比与可见之物的联系更为紧密。'这也是一种可能性，而James式的实用主义者乐于考虑它。

paragraphs.en: Before the Brooklyn Bridge was built, a ferry carried passengers from one side of the river to the other. Walt Whitman was often among the crowd. The American poet was one of James's longstanding heroes, the embodiment of the capacious 'healthy mind' he describes in the Varieties. James occasionally sensed the sublime or the religious on his hikes in the Adirondacks or in the testament of mystics, but Whitman could tap into it on a routine basis, even on a dirty ferry ride, which most people would regard as a rather annoying commute. It wasn't annoying for Whitman. In his poem 'Crossing Brooklyn Ferry' (1855), he described the spectacle – the experience of nature and the experience of the human throng. Both were inexplicable and hopeful and shared.
paragraphs.cn: 在布鲁克林大桥建成之前，渡船载着乘客从河的一边到另一边。Walt Whitman常常混迹于人群之中。这位美国诗人是James长久的英雄之一，是他在《宗教经验之种种》中描述的那种心胸开阔的'健康心灵'的化身。James偶尔在阿迪朗达克山脉的徒步中或在神秘主义者的见证中感受到崇高或宗教性，但Whitman能在日常生活中触及它，甚至在一次肮脏的渡船之旅中——大多数人会认为那不过是一段相当烦人的通勤。对Whitman来说并不烦人。在他的诗《横渡布鲁克林渡船》(1855)中，他描述了那番景象——对自然的体验和对人潮的体验。两者都是不可解释的，充满希望的，也是共享的。

paragraphs.en: James read and reread this poem. This was wonder, and there was enough of it to go around. It turns out that one can probably set aside the nuttier aspects of the Society for Psychical Research and still retain a Whitman-esque experience of the world, the numinous immanence of an all-too-human ferry ride. That, at least, was James's hope. Whitman's vision, in James's words, was sufficient 'to prompt our curiosities and hopes and suspicions'. The world is not always, or ever, exactly as it seems. A dirty ferry ride might be more than just a dirty ferry ride. There is something more – at least it is possible. Whitman's was a type of religious experience – and so very different from the way that most people experience the world.
