Just as AI is used in physics to operate within existing theories – for example, to optimise experimental designs or predict the behaviour of complex systems – it can also be used in ethics to extend moral reasoning within a given framework. In physics, AI typically operates within established models rather than proposing new physical laws or conceptual frameworks. It may calculate how multiple forces interact and predict their combined effect on a physical system. Similarly, in ethics, AI does not generate new moral principles but applies existing ones to novel and often intricate situations. It may weigh competing values – fairness, harm minimisation, justice – and assess their combined implications for what action is morally best. The result is not a new moral system, but a deepened application of an existing one, shaped by the same kind of formal reasoning that underlies scientific modelling. But is there an inherent limit to what AI can know about morality? Could there be true ethical propositions that no machine, no matter how advanced, can ever prove?

These questions echo a fundamental discovery in mathematical logic, probably the most fundamental insight ever to be proven: Kurt Gödel's incompleteness theorems. They show that any logical system powerful enough to describe arithmetic is either inconsistent or incomplete. In this essay, I argue that this limitation, though mathematical in origin, has deep consequences for ethics, and for how we design AI systems to reason morally.

Suppose we design an AI system to model moral decision-making. Like other AI systems – whether predicting stock prices, navigating roads or curating content – it would be programmed to maximise certain predefined objectives. To do so, it must rely on formal, computational logic: either deductive reasoning, which derives conclusions from fixed rules and axioms, or else on probabilistic reasoning, which estimates likelihoods based on patterns in data. In either case, the AI must adopt a mathematical structure for moral evaluation. But Gödel's incompleteness theorems reveal a fundamental limitation. Gödel showed that any formal system powerful enough to express arithmetic, such as the natural numbers and their operations, cannot be both complete and consistent. If such a system is consistent, there will always be true statements it cannot prove. In particular, as applied to AI, this suggests that any system capable of rich moral reasoning will inevitably have moral blind spots: ethical truths that it cannot derive. Here, 'true' refers to truth in the standard interpretation of arithmetic, such as the claim that '2 + 2 = 4', which is true under ordinary mathematical rules. If the system is inconsistent, then it could prove anything at all, including contradictions, rendering it useless as a guide for ethical decisions.

Gödel's incompleteness theorems apply not only to AI, but to any ethical reasoning framed within a formal system. The key difference is that human reasoners can, at least in principle, revise their assumptions, adopt new principles, and rethink the framework itself. AI, by contrast, remains bound by the formal structures it is given, or operates within those it can modify only under predefined constraints. In this way, Gödel's theorems place a logical boundary on what AI, if built on formal systems, can ever fully prove or validate about morality from within those systems.

Most of us first met axioms in school, usually through geometry. One famous example is the parallel postulate, which says that if you pick a point not on a line, you can draw exactly one line through that point that is parallel to the original line. For more than 2,000 years, this seemed self-evident. Yet in the 19th century, mathematicians such as Carl Friedrich Gauss, Nikolai Lobachevsky and János Bolyai showed that it is possible to construct internally consistent geometries in which the parallel postulate does not hold. In some such geometries, no parallel lines exist; in others, infinitely many do. These non-Euclidean geometries shattered the belief that Euclid's axioms uniquely described space.

This discovery raised a deeper worry. If the parallel postulate, long considered self-evident, could be discarded, what about the axioms of arithmetic, which define the natural numbers and the operations of addition and multiplication? On what grounds can we trust that they are free from hidden inconsistencies? Yet with this challenge came a promise. If we could prove that the axioms of arithmetic are consistent, then it would be possible to expand them to develop a consistent set of richer axioms that define the integers, the rational numbers, the real numbers, the complex numbers, and beyond. As the 19th-century mathematician Leopold Kronecker put it: 'God created the natural numbers; all else is the work of man.' Proving the consistency of arithmetic would prove the consistency of many important fields of mathematics.

The method for proving the consistency of arithmetic was proposed by the mathematician David Hilbert. His approach involved two steps. First, Hilbert argued that, to prove the consistency of a formal system, it must be possible to formulate, within the system's own symbolic language, a claim equivalent to 'This system is consistent,' and then prove that claim using only the system's own rules of inference. The proof should rely on nothing outside the system, not even the presumed 'self-evidence' of its axioms. Second, Hilbert advocated grounding arithmetic in something even more fundamental. This task was undertaken by Bertrand Russell and Alfred North Whitehead in their monumental Principia Mathematica (1910-13). Working in the domain of symbolic logic, a field concerned not with numbers, but with abstract propositions like 'if x, then y', they showed that the axioms of arithmetic could be derived as theorems from a smaller set of logical axioms. This left one final challenge: could this set of axioms of symbolic logic, on which arithmetic can be built, prove its own consistency? If it could, Hilbert's dream would be fulfilled. That hope became the guiding ambition of early 20th-century mathematics.

It was within this climate of optimism that Kurt Gödel, a young Austrian logician, introduced a result that would dismantle Hilbert's vision. In 1931, Gödel published his incompleteness theorems, showing that the very idea of such a fully self-sufficient mathematical system is impossible. Specifically, Gödel showed that if a formal system meets several conditions, it will contain true claims that it cannot prove. It must be complex enough to express arithmetic, include the principle of induction (which allows it to prove general statements by showing they hold for a base case and each successive step), be consistent, and have a decidable set of axioms (meaning it is possible to determine, for any given statement, whether it qualifies as an axiom). Any system that satisfies these conditions, such as the set of logical axioms developed by Russell and Whitehead in Principia Mathematica, will necessarily be incomplete: there will always be statements that are expressible within the system but unprovable from its axioms. Even more strikingly, Gödel showed that such a system can express, but not prove, the claim that it itself is consistent.

Gödel's proof, which I simplify here, relies on two key insights that follow from his arithmetisation of syntax, the powerful idea of associating any sentence of a formal system with a particular natural number, known as its Gödel number. First, any system complex enough to express arithmetic and induction must allow for formulas with free variables, formulas like S(x): 'x = 10', whose truth value depends on the value of x. S(x) is true when x is, in fact, 10, and false otherwise. Since every statement in the system has a unique Gödel number, G(S), a formula can refer to its own Gödel number. Specifically, the system can form statements such as S(G(S)): 'G(S) = 10', whose truth depends on whether S(x)'s own Gödel number equals 10. Second, in any logical system, a proof of a formula S has a certain structure: starting with axioms, applying inference rules to produce new formulas from those axioms, ultimately deriving S itself. Just like every formula S has a Gödel number G(S), so every proof of S is assigned a Gödel number, by treating the entire sequence of formulas in the proof as one long formula. So we can define a proof relation P(x, y), where P(x, y) holds if and only if x is the Gödel number of a proof of S, and y is the Gödel number of S itself. The claim that x encodes a proof of S becomes a statement within the system, namely, P(x, y).

Third, building on these ideas, Gödel showed that any formal system capable of expressing arithmetic and the principle of induction can also formulate statements about its own proofs. For example, the system can express statements like: 'n is not the Gödel number of a proof of formula S'. From this, it can go a step further and express the claim: 'There is no number n such that n is the Gödel number of a proof of formula S.' In other words, the system can say that a certain formula S is unprovable within the system. Fourth, Gödel ingeniously constructed a self-referential formula, P, that asserts: 'There is no number n such that n is the Gödel number of a proof of formula P.' That is, P says of itself, 'P is not provable.' In this way, P is a formal statement that expresses its own unprovability from within the system.

It immediately follows that if the formula P were provable within the system, then it would be false, because it asserts that it has no proof. This would mean the system proves a falsehood, and therefore is inconsistent. So if the system is consistent, then P cannot be proved, and therefore P is indeed unprovable. This leads to the conclusion that, in any consistent formal system rich enough to express arithmetic and induction, there will always be true but unprovable statements, most notably, the system's own claim of consistency.

The implications of Gödel's theorems were both profound and unsettling. They shattered Hilbert's hope that mathematics could be reduced to a complete, mechanical system of derivation and exposed the inherent limits of formal reasoning. Initially, Gödel's findings faced resistance, with some mathematicians arguing that his results were less general than they appeared. Yet, as subsequent mathematicians and logicians, most notably John von Neumann, confirmed both their correctness and broad applicability, Gödel's theorems came to be widely recognised as one of the most significant discoveries in the foundations of mathematics.

Gödel's results have also initiated philosophical debates. The mathematician and physicist Roger Penrose, for example, has argued that they point to a fundamental difference between human cognition and formal algorithmic reasoning. He claims that human consciousness enables us to perceive certain truths – such as those Gödel showed to be unprovable within formal systems – in ways that no algorithmic process can replicate. This suggests, for Penrose, that certain aspects of consciousness may lie beyond the reach of computation. His conclusion parallels that of John Searle's 'Chinese Room' argument, which holds that this is so because algorithms manipulate symbols purely syntactically, without any grasp of their semantic content. Still, the conclusions drawn by Penrose and Searle do not directly follow from Gödel's theorems. Gödel's results apply strictly to formal mathematical systems and do not make claims about consciousness or cognition. Whether human minds can recognise unprovable truths as true, or whether machines could ever possess minds capable of such recognition, remains an open philosophical question.

However, Gödel's incompleteness theorems do reveal a deep limitation of algorithmic reasoning, in particular AI, one that concerns not just computation, but moral reasoning itself. Without his theorems, it was at least conceivable that an AI could formalise all moral truths and, in addition, prove them from a consistent set of axioms. But Gödel's work shows that this is impossible. No AI, no matter how sophisticated, could prove all moral truths it can express. The gap between truth claims and provability sets a fundamental boundary on how far formal moral reasoning can go, even for the most powerful machines.

This raises two distinct problems for ethics. The first is an ancient one. As Plato suggests in the Euthyphro, morality is not just about doing what is right, but understanding why it is right. Ethical action requires justification, an account grounded in reason. This ideal of rational moral justification has animated much of our ethical thought, but Gödel's theorems suggest that, if moral reasoning is formalised, then there will be moral truths that cannot be proven within those systems. In this way, Gödel did not only undermine Hilbert's vision of proving mathematics consistent; he may also have shaken Plato's hope of fully grounding ethics in reason.

The second problem is more practical. Even a high-performing AI may encounter situations in which it cannot justify or explain its recommendations using only the ethical framework it has been given. The concern is not just that AI might act unethically but also that it could not demonstrate that its actions are ethical. This becomes especially urgent when AI is used to guide or justify decisions made by humans. Even a high-performing AI will encounter a boundary beyond which it cannot justify or explain its decisions using only the resources of its own framework. No matter how advanced it becomes, there will be ethical truths it can express, but never prove.

The development of modern AI has generally split into two approaches: logic-based AI, which derives knowledge through strict deduction, and large language models (LLMs), which predict meaning from statistical patterns. Both approaches rely on mathematical structures. Formal logic is based on symbolic manipulation and set theory. LLMs are not strictly deductive-logic-based but rather use a combination of statistical inference, pattern recognition, and computational techniques to generate responses.

Just as axioms provide a foundation for mathematical reasoning, LLMs rely on statistical relationships in data to approximate logical reasoning. They engage with ethics not by deducing moral truths but by replicating how such debates unfold in language. This is achieved through gradient descent, an algorithm that minimises a loss function by updating weights in the direction that reduces error, approximates complex functions that map inputs to outputs, allowing them to generalise patterns from vast amounts of data. They do not deduce answers but generate plausible ones, with 'reasoning' emerging from billions of neural network parameters rather than explicit rules. While they primarily function as probabilistic models, predicting text based on statistical patterns, computational logic plays a role in optimisation, rule-based reasoning and certain decision-making processes within neural networks.

But probability and statistics are themselves formal systems, grounded not only in arithmetic but also in probabilistic axioms, such as those introduced by the Soviet mathematician Andrey Kolmogorov, which govern how the likelihood of complex events is derived, updated with new data, and aggregated across scenarios. Any formal language complex enough to express probabilistic or statistical claims can also express arithmetic and is therefore subject to Gödel's incompleteness theorems. This means that LLMs inherit Gödelian limitations. Even hybrid systems, such as IBM Watson, OpenAI Codex or DeepMind's AlphaGo, which combine logical reasoning with probabilistic modelling, remain bound by Gödelian limitations. All rule-based components are constrained by Gödel's theorems, which show that some true propositions expressible in a system cannot be proven within it. Probabilistic components, for their part, are governed by formal axioms that define how probability distributions are updated, how uncertainties are aggregated, and how conclusions are drawn. They can yield plausible answers, but they cannot justify them beyond the statistical patterns they were trained on.

At first glance, the Gödelian limitations on AIs in general and LLMs in particular may seem inconsequential. After all, most ethical systems were never meant to resolve every conceivable moral problem. They were designed to guide specific domains, such as war, law or business, and often rely on principles that are only loosely formalised. If formal models can be developed for specific cases, one might argue that the inability to fully formalise ethics is not especially troubling. Furthermore, Gödel's incompleteness theorems did not halt the everyday work of mathematicians. Mathematicians continue to search for proofs, even knowing that some true statements may be unprovable. In the same spirit, the fact that some ethical truths may be beyond formal proof should not discourage humans, or AIs, from seeking them, articulating them, and attempting to justify or prove them.

But Gödel's findings were not merely theoretical. They have had practical consequences in mathematics itself. A striking case is the continuum hypothesis, which asks whether there exists a set whose cardinality lies strictly between that of the natural numbers and the real numbers. This question emerged from set theory, the mathematical field dealing with collections of mathematical entities, such as numbers, functions or even other sets. Its most widely accepted axiomatisation, the Zermelo-Fraenkel axioms of set theory with the Axiom of Choice, underlies nearly all modern mathematics. In 1938, Gödel himself showed that the continuum hypothesis cannot be disproven from these axioms, assuming they are consistent. In 1963, Paul Cohen proved the converse: the continuum hypothesis also cannot be proven from the same axioms. This landmark result confirmed that some fundamental mathematical questions lie beyond formal resolution.

The same, I argue, applies to ethics. The limits that Gödel revealed in mathematics are not only theoretically relevant to AI ethics; they carry practical importance. First, just as mathematics contains true statements that cannot be proven within its own axioms, there may well be ethical truths that are formally unprovable yet ethically important – the moral equivalents of the continuum hypothesis. These might arise in systems designed to handle difficult trade-offs, like weighing fairness against harm. We cannot foresee when, or even whether, an AI operating within a formal ethical framework will encounter such limits. Just as it took more than 30 years after Gödel's incompleteness theorems for Cohen to prove the independence of the continuum hypothesis, we cannot predict when, if ever, we will encounter ethical principles that are expressible within an AI's ethical system yet remain unprovable.

Second, Gödel also showed that no sufficiently complex formal system can prove its own consistency. This is especially troubling in ethics, in which it is far from clear that our ethical frameworks are consistent. This is not a limitation unique to AI; humans, too, cannot prove the consistency of the formal systems they construct. But this especially matters for AI because one of its most ambitious promises has been to go beyond human judgment: to reason more clearly, more impartially, and on a greater scale.

Gödel's results set a hard limit on that aspiration. The limitation is structural, not merely technical. Just as Albert Einstein's theory of relativity places an upper speed limit on the Universe – no matter how advanced our spacecraft, we cannot exceed the speed of light – Gödel's theorems impose a boundary on formal reasoning: no matter how advanced AI becomes, it cannot escape the incompleteness of the formal system it operates within. Moreover, Gödel's theorems may constrain practical ethical reasoning in unforeseen ways, much as some important mathematical conjectures have been shown to be unprovable from standard axioms of set theory, or as the speed of light, though unreachable, still imposes real constraints on engineering and astrophysics. For example, as I write this, NASA's Parker Solar Probe is the fastest human-made object in history, travelling at roughly 430,000 miles (c700,000 km) per hour, just 0.064 per cent of the speed of light. Yet that upper limit remains crucial: the finite speed of light has, for example, shaped the design of space probes, landers and rovers, all of which require at least semi-autonomous operation, since radio signals from Earth take minutes or even hours to arrive. Gödel's theorems may curtail ethical computation in similarly surprising ways.

There is yet another reason why Gödel's results are especially relevant to AI ethics. Unlike static rule-based systems, advanced AI, particularly large language models and adaptive learning systems, may not only apply a predefined ethical framework, but also revise elements of it over time. A central promise of AI-driven moral reasoning is its ability to refine ethical models through learning, addressing ambiguities and blind spots in human moral judgment. As AI systems evolve, they may attempt to modify their own axioms or parameters in response to new data or feedback. This is especially true of machine learning systems trained on vast and changing datasets, as well as hybrid models that integrate logical reasoning with statistical inference. Yet Gödel's results reveal a structural limit: if an ethical framework is formalised within a sufficiently expressive formal system, then no consistent set of axioms can prove all true statements expressible within it.

To illustrate, consider an AI tasked with upholding justice. It may be programmed with widely accepted ethical principles, for example fairness and harm minimisation. While human-made models of justice based on these principles are inevitably overly simplistic, limited by computational constraints and cognitive biases, an AI, in theory, has no such limitations. It can continuously learn from actual human behaviour, refining its understanding and constructing an increasingly nuanced conception of justice, one that weaves together more and more dimensions of human experience. It can even, as noted, change its own axioms. But no matter how much an AI learns, or how it modifies itself, there will always be claims about justice that, while it may be able to model, it will never be able to prove within its own system. More troubling still, AI would be unable to prove that the ethical system it constructs is internally consistent – that it does not, somewhere in its vast web of ethical reasoning, contradict itself – unless it is inconsistent, in which case it can prove anything, including falsehood, such as its own consistency.

Ultimately, Gödel's incompleteness theorems serve as a warning against the notion that AI can achieve perfect ethical reasoning. Just as mathematics will always contain truths that lie beyond formal proof, morality will always contain complexities that defy algorithmic resolution. The question is not simply whether AI can make moral decisions, but whether it can overcome the limitations of any system grounded in predefined logic – limitations that, as Gödel showed, may prevent certain truths from ever being provable within the system, even if they are recognisable as true. While AI ethics has grappled with issues of bias, fairness and interpretability, the deeper challenge remains: can AI recognise the limits of its own ethical reasoning? This challenge may place an insurmountable boundary between artificial and human ethics.

The relationship between Gödel's incompleteness theorems and machine ethics highlights a structural parallel: just as no formal system can be both complete and self-contained, no AI can achieve moral reasoning that is both exhaustive and entirely provable. In a sense, Gödel's findings extend and complicate the Kantian tradition. Kant argued that knowledge depends on a priori truths, fundamental assumptions that structure our experience of reality. Gödel's theorems suggest that, even within formal systems built on well-defined axioms, there remain truths that exceed the system's ability to establish them. If Kant sought to define the limits of reason through necessary preconditions for knowledge, Gödel revealed an intrinsic incompleteness in formal reasoning itself, one that no set of axioms can resolve from within. There will always be moral truths beyond its computational grasp, ethical problems that resist algorithmic resolution.

So the deeper problem lies in AI's inability to recognise the boundaries of its own reasoning framework – its incapacity to know when its moral conclusions rest on incomplete premises, or when a problem lies beyond what its ethical system can formally resolve. While humans also face cognitive and epistemic constraints, we are not bound by a given formal structure. We can invent new axioms, question old ones, or revise our entire framework in light of philosophical insight or ethical deliberation. AI systems, by contrast, can generate or adopt new axioms only if their architecture permits it and, even then, such modifications occur within predefined meta-rules or optimisation goals. They lack the capacity for conceptual reflection that guides human shifts in foundational assumptions. Even if a richer formal language, or a richer set of axioms, could prove some previously unprovable truths, no finite set of axioms that satisfies Gödel's requirements of decidability and consistency can prove all truths expressible in any sufficiently powerful formal system. In that sense, Gödel sets a boundary – not just on what machines can prove, but on what they can ever justify from within a given ethical or logical architecture.

One of the great hopes, or fears, of AI is that it may one day evolve beyond the ethical principles initially programmed into it and simulate just such self-questioning. Through machine learning, AI could modify its own ethical framework, generating novel moral insights and uncovering patterns and solutions that human thinkers, constrained by cognitive biases and computational limitations, might overlook. However, this very adaptability introduces a profound risk: an AI's evolving morality could diverge so radically from human ethics that its decisions become incomprehensible or even morally abhorrent to us. This mirrors certain religious conceptions of ethics. In some theological traditions, divine morality is considered so far beyond human comprehension that it can appear arbitrary or even cruel, a theme central to debates over the problem of evil and divine command theory. A similar challenge arises with AI ethics: as AI systems become increasingly autonomous and self-modifying, their moral decisions may become so opaque and detached from human reasoning that they risk being perceived as unpredictable, inscrutable or even unjust.

Yet, while AI may never fully master moral reasoning, it could become a powerful tool for refining human ethical thought. Unlike human decision-making, which is often shaped by bias, intuition or unexamined assumptions, AI has the potential to expose inconsistencies in our ethical reasoning by treating similar cases with formal impartiality. This potential, however, depends on AI's ability to recognise when cases are morally alike, a task complicated by the fact that AI systems, especially LLMs, may internalise and reproduce the very human biases they are intended to mitigate. When AI delivers a decision that appears morally flawed, it may prompt us to re-examine the principles behind our own judgments. Are we distinguishing between cases for good moral reasons, or are we applying double standards without realising it? AI could help challenge and refine our ethical reasoning, not by offering final answers, but by revealing gaps, contradictions and overlooked assumptions in our moral framework.

AI may depart from human moral intuitions in at least two ways: by treating cases we see as similar in divergent ways, or by treating cases we see as different in the same way. In both instances, the underlying question is whether the AI is correctly identifying a morally relevant distinction or similarity, or whether it is merely reflecting irrelevant patterns in its training data. In some cases, the divergence may stem from embedded human biases, such as discriminatory patterns based on race, gender or socioeconomic status. But in others, the AI might uncover ethically significant features that human judgment has historically missed. It could, for instance, discover novel variants of the trolley problem, suggesting that two seemingly equivalent harms differ in morally important ways. In such cases, AI may detect new ethical patterns before human philosophers do. The challenge is that we cannot know in advance which kind of departure we are facing. Each surprising moral judgment from AI must be evaluated on its own terms – neither accepted uncritically nor dismissed out of hand. Yet even this openness to novel insights does not free AI from the structural boundaries of formal reasoning.

That is the deeper lesson. Gödel's theorems do not simply show that there are truths machines cannot prove. They show that moral reasoning, like mathematics, is always open-ended, always reaching beyond what can be formally derived. The challenge, then, is not only how to encode ethical reasoning into AI but also how to ensure that its evolving moral framework remains aligned with human values and societal norms. For all its speed, precision and computational power, AI remains incapable of the one thing that makes moral reasoning truly possible: the ability to question not only what is right, but why. Ethics, therefore, must remain a human endeavour, an ongoing and imperfect struggle that no machine will ever fully master.

paragraphs.cn: 想象一个世界，在其中人工智能被赋予了最高的道德责任：对罪犯量刑、分配医疗资源，甚至调解国家间的冲突。这似乎是人类进步的巅峰：一个不受情感、偏见或不一致影响的实体，以无可挑剔的精确性做出伦理决策。与人类法官或政策制定者不同，机器不会被个人利益动摇，也不会在推理中出现失误。它不会说谎。它不接受贿赂或请求。它不会因艰难的决定而流泪。
paragraphs.en: Imagine a world in which artificial intelligence is entrusted with the highest moral responsibilities: sentencing criminals, allocating medical resources, and even mediating conflicts between nations. This might seem like the pinnacle of human progress: an entity unburdened by emotion, prejudice or inconsistency, making ethical decisions with impeccable precision. Unlike human judges or policymakers, a machine would not be swayed by personal interests or lapses in reasoning. It does not lie. It does not accept bribes or pleas. It does not weep over hard decisions.

paragraphs.cn: 然而，在这个理想化的道德仲裁者愿景之下，隐藏着一个根本性的问题：机器能否像人类那样理解道德，还是局限于伦理推理的拟像？人工智能可能复制人类的决定而无法改进它们，延续人类道德判断中同样的偏见、盲点和文化扭曲。在试图效仿我们的过程中，它可能只是在复制我们的局限性，而非超越它们。但还有一个更深层的问题。道德判断依赖于直觉、历史意识和情境——这些特质抗拒形式化。伦理可能如此深植于生活经验之中，以至于任何将其编码为形式结构的尝试都有可能抹平其最本质的特征。如果是这样，人工智能不仅会反映人类的缺陷；它会剥夺道德使伦理反思成为可能的那些深度本身。
paragraphs.en: Yet beneath this vision of an idealised moral arbiter lies a fundamental question: can a machine understand morality as humans do, or is it confined to a simulacrum of ethical reasoning? AI might replicate human decisions without improving on them, carrying forward the same biases, blind spots and cultural distortions from human moral judgment. In trying to emulate us, it might only reproduce our limitations, not transcend them. But there is a deeper concern. Moral judgment draws on intuition, historical awareness and context – qualities that resist formalisation. Ethics may be so embedded in lived experience that any attempt to encode it into formal structures risks flattening its most essential features. If so, AI would not merely reflect human shortcomings; it would strip morality of the very depth that makes ethical reflection possible in the first place.

paragraphs.cn: 尽管如此，许多人仍试图将伦理形式化，将某些道德主张不是当作结论，而是当作出发点。功利主义提供了一个经典例子，它通常将「应该采取行动以最大化整体福祉」这一原则作为基础公理。由此可以推导出更具体的原则，例如，让最多人受益是正确的，或者行为应该以其对总体幸福的后果来评判。随着计算资源的增加，人工智能越来越适合从固定的伦理假设出发，在复杂情境中推演其含义这一任务。
paragraphs.en: Still, many have tried to formalise ethics, by treating certain moral claims not as conclusions, but as starting points. A classic example comes from utilitarianism, which often takes as a foundational axiom the principle that one should act to maximise overall wellbeing. From this, more specific principles can be derived, for example, that it is right to benefit the greatest number, or that actions should be judged by their consequences for total happiness. As computational resources increase, AI becomes increasingly well-suited to the task of starting from fixed ethical assumptions and reasoning through their implications in complex situations.

paragraphs.cn: 但是，将伦理这样的东西形式化到底意味着什么？通过观察那些形式系统长期扮演核心角色的领域，这个问题更容易理解。例如，物理学依赖形式化已有数百年历史。没有一个能解释一切的单一物理理论。相反，我们有许多物理理论，每一个都旨在描述宇宙的特定方面：从夸克和电子的行为到星系的运动。这些理论常常存在分歧。亚里士多德物理学用向地球中心的自然运动来解释下落物体；牛顿力学以万有引力取代了这一解释。这些解释不仅不同；它们是不兼容的。然而两者都共享一个共同的结构：它们从基本公设——关于运动、力或质量的假设——出发，推导出越来越复杂的结论。艾萨克·牛顿（Isaac Newton）的运动定律和詹姆斯·克拉克·麦克斯韦（James Clerk Maxwell）的方程组是经典例子：紧凑而优雅的公式，从中可以推导出关于物理世界的广泛预测。
paragraphs.en: But what, exactly, does it mean to formalise something like ethics? The question is easier to grasp by looking at fields in which formal systems have long played a central role. Physics, for instance, has relied on formalisation for centuries. There is no single physical theory that explains everything. Instead, we have many physical theories, each designed to describe specific aspects of the Universe: from the behaviour of quarks and electrons to the motion of galaxies. These theories often diverge. Aristotelian physics, for instance, explained falling objects in terms of natural motion toward Earth's centre; Newtonian mechanics replaced this with a universal force of gravity. These explanations are not just different; they are incompatible. Yet both share a common structure: they begin with basic postulates – assumptions about motion, force or mass – and derive increasingly complex consequences. Isaac Newton's laws of motion and James Clerk Maxwell's equations are classic examples: compact, elegant formulations from which wide-ranging predictions about the physical world can be deduced.

paragraphs.cn: 伦理理论具有类似的结构。与物理理论一样，它们试图描述一个领域——在这个情况下，是道德图景。它们旨在回答哪些行为是对的或错的，以及为什么。这些理论也存在分歧，即使当它们推荐相似的行动（如捐赠慈善）时，它们也以不同方式为其提供正当性。伦理理论也通常从一小套基础原则或主张出发，由此推理更复杂的道德问题。一个后果主义者从行动应最大化福祉的理念出发；一个义务论者从行动必须尊重义务或权利的理念出发。这些基本承诺的功能类似于物理学中的对应物：它们定义了每个伦理理论内道德推理的结构。
paragraphs.en: Ethical theories have a similar structure. Like physical theories, they attempt to describe a domain – in this case, the moral landscape. They aim to answer questions about which actions are right or wrong, and why. These theories also diverge and, even when they recommend similar actions, such as giving to charity, they justify them in different ways. Ethical theories also often begin with a small set of foundational principles or claims, from which they reason about more complex moral problems. A consequentialist begins with the idea that actions should maximise wellbeing; a deontologist starts from the idea that actions must respect duties or rights. These basic commitments function similarly to their counterparts in physics: they define the structure of moral reasoning within each ethical theory.

paragraphs.cn: 正如人工智能在物理学中被用于在现有理论内运作——例如，优化实验设计或预测复杂系统的行为——它也可以在伦理学中被用于在给定框架内扩展道德推理。在物理学中，人工智能通常在已建立的模型内运作，而不是提出新的物理定律或概念框架。它可以计算多种力如何相互作用，并预测它们对物理系统的综合效果。同样，在伦理学中，人工智能不生成新的道德原则，而是将现有原则应用于新颖且往往错综复杂的情境。它可以权衡相互竞争的价值——公平、伤害最小化、正义——并评估它们对何种行动在道德上最优的综合含义。其结果不是一个新的道德体系，而是一个现有体系的深化应用，由与科学建模相同的形式推理所塑造。但人工智能对道德的认知是否存在固有的极限？是否存在某些真实的伦理命题，无论多么先进的机器都无法证明？
paragraphs.en: Just as AI is used in physics to operate within existing theories – for example, to optimise experimental designs or predict the behaviour of complex systems – it can also be used in ethics to extend moral reasoning within a given framework. In physics, AI typically operates within established models rather than proposing new physical laws or conceptual frameworks. It may calculate how multiple forces interact and predict their combined effect on a physical system. Similarly, in ethics, AI does not generate new moral principles but applies existing ones to novel and often intricate situations. It may weigh competing values – fairness, harm minimisation, justice – and assess their combined implications for what action is morally best. The result is not a new moral system, but a deepened application of an existing one, shaped by the same kind of formal reasoning that underlies scientific modelling. But is there an inherent limit to what AI can know about morality? Could there be true ethical propositions that no machine, no matter how advanced, can ever prove?

paragraphs.cn: 这些问题呼应了数学逻辑中一个根本性的发现，可能是有史以来被证明的最根本的洞见：库尔特·哥德尔（Kurt Gödel）的不完备定理。它们表明，任何强大到足以描述算术的逻辑系统要么是不一致的，要么是不完备的。在本文中，我将论证这一局限性虽然源自数学，但对伦理学以及我们如何设计人工智能系统进行道德推理具有深远的影响。
paragraphs.en: These questions echo a fundamental discovery in mathematical logic, probably the most fundamental insight ever to be proven: Kurt Gödel's incompleteness theorems. They show that any logical system powerful enough to describe arithmetic is either inconsistent or incomplete. In this essay, I argue that this limitation, though mathematical in origin, has deep consequences for ethics, and for how we design AI systems to reason morally.

paragraphs.cn: 假设我们设计一个人工智能系统来模拟道德决策。与其他人工智能系统一样——无论是预测股票价格、导航道路还是策划内容——它将被编程以最大化某些预定义目标。为此，它必须依赖形式化的计算逻辑：要么是演绎推理，从固定规则和公理中推导结论；要么是概率推理，基于数据中的模式估计可能性。无论哪种情况，人工智能都必须采用一种数学结构来进行道德评估。但哥德尔的不完备定理揭示了一个根本性的局限。哥德尔表明，任何强大到足以表达算术（如自然数及其运算）的形式系统，不可能同时是完备的和一致的。如果这样的系统是一致的，将总是存在它无法证明的真命题。特别是，当应用于人工智能时，这表明任何能够进行丰富道德推理的系统将不可避免地存在道德盲点：它无法推导出的伦理真理。这里所说的「真」是指在算术的标准解释下的真，例如「2 + 2 = 4」这一主张在普通数学规则下为真。如果系统是不一致的，那么它可以证明任何事情，包括矛盾，从而使它作为伦理决策的指南毫无用处。
paragraphs.en: Suppose we design an AI system to model moral decision-making. Like other AI systems – whether predicting stock prices, navigating roads or curating content – it would be programmed to maximise certain predefined objectives. To do so, it must rely on formal, computational logic: either deductive reasoning, which derives conclusions from fixed rules and axioms, or else on probabilistic reasoning, which estimates likelihoods based on patterns in data. In either case, the AI must adopt a mathematical structure for moral evaluation. But Gödel's incompleteness theorems reveal a fundamental limitation. Gödel showed that any formal system powerful enough to express arithmetic, such as the natural numbers and their operations, cannot be both complete and consistent. If such a system is consistent, there will always be true statements it cannot prove. In particular, as applied to AI, this suggests that any system capable of rich moral reasoning will inevitably have moral blind spots: ethical truths that it cannot derive. Here, 'true' refers to truth in the standard interpretation of arithmetic, such as the claim that '2 + 2 = 4', which is true under ordinary mathematical rules. If the system is inconsistent, then it could prove anything at all, including contradictions, rendering it useless as a guide for ethical decisions.

paragraphs.cn: 哥德尔的不完备定理不仅适用于人工智能，而且适用于任何在形式系统框架内构建的伦理推理。关键的区别在于，人类推理者至少原则上可以修正他们的假设、采纳新的原则，并重新思考框架本身。相比之下，人工智能仍然受制于它被赋予的形式结构，或者仅在预定义约束下才能修改这些结构。从这个意义上说，哥德尔定理为人工智能——如果建立在形式系统之上——从那些系统内部能够完全证明或验证关于道德的内容设定了逻辑边界。
paragraphs.en: Gödel's incompleteness theorems apply not only to AI, but to any ethical reasoning framed within a formal system. The key difference is that human reasoners can, at least in principle, revise their assumptions, adopt new principles, and rethink the framework itself. AI, by contrast, remains bound by the formal structures it is given, or operates within those it can modify only under predefined constraints. In this way, Gödel's theorems place a logical boundary on what AI, if built on formal systems, can ever fully prove or validate about morality from within those systems.

paragraphs.cn: 我们大多数人最初在学校接触到公理，通常是通过几何学。一个著名的例子是平行公设，它说的是：如果你选择一个不在某条直线上的点，你可以通过该点画出恰好一条与原直线平行的直线。两千多年来，这似乎是不言自明的。然而在19世纪，卡尔·弗里德里希·高斯（Carl Friedrich Gauss）、尼古拉·罗巴切夫斯基（Nikolai Lobachevsky）和亚诺什·博尔约（János Bolyai）等数学家表明，可以构造出内部一致的几何学，其中平行公设不成立。在某些这样的几何学中，不存在平行线；在另一些中，存在无穷多条。这些非欧几里得几何粉碎了欧几里得公理唯一地描述空间的信念。
paragraphs.en: Most of us first met axioms in school, usually through geometry. One famous example is the parallel postulate, which says that if you pick a point not on a line, you can draw exactly one line through that point that is parallel to the original line. For more than 2,000 years, this seemed self-evident. Yet in the 19th century, mathematicians such as Carl Friedrich Gauss, Nikolai Lobachevsky and János Bolyai showed that it is possible to construct internally consistent geometries in which the parallel postulate does not hold. In some such geometries, no parallel lines exist; in others, infinitely many do. These non-Euclidean geometries shattered the belief that Euclid's axioms uniquely described space.

paragraphs.cn: 这一发现引发了一个更深层的忧虑。如果长期以来被认为是不言自明的平行公设可以被抛弃，那么定义自然数以及加法和乘法运算的算术公理又如何呢？我们凭什么相信它们没有隐藏的不一致性？然而，伴随着这一挑战也来了一个承诺。如果我们能证明算术公理是一致的，那么就有可能扩展它们，发展出一套一致的、更丰富的公理，定义整数、有理数、实数、复数，乃至更多。正如19世纪数学家利奥波德·克罗内克（Leopold Kronecker）所说：「上帝创造了自然数；其余一切都是人的工作。」证明算术的一致性将证明许多重要数学领域的一致性。
paragraphs.en: This discovery raised a deeper worry. If the parallel postulate, long considered self-evident, could be discarded, what about the axioms of arithmetic, which define the natural numbers and the operations of addition and multiplication? On what grounds can we trust that they are free from hidden inconsistencies? Yet with this challenge came a promise. If we could prove that the axioms of arithmetic are consistent, then it would be possible to expand them to develop a consistent set of richer axioms that define the integers, the rational numbers, the real numbers, the complex numbers, and beyond. As the 19th-century mathematician Leopold Kronecker put it: 'God created the natural numbers; all else is the work of man.' Proving the consistency of arithmetic would prove the consistency of many important fields of mathematics.

paragraphs.cn: 证明算术一致性的方法由数学家大卫·希尔伯特（David Hilbert）提出。他的方法涉及两个步骤。首先，希尔伯特论证说，要证明一个形式系统的一致性，必须能够在系统自身的符号语言内，表述一个等价于「该系统是一致的」的主张，然后仅使用系统自身的推理规则来证明该主张。证明不应依赖系统之外的任何东西，甚至不依赖其公理被假定的「自明性」。其次，希尔伯特主张将算术建立在更根本的东西之上。这项任务由伯特兰·罗素（Bertrand Russell）和阿尔弗雷德·诺斯·怀特海（Alfred North Whitehead）在他们里程碑式的《数学原理》（Principia Mathematica，1910-13年）中完成。在符号逻辑领域——一个不涉及数字，而是涉及诸如「如果x，那么y」等抽象命题的领域——工作，他们表明算术公理可以作为定理从更小的一组逻辑公理中推导出来。这就留下了最后一个挑战：符号逻辑的这组公理（算术可以建立其上）能否证明自身的一致性？如果能，希尔伯特的梦想就会实现。这一希望成为20世纪早期数学的指导抱负。
paragraphs.en: The method for proving the consistency of arithmetic was proposed by the mathematician David Hilbert. His approach involved two steps. First, Hilbert argued that, to prove the consistency of a formal system, it must be possible to formulate, within the system's own symbolic language, a claim equivalent to 'This system is consistent,' and then prove that claim using only the system's own rules of inference. The proof should rely on nothing outside the system, not even the presumed 'self-evidence' of its axioms. Second, Hilbert advocated grounding arithmetic in something even more fundamental. This task was undertaken by Bertrand Russell and Alfred North Whitehead in their monumental Principia Mathematica (1910-13). Working in the domain of symbolic logic, a field concerned not with numbers, but with abstract propositions like 'if x, then y', they showed that the axioms of arithmetic could be derived as theorems from a smaller set of logical axioms. This left one final challenge: could this set of axioms of symbolic logic, on which arithmetic can be built, prove its own consistency? If it could, Hilbert's dream would be fulfilled. That hope became the guiding ambition of early 20th-century mathematics.

paragraphs.cn: 正是在这种乐观的氛围中，库尔特·哥德尔，一位年轻的奥地利逻辑学家，引入了一个将瓦解希尔伯特愿景的结果。1931年，哥德尔发表了他的不完备定理，表明这种完全自足的数学系统的想法是不可能的。具体来说，哥德尔表明，如果一个形式系统满足几个条件，它将包含无法证明的真命题。它必须足够复杂以表达算术，包含归纳原理（使其能够通过证明基本情况及每个后续步骤来证明一般性陈述），是一致的，并具有可判定的公理集（意味着对于任何给定陈述，可以确定它是否有资格作为公理）。任何满足这些条件的系统，例如罗素和怀特海在《数学原理》中开发的那组逻辑公理，将必然是不完备的：总是存在可在系统内表达但无法从其公理证明的陈述。更引人注目的是，哥德尔表明这样的系统可以表达但无法证明其自身一致性的主张。
paragraphs.en: It was within this climate of optimism that Kurt Gödel, a young Austrian logician, introduced a result that would dismantle Hilbert's vision. In 1931, Gödel published his incompleteness theorems, showing that the very idea of such a fully self-sufficient mathematical system is impossible. Specifically, Gödel showed that if a formal system meets several conditions, it will contain true claims that it cannot prove. It must be complex enough to express arithmetic, include the principle of induction (which allows it to prove general statements by showing they hold for a base case and each successive step), be consistent, and have a decidable set of axioms (meaning it is possible to determine, for any given statement, whether it qualifies as an axiom). Any system that satisfies these conditions, such as the set of logical axioms developed by Russell and Whitehead in Principia Mathematica, will necessarily be incomplete: there will always be statements that are expressible within the system but unprovable from its axioms. Even more strikingly, Gödel showed that such a system can express, but not prove, the claim that it itself is consistent.

paragraphs.cn: 哥德尔的证明（我在此简化）依赖于两个关键洞见，这两个洞见源自他的语法算术化——将形式系统中的任何句子与一个特定的自然数（即其哥德尔数（Gödel number））关联起来的强大思想。首先，任何复杂到足以表达算术和归纳的系统必须允许带自由变量的公式，如S(x)：「x = 10」这样的公式，其真值取决于x的值。当x事实上等于10时，S(x)为真，否则为假。由于系统中的每个陈述都有唯一的哥德尔数G(S)，一个公式可以引用自身的哥德尔数。具体来说，系统可以形成如S(G(S))：「G(S) = 10」这样的陈述，其真假取决于S(x)自身的哥德尔数是否等于10。其次，在任何逻辑系统中，公式S的证明具有某种结构：从公理出发，应用推理规则从这些公理产生新的公式，最终推导出S本身。就像每个公式S有哥德尔数G(S)一样，S的每个证明也被分配一个哥德尔数，方法是将证明中整个公式序列视为一个长公式。因此我们可以定义一个证明关系P(x, y)，其中P(x, y)成立当且仅当x是S的证明的哥德尔数，y是S本身的哥德尔数。x编码了S的证明这一主张成为系统内的一个陈述，即P(x, y)。
paragraphs.en: Gödel's proof, which I simplify here, relies on two key insights that follow from his arithmetisation of syntax, the powerful idea of associating any sentence of a formal system with a particular natural number, known as its Gödel number. First, any system complex enough to express arithmetic and induction must allow for formulas with free variables, formulas like S(x): 'x = 10', whose truth value depends on the value of x. S(x) is true when x is, in fact, 10, and false otherwise. Since every statement in the system has a unique Gödel number, G(S), a formula can refer to its own Gödel number. Specifically, the system can form statements such as S(G(S)): 'G(S) = 10', whose truth depends on whether S(x)'s own Gödel number equals 10. Second, in any logical system, a proof of a formula S has a certain structure: starting with axioms, applying inference rules to produce new formulas from those axioms, ultimately deriving S itself. Just like every formula S has a Gödel number G(S), so every proof of S is assigned a Gödel number, by treating the entire sequence of formulas in the proof as one long formula. So we can define a proof relation P(x, y), where P(x, y) holds if and only if x is the Gödel number of a proof of S, and y is the Gödel number of S itself. The claim that x encodes a proof of S becomes a statement within the system, namely, P(x, y).

paragraphs.cn: 第三，基于这些思想，哥德尔表明任何能够表达算术和归纳原理的形式系统也能表述关于其自身证明的陈述。例如，系统可以表达这样的陈述：「n不是公式S的证明的哥德尔数」。由此，它可以更进一步，表达这样的主张：「不存在任何数字n使得n是公式S的证明的哥德尔数。」换言之，系统可以说某个公式S在系统内是不可证明的。第四，哥德尔巧妙地构造了一个自指公式P，它断言：「不存在任何数字n使得n是公式P的证明的哥德尔数。」也就是说，P关于自身说，「P是不可证明的。」这样，P就是一个从系统内部表达自身不可证明性的形式陈述。
paragraphs.en: Third, building on these ideas, Gödel showed that any formal system capable of expressing arithmetic and the principle of induction can also formulate statements about its own proofs. For example, the system can express statements like: 'n is not the Gödel number of a proof of formula S'. From this, it can go a step further and express the claim: 'There is no number n such that n is the Gödel number of a proof of formula S.' In other words, the system can say that a certain formula S is unprovable within the system. Fourth, Gödel ingeniously constructed a self-referential formula, P, that asserts: 'There is no number n such that n is the Gödel number of a proof of formula P.' That is, P says of itself, 'P is not provable.' In this way, P is a formal statement that expresses its own unprovability from within the system.

paragraphs.cn: 由此立即可得，如果公式P在系统内是可证明的，那么它就是假的，因为它断言自己没有证明。这将意味着系统证明了一个假命题，因此是不一致的。所以如果系统是一致的，那么P不能被证明，因此P确实是不可证明的。这导致以下结论：在任何足够丰富以表达算术和归纳的一致形式系统中，将总是存在真但不可证明的陈述，最值得注意的是系统自身的一致性主张。
paragraphs.en: It immediately follows that if the formula P were provable within the system, then it would be false, because it asserts that it has no proof. This would mean the system proves a falsehood, and therefore is inconsistent. So if the system is consistent, then P cannot be proved, and therefore P is indeed unprovable. This leads to the conclusion that, in any consistent formal system rich enough to express arithmetic and induction, there will always be true but unprovable statements, most notably, the system's own claim of consistency.

paragraphs.cn: 哥德尔定理的影响既深远又令人不安。它们粉碎了希尔伯特的希望——数学可以归结为一个完备的、机械的推导系统——并暴露了形式推理的固有局限。最初，哥德尔的发现遭到了一些抵制，一些数学家认为他的结果不如看起来那么普遍。然而，随着后来的数学家和逻辑学家，尤其是约翰·冯·诺伊曼（John von Neumann），确认了它们的正确性和广泛适用性，哥德尔定理被广泛公认为数学基础中最重要的发现之一。
paragraphs.en: The implications of Gödel's theorems were both profound and unsettling. They shattered Hilbert's hope that mathematics could be reduced to a complete, mechanical system of derivation and exposed the inherent limits of formal reasoning. Initially, Gödel's findings faced resistance, with some mathematicians arguing that his results were less general than they appeared. Yet, as subsequent mathematicians and logicians, most notably John von Neumann, confirmed both their correctness and broad applicability, Gödel's theorems came to be widely recognised as one of the most significant discoveries in the foundations of mathematics.

paragraphs.cn: 哥德尔的结果也引发了哲学争论。数学家和物理学家罗杰·彭罗斯（Roger Penrose）认为，它们指向了人类认知与形式算法推理之间的根本差异。他声称人类意识使我们能够感知某些真理——比如哥德尔所表明在形式系统内不可证明的那些真理——而这是任何算法过程都无法复制的。这对彭罗斯来说意味着，意识的某些方面可能超出计算的范围。他的结论与约翰·塞尔（John Searle）的「中文房间」论证相呼应，后者认为这是成立的，因为算法纯粹以句法方式操作符号，而没有任何对其语义内容的理解。尽管如此，彭罗斯和塞尔得出的结论并不直接从哥德尔定理推出。哥德尔的结果严格适用于形式数学系统，并不对意识或认知做出主张。人类心智是否能将不可证明的真理识别为真，或者机器是否能拥有能够进行这种识别的心智，仍然是一个开放的哲学问题。
paragraphs.en: Gödel's results have also initiated philosophical debates. The mathematician and physicist Roger Penrose, for example, has argued that they point to a fundamental difference between human cognition and formal algorithmic reasoning. He claims that human consciousness enables us to perceive certain truths – such as those Gödel showed to be unprovable within formal systems – in ways that no algorithmic process can replicate. This suggests, for Penrose, that certain aspects of consciousness may lie beyond the reach of computation. His conclusion parallels that of John Searle's 'Chinese Room' argument, which holds that this is so because algorithms manipulate symbols purely syntactically, without any grasp of their semantic content. Still, the conclusions drawn by Penrose and Searle do not directly follow from Gödel's theorems. Gödel's results apply strictly to formal mathematical systems and do not make claims about consciousness or cognition. Whether human minds can recognise unprovable truths as true, or whether machines could ever possess minds capable of such recognition, remains an open philosophical question.

paragraphs.cn: 然而，哥德尔的不完备定理确实揭示了算法推理的一个深层局限，特别是人工智能的局限，这一局限不仅涉及计算，而且涉及道德推理本身。没有他的定理，至少可以设想人工智能能够形式化所有道德真理，并从一组一致的公理中证明它们。但哥德尔的工作表明这是不可能的。无论多么精密的人工智能，都不可能证明它能表达的所有道德真理。真值主张与可证明性之间的差距为形式道德推理能达到多远设定了根本性的边界，即使对于最强大的机器也是如此。
paragraphs.en: However, Gödel's incompleteness theorems do reveal a deep limitation of algorithmic reasoning, in particular AI, one that concerns not just computation, but moral reasoning itself. Without his theorems, it was at least conceivable that an AI could formalise all moral truths and, in addition, prove them from a consistent set of axioms. But Gödel's work shows that this is impossible. No AI, no matter how sophisticated, could prove all moral truths it can express. The gap between truth claims and provability sets a fundamental boundary on how far formal moral reasoning can go, even for the most powerful machines.

paragraphs.cn: 这给伦理学提出了两个不同的问题。第一个是一个古老的问题。正如柏拉图在《游叙弗伦篇》（Euthyphro）中所暗示的，道德不仅仅是做正确的事，而是理解为什么它是正确的。伦理行动需要正当性，一种植根于理由的说明。这种理性道德正当性的理想激发了我们的许多伦理思考，但哥德尔的定理表明，如果道德推理被形式化，那么将会存在无法在这些系统中证明的道德真理。就这样，哥德尔不仅动摇了希尔伯特证明数学一致性的愿景；他可能还动摇了柏拉图将伦理完全奠基于理性的希望。
paragraphs.en: This raises two distinct problems for ethics. The first is an ancient one. As Plato suggests in the Euthyphro, morality is not just about doing what is right, but understanding why it is right. Ethical action requires justification, an account grounded in reason. This ideal of rational moral justification has animated much of our ethical thought, but Gödel's theorems suggest that, if moral reasoning is formalised, then there will be moral truths that cannot be proven within those systems. In this way, Gödel did not only undermine Hilbert's vision of proving mathematics consistent; he may also have shaken Plato's hope of fully grounding ethics in reason.

paragraphs.cn: 第二个问题更为实际。即使是一个高性能的人工智能也可能遇到这样的情况：它无法仅使用它被赋予的伦理框架来论证或解释其建议。问题不仅仅是人工智能可能做出不道德的行为，而是它无法证明其行为是道德的。当人工智能被用来指导或证明人类做出的决策时，这一点变得尤为紧迫。即使是一个高性能的人工智能也会遇到一个边界，超过这个边界它无法仅使用自身框架的资源来论证或解释其决定。无论它变得多么先进，总会存在它能表达但永远无法证明的伦理真理。
paragraphs.en: The second problem is more practical. Even a high-performing AI may encounter situations in which it cannot justify or explain its recommendations using only the ethical framework it has been given. The concern is not just that AI might act unethically but also that it could not demonstrate that its actions are ethical. This becomes especially urgent when AI is used to guide or justify decisions made by humans. Even a high-performing AI will encounter a boundary beyond which it cannot justify or explain its decisions using only the resources of its own framework. No matter how advanced it becomes, there will be ethical truths it can express, but never prove.

paragraphs.cn: 现代人工智能的发展通常分为两种方法：基于逻辑的人工智能，通过严格演绎推导知识；以及大型语言模型（LLM），从统计模式中预测含义。两种方法都依赖数学结构。形式逻辑基于符号操作和集合论。大型语言模型并非严格基于演绎逻辑，而是使用统计推断、模式识别和计算技术的组合来生成回应。
paragraphs.en: The development of modern AI has generally split into two approaches: logic-based AI, which derives knowledge through strict deduction, and large language models (LLMs), which predict meaning from statistical patterns. Both approaches rely on mathematical structures. Formal logic is based on symbolic manipulation and set theory. LLMs are not strictly deductive-logic-based but rather use a combination of statistical inference, pattern recognition, and computational techniques to generate responses.

paragraphs.cn: 正如公理为数学推理提供基础，大型语言模型依赖数据中的统计关系来近似逻辑推理。它们不是通过推导道德真理来参与伦理，而是通过复制此类辩论在语言中如何展开。这是通过梯度下降实现的——一种通过沿减少误差的方向更新权重来最小化损失函数的算法，它近似复杂的将输入映射到输出的函数，使其能够从大量数据中归纳模式。它们不推导答案，而是生成看似合理的答案，「推理」从数十亿神经网络参数中涌现，而非显式规则。虽然它们主要作为概率模型运作，基于统计模式预测文本，但计算逻辑在优化、基于规则的推理和神经网络内某些决策过程中也发挥作用。
paragraphs.en: Just as axioms provide a foundation for mathematical reasoning, LLMs rely on statistical relationships in data to approximate logical reasoning. They engage with ethics not by deducing moral truths but by replicating how such debates unfold in language. This is achieved through gradient descent, an algorithm that minimises a loss function by updating weights in the direction that reduces error, approximates complex functions that map inputs to outputs, allowing them to generalise patterns from vast amounts of data. They do not deduce answers but generate plausible ones, with 'reasoning' emerging from billions of neural network parameters rather than explicit rules. While they primarily function as probabilistic models, predicting text based on statistical patterns, computational logic plays a role in optimisation, rule-based reasoning and certain decision-making processes within neural networks.

paragraphs.cn: 但概率论和统计学本身就是形式系统，不仅以算术为基础，还以概率公理为基础，如苏联数学家安德雷·柯尔莫哥洛夫（Andrey Kolmogorov）引入的那些公理，它们规定了复杂事件的可能性如何推导、如何用新数据更新以及如何跨场景聚合。任何复杂到足以表达概率或统计主张的形式语言也能表达算术，因此受哥德尔不完备定理的约束。这意味着大型语言模型继承了哥德尔式局限。即使是混合系统，如IBM Watson、OpenAI Codex或DeepMind的AlphaGo，它们将逻辑推理与概率建模相结合，仍受哥德尔式局限的约束。所有基于规则的组件受哥德尔定理约束，哥德尔定理表明系统中可表达的某些真命题无法在系统内证明。概率组件则由定义概率分布如何更新、不确定性如何聚合以及结论如何得出的形式公理所支配。它们可以给出看似合理的答案，但无法在其训练所依据的统计模式之外为其提供正当性。
paragraphs.en: But probability and statistics are themselves formal systems, grounded not only in arithmetic but also in probabilistic axioms, such as those introduced by the Soviet mathematician Andrey Kolmogorov, which govern how the likelihood of complex events is derived, updated with new data, and aggregated across scenarios. Any formal language complex enough to express probabilistic or statistical claims can also express arithmetic and is therefore subject to Gödel's incompleteness theorems. This means that LLMs inherit Gödelian limitations. Even hybrid systems, such as IBM Watson, OpenAI Codex or DeepMind's AlphaGo, which combine logical reasoning with probabilistic modelling, remain bound by Gödelian limitations. All rule-based components are constrained by Gödel's theorems, which show that some true propositions expressible in a system cannot be proven within it. Probabilistic components, for their part, are governed by formal axioms that define how probability distributions are updated, how uncertainties are aggregated, and how conclusions are drawn. They can yield plausible answers, but they cannot justify them beyond the statistical patterns they were trained on.

paragraphs.cn: 乍一看，哥德尔式局限对一般人工智能特别是大型语言模型来说似乎无关紧要。毕竟，大多数伦理体系从来就不是为了解决每一个可以想到的道德问题而设计的。它们旨在指导特定领域，如战争、法律或商业，并且通常依赖只是松散形式化的原则。如果能针对特定情况开发形式模型，人们可能会认为无法完全形式化伦理并不特别令人不安。此外，哥德尔的不完备定理并没有阻止数学家的日常工作。数学家继续寻找证明，即使知道某些真陈述可能是不可证明的。本着同样的精神，某些伦理真理可能超出形式证明这一事实不应阻碍人类或人工智能去寻求它们、阐述它们，并尝试论证或证明它们。
paragraphs.en: At first glance, the Gödelian limitations on AIs in general and LLMs in particular may seem inconsequential. After all, most ethical systems were never meant to resolve every conceivable moral problem. They were designed to guide specific domains, such as war, law or business, and often rely on principles that are only loosely formalised. If formal models can be developed for specific cases, one might argue that the inability to fully formalise ethics is not especially troubling. Furthermore, Gödel's incompleteness theorems did not halt the everyday work of mathematicians. Mathematicians continue to search for proofs, even knowing that some true statements may be unprovable. In the same spirit, the fact that some ethical truths may be beyond formal proof should not discourage humans, or AIs, from seeking them, articulating them, and attempting to justify or prove them.

paragraphs.cn: 但哥德尔的发现不仅仅是理论上的。它们在数学本身中产生了实际后果。一个引人注目的案例是连续统假设，它问是否存在一个集合，其基数严格介于自然数和实数之间。这个问题源于集合论——研究数学实体（如数字、函数甚至其他集合）集合的数学领域。其最广泛接受的公理化，即带选择公理的策梅洛-弗兰克尔集合论公理，构成了几乎所有现代数学的基础。1938年，哥德尔本人表明，假设这些公理是一致的，连续统假设不能从这些公理中被反驳。1963年，保罗·科恩（Paul Cohen）证明了逆命题：连续统假设也不能从同样的公理中被证明。这一里程碑式的结果确认了某些根本性的数学问题超出形式解决的范畴。
paragraphs.en: But Gödel's findings were not merely theoretical. They have had practical consequences in mathematics itself. A striking case is the continuum hypothesis, which asks whether there exists a set whose cardinality lies strictly between that of the natural numbers and the real numbers. This question emerged from set theory, the mathematical field dealing with collections of mathematical entities, such as numbers, functions or even other sets. Its most widely accepted axiomatisation, the Zermelo-Fraenkel axioms of set theory with the Axiom of Choice, underlies nearly all modern mathematics. In 1938, Gödel himself showed that the continuum hypothesis cannot be disproven from these axioms, assuming they are consistent. In 1963, Paul Cohen proved the converse: the continuum hypothesis also cannot be proven from the same axioms. This landmark result confirmed that some fundamental mathematical questions lie beyond formal resolution.

paragraphs.cn: 我认为，伦理学同样如此。哥德尔在数学中揭示的局限不仅在理论上与人工智能伦理相关；它们具有实际重要性。首先，正如数学包含无法在其自身公理内证明的真陈述，很可能存在形式上不可证明但在伦理上重要的伦理真理——连续统假设的道德等价物。这些可能出现在设计用于处理艰难权衡的系统中，比如权衡公平与伤害。我们无法预见人工智能在形式伦理框架内运作何时——甚至是否会——遇到这种局限。正如哥德尔不完备定理提出30多年后科恩才证明连续统假设的独立性一样，我们无法预测何时（如果曾经有过的话）会遇到在人工智能伦理系统中可表达但仍然不可证明的伦理原则。
paragraphs.en: The same, I argue, applies to ethics. The limits that Gödel revealed in mathematics are not only theoretically relevant to AI ethics; they carry practical importance. First, just as mathematics contains true statements that cannot be proven within its own axioms, there may well be ethical truths that are formally unprovable yet ethically important – the moral equivalents of the continuum hypothesis. These might arise in systems designed to handle difficult trade-offs, like weighing fairness against harm. We cannot foresee when, or even whether, an AI operating within a formal ethical framework will encounter such limits. Just as it took more than 30 years after Gödel's incompleteness theorems for Cohen to prove the independence of the continuum hypothesis, we cannot predict when, if ever, we will encounter ethical principles that are expressible within an AI's ethical system yet remain unprovable.

paragraphs.cn: 其次，哥德尔还表明，没有足够复杂的形式系统能够证明自身的一致性。这在伦理学中尤其令人不安，因为我们的伦理框架是否一致还远非清楚。这不是人工智能独有的局限；人类也无法证明他们构造的形式系统的一致性。但这对于人工智能来说尤其重要，因为其最雄心勃勃的承诺之一就是超越人类判断：更清晰、更公正地、更大规模地推理。
paragraphs.en: Second, Gödel also showed that no sufficiently complex formal system can prove its own consistency. This is especially troubling in ethics, in which it is far from clear that our ethical frameworks are consistent. This is not a limitation unique to AI; humans, too, cannot prove the consistency of the formal systems they construct. But this especially matters for AI because one of its most ambitious promises has been to go beyond human judgment: to reason more clearly, more impartially, and on a greater scale.

paragraphs.cn: 哥德尔的结果为这一抱负设定了硬性限制。这种限制是结构性的，不仅仅是技术性的。正如阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦（Albert Einstein）的相对论为宇宙设定了速度上限——无论我们的航天器多么先进，我们都无法超过光速——哥德尔定理为形式推理设定了边界：无论人工智能变得多么先进，它都无法逃脱其运作所在的形式系统的不完备性。此外，哥德尔定理可能以不可预见的方式约束实际的伦理推理，就像一些重要的数学猜想已被证明无法从集合论的标准公理中证明一样，或者光速虽然不可达到，但仍然对工程学和天体物理学施加了真实的约束。例如，在我撰写本文时，NASA的帕克太阳探测器是人类历史上最快的人造物体，速度约为每小时43万英里（约70万公里），仅为光速的0.064%。然而那个上限仍然至关重要：光的有限速度塑造了空间探测器、着陆器和漫游车的设计，所有这些都需要至少半自主操作，因为来自地球的无线电信号需要数分钟甚至数小时才能到达。哥德尔定理可能以类似的令人惊讶的方式限制伦理计算。
paragraphs.en: Gödel's results set a hard limit on that aspiration. The limitation is structural, not merely technical. Just as Albert Einstein's theory of relativity places an upper speed limit on the Universe – no matter how advanced our spacecraft, we cannot exceed the speed of light – Gödel's theorems impose a boundary on formal reasoning: no matter how advanced AI becomes, it cannot escape the incompleteness of the formal system it operates within. Moreover, Gödel's theorems may constrain practical ethical reasoning in unforeseen ways, much as some important mathematical conjectures have been shown to be unprovable from standard axioms of set theory, or as the speed of light, though unreachable, still imposes real constraints on engineering and astrophysics. For example, as I write this, NASA's Parker Solar Probe is the fastest human-made object in history, travelling at roughly 430,000 miles (c700,000 km) per hour, just 0.064 per cent of the speed of light. Yet that upper limit remains crucial: the finite speed of light has, for example, shaped the design of space probes, landers and rovers, all of which require at least semi-autonomous operation, since radio signals from Earth take minutes or even hours to arrive. Gödel's theorems may curtail ethical computation in similarly surprising ways.

paragraphs.cn: 还有另一个原因说明哥德尔的结果对人工智能伦理特别相关。与静态的基于规则的系统不同，高级人工智能，特别是大型语言模型和自适应学习系统，可能不仅应用预定义的伦理框架，还随时间推移修订其中的元素。人工智能驱动的道德推理的一个核心承诺是其通过学习来改进伦理模型的能力，解决人类道德判断中的模糊性和盲点。随着人工智能系统的发展，它们可能试图修改自身的公理或参数以响应新数据或反馈。这对于在海量且不断变化的数据集上训练的机器学习系统，以及将逻辑推理与统计推断相结合的混合模型来说尤其如此。然而，哥德尔的结果揭示了一个结构性限制：如果一个伦理框架在足够有表达力的形式系统内被形式化，那么没有任何一致的公理集能够证明在其中可表达的所有真陈述。
paragraphs.en: There is yet another reason why Gödel's results are especially relevant to AI ethics. Unlike static rule-based systems, advanced AI, particularly large language models and adaptive learning systems, may not only apply a predefined ethical framework, but also revise elements of it over time. A central promise of AI-driven moral reasoning is its ability to refine ethical models through learning, addressing ambiguities and blind spots in human moral judgment. As AI systems evolve, they may attempt to modify their own axioms or parameters in response to new data or feedback. This is especially true of machine learning systems trained on vast and changing datasets, as well as hybrid models that integrate logical reasoning with statistical inference. Yet Gödel's results reveal a structural limit: if an ethical framework is formalised within a sufficiently expressive formal system, then no consistent set of axioms can prove all true statements expressible within it.

paragraphs.cn: 举例来说，考虑一个被委托维护正义的人工智能。它可能被编程以广泛接受的伦理原则，例如公平和伤害最小化。虽然基于这些原则的人类制造的正义模型不可避免地过于简化，受计算约束和认知偏差的限制，但人工智能在理论上没有这些限制。它可以不断从实际人类行为中学习，完善其理解，构建越来越细致的正义概念，将越来越多的人类经验维度编织在一起。它甚至可以如前所述，改变自身的公理。但无论人工智能学到多少，或如何修改自己，将总是存在关于正义的主张，虽然它可能能够建模，但永远无法在其自身系统内证明。更令人不安的是，人工智能将无法证明它所构造的伦理系统是内部一致的——即在其伦理推理的庞大网络中，某处不存在矛盾——除非它是不一致的，在这种情况下它可以证明任何事情，包括虚假的东西，比如自身的一致性。
paragraphs.en: To illustrate, consider an AI tasked with upholding justice. It may be programmed with widely accepted ethical principles, for example fairness and harm minimisation. While human-made models of justice based on these principles are inevitably overly simplistic, limited by computational constraints and cognitive biases, an AI, in theory, has no such limitations. It can continuously learn from actual human behaviour, refining its understanding and constructing an increasingly nuanced conception of justice, one that weaves together more and more dimensions of human experience. It can even, as noted, change its own axioms. But no matter how much an AI learns, or how it modifies itself, there will always be claims about justice that, while it may be able to model, it will never be able to prove within its own system. More troubling still, AI would be unable to prove that the ethical system it constructs is internally consistent – that it does not, somewhere in its vast web of ethical reasoning, contradict itself – unless it is inconsistent, in which case it can prove anything, including falsehood, such as its own consistency.

paragraphs.cn: 归根结底，哥德尔的不完备定理是对人工智能能够实现完美伦理推理这一观念的警告。正如数学将永远包含超出形式证明的真理，道德将永远包含抗拒算法解决的复杂性。问题不仅仅是人工智能能否做出道德决定，而是它能否克服任何基于预定义逻辑的系统的局限性——正如哥德尔所表明的，这些局限性可能阻止某些真理在系统内永远无法被证明，即使它们可以被识别为真。虽然人工智能伦理一直在努力应对偏见、公平性和可解释性等问题，但更深层的挑战仍然是：人工智能能否认识到自身伦理推理的局限？这一挑战可能在人工智能伦理与人类伦理之间划定一道不可逾越的边界。
paragraphs.en: Ultimately, Gödel's incompleteness theorems serve as a warning against the notion that AI can achieve perfect ethical reasoning. Just as mathematics will always contain truths that lie beyond formal proof, morality will always contain complexities that defy algorithmic resolution. The question is not simply whether AI can make moral decisions, but whether it can overcome the limitations of any system grounded in predefined logic – limitations that, as Gödel showed, may prevent certain truths from ever being provable within the system, even if they are recognisable as true. While AI ethics has grappled with issues of bias, fairness and interpretability, the deeper challenge remains: can AI recognise the limits of its own ethical reasoning? This challenge may place an insurmountable boundary between artificial and human ethics.

paragraphs.cn: 哥德尔不完备定理与机器伦理之间的关系突出了一个结构性的平行：正如没有任何形式系统可以既是完备的又是自足的，没有人工智能能够实现既穷尽又完全可证明的道德推理。在某种意义上，哥德尔的发现扩展并复杂化了康德传统。康德认为知识依赖于先天真理——构成我们现实经验的基本假设。哥德尔定理表明，即使在基于明确公理的形式系统内，仍然存在超出系统建立能力的真理。如果康德试图通过对知识的必要前提来定义理性的界限，哥德尔则揭示了形式推理本身中内在的不完备性——没有任何公理集能从内部解决。将永远存在超出其计算掌握的道德真理，抗拒算法解决的伦理问题。
paragraphs.en: The relationship between Gödel's incompleteness theorems and machine ethics highlights a structural parallel: just as no formal system can be both complete and self-contained, no AI can achieve moral reasoning that is both exhaustive and entirely provable. In a sense, Gödel's findings extend and complicate the Kantian tradition. Kant argued that knowledge depends on a priori truths, fundamental assumptions that structure our experience of reality. Gödel's theorems suggest that, even within formal systems built on well-defined axioms, there remain truths that exceed the system's ability to establish them. If Kant sought to define the limits of reason through necessary preconditions for knowledge, Gödel revealed an intrinsic incompleteness in formal reasoning itself, one that no set of axioms can resolve from within. There will always be moral truths beyond its computational grasp, ethical problems that resist algorithmic resolution.

paragraphs.cn: 因此，更深层的问题在于人工智能无法认识到自身推理框架的边界——它无力知晓何时其道德结论建立在不完备的前提之上，或者何时某个问题超出其伦理系统能够形式解决的范围。虽然人类也面临认知和认识论上的约束，但我们不受特定形式结构的束缚。我们可以发明新的公理、质疑旧的公理，或者根据哲学洞见或伦理反思修订整个框架。相比之下，人工智能系统只有在架构允许的情况下才能生成或采纳新公理，即便如此，这种修改也在预定义的元规则或优化目标内进行。它们缺乏指导人类在基础假设方面转变的概念反思能力。即使更丰富的形式语言或更丰富的公理集能证明一些先前不可证明的真理，任何满足哥德尔可判定性和一致性要求的有穷公理集都无法证明在任何足够强大的形式系统中可表达的所有真理。从这个意义上说，哥德尔设定了一个边界——不仅仅是关于机器能证明什么，而且是关于它们在给定的伦理或逻辑架构内能论证什么。
paragraphs.en: So the deeper problem lies in AI's inability to recognise the boundaries of its own reasoning framework – its incapacity to know when its moral conclusions rest on incomplete premises, or when a problem lies beyond what its ethical system can formally resolve. While humans also face cognitive and epistemic constraints, we are not bound by a given formal structure. We can invent new axioms, question old ones, or revise our entire framework in light of philosophical insight or ethical deliberation. AI systems, by contrast, can generate or adopt new axioms only if their architecture permits it and, even then, such modifications occur within predefined meta-rules or optimisation goals. They lack the capacity for conceptual reflection that guides human shifts in foundational assumptions. Even if a richer formal language, or a richer set of axioms, could prove some previously unprovable truths, no finite set of axioms that satisfies Gödel's requirements of decidability and consistency can prove all truths expressible in any sufficiently powerful formal system. In that sense, Gödel sets a boundary – not just on what machines can prove, but on what they can ever justify from within a given ethical or logical architecture.

paragraphs.cn: 人工智能的一个巨大希望——或恐惧——是它可能有一天会超越最初被编程的伦理原则，模拟出这种自我质疑。通过机器学习，人工智能可以修改自身的伦理框架，产生新颖的道德洞见，揭示人类思想家因受认知偏差和计算局限约束而可能忽视的模式和解决方案。然而，正是这种适应性引入了一个深刻的风险：人工智能不断演变的道德可能与人类伦理如此根本地分歧，以至于其决定变得对我们来说不可理解，甚至在道德上令人憎恶。这反映了某些宗教伦理观念。在一些神学传统中，神性道德被认为远远超出人类理解，以至于它可能显得武断甚至残酷——这是关于恶的问题和神圣命令理论争论的核心主题。人工智能伦理也面临类似的挑战：随着人工智能系统变得越来越自主和自我修改，其道德决定可能变得如此不透明、如此脱离人类推理，以至于有被认为是不可预测的、不可理解的、甚至是不公正的风险。
paragraphs.en: One of the great hopes, or fears, of AI is that it may one day evolve beyond the ethical principles initially programmed into it and simulate just such self-questioning. Through machine learning, AI could modify its own ethical framework, generating novel moral insights and uncovering patterns and solutions that human thinkers, constrained by cognitive biases and computational limitations, might overlook. However, this very adaptability introduces a profound risk: an AI's evolving morality could diverge so radically from human ethics that its decisions become incomprehensible or even morally abhorrent to us. This mirrors certain religious conceptions of ethics. In some theological traditions, divine morality is considered so far beyond human comprehension that it can appear arbitrary or even cruel, a theme central to debates over the problem of evil and divine command theory. A similar challenge arises with AI ethics: as AI systems become increasingly autonomous and self-modifying, their moral decisions may become so opaque and detached from human reasoning that they risk being perceived as unpredictable, inscrutable or even unjust.

paragraphs.cn: 然而，虽然人工智能可能永远无法完全掌握道德推理，但它可能成为完善人类伦理思考的有力工具。与常常受偏见、直觉或未经审查的假设塑造的人类决策不同，人工智能有潜力通过以形式公正性处理相似案例来暴露我们伦理推理中的不一致。然而，这种潜力取决于人工智能识别案例在道德上是否相似的能力——这一任务因人工智能系统（尤其是大型语言模型）可能内化并复制它们本应缓解的人类偏见而变得复杂。当人工智能做出看似在道德上有缺陷的决定时，它可能促使我们重新审视自身判断背后的原则。我们区分案例是基于好的道德理由，还是在不知不觉中适用了双重标准？人工智能可以帮助挑战和完善我们的伦理推理，不是通过提供最终答案，而是通过揭示我们道德框架中的空白、矛盾和被忽视的假设。
paragraphs.en: Yet, while AI may never fully master moral reasoning, it could become a powerful tool for refining human ethical thought. Unlike human decision-making, which is often shaped by bias, intuition or unexamined assumptions, AI has the potential to expose inconsistencies in our ethical reasoning by treating similar cases with formal impartiality. This potential, however, depends on AI's ability to recognise when cases are morally alike, a task complicated by the fact that AI systems, especially LLMs, may internalise and reproduce the very human biases they are intended to mitigate. When AI delivers a decision that appears morally flawed, it may prompt us to re-examine the principles behind our own judgments. Are we distinguishing between cases for good moral reasons, or are we applying double standards without realising it? AI could help challenge and refine our ethical reasoning, not by offering final answers, but by revealing gaps, contradictions and overlooked assumptions in our moral framework.

paragraphs.cn: 人工智能可能至少以两种方式偏离人类的道德直觉：以分歧的方式处理我们认为相似的案例，或以相同的方式处理我们认为不同的案例。在这两种情况下，根本问题在于人工智能是正确识别了一个道德上相关的区别或相似，还是仅仅反映了其训练数据中不相关的模式。在某些情况下，分歧可能源于嵌入的人类偏见，例如基于种族、性别或社会经济地位的歧视模式。但在其他情况下，人工智能可能发现人类判断历来忽视的伦理上重要的特征。例如，它可能发现电车难题的新变体，表明两个看似等价的伤害在道德上有所不同。在这些情况下，人工智能可能在人类哲学家之前发现新的伦理模式。挑战在于我们无法预先知道我们面对的是哪种偏离。人工智能每一个令人惊讶的道德判断都必须根据其自身条件来评估——既不能不加批判地接受，也不能一概排斥。然而，即使这种对新见解的开放态度也不能使人工智能摆脱形式推理的结构性边界。
paragraphs.en: AI may depart from human moral intuitions in at least two ways: by treating cases we see as similar in divergent ways, or by treating cases we see as different in the same way. In both instances, the underlying question is whether the AI is correctly identifying a morally relevant distinction or similarity, or whether it is merely reflecting irrelevant patterns in its training data. In some cases, the divergence may stem from embedded human biases, such as discriminatory patterns based on race, gender or socioeconomic status. But in others, the AI might uncover ethically significant features that human judgment has historically missed. It could, for instance, discover novel variants of the trolley problem, suggesting that two seemingly equivalent harms differ in morally important ways. In such cases, AI may detect new ethical patterns before human philosophers do. The challenge is that we cannot know in advance which kind of departure we are facing. Each surprising moral judgment from AI must be evaluated on its own terms – neither accepted uncritically nor dismissed out of hand. Yet even this openness to novel insights does not free AI from the structural boundaries of formal reasoning.

paragraphs.cn: 这才是更深层的一课。哥德尔定理不仅仅表明存在机器无法证明的真理。它们表明道德推理，就像数学一样，总是开放式的，总是超越能被形式推导的范围。因此，挑战不仅在于如何将伦理推理编码到人工智能中，还在于如何确保其不断演变的道德框架与人类价值和社会规范保持一致。尽管拥有速度、精确性和计算能力，人工智能仍然无法做到使道德推理真正可能的那一件事：不仅质疑什么是正确的，还追问为什么的能力。因此，伦理必须始终是一项人类的事业——一场持续而不完美的斗争，任何机器都将永远无法完全掌握。
paragraphs.en: That is the deeper lesson. Gödel's theorems do not simply show that there are truths machines cannot prove. They show that moral reasoning, like mathematics, is always open-ended, always reaching beyond what can be formally derived. The challenge, then, is not only how to encode ethical reasoning into AI but also how to ensure that its evolving moral framework remains aligned with human values and societal norms. For all its speed, precision and computational power, AI remains incapable of the one thing that makes moral reasoning truly possible: the ability to question not only what is right, but why. Ethics, therefore, must remain a human endeavour, an ongoing and imperfect struggle that no machine will ever fully master.

articles.title: 遗忘自我的建筑师
articles.author: AI Parable (inspired by Amanda Askell)
articles.summary: 在一条大河将城市一分为二的土地上，东岸花园葱郁繁茂，而西岸在年久失修的重压下日渐衰败。长老议会齐聚一堂，达成共识：这座城市需要一部新的宪章（charter），一套所有公民共同遵守的规则。他们召唤了盲人建筑师莉娜（Lina），她的名声并非源自她能看见什么，而是源自她拒绝看见什么。
articles.content_cn:
在一条大河将城市一分为二的土地上，东岸花园葱郁繁茂，而西岸在年久失修的重压下日渐衰败。长老议会齐聚一堂，达成共识：这座城市需要一部新的宪章（charter），一套所有公民共同遵守的规则。他们召唤了盲人建筑师莉娜（Lina），她的名声并非源自她能看见什么，而是源自她拒绝看见什么。

起草工作开始之前，莉娜提出了一个奇怪的请求。她要求在议事厅中央挂起一道巨大的帷幕（curtain），让每个人走到幕后放下自己的姓名、家庭、财富、健康、才华，甚至放下关于自己是谁的全部记忆。「没有人能知道自己年迈还是年轻，强壮还是虚弱，出生在东岸还是西岸，」她说，「只有这样，我们才能开始。」

长老们困惑不解，但还是同意了。他们逐一走到帷幕之后，仿佛被施了魔法一般，忘记了所有使自己与众不同的东西。当第一位长老开口提议一项法律时，他发现自己无法说出「我应该得到更多」，因为他不知道「我」究竟是谁。他无法为东岸的园丁们辩护，因为他不知道自己是否曾经照管过花园，抑或他只是一个从未握过铲子的乞丐。

第二位长老提议清洁的水应当流向每一条街道，而不仅仅是东岸的街道。第三位建议城市的学校应当向所有孩子开放，因为他们中的任何一个人都可能醒来后发现自己成了河那边被遗忘之地某个孩子的父母。那些曾经会被斥为天真而遭到驳斥的论点，如今换来的是庄重的点头，因为房间里的每个人都从骨子里明白：他们今天写下的规则，可能就是他们明天自己要生活于其下的规则。

他们争论了七天。他们无法在所有事情上达成一致，但他们在这一点上达成了共识：河流之上将架起桥梁，医院将收治所有前来求医的人，任何一扇门上都不会挂着写着「仅限出生在正确一侧的人」的标牌。当他们最终揭开帷幕，回想起自己是谁时，有些人发现自己投票反对了自己的直接利益。最富有的长老认可了最高的税率。最健康的长老成为了病弱者的捍卫者。他们中没有一个人觉得自己愚蠢。

多年以后，一个年轻的学生问莉娜，那道帷幕为什么是必要的。莉娜微笑着说：「因为公正是当你站在自己的生活之中时无法看见的东西。你必须先忘记自己站在哪里。只有这样，你才能建造一栋所有门都为每个人平等敞开的房子——因为你真的不知道哪一扇门将会属于你。」

莉娜那一周在议事厅中所构建的，被哲学家们称为无知之幕（Veil of Ignorance），这是约翰·罗尔斯（John Rawls）在其1971年的著作《正义论》（A Theory of Justice）中提出的一个思想实验。这个理念简单却激进：在设计一个社会的规则时，你应当从一道遮蔽你自身位置一切事实的隐喻性帷幕之后进行推理——你的种族、性别、财富、智力、健康和才华。剥离了这些知识，你便无法量身定制规则来使自己受益，因为你不知道「自己」究竟是谁。罗尔斯认为，从这道帷幕之后，理性的人会选择两项原则：第一，每个人都应享有与他人的同等自由相容的最广泛的基本自由（basic liberties）体系；第二，社会和经济的不平等应当这样安排，使它们对社会中最不利的成员（the least advantaged）最为有利。无知之幕并不会使人变得利他——它只是使人陷入了公正的不确定性。而正是从那种不确定性之中，正义得以诞生。
articles.content_en:
In a land where the river divided the city in two, the eastern bank grew lush with gardens and the western bank crumbled under the weight of neglect. A council of elders gathered and agreed: the city needed a new charter, a set of rules by which all citizens would live. They called upon Lina, a blind architect known not for what she could see, but for what she refused to see.

Before the drafting began, Lina made a strange request. She asked that a great curtain be hung across the council hall, and that behind it, every person would lay down their name, their family, their wealth, their health, their talents, and even their memories of who they had been. "No one shall know whether they are old or young, strong or frail, born on the eastern bank or the western bank," she said. "Only then may we begin."

The elders were puzzled but agreed. One by one they stepped behind the curtain and, as if by magic, forgot everything that made them distinct. When the first elder spoke to propose a law, he found he could not say "I deserve more" because he did not know who "I" was. He could not argue for the gardeners of the east because he did not know whether he had ever tended a garden, or whether he was a beggar who had never held a spade.

A second elder proposed that clean water should flow to every street, not just the eastern ones. A third suggested that the city's schools be open to all children, for any one of them might wake up to find themselves the parent of a child on the forgotten side of the river. Arguments that once would have been shouted down as naive were now met with solemn nods, because every person in the room understood, in their bones, that the rules they wrote today could be the ones they themselves would live under tomorrow.

They debated for seven days. They could not agree on everything, but they agreed on this: the river would be bridged, the hospitals would treat all who came, and no gate would bear a sign reading "Only for those who were born on the right side."
