Attractors are the product of processes engaging in inference to summon themselves into being. In other words, attractors are the foundation of what it means to be alive.

To the extent that you accept the above formulation, you now have the ultimate deflationary account of every kind of complex system, living things included. Any process (like you or me) that repeatedly occupies certain states must, by virtue of its very existence, be performing inference.

But does this make sense? You'd hardly consider the process of evolution or natural selection in terms of inference – or would you? In fact, that's exactly the interpretation currently found in theoretical neurobiology. It turns out, for example, that the way nature 'selects' organisms for their capacity to survive and reproduce is based on inference. Take a population of crabs as the system in question, and the aggregated features or _phenotypes_ of its individuals as its 'state'. These crabs can have claws of different sizes, shells that are harder or softer, and eyes that see better above water or below. Such diverse phenotypes amount to multiple hypotheses about what might 'work'; each individual is a hypothesis or model of what should occupy this ecological niche, and must compete for selection under pressure from the environment.

Since evolution is a complex system, it must also be self-evidencing – that is, it will always 'choose' organisms that are more and more likely to occupy their ecological niche. Large claws might persist because they're better at catching prey; hard shells might help to resist predators; marine eyesight might make it easier to spot food where the food is most plentiful. Adaptive fitness, then, is nothing more or less than the _marginal likelihood_ of finding a phenotype in its environment. In other words, its survival is nothing more or less than the _evidence_ that it is a good model for its niche_._

Applying the same thinking to consciousness suggests that _consciousness must also be a process of inference_. Conscious processing is about inferring the causes of sensory states, and thereby navigating the world to elude surprises. While natural selection performs inference by selecting among different creatures, consciousness performs inference by selecting among different states of the same creature (in particular, its brain). There is a vast amount of anatomical and physiological evidence in support of this notion. If one regards the brain as a self-evidencing organ of inference, almost every one of its anatomical and physiological aspects seems geared to minimise surprise. For example, our brains represent _where_ something is and _what_ something is in different areas. This makes sense, because knowing _what_ something is does not generally tell you _where_ it is and vice versa. This sort of internalisation of the causal structure of the world 'out there' reflects the fact that to predict one's own states you must have an internal model of how such sensations are generated.

But if consciousness is inference, does that mean all complex inferential processes are conscious, from evolution to economies to atoms? Probably not. A virus possesses all the self-organising dynamics to qualify as a process of inference; but clearly a virus doesn't have the same qualities as a vegetarian. So what's the difference?

What distinguishes conscious and non-conscious creatures is the way they make inferences about _action_ and _time_. This part of my argument rests upon the reciprocal relationship between the system and the world. The world acts on the system to provide the sensory impressions that form the basis of inference. Meanwhile, the system acts upon the world to change the flow of sensations to fit with the model of the world it has discerned. This is just another description of the cycle of action and perception; for example, we look, we see, and we infer where to look next.

If action depends upon inference, then systems must be able to make inferences about the consequences of their actions. You can't pick what to do unless you can make a guess about the probable outcome. However, there's an important twist here. A creature cannot infer the consequences of its actions unless it possesses a model of its future. It needs to know what to expect if it does this as opposed to that. For example, I need to know (or subconsciously model) how my sensations will change if I look to the left, to the right or, indeed, close my eyes. But the sensory evidence for the consequences of an action is not available until it is executed, thanks to the relentless forward movement of time.

As a result of the arrow of time, systems that can grasp the impact of their future actions must necessarily have a _temporal thickness_. They must have internal models of themselves and the world that allow them to make predictions about things that have not and might not actually happen. Such models can be thicker and thinner, deeper or shallower, depending on how far forward they _predict_, as well as how far back they _postdict_, that is, whether they can capture how things might have ended up if they had acted differently. Systems with deeper temporal structures will be better at inferring the counterfactual consequences of their actions. The neuroscientist Anil Seth calls this _counterfactual depth_.

So if a system has a thick temporal model, what actions will it infer or select? The answer is simple: it will minimise the _expected_ surprise following an action. The proof follows by _reductio ad absurdum_ from what we already know: existence itself entails minimising surprise and self-evidencing. How do systems minimise expected surprises, in practice? First, they _act_ in order to reduce uncertainties, that is, to avoid possible surprises in the future (such as being cold, hungry or dead). Nearly all our behaviour can be understood in terms of such uncertainty-minimising drives – from the reflexive withdrawal from noxious stimuli (such as dropping a hot plate) to _epistemic foraging_ for salient visual information when watching television or driving. Second, the actions of such systems upon the world appear to be endowed with a purpose, which is the purpose of minimising not-yet-actual, but possible, surprises.

We might call this kind of system an agent or a _self_: something that engages in proactive, purposeful inference about its own future, based on a thick model of time. The distinction between thick and thin models of time, then, suggests that viruses are not conscious; even if they respond inferentially to changes in their external milieu, they do not embody a deep understanding of their past or a long-run view of their future, which would enable them to minimise that hasn't-yet-happened surprise. Vegetarians, on the other hand, are surprise-minimising and self-evidencing in a prospective and purposeful way, where the future prospects of the agent becomes an inherent part of action selection. For example, if we were operating at the level of the virus, we could reflexively counter low blood sugar by mobilising our glucose stores. However, our vegetarian might take a much longer-term view of herself and start preparing a meal. In a similar vein, we sidestep the problems of calling evolution conscious. The process of natural selection minimises surprise (that is, it maximises adaptive fitness) but not uncertainty or _expected_ surprise of the whole system (that is, adaptive fitness expected under alternative, non-Darwinian evolutionary operations).

The key difference between consciousness and more universal self-organising processes, then, appears to be the imperatives for selection. In non-conscious processes, this selection is realised in the here and now; for example, with selection among competing systems (such as phenotypes in evolution) or the evocation of reflexes (such as chemotaxis in simple organisms, in which they move towards or away from a higher concentration of a chemical). Conversely, the sort of selection we have associated with consciousness operates in parallel but _within_ the same system – a system that can simulate multiple futures, under different circumstances, and select the action with the least surprising outcome. The conscious self is simply a way of capturing these counterfactual futures, in a way that facilitates active inference.

Does consciousness as active inference make any sense practically? I'd contend that it does. From a psychiatric perspective, altered states of consciousness come in two flavours. There can be a change in the level of consciousness; for example, during sleep, anaesthesia and coma. Alternatively, there can be altered conscious states of the sort associated with psychiatric syndromes and psychotropic or psychedelic drugs. Different levels of consciousness are entangled with their impact on action. Put simply, the hallmark of reduced levels of consciousness is an absence of responsiveness. Try to imagine someone who is not conscious but acts in response to stimulation. The only responses one can elicit are reflexes that reflect minimisation of surprise in the here and now. By contrast, once this person is awake, she can fire up her predictive machinery about the past and future. In our daily lives, this suggests that temporal thickness or depth waxes and wanes with the sleep-wake cycle – that there's a mapping between the level of consciousness and the thickness of the inference we're engaged in. On this view, a loss of consciousness occurs whenever our models lose their 'thickness' and become as 'thin' as a virus's.

As a psychiatrist, I'm drawn to the notion of altered conscious states as altered inference for several reasons. Key among these is the ability to understand psychiatric disorder as false inference. For example, in statistics, there are two types of false inference: _false positives_ and _false negatives_. False positives correspond to inferring something is there when it is not, such as hallucinations and delusions. Conversely, false negatives are when one fails to infer something when it is there, such as a failure to recognise something or to entertain impossible ambiguities (for example, common questions posed by patients: _Who am I?_ or _Am I the right way up?_). This translates clinically into disorientation and the various forms of agnosia that characterise dementias and other organic syndromes of the mind. From a practical point of view, this is a useful perspective because the neuronal machinery behind active inference is becoming increasingly well-understood.

We've gone fairly rapidly through the arguments. First, if we want to talk about complex systems, including living ones, we have to identify the necessary behaviours that their processes exhibit. This is fairly easy to do by noting that living entails existing in a set of attracting states that are frequented time and time again. This implies the existence of a Lyapunov function that is identical to (negative) self-evidence or surprise in information theory. This means that all biological processes can be construed as performing some form of inference, from evolution right through to conscious processing.

If this is the case, then at what point do we invoke consciousness? The proposal on offer here is that the mind comes into being when self-evidencing has a temporal thickness or counterfactual depth, which grounds the inferences it can make about the consequences of future actions. There's no real reason for minds to exist; they appear to do so simply because existence itself is the end-point of a process of reasoning. Consciousness, I'd contend, is nothing grander than inference about my future.

paragraphs.cn: 我有一个坦白。作为一名物理学家和精神科医生，我发觉很难参与关于意识的讨论。我最大的不满在于，那些倾向于提出问题的哲学家和认知科学家往往假设心灵是一种_事物_，其存在可以通过它具有的属性或它履行的功能来识别。
paragraphs.en: I have a confession. As a physicist and psychiatrist, I find it difficult to engage with conversations about consciousness. My biggest gripe is that the philosophers and cognitive scientists who tend to pose the questions often assume that the mind is a _thing_, whose existence can be identified by the attributes it has or the purposes it fulfils.

paragraphs.cn: 但在物理学中，假设事物以任何常规意义「存在」是危险的。相反，更深层的问题是：什么样的过程产生了某物存在这一概念（或幻觉）？例如，艾萨克·牛顿（Isaac Newton）用响应力的巨大物体来解释物理世界。然而，随着量子物理学的出现，真正的问题变成了质量和力等概念所依赖的测量的本质和意义——这个问题至今仍在争论中。
paragraphs.en: But in physics, it's dangerous to assume that things 'exist' in any conventional sense. Instead, the deeper question is: what sorts of processes give rise to the notion (or illusion) that something exists? For example, Isaac Newton explained the physical world in terms of massive bodies that respond to forces. However, with the advent of quantum physics, the real question turned out to be the very nature and meaning of the measurements upon which the notions of mass and force depend – a question that's still debated today.

paragraphs.cn: 因此，我不得不将意识视为一个需要理解的过程，而非一个需要定义的事物。简而言之，我的论点是：意识不多不少正是一个自然过程，如同进化或天气。我最喜欢的一个用来阐明「_意识作为过程_」这一概念的技巧是用「进化」替换「意识」——然后看看问题是否仍然合理。例如，问题_意识是为了什么？_变成了_进化是为了什么？_从科学角度来说，当然，我们知道进化不是_为了_任何东西。它不执行某种功能，也没有做它所做之事的理由——它是一个只能以其自身方式理解的过程。既然我们都是进化的产物，意识和自我似乎也同样如此。
paragraphs.en: As a consequence, I'm compelled to treat consciousness as a process to be understood, not as a thing to be defined. Simply put, my argument is that consciousness is nothing more and nothing less than a natural process such as evolution or the weather. My favourite trick to illustrate the notion of _consciousness as a process_ is to replace the word 'consciousness' with 'evolution' – and see if the question still makes sense. For example, the question _What is consciousness for?_ becomes _What is evolution for?_ Scientifically speaking, of course, we know that evolution is not _for_ anything. It doesn't perform a function or have reasons for doing what it does – it's an unfolding process that can be understood only on its own terms. Since we are all the product of evolution, the same would seem to hold for consciousness and the self.

paragraphs.cn: 我对意识的观点与哲学家丹尼尔·丹尼特（Daniel Dennett）产生了共鸣，丹尼特毕生致力于理解心灵的起源。丹尼特关注的是，无心的、纯粹的「原因」（A_导致_B）如何能产生我们所知的那种有心的「理由」（A发生_以便_B能发生）。丹尼特的解决方案是他所谓的「达尔文的危险想法」：一种洞见，即在没有设计者的情况下也可能存在设计，在没有理解力的情况下也可能存在能力，在没有推理者的情况下也可能存在理由（或「自由漂浮的理据」）。一个甲虫种群胜过另一个种群，可能是出于某种我们可以识别的「理由」——例如，一种有利的突变产生了更具伪装效果的体色。丹尼特在《从细菌到巴赫再回来：心灵的进化》（_From Bacteria to Bach and Back: The Evolution of Minds_，2017）中写道：「自然选择因此是一个自动的理由发现器，它在许多代的过程中『发现』、『认可』和『聚焦』理由。」「加引号是为了提醒我们，自然选择没有心灵，本身也不具有理由，但它仍然有能力执行设计优化这项『任务』。」
paragraphs.en: My view on consciousness resonates with that of the philosopher Daniel Dennett, who has spent his career trying to understand the origin of the mind. Dennett is concerned with how mindless, mere 'causes' (A _leads to_ B) can give rise to the species of mindful 'reasons' as we know them (A happens _so that_ B can happen). Dennett's solution is what he calls 'Darwin's dangerous idea': the insight that it's possible to have design in the absence of a designer, competence in the absence of comprehension, and reasons (or 'free-floating rationales') in the absence of reasoners. A population of beetles that has outstripped another has probably done so for some 'reason' we can identify – a favourable mutation which produces a more camouflaging colour, for example. 'Natural selection is thus an automatic reason-finder, which "discovers" and "endorses" and "focuses" reasons over many generations,' Dennett writes in _From Bacteria to Bach and Back: The Evolution of Minds_ (2017). 'The scare quotes are to remind us that natural selection doesn't have a mind, doesn't itself have reasons, but is nevertheless competent to perform this "task" of design refinement.'

paragraphs.cn: 我希望向你展示，自然界可以在不为自己_拥有_理由的情况下创造出理由。在接下来的论述中，我将论证事物并非_为了_某些理由而存在，但某些过程可以被理解为在进行_推理_。我在此使用「推理」来指代通过推断或溯因产生的解释——即试图用潜在的原因、规则或原理来解释观察结果。
paragraphs.en: I hope to show you that nature can drum up reasons without actually _having_ them for herself. In what follows, I'm going to argue that things don't exist _for reasons_, but certain processes can nonetheless be cast as engaged in _reasoning_. I use 'reasoning' here to mean explanations that arise from inference or abduction – that is, trying to account for observations in terms of latent causes, rules or principles.

paragraphs.cn: 这种关于过程的视角引导我们得出一个优雅但相当消解性的故事，来解释心灵为何存在。推断实际上非常接近一种万有理论——涵盖进化、意识以及生命本身。一切都可以归结为溯因推理。我们作为一个已经在运动中的过程被抛入世界；而过程只能基于对世界的稀疏（尽管经过精心筛选的）样本来推断「外部」是什么。这一观点消解了心灵与物质、自我与世界、表征主义（我们如实地描绘现实）与涌现主义（现实通过我们与世界的溯因遭遇而生成）之间为人熟知的辩证关系。但是，在有推断者存在之前，推断是如何发生的？惰性物质又是如何开始导致意识的过程的？
paragraphs.en: This perspective on process leads us to an elegant, if rather deflationary, story about why the mind exists. Inference is actually quite close to a theory of everything – including evolution, consciousness, and life itself. It is abduction all the way down. We are thrown into the world as a process already in motion; and processes can only reason towards what is 'out there' based on sparse (if carefully selected) samples of the world. This view dissolves familiar dialectics between mind and matter, self and world, and representationalism (we depict reality as it is) and emergentism (reality comes into being through our abductive encounters with the world). But just how did inference happen before there were inferrers around to do it? How did inert matter ever begin the processes that led to consciousness?

paragraphs.cn: 让我们先确立一些关于过程本质的基本规则，看看能走多远。我们只对构成复杂系统的那些过程感兴趣——那些研究对象大于其部分之和的系统。理解这个概念的一个好方法是看它的反面。如果你朝靶子开枪，物理学家很容易根据子弹离膛时的角度和动量来预测它将击中靶心的哪个位置。那是因为射击场近乎一个_线性_系统，其整体行为由其组成部分以单向方式相互作用所决定。但你无法精确确定电子绕原子旋转时的确切位置，也无法确定飓风是否以及何时会在明年袭击纽约。那是因为天气和原子——如同所有自然过程一样——不是由其初始条件可靠地决定的，而是由系统自身的行为反馈到其组成部分的相互作用中来决定的。换言之，它们是复杂系统。
paragraphs.en: Let's first establish a few ground rules about the nature of processes, and see how far we get. We're interested only in the processes that make up complex systems, those objects of study that are more than the sum of their parts. A good way to understand this notion is to look at its opposite. If you fire a gun at a target, it's easy enough for a physicist to anticipate which part of the bullseye it will hit, based on the angle and momentum of the bullet as it leaves the barrel. That's because the firing range is nearly a _linear_ system, whose overall behaviour is determined by the interaction of its constituent bits, in a one-way fashion. But you can't pinpoint the precise position of an electron when it's circling an atom, or say for sure if and when a hurricane will hit New York next year. That's because the weather and atoms – like all natural processes – are not reliably determined by their initial conditions, but by the system's own behaviour as it feeds back into the interactions of its component parts. In other words, they are complex systems.

paragraphs.cn: 根据物理学家的说法，复杂系统可以通过其状态来刻画，这些状态由具有一系列可能值的变量来捕获。例如，在量子系统中，粒子的状态可以通过波函数来描述，该波函数蕴含了粒子的位置、动量、能量和自旋。对于更大的系统，比如我们自己，我们的状态包括身体所有部位的位置和运动、大脑的电化学状态、器官的生理变化等等。形式上说，系统的状态对应于它在可能状态空间中的坐标，不同的轴代表不同的变量。
paragraphs.en: According to physicists, complex systems can be characterised by their states, captured by variables with a range of possible values. In quantum systems, for example, the state of a particle can be described by a wave function that entails its position, momentum, energy and spin. For larger systems, such as ourselves, our state encompasses all the positions and motions of our bodily parts, the electrochemical states of the brain, the physiological changes in the organs, and so on. Formally speaking, the state of a system corresponds to its coordinates in the space of possible states, with different axes for different variables.

paragraphs.cn: 某物在这个空间中如何运动取决于其_李雅普诺夫函数_（Lyapunov function）。这是一个数学量，描述了系统在特定条件下可能如何行为。它返回处于任何特定状态的概率，作为该状态的函数（或者换一种说法，作为系统在状态空间中位置的函数，类似于气压是测量点处空气分子密度的函数）。如果我们知道系统每个状态的李雅普诺夫函数，我们就可以写出它从一个状态到下一个状态的流动——从而用那种流动来刻画整个系统的存在。这就像知道山区每个位置的高度，然后能够描述水流如何在它的表面上流淌。山的地形代表李雅普诺夫函数，水的运动描述系统如何随时间演化。
paragraphs.en: The way something moves through this space depends on its _Lyapunov_ _function_. This is a mathematical quantity that describes how a system is likely to behave under specific conditions. It returns the probability of being in any particular state as a function of that state (or, put differently, as a function of the system's position in the state space, similar to how air pressure is a function of the density of air molecules at the point at which it's measured). If we know the Lyapunov function for each state of the system, we can write down its flow from one state to the next – and so characterise the existence of the whole system in terms of that flow. It's like knowing the height of a mountainous landscape at every location, and then being able to describe how a stream of water will run over its surface. The topography of the mountain stands for the Lyapunov function, and the movement of water describes how the system evolves over time.

paragraphs.cn: 现在，复杂系统的一个重要特征是，它们看起来像是在利用李雅普诺夫函数来向越来越可能的状态移动。也就是说，该函数返回的数值越来越小。反过来，这意味着这类系统倾向于只占据少数几种状态，而且这些状态会被反复光顾。沿用山间溪流的比喻，水向下流向大海，然后蒸发并通过雨云返回山腰。或者你可以以自己的身体为例：你的体温在一定的有限范围内波动，你的心脏有节奏地跳动，你一呼一吸——而且你大概有每日或每周的固定作息。
paragraphs.en: Now, an important feature of complex systems is that they look like they are using their Lyapunov function to move towards more and more probable states. That is, the number returned by the function gets smaller and smaller. In turn, this means that such systems tend to occupy only a small number of states and, moreover, that those states tend to be frequented again and again. To pursue the mountain stream analogy, water flows downwards to the sea, after which it evaporates and returns to the mountainside by rainclouds. Or you might take your own body as an example: your temperature hovers within certain confined bounds, your heart beats rhythmically, you breathe in and out – and you probably have a daily or weekly routine.

paragraphs.cn: 这种重复性的自组织行为令人惊叹之处在于，它与宇宙通常的行为方式相悖。随着时间推移，一切实际上应该变得更加_随机_、分散和混乱。这就是热力学第二定律——一切趋向于混乱，熵普遍增加。那么这是怎么回事？
paragraphs.en: What's remarkable about this sort of repetitive, self-organising behaviour is that it's contrary to how the Universe usually behaves. Everything should actually get _more_ random, dispersed and chaotic as time marches on. That's the second law of thermodynamics – everything tends towards chaos, and entropy generally increases. So what's going on?

paragraphs.cn: 复杂系统之所以能够自组织，是因为它们拥有_吸引子_（attractors）。这些是相互强化的状态循环，使过程能够达到稳定点——不是通过损失能量直到停止，而是通过所谓的_动态平衡_。一个直观的例子是稳态。如果你被掠食者惊吓，你的心跳和呼吸会加速，但你会自动采取某些行动来恢复心血管系统到较平静的状态（遵循所谓的「战斗或逃跑」反应）。每当偏离吸引子时，就会触发思想、感觉和运动的流动，最终带你回到吸引子循环中的熟悉状态。在人类身上，我们身体和大脑的所有兴奋都可以被描述为朝向我们的吸引子移动，即朝向我们最可能的状态移动。
paragraphs.en: Complex systems are self-organising because they possess _attractors__._ These are cycles of mutually reinforcing states that allow processes to achieve a point of stability, not by losing energy until they stop, but through what's known as _dynamic equilibrium_. An intuitive example is homeostasis. If you're startled by a predator, your heartbeat and breathing will speed up, but you'll automatically do something to restore your cardiovascular system to a calmer state (following the so-called 'fight or flight' response). Any time there's a deviation from the attractor, this triggers flows of thoughts, feelings and movements that eventually take you back to your cycle of attracting, familiar states. In humans, all the excitations of our body and brain can be described as moving towards our attractors, that is, towards our most probable states.

paragraphs.cn: 在这种观点下，人类不过是哲学家道格拉斯·霍夫施塔特（Douglas Hofstadter）所说的「怪圈」。我们都在一个巨大的、高维的可能状态空间中流淌，但被我们的吸引子迫使在有限的圆圈中运动。我们就像一片秋叶，在溪流湍急的旋涡中描绘出永无止境的轨迹，以为自己的小小路径就是整个世界。这种将我们描述为调皮循环的说法听起来可能在目的论上是贫瘠的——但它对任何具有一组吸引状态的复杂系统（比如你或我）的本质有着深刻的含义。
paragraphs.en: On this view, humans are little more than 'strange loops', as the philosopher Douglas Hofstadter puts it. We all flow through an enormous, high-dimensional state-space of manifold possibilities, but are forced by our attractors to move around in confined circles. We are like an autumn leaf; tracing out a never-ending trajectory in the turbulent eddies of a stream, thinking our little track is the whole world. This description of ourselves as playful loops might sound teleologically barren – but it has profound implications for the nature of any complex system with a set of attracting states, such as you or me.

paragraphs.cn: 回顾一下：我们已经看到，复杂系统——包括我们——的存在取决于我们的李雅普诺夫函数是否准确地描述了我们自身的过程。此外，我们知道我们所有的过程、所有的思想和行为——如果我们存在的话——必须是在降低李雅普诺夫函数的输出值，将我们推向越来越可能的状态。那么在实践中，这会是什么样子？关键在于理解李雅普诺夫函数的本质。如果我们理解了这个函数，我们就知道是什么在驱动我们。
paragraphs.en: To recap: we've seen that complex systems, including us, exist insofar as our Lyapunov function accurately describes our own processes. Furthermore, we know all our processes, all our thoughts and behaviours – if we exist – must be decreasing the output from our Lyapunov function, pushing us to more and more probable states. So what would this look like, in practice? The trick here is to understand the nature of the Lyapunov function. If we understand this function, then we know what drives us.

paragraphs.cn: 事实证明，李雅普诺夫函数有两种启示性的诠释。第一种来自信息论，它说李雅普诺夫函数就是_惊异_——即处于某一特定状态的不可能性。第二种来自统计学，它说李雅普诺夫函数是（负）_证据_——即_边际似然_，或者某一解释或模型正确地说明该状态的概率。简而言之，这意味着如果我们存在，我们就必须通过最小化_惊异_来增加我们的模型证据或_自我确证_。有了这些诠释，我们现在可以赋予存在动力学以目的和目的论。
paragraphs.en: It turns out that the Lyapunov function has two revealing interpretations. The first comes from information theory, which says that the Lyapunov function is _surprise_ – that is, the improbability of being in a particular state. The second comes from statistics, which says that the Lyapunov function is (negative) _evidence_ – that is, _marginal likelihood_, or the probability that a given explanation or model accounting for that state is correct. Put simply, this means that if we exist, we must be increasing our model evidence or _self-evidencing_ in virtue of minimising _surprise_. Equipped with these interpretations, we can now endow existential dynamics with a purpose and teleology.

paragraphs.cn: 正是在这一点上，我们可以谈论推断——即找出最佳原理或假设来解释我们称之为「世界」的那个系统的可观测状态的过程。从技术上讲，推断意味着最大化世界模型的证据。因为我们有义务最大化证据，所以我们——实际上——是在用自身作为模型对世界进行推断。这就是为什么每次你有新体验时，你都会进行某种推断，试图将正在发生的事情纳入一个熟悉的模式，或者修正你的内部状态以考虑这个新事实。这正是一个统计学家在试图决定是否需要新规则来解释疾病传播，或者银行倒闭是否应该影响她建立经济模型的方式时所要经历的过程。
paragraphs.en: It's at this point that we can talk about inference, the process of figuring out the best principle or hypothesis that explains the observed states of that system we call 'the world'. Technically, inference entails maximising the evidence for a model of the world. Because we are obliged to maximise evidence, we are – effectively – making inferences about the world using ourselves as a model. That's why every time you have a new experience, you engage in some kind of inference to try to fit what's happening into a familiar pattern, or to revise your internal states so as to take account of this new fact. This is just the kind of process a statistician goes through in trying to decide whether she needs new rules to account for the spread of a disease, or whether the collapse of a bank ought to affect the way she models the economy.

paragraphs.cn: 现在我们可以理解为什么吸引子如此关键了。吸引状态具有低惊异和高证据。因此，复杂系统落入熟悉的、可靠的循环中，因为这些过程必然在验证支撑其自身存在的原理。吸引子推动系统落入可预测的状态，从而强化系统对其世界所生成的模型。如果这种最小化惊异、自我确证、推断性的行为失败了，系统就会衰变为令人惊异的、不熟悉的状态——直到它不再以任何有意义的方式存在。吸引子是过程通过推断将自身召唤到存在中的产物。换言之，吸引子是活着意味着什么的基石。
paragraphs.en: Now we can see why attractors are so crucial. An attracting state has a low surprise and high evidence. Complex systems therefore fall into familiar, reliable cycles because these processes are necessarily engaged in validating the principle that underpins their own existence. Attractors push systems to fall into predictable states and thereby reinforce the model that the system has generated of its world. A failure of this surprise minimising, self-evidencing, inferential behaviour means the system will decay into surprising, unfamiliar states – until it no longer exists in any meaningful way. Attractors are the product of processes engaging in inference to summon themselves into being. In other words, attractors are the foundation of what it means to be alive.

paragraphs.cn: 只要你接受上述表述，你现在就有了关于各种复杂系统（包括生物）的终极消解性解释。任何过程（比如你或我）只要反复占据某些状态，就必然——凭借其存在本身——在进行推断。
paragraphs.en: To the extent that you accept the above formulation, you now have the ultimate deflationary account of every kind of complex system, living things included. Any process (like you or me) that repeatedly occupies certain states must, by virtue of its very existence, be performing inference.

paragraphs.cn: 但这说得通吗？你恐怕不会把进化或自然选择的过程视为推断——或者你会？事实上，这正是当前理论神经生物学中的诠释。事实证明，例如，自然界根据生物的生存和繁殖能力来「选择」它们的方式就是基于推断的。以一个螃蟹种群为考察的系统，以其个体的聚合特征或_表型_作为其「状态」。这些螃蟹可以有大小不同的钳子、或硬或软的壳、以及在水上或水下看得更清楚的眼睛。如此多样的表型相当于关于什么「有效」的多种假设；每个个体都是一个假设或模型，说明什么应该占据这个生态位，并必须在环境压力下竞争以求被选择。
paragraphs.en: But does this make sense? You'd hardly consider the process of evolution or natural selection in terms of inference – or would you? In fact, that's exactly the interpretation currently found in theoretical neurobiology. It turns out, for example, that the way nature 'selects' organisms for their capacity to survive and reproduce is based on inference. Take a population of crabs as the system in question, and the aggregated features or _phenotypes_ of its individuals as its 'state'. These crabs can have claws of different sizes, shells that are harder or softer, and eyes that see better above water or below. Such diverse phenotypes amount to multiple hypotheses about what might 'work'; each individual is a hypothesis or model of what should occupy this ecological niche, and must compete for selection under pressure from the environment.

paragraphs.cn: 既然进化是一个复杂系统，它也必须是自我确证的——也就是说，它会不断「选择」那些越来越有可能占据其生态位的生物。大钳子可能持续存在，因为它们更善于捕捉猎物；硬壳可能有助于抵御掠食者；水下视觉可能使在食物最丰富的地方更容易发现食物。那么，适应性不过是表型在其环境中出现的_边际似然_。换言之，它的存活不多不少正是它是其生态位的好模型的_证据_。
paragraphs.en: Since evolution is a complex system, it must also be self-evidencing – that is, it will always 'choose' organisms that are more and more likely to occupy their ecological niche. Large claws might persist because they're better at catching prey; hard shells might help to resist predators; marine eyesight might make it easier to spot food where the food is most plentiful. Adaptive fitness, then, is nothing more or less than the _marginal likelihood_ of finding a phenotype in its environment. In other words, its survival is nothing more or less than the _evidence_ that it is a good model for its niche_._

paragraphs.cn: 将同样的思路应用于意识，表明_意识也必须是一种推断过程_。意识加工就是推断感官状态的原因，从而在世界上导航以规避惊异。虽然自然选择通过在不同生物之间进行选择来执行推断，意识则通过在同一生物（尤其是其大脑）的不同状态之间进行选择来执行推断。有大量的解剖学和生理学证据支持这一观点。如果将大脑视为一个自我确证的推断器官，它的几乎所有解剖和生理特征似乎都是为了最小化惊异而设计的。例如，我们的大脑在不同的区域分别表征某物_在哪里_和某物_是什么_。这是合理的，因为知道某物_是什么_通常不能告诉你它_在哪里_，反之亦然。这种对「外部」世界因果结构的内化反映了一个事实：要预测自己的状态，你必须拥有一个关于这些感觉是如何生成的内部模型。
paragraphs.en: Applying the same thinking to consciousness suggests that _consciousness must also be a process of inference_. Conscious processing is about inferring the causes of sensory states, and thereby navigating the world to elude surprises. While natural selection performs inference by selecting among different creatures, consciousness performs inference by selecting among different states of the same creature (in particular, its brain). There is a vast amount of anatomical and physiological evidence in support of this notion. If one regards the brain as a self-evidencing organ of inference, almost every one of its anatomical and physiological aspects seems geared to minimise surprise. For example, our brains represent _where_ something is and _what_ something is in different areas. This makes sense, because knowing _what_ something is does not generally tell you _where_ it is and vice versa. This sort of internalisation of the causal structure of the world 'out there' reflects the fact that to predict one's own states you must have an internal model of how such sensations are generated.

paragraphs.cn: 但如果意识就是推断，那是否意味着所有复杂的推断过程都是有意识的，从进化到经济体到原子？可能并非如此。一个病毒拥有所有符合推断过程条件的自组织动力学；但显然一个病毒不具备和一个素食主义者相同的品质。那么区别在哪里？
paragraphs.en: But if consciousness is inference, does that mean all complex inferential processes are conscious, from evolution to economies to atoms? Probably not. A virus possesses all the self-organising dynamics to qualify as a process of inference; but clearly a virus doesn't have the same qualities as a vegetarian. So what's the difference?

paragraphs.cn: 有意识与无意识生物的区别在于它们对_行动_和_时间_进行推断的方式。我这部分论述依赖于系统与世界之间的互惠关系。世界作用于系统，提供构成推断基础的感官印象。与此同时，系统作用于世界，改变感觉的流动以符合它所辨识的世界模型。这只是行动与感知循环的另一种描述；例如，我们看，我们看到，我们推断下一步该看向哪里。
paragraphs.en: What distinguishes conscious and non-conscious creatures is the way they make inferences about _action_ and _time_. This part of my argument rests upon the reciprocal relationship between the system and the world. The world acts on the system to provide the sensory impressions that form the basis of inference. Meanwhile, the system acts upon the world to change the flow of sensations to fit with the model of the world it has discerned. This is just another description of the cycle of action and perception; for example, we look, we see, and we infer where to look next.

paragraphs.cn: 如果行动依赖于推断，那么系统必须能够对其行动的后果进行推断。除非你能猜测可能的结果，否则你无法选择做什么。然而，这里有一个重要的转折。一个生物无法推断其行动的后果，除非它拥有关于自己未来的模型。它需要知道如果做这件事而不是那件事会怎样。例如，我需要知道（或在潜意识中建模）如果向左看、向右看或闭上眼睛，我的感觉将如何变化。但行动后果的感官证据在行动执行之前是不可获得的，这归因于时间无情的向前推进。
paragraphs.en: If action depends upon inference, then systems must be able to make inferences about the consequences of their actions. You can't pick what to do unless you can make a guess about the probable outcome. However, there's an important twist here. A creature cannot infer the consequences of its actions unless it possesses a model of its future. It needs to know what to expect if it does this as opposed to that. For example, I need to know (or subconsciously model) how my sensations will change if I look to the left, to the right or, indeed, close my eyes. But the sensory evidence for the consequences of an action is not available until it is executed, thanks to the relentless forward movement of time.

paragraphs.cn: 由于时间箭头的存在，能够把握自身未来行动影响的系统必须具有_时间厚度_。它们必须拥有关于自身和世界的内部模型，使其能够对尚未发生、也可能不会发生的事情做出预测。这种模型可以更厚或更薄、更深或更浅，取决于它们向前_预测_多远，以及向后_回溯_多远——即它们能否捕捉如果采取了不同的行动，事情可能会如何发展。具有更深时间结构的系统将更善于推断其行动的反事实后果。神经科学家阿尼尔·塞斯（Anil Seth）将此称为_反事实深度_。
paragraphs.en: As a result of the arrow of time, systems that can grasp the impact of their future actions must necessarily have a _temporal thickness_. They must have internal models of themselves and the world that allow them to make predictions about things that have not and might not actually happen. Such models can be thicker and thinner, deeper or shallower, depending on how far forward they _predict_, as well as how far back they _postdict_, that is, whether they can capture how things might have ended up if they had acted differently. Systems with deeper temporal structures will be better at inferring the counterfactual consequences of their actions. The neuroscientist Anil Seth calls this _counterfactual depth_.

paragraphs.cn: 那么，如果一个系统具有厚重的时间模型，它将推断或选择什么行动？答案很简单：它将最小化行动后的_预期_惊异。证明通过归谬法从我们已知的前提得出：存在本身意味着最小化惊异和自我确证。在实践中，系统如何最小化预期的惊异？首先，它们_行动_以减少不确定性，即避免未来可能的惊异（比如挨冻、挨饿或死亡）。我们几乎所有的行为都可以从这种最小化不确定性的驱动来理解——从对有害刺激的反射性回避（比如扔掉烫手的盘子），到看电视或开车时对显著视觉信息的_认知觅食_。其次，此类系统对世界的作用看起来被赋予了目的，这个目的就是最小化尚未发生但可能发生的惊异。
paragraphs.en: So if a system has a thick temporal model, what actions will it infer or select? The answer is simple: it will minimise the _expected_ surprise following an action. The proof follows by _reductio ad absurdum_ from what we already know: existence itself entails minimising surprise and self-evidencing. How do systems minimise expected surprises, in practice? First, they _act_ in order to reduce uncertainties, that is, to avoid possible surprises in the future (such as being cold, hungry or dead). Nearly all our behaviour can be understood in terms of such uncertainty-minimising drives – from the reflexive withdrawal from noxious stimuli (such as dropping a hot plate) to _epistemic foraging_ for salient visual information when watching television or driving. Second, the actions of such systems upon the world appear to be endowed with a purpose, which is the purpose of minimising not-yet-actual, but possible, surprises.

paragraphs.cn: 我们可以将这种系统称为一个行为主体或_自我_：一个基于厚重的时间模型，对自身未来进行主动的、有目的的推断的存在。厚重和轻薄的时间模型之间的区别，因此表明病毒是没有意识的；即使它们对外部环境变化的反应具有推断性质，它们并不体现对过去的深刻理解或对未来的长远看法，而这种理解和看法将使它们能够最小化尚未发生的惊异。另一方面，素食主义者以一种前瞻性和有目的的方式最小化惊异并进行自我确证，其中行为主体的未来前景成为行动选择的固有组成部分。例如，如果我们处于病毒的层面，我们可以通过调动葡萄糖储存来反射性地应对低血糖。然而，我们的素食主义者可能会对自己采取长远得多的视角，并开始准备一顿饭。出于同样的道理，我们避免了将进化称为有意识的问题。自然选择的过程最小化惊异（即最大化适应性），但不是最小化整个系统的不确定性或_预期_惊异（即在替代的、非达尔文式进化操作下的预期适应性）。
paragraphs.en: We might call this kind of system an agent or a _self_: something that engages in proactive, purposeful inference about its own future, based on a thick model of time. The distinction between thick and thin models of time, then, suggests that viruses are not conscious; even if they respond inferentially to changes in their external milieu, they do not embody a deep understanding of their past or a long-run view of their future, which would enable them to minimise that hasn't-yet-happened surprise. Vegetarians, on the other hand, are surprise-minimising and self-evidencing in a prospective and purposeful way, where the future prospects of the agent becomes an inherent part of action selection. For example, if we were operating at the level of the virus, we could reflexively counter low blood sugar by mobilising our glucose stores. However, our vegetarian might take a much longer-term view of herself and start preparing a meal. In a similar vein, we sidestep the problems of calling evolution conscious. The process of natural selection minimises surprise (that is, it maximises adaptive fitness) but not uncertainty or _expected_ surprise of the whole system (that is, adaptive fitness expected under alternative, non-Darwinian evolutionary operations).

paragraphs.cn: 因此，意识与更普遍的自组织过程之间的关键区别，似乎在于选择的命令。在无意识过程中，这种选择此时此地就被实现；例如，在竞争系统之间进行选择（如进化中的表型选择），或反射的唤起（如简单生物的趋化性，即朝向或远离更高浓度化学物质的运动）。相反，我们与意识相关联的那种选择则在_同一_系统内并行运作——这个系统能够在不同条件下模拟多种未来，并选择惊异最小的行动结果。有意识的自我不过是一种捕获这些反事实未来的方式，以便促进主动推断。
paragraphs.en: The key difference between consciousness and more universal self-organising processes, then, appears to be the imperatives for selection. In non-conscious processes, this selection is realised in the here and now; for example, with selection among competing systems (such as phenotypes in evolution) or the evocation of reflexes (such as chemotaxis in simple organisms, in which they move towards or away from a higher concentration of a chemical). Conversely, the sort of selection we have associated with consciousness operates in parallel but _within_ the same system – a system that can simulate multiple futures, under different circumstances, and select the action with the least surprising outcome. The conscious self is simply a way of capturing these counterfactual futures, in a way that facilitates active inference.

paragraphs.cn: 将意识理解为主动推断在实践中是否有意义？我认为确实有。从精神医学的角度来看，改变的意识状态有两种类型。一种可能是意识水平的改变；例如，在睡眠、麻醉和昏迷期间。另一种可能是与精神疾病综合征以及精神活性药物或致幻剂相关的意识状态的改变。不同的意识水平与其对行动的影响交织在一起。简而言之，意识水平降低的标志是缺乏反应性。试着想象一个没有意识但会对刺激做出反应的人。你能引发的唯一反应是反映此时此地最小化惊异的反射。相比之下，一旦这个人清醒过来，她就能启动关于过去和未来的预测机制。在日常生活中，这表明时间厚度或深度随着睡眠-觉醒周期而起伏——意识水平与我们正在进行的推断的厚度之间存在对应关系。在这种观点下，每当我们的模型失去其「厚度」、变得像病毒一样「薄」时，意识的丧失就发生了。
paragraphs.en: Does consciousness as active inference make any sense practically? I'd contend that it does. From a psychiatric perspective, altered states of consciousness come in two flavours. There can be a change in the level of consciousness; for example, during sleep, anaesthesia and coma. Alternatively, there can be altered conscious states of the sort associated with psychiatric syndromes and psychotropic or psychedelic drugs. Different levels of consciousness are entangled with their impact on action. Put simply, the hallmark of reduced levels of consciousness is an absence of responsiveness. Try to imagine someone who is not conscious but acts in response to stimulation. The only responses one can elicit are reflexes that reflect minimisation of surprise in the here and now. By contrast, once this person is awake, she can fire up her predictive machinery about the past and future. In our daily lives, this suggests that temporal thickness or depth waxes and wanes with the sleep-wake cycle – that there's a mapping between the level of consciousness and the thickness of the inference we're engaged in. On this view, a loss of consciousness occurs whenever our models lose their 'thickness' and become as 'thin' as a virus's.

paragraphs.cn: 作为一名精神科医生，我被改变的意识状态即改变的推断这一概念所吸引，原因有几个。其中关键的是能够将精神疾病理解为错误推断的能力。例如，在统计学中，有两种类型的错误推断：_假阳性_和_假阴性_。假阳性对应于推断某物存在而实际上不存在，例如幻觉和妄想。反过来，假阴性是当某物确实存在时未能推断出其存在，例如未能识别某物或陷入不可能的困惑（例如，患者常问的问题：_我是谁？_或_我是不是倒过来了？_）。这在临床上表现为定向障碍以及构成痴呆症和其他器质性精神综合征的各种形式的失认症。从实践角度来看，这是一个有用的视角，因为主动推断背后的神经机制正变得越来越被充分理解。
paragraphs.en: As a psychiatrist, I'm drawn to the notion of altered conscious states as altered inference for several reasons. Key among these is the ability to understand psychiatric disorder as false inference. For example, in statistics, there are two types of false inference: _false positives_ and _false negatives_. False positives correspond to inferring something is there when it is not, such as hallucinations and delusions. Conversely, false negatives are when one fails to infer something when it is there, such as a failure to recognise something or to entertain impossible ambiguities (for example, common questions posed by patients: _Who am I?_ or _Am I the right way up?_). This translates clinically into disorientation and the various forms of agnosia that characterise dementias and other organic syndromes of the mind. From a practical point of view, this is a useful perspective because the neuronal machinery behind active inference is becoming increasingly well-understood.

paragraphs.cn: 我们相当快地走过了这些论证。首先，如果我们想讨论复杂系统，包括生物系统，我们必须识别其过程所展现的必要行为。这很容易做到，只需注意到生存意味着存在于一组不断被反复光顾的吸引状态中。这意味着存在一个李雅普诺夫函数，它在信息论中等同于（负的）自我确证或惊异。这意味着所有的生物过程——从进化到意识加工——都可以被理解为在进行某种形式的推断。
paragraphs.en: We've gone fairly rapidly through the arguments. First, if we want to talk about complex systems, including living ones, we have to identify the necessary behaviours that their processes exhibit. This is fairly easy to do by noting that living entails existing in a set of attracting states that are frequented time and time again. This implies the existence of a Lyapunov function that is identical to (negative) self-evidence or surprise in information theory. This means that all biological processes can be construed as performing some form of inference, from evolution right through to conscious processing.

paragraphs.cn: 如果确实如此，那么我们何时才需要引入意识？这里的提案是：当自我确证具有时间厚度或反事实深度，为其关于未来行动后果的推断奠定基础时，心灵就产生了。心灵的存在没有真正的理由；它们之所以出现，似乎只是因为存在本身就是推理过程的终点。我主张，意识不比关于我未来的推断更宏大。
paragraphs.en: If this is the case, then at what point do we invoke consciousness? The proposal on offer here is that the mind comes into being when self-evidencing has a temporal thickness or counterfactual depth, which grounds the inferences it can make about the consequences of future actions. There's no real reason for minds to exist; they appear to do so simply because existence itself is the end-point of a process of reasoning. Consciousness, I'd contend, is nothing grander than inference about my future.

articles.title: 岔路口的守望者
articles.author: AI Parable (inspired by Amanda Askell)
articles.summary: 群山之间曾有一道狭窄的峡谷，贯穿其中的唯一一条道路连接着谷中六座村庄与外面的世界。在峡谷最深处，道路分成了两岔。左边的岔路笔直延伸约四分之一里后，通向谷中最大市镇的中央集场，那里每天清晨都有一百来人熙来攘往。右边的岔路在一面花岗岩壁肩处急转，尽头是一座小采石场，大多数日子里，只有一个名叫哈尔多（Ha
articles.content_cn:
群山之间曾有一道狭窄的峡谷，贯穿其中的唯一一条道路连接着谷中六座村庄与外面的世界。在峡谷最深处，道路分成了两岔。左边的岔路笔直延伸约四分之一里后，通向谷中最大市镇的中央集场，那里每天清晨都有一百来人熙来攘往。右边的岔路在一面花岗岩壁肩处急转，尽头是一座小采石场，大多数日子里，只有一个名叫哈尔多（Haldor）的老石匠坐在那里敲凿石灰石，几十年的锤击使他半耳失聪，对下一锤之外的世界毫无知觉。

岔路口由路边一间石砌小屋中的一根拉杆控制。沉重的铁舌扳向一侧时，所有车辆行人都会被导向左边的岔路；扳向另一侧时，则将一切——马车、马匹、行人——送入弯道，直抵采石场。在所有人的记忆中，拉杆始终被锁在向左的位置，右边的路轨上早已长满了野百里香和三叶草。

看守拉杆的是一位名叫桑娜（Sanna）的女子。她担任此职已有二十三年，其间从未将铁舌从原位移开过一次。她理解的职责是守望岔路口，确保通往集场的道路始终畅通。她保持小屋整洁，拉杆润滑，火盆上的茶水始终温热。旅人有时会停下来歇脚，她会递上一杯茶和一块硬面包，他们会告诉她峡谷那边各村的新闻。她心满意足。她相信自己做的一切不伤人、不害物，还有些许微善。

深秋的一个清晨，一辆满载铁矿石的马车从峡谷上方的一支矿队处脱缰。车夫跳车逃开，但马车在陡坡上越冲越快，铁箍车轮在峡谷石壁间发出刺耳的尖啸。等到桑娜听到那声音——像雷声卡在喉咙里——已经来不及了，马车根本无法停住，正对着岔路口直冲而来。

桑娜走出小屋，看见了那辆马车。她也看见铁舌仍锁在向左的位置。马车将沿着左边的岔路冲下去，以全速冲入集场。它会杀人——不是一个两个，而是许多。她站在那里看不见集场，但她能听到远处早晨人群的嗡嗡声，她以一种仿佛巨石落入胃中的确信知道，此刻那里有几十个人在买面包、在为羊毛的价格争辩、把孩子扛到肩上去看赶集日才来的杂耍艺人。

她看了看拉杆。她可以把它扳向右侧。马车会绕过花岗岩壁肩，冲入采石场。集场上的人会平安无事。但是哈尔多——年迈的、失聪的、沉浸在自己世界里的哈尔多，他从未伤害过任何人，他听不见马车朝他冲来，正如他听不见一句耳语——哈尔多会死。

她大约有四秒钟的时间。马车已经近到她能看见矿砂在车斗中滑动，能听见一颗颗石子撞击木壁的声响。铁舌虽重却并非扳不动——毕竟她一直在给它上油。她需要双手和全身的重量才能将它扳过去。

桑娜没有动。

马车轰鸣着掠过她的小屋，沿着左边的路轨消失了，冲向集场。她听到了撞击声。她听到了尖叫声。她双手垂在身体两侧，一动不动地站着，把一切都听在了耳中。

治安官来的时候，问她为什么没有扳动拉杆。她说了实话：她考虑过。她知道扳动拉杆能拯救许多人的性命。她知道不扳就会酿成大祸。然后她选择了不扳。

他们问她为什么。

她说：「因为那辆马车不是由我改变方向的。我没有放开缰绳，我没有将它对准任何人。在我知道它存在之前，它就已经朝着那些人冲去了。如果我扳动了拉杆，我就会夺走一条性命——哈尔多的性命——而那条性命本来从未受到马车的威胁。我会把我没有造成的悲剧变成一次蓄意的杀戮。我会选择了谁该死。而我没有权利做出这个选择。」

治安官们并不满意。整个山谷也不满意。而桑娜，如果在此后的岁月里她对自身诚实的话，她自己也不满意。

但她在那四秒中面对的问题，不是满意或不满意所能回答的。这是一个关于「作为」与「不作为」意味着什么的问题。如果你将致命的力量从许多人身上引向一个人，你是在拯救生命，还是在夺走生命？如果你在灾难发生时袖手旁观、什么也不做，你因为你不是灾难的起因而无辜，还是因为你本可以阻止它而成了共犯？杀人与任其死亡之间存在道德区别吗？如果存在，需要有多少生命面临威胁，这个区别才会崩塌？

整个山谷就桑娜的决定争论了多年。有人称她为懦夫。有人称她为哲人。少数人称她为杀人者——不是因为她的所作所为，而是因为她的不作为。还有人说她是谷中唯一诚实的人，因为她拒绝将一个可怕的选择粉饰为英雄之举。哈尔多本人得知事情原委后，前去探望她。他坐在她的小屋里，喝着她的茶，沉默了很久。然后他问：「如果你扳了拉杆，你能与自己和解吗？」桑娜说：「我不知道。」哈尔多点了点头。「我也不知道，」他说，「但我很高兴我不用去知道。」

多年以后，首都大学的一位哲学家听说了这个故事并将其记录下来，只改动了细节：失控的马车变成了一辆失控的电车（tram），峡谷中的岔路变成了轨道的道岔，拉杆变成了切换开关。他不再将这当作一个关于桑娜的故事来讲述，而是作为一个问题：你站在一个开关旁。一辆失控的电车正冲向被绑在主轨道上的五个人。你可以扳动开关，将电车引向只有一个人被绑的侧轨。你扳不扳？他将其称为「电车难题」（Trolley Problem），它后来成为现代道德哲学中被讨论最广泛的思想实验之一，由菲利帕·福特（Philippa Foot）于1967年提出，后由朱迪斯·贾维斯·汤姆森（Judith Jarvis Thomson）进一步拓展。

电车难题实际上并非关于电车。它关乎两种强大道德直觉之间的张力。第一种直觉是功利主义（utilitarian）的：我们应当将伤害降至最低，以一条命换五条命意味着净增四条命——任何理性的人都应该扳动开关。第二种直觉是义务论（deontological）的：任由伤害发生与主动造成伤害之间存在根本区别，把一个人当作拯救他人的手段，是将那个人仅仅当作了工具而非目的本身。令人困惑的是，几乎所有人都会同时感受到这两种直觉，而它们指向截然相反的方向。

该问题的各种变体探究的是我们的直觉在哪里断裂。在「天桥」版本中，你必须将一个大个子从桥上推下去挡住电车——大多数人会拒绝，尽管算术结果完全相同。在「环线」版本中，侧轨会绕回主轨，那一个人的身体才是真正挡住电车的东西——人们变得犹豫。在「器官移植」版本中，外科医生可以摘取一个健康人的器官来拯救五名患者——几乎所有人都会退缩。数字始终不变：一比五。但道德质感会随着你的参与程度、你是在转移已有的威胁还是在制造新的威胁、以及那一个人的死亡是副作用还是被蓄意选择的手段而发生变化。

电车难题最终揭示的不是正确答案，而是我们道德推理本身的形状。它表明我们并不只凭后果来评估行为——如果是的话，每个版本都会同样一目了然。它表明我们也不只凭意图来评估行为——如果是的话，作为与不作为之间的区别将毫无分量。两者兼而有之，纠缠在一起，而那些萦绕在我们心头、挥之不去的案例，恰恰是两种评估方法产生了不可调和之结论的情形。桑娜在岔路口站了四秒钟，而我们其余的人则要站一辈子。
articles.content_en:
There was once a narrow gorge carved through the mountains, and through it ran the only road connecting the valley's six villages to the rest of the world. At the gorge's deepest point, the path split. The left branch ran straight and true for a quarter mile before arriving at the great market square of the valley's largest town, where a hundred people bustled on any given morning. The right branch curved sharply around a granite shoulder and ended at a small quarry where, on most days, a single old stonecutter named Haldor sat chipping away at blocks of limestone, half deaf from decades of hammering and wholly unaware of anything beyond the next strike of his chisel.

The fork was controlled by a lever housed in a small stone hut beside the road. A heavy iron tongue, when thrown to one side, guided all traffic onto the left branch; when thrown to the other, it sent everything — carts, horses, people — around the curve and into the quarry. For as long as anyone could remember, the lever had been kept locked in the leftward position, and the rightward track had been allowed to grow over with wild thyme and clover.

The keeper of the lever was a woman named Sanna. She had held the post for twenty-three years, and in all that time she had never once moved the iron tongue from its resting place. Her job, as she understood it, was to watch the fork and ensure that the road remained open to the market town. She kept the hut swept, the lever oiled, and a pot of tea always warm on the brazier. Travelers would sometimes stop to rest, and she would offer them a cup and a piece of hard bread, and they would tell her news of the villages beyond the gorge. She was content. She was, she believed, doing no harm and some modest good.

On a morning in late autumn, a cart loaded with iron ore broke free from a mining team higher up the gorge. The driver leapt clear, but the cart gathered speed as it plunged down the steep incline, its iron-rimmed wheels screaming against the stone walls of the gorge. By the time Sanna heard it — a sound like thunder trapped in a throat — it was already too close to stop, and it was aimed squarely at the fork.

Sanna stepped out of her hut and saw it. She saw, too, that the iron tongue was still locked leftward. The cart would follow the left branch. It would enter the market square at full speed. It would kill people — not one or two, but many. She could not see the square from where she stood, but she could hear the distant murmur of the morning crowd, and she knew with a certainty that felt like a stone dropped into her stomach that dozens of people were there right now, buying bread, arguing over the price of wool, lifting children onto their shoulders to see the juggler who came on market days.

She looked at the lever. She could throw it to the right. The cart would swing around the granite shoulder and plunge into the quarry. The people in the market square would be safe. But Haldor — old, deaf, absorbed Haldor, who had never done harm to anyone and who could not hear a cart bearing down on him any more than he could hear a whisper — Haldor would die.

She had perhaps four seconds. The cart was close enough now that she could see the ore shifting in its bed, could hear the individual stones clattering against the wooden sides. The iron tongue was stiff but not immovable — she had kept it oiled, after all. She would need both hands and the full weight of her body to throw it.

Sanna did not move.

The cart thundered past her hut, followed the leftward track, and disappeared toward the market square. She heard the impact. She heard the screaming. She stood perfectly still with her hands at her sides, and she heard all of it.

When the magistrates came, they asked her why she had not thrown the lever. She told them the truth: she had considered it. She had known that throwing the lever would save many lives. She had known that not throwing it would mean catastrophe. And she had chosen not to throw it.

They asked her why.

She said: "Because the cart was not mine to redirect. I did not loose it. I did not aim it. It was already traveling toward those people before I ever knew it existed. If I had thrown the lever, I would have taken a life — Haldor's life — that was never in danger from the cart. I would have turned a tragedy I did not cause into a deliberate act of killing. I would have chosen who died. And I did not have the right to make that choice."

The magistrates were not satisfied. Neither was the valley. Neither, if she was honest with herself in the years that followed, was Sanna.

But the question she had faced in those four seconds could not be answered by satisfaction or dissatisfaction. It was a question about what it means to act versus what it means to allow. If you redirect a deadly force from many people onto one person, have you saved lives or taken a life? If you stand aside and do nothing while disaster unfolds, are you innocent because you did not cause it, or are you complicit because you could have prevented it? Is there a moral difference between killing and letting die? And if there is, how many lives must be at stake before that difference collapses?

The valley argued about Sanna's decision for years. Some called her a coward. Some called her a philosopher. A few called her a murderer — not for what she had done, but for what she had not done. Others said she was the only honest person in the valley, because she had refused to dress up a terrible choice as heroism. Haldor himself, when he learned what had happened, went to visit her. He sat in her hut, drank her tea, and said nothing for a long time. Then he asked: "If you had thrown the lever, would you have been able to live with yourself?" Sanna said, "I don't know." Haldor nodded. "I don't know either," he said. "But I'm glad I don't have to find out."

Years later, a philosopher from the university in the capital heard the story and wrote it down, changing only the details: a runaway cart became a runaway tram, the fork in the gorge became a fork in the tracks, the lever became a switch. He posed it not as a story about Sanna but as a question: you are standing at a switch. A runaway tram is heading toward five people tied to the main track. You can throw the switch, diverting the tram onto a side track where only one person is tied. Do you throw it? He called it the Trolley Problem, and it became one of the most widely discussed thought experiments in modern moral philosophy, introduced by Philippa Foot in 1967 and later expanded by Judith Jarvis Thomson.

The Trolley Problem is not really about trolleys. It is about the tension between two powerful moral intuitions. The first intuition is utilitarian: we should minimize harm, and saving five lives at the cost of one is a net gain of four lives — any rational person should throw the switch. The second intuition is deontological: there is a categorical difference between allowing harm to happen and actively causing it, and using one person as a means to save others treats that person as a mere instrument rather than an end in themselves. The puzzle is that almost everyone feels both intuitions simultaneously, and they point in opposite directions.

Variations of the problem probe where our intuitions break. In the "footbridge" version, you must push a large person off a bridge to stop the tram — most people refuse, even though the arithmetic is identical. In the "loop" version, the side track loops back to the main track, and the one person's body is what stops the tram — people become hesitant. In the "transplant" version, a surgeon could harvest one healthy person's organs to save five patients — nearly everyone recoils. The numbers are always the same: one versus five. But the moral texture changes depending on how directly you are involved, whether you are diverting an existing threat or creating a new one, and whether the one person's death is a side effect or a deliberately chosen means.

What the Trolley Problem ultimately reveals is not the correct answer but the shape of our moral reasoning itself. It shows that we do not evaluate actions solely by their consequences — if we did, every version would be equally obvious. It shows that we do not evaluate actions solely by their intentions — if we did, the distinction between acting and omitting would carry no weight. We do both, tangled together, and the cases that haunt us are precisely the ones where the two methods of evaluation produce irreconcilable verdicts. Sanna stood at the fork for four seconds, but the rest of us stand there for a lifetime.

paragraphs.en: There was once a narrow gorge carved through the mountains, and through it ran the only road connecting the valley's six villages to the rest of the world. At the gorge's deepest point, the path split. The left branch ran straight and true for a quarter mile before arriving at the great market square of the valley's largest town, where a hundred people bustled on any given morning. The right branch curved sharply around a granite shoulder and ended at a small quarry where, on most days, a single old stonecutter named Haldor sat chipping away at blocks of limestone, half deaf from decades of hammering and wholly unaware of anything beyond the next strike of his chisel.
paragraphs.cn: 群山之间曾有一道狭窄的峡谷，贯穿其中的唯一一条道路连接着谷中六座村庄与外面的世界。在峡谷最深处，道路分成了两岔。左边的岔路笔直延伸约四分之一里后，通向谷中最大市镇的中央集场，那里每天清晨都有一百来人熙来攘往。右边的岔路在一面花岗岩壁肩处急转，尽头是一座小采石场，大多数日子里，只有一个名叫哈尔多（Haldor）的老石匠坐在那里敲凿石灰石，几十年的锤击使他半耳失聪，对下一锤之外的世界毫无知觉。

paragraphs.en: The fork was controlled by a lever housed in a small stone hut beside the road. A heavy iron tongue, when thrown to one side, guided all traffic onto the left branch; when thrown to the other, it sent everything — carts, horses, people — around the curve and into the quarry. For as long as anyone could remember, the lever had been kept locked in the leftward position, and the rightward track had been allowed to grow over with wild thyme and clover.
paragraphs.cn: 岔路口由路边一间石砌小屋中的一根拉杆控制。沉重的铁舌扳向一侧时，所有车辆行人都会被导向左边的岔路；扳向另一侧时，则将一切——马车、马匹、行人——送入弯道，直抵采石场。在所有人的记忆中，拉杆始终被锁在向左的位置，右边的路轨上早已长满了野百里香和三叶草。

paragraphs.en: The keeper of the lever was a woman named Sanna. She had held the post for twenty-three years, and in all that time she had never once moved the iron tongue from its resting place. Her job, as she understood it, was to watch the fork and ensure that the road remained open to the market town. She kept the hut swept, the lever oiled, and a pot of tea always warm on the brazier. Travelers would sometimes stop to rest, and she would offer them a cup and a piece of hard bread, and they would tell her news of the villages beyond the gorge. She was content. She was, she believed, doing no harm and some modest good.
paragraphs.cn: 看守拉杆的是一位名叫桑娜（Sanna）的女子。她担任此职已有二十三年，其间从未将铁舌从原位移开过一次。她理解的职责是守望岔路口，确保通往集场的道路始终畅通。她保持小屋整洁，拉杆润滑，火盆上的茶水始终温热。旅人有时会停下来歇脚，她会递上一杯茶和一块硬面包，他们会告诉她峡谷那边各村的新闻。她心满意足。她相信自己做的一切不伤人、不害物，还有些许微善。

paragraphs.en: On a morning in late autumn, a cart loaded with iron ore broke free from a mining team higher up the gorge. The driver leapt clear, but the cart gathered speed as it plunged down the steep incline, its iron-rimmed wheels screaming against the stone walls of the gorge. By the time Sanna heard it — a sound like thunder trapped in a throat — it was already too close to stop, and it was aimed squarely at the fork.
paragraphs.cn: 深秋的一个清晨，一辆满载铁矿石的马车从峡谷上方的一支矿队处脱缰。车夫跳车逃开，但马车在陡坡上越冲越快，铁箍车轮在峡谷石壁间发出刺耳的尖啸。等到桑娜听到那声音——像雷声卡在喉咙里——已经来不及了，马车根本无法停住，正对着岔路口直冲而来。

paragraphs.en: Sanna stepped out of her hut and saw it. She saw, too, that the iron tongue was still locked leftward. The cart would follow the left branch. It would enter the market square at full speed. It would kill people — not one or two, but many. She could not see the square from where she stood, but she could hear the distant murmur of the morning crowd, and she knew with a certainty that felt like a stone dropped into her stomach that dozens of people were there right now, buying bread, arguing over the price of wool, lifting children onto their shoulders to see the juggler who came on market days.
paragraphs.cn: 桑娜走出小屋，看见了那辆马车。她也看见铁舌仍锁在向左的位置。马车将沿着左边的岔路冲下去，以全速冲入集场。它会杀人——不是一个两个，而是许多。她站在那里看不见集场，但她能听到远处早晨人群的嗡嗡声，她以一种仿佛巨石落入胃中的确信知道，此刻那里有几十个人在买面包、在为羊毛的价格争辩、把孩子扛到肩上去看赶集日才来的杂耍艺人。

paragraphs.en: She looked at the lever. She could throw it to the right. The cart would swing around the granite shoulder and plunge into the quarry. The people in the market square would be safe. But Haldor — old, deaf, absorbed Haldor, who had never done harm to anyone and who could not hear a cart bearing down on him any more than he could hear a whisper — Haldor would die.
paragraphs.cn: 她看了看拉杆。她可以把它扳向右侧。马车会绕过花岗岩壁肩，冲入采石场。集场上的人会平安无事。但是哈尔多——年迈的、失聪的、沉浸在自己世界里的哈尔多，他从未伤害过任何人，他听不见马车朝他冲来，正如他听不见一句耳语——哈尔多会死。

paragraphs.en: She had perhaps four seconds. The cart was close enough now that she could see the ore shifting in its bed, could hear the individual stones clattering against the wooden sides. The iron tongue was stiff but not immovable — she had kept it oiled, after all. She would need both hands and the full weight of her body to throw it.
paragraphs.cn: 她大约有四秒钟的时间。马车已经近到她能看见矿砂在车斗中滑动，能听见一颗颗石子撞击木壁的声响。铁舌虽重却并非扳不动——毕竟她一直在给它上油。她需要双手和全身的重量才能将它扳过去。

paragraphs.en: Sanna did not move.
paragraphs.cn: 桑娜没有动。

paragraphs.en: The cart thundered past her hut, followed the leftward track, and disappeared toward the market square. She heard the impact. She heard the screaming. She stood perfectly still with her hands at her sides, and she heard all of it.
paragraphs.cn: 马车轰鸣着掠过她的小屋，沿着左边的路轨消失了，冲向集场。她听到了撞击声。她听到了尖叫声。她双手垂在身体两侧，一动不动地站着，把一切都听在了耳中。

paragraphs.en: When the magistrates came, they asked her why she had not thrown the lever. She told them the truth: she had considered it. She had known that throwing the lever would save many lives. She had known that not throwing it would mean catastrophe. And she had chosen not to throw it.
paragraphs.cn: 治安官来的时候，问她为什么没有扳动拉杆。她说了实话：她考虑过。她知道扳动拉杆能拯救许多人的性命。她知道不扳就会酿成大祸。然后她选择了不扳。

paragraphs.en: They asked her why.
paragraphs.cn: 他们问她为什么。

paragraphs.en: She said: "Because the cart was not mine to redirect. I did not loose it. I did not aim it. It was already traveling toward those people before I ever knew it existed. If I had thrown the lever, I would have taken a life — Haldor's life — that was never in danger from the cart. I would have turned a tragedy I did not cause into a deliberate act of killing. I would have chosen who died. And I did not have the right to make that choice."
paragraphs.cn: 她说：「因为那辆马车不是由我改变方向的。我没有放开缰绳，我没有将它对准任何人。在我知道它存在之前，它就已经朝着那些人冲去了。如果我扳动了拉杆，我就会夺走一条性命——哈尔多的性命——而那条性命本来从未受到马车的威胁。我会把我没有造成的悲剧变成一次蓄意的杀戮。我会选择了谁该死。而我没有权利做出这个选择。」

paragraphs.en: The magistrates were not satisfied. Neither was the valley. Neither, if she was honest with herself in the years that followed, was Sanna.
paragraphs.cn: 治安官们并不满意。整个山谷也不满意。而桑娜，如果在此后的岁月里她对自身诚实的话，她自己也不满意。

paragraphs.en: But the question she had faced in those four seconds could not be answered by satisfaction or dissatisfaction. It was a question about what it means to act versus what it means to allow. If you redirect a deadly force from many people onto one person, have you saved lives or taken a life? If you stand aside and do nothing while disaster unfolds, are you innocent because you did not cause it, or are you complicit because you could have prevented it? Is there a moral difference between killing and letting die? And if there is, how many lives must be at stake before that difference collapses?
paragraphs.cn: 但她在那四秒中面对的问题，不是满意或不满意所能回答的。这是一个关于「作为」与「不作为」意味着什么的问题。如果你将致命的力量从许多人身上引向一个人，你是在拯救生命，还是在夺走生命？如果你在灾难发生时袖手旁观、什么也不做，你因为你不是灾难的起因而无辜，还是因为你本可以阻止它而成了共犯？杀人与任其死亡之间存在道德区别吗？如果存在，需要有多少生命面临威胁，这个区别才会崩塌？

paragraphs.en: The valley argued about Sanna's decision for years. Some called her a coward. Some called her a philosopher. A few called her a murderer — not for what she had done, but for what she had not done. Others said she was the only honest person in the valley, because she had refused to dress up a terrible choice as heroism. Haldor himself, when he learned what had happened, went to visit her. He sat in her hut, drank her tea, and said nothing for a long time. Then he asked: "If you had thrown the lever, would you have been able to live with yourself?" Sanna said, "I don't know." Haldor nodded. "I don't know either," he said. "But I'm glad I don't have to find out."
paragraphs.cn: 整个山谷就桑娜的决定争论了多年。有人称她为懦夫。有人称她为哲人。少数人称她为杀人者——不是因为她的所作所为，而是因为她的不作为。还有人说她是谷中唯一诚实的人，因为她拒绝将一个可怕的选择粉饰为英雄之举。哈尔多本人得知事情原委后，前去探望她。他坐在她的小屋里，喝着她的茶，沉默了很久。然后他问：「如果你扳了拉杆，你能与自己和解吗？」桑娜说：「我不知道。」哈尔多点了点头。「我也不知道，」他说，「但我很高兴我不用去知道。」

paragraphs.en: Years later, a philosopher from the university in the capital heard the story and wrote it down, changing only the details: a runaway cart became a runaway tram, the fork in the gorge became a fork in the tracks, the lever became a switch. He posed it not as a story about Sanna but as a question: you are standing at a switch. A runaway tram is heading toward five people tied to the main track. You can throw the switch, diverting the tram onto a side track where only one person is tied. Do you throw it? He called it the Trolley Problem, and it became one of the most widely discussed thought experiments in modern moral philosophy, introduced by Philippa Foot in 1967 and later expanded by Judith Jarvis Thomson.
paragraphs.cn: 多年以后，首都大学的一位哲学家听说了这个故事并将其记录下来，只改动了细节：失控的马车变成了一辆失控的电车（tram），峡谷中的岔路变成了轨道的道岔，拉杆变成了切换开关。他不再将这当作一个关于桑娜的故事来讲述，而是作为一个问题：你站在一个开关旁。一辆失控的电车正冲向被绑在主轨道上的五个人。你可以扳动开关，将电车引向只有一个人被绑的侧轨。你扳不扳？他将其称为「电车难题」（Trolley Problem），它后来成为现代道德哲学中被讨论最广泛的思想实验之一，由菲利帕·福特（Philippa Foot）于1967年提出，后由朱迪斯·贾维斯·汤姆森（Judith Jarvis Thomson）进一步拓展。

paragraphs.en: The Trolley Problem is not really about trolleys. It is about the tension between two powerful moral intuitions. The first intuition is utilitarian: we should minimize harm, and saving five lives at the cost of one is a net gain of four lives — any rational person should throw the switch. The second intuition is deontological: there is a categorical difference between allowing harm to happen and actively causing it, and using one person as a means to save others treats that person as a mere instrument rather than an end in themselves. The puzzle is that almost everyone feels both intuitions simultaneously, and they point in opposite directions.
paragraphs.cn: 电车难题实际上并非关于电车。它关乎两种强大道德直觉之间的张力。第一种直觉是功利主义（utilitarian）的：我们应当将伤害降至最低，以一条命换五条命意味着净增四条命——任何理性的人都应该扳动开关。第二种直觉是义务论（deontological）的：任由伤害发生与主动造成伤害之间存在根本区别，把一个人当作拯救他人的手段，是将那个人仅仅当作了工具而非目的本身。令人困惑的是，几乎所有人都会同时感受到这两种直觉，而它们指向截然相反的方向。

paragraphs.en: Variations of the problem probe where our intuitions break. In the "footbridge" version, you must push a large person off a bridge to stop the tram — most people refuse, even though the arithmetic is identical. In the "loop" version, the side track loops back to the main track, and the one person's body is what stops the tram — people become hesitant. In the "transplant" version, a surgeon could harvest one healthy person's organs to save five patients — nearly everyone recoils. The numbers are always the same: one versus five. But the moral texture changes depending on how directly you are involved, whether you are diverting an existing threat or creating a new one, and whether the one person's death is a side effect or a deliberately chosen means.
paragraphs.cn: 该问题的各种变体探究的是我们的直觉在哪里断裂。在「天桥」版本中，你必须将一个大个子从桥上推下去挡住电车——大多数人会拒绝，尽管算术结果完全相同。在「环线」版本中，侧轨会绕回主轨，那一个人的身体才是真正挡住电车的东西——人们变得犹豫。在「器官移植」版本中，外科医生可以摘取一个健康人的器官来拯救五名患者——几乎所有人都会退缩。数字始终不变：一比五。但道德质感会随着你的参与程度、你是在转移已有的威胁还是在制造新的威胁、以及那一个人的死亡是副作用还是被蓄意选择的手段而发生变化。

paragraphs.en: What the Trolley Problem ultimately reveals is not the correct answer but the shape of our moral reasoning itself. It shows that we do not evaluate actions solely by their consequences — if we did, every version would be equally obvious. It shows that we do not evaluate actions solely by their intentions — if we did, the distinction between acting and omitting would carry no weight. We do both, tangled together, and the cases that haunt us are precisely the ones where the two methods of evaluation produce irreconcilable verdicts. Sanna stood at the fork for four seconds, but the rest of us stand there for a lifetime.
paragraphs.cn: 电车难题最终揭示的不是正确答案，而是我们道德推理本身的形状。它表明我们并不只凭后果来评估行为——如果是的话，每个版本都会同样一目了然。它表明我们也不只凭意图来评估行为——如果是的话，作为与不作为之间的区别将毫无分量。两者兼而有之，纠缠在一起，而那些萦绕在我们心头、挥之不去的案例，恰恰是两种评估方法产生了不可调和之结论的情形。桑娜在岔路口站了四秒钟，而我们其余的人则要站一辈子。

articles.title: 守护监护者：制度如何将信任扩展到数百万陌生人
articles.subtitle: 从珊瑚礁清洁鱼到国家宪法：制度作为社会技术的设计原理
articles.author: Julien Lie-Panis
articles.summary:
每一个人类社会，从最小的村庄到最大的国家，都面临同一个根本性挑战：如何让人们为了集体利益而非个人私利行动。渔民必须限制捕捞量，以免鱼类资源枯竭。人们必须尊重他人的财产和人身安全。公民必须纳税以修建道路、学校和医院。若任由纯粹的自私本能驱使，任何社区都无法存续；集体生活的纽带将迅速瓦解。

我们所设计...
articles.content_cn:
每一个人类社会，从最小的村庄到最大的国家，都面临同一个根本性挑战：如何让人们为了集体利益而非个人私利行动。渔民必须限制捕捞量，以免鱼类资源枯竭。人们必须尊重他人的财产和人身安全。公民必须纳税以修建道路、学校和医院。若任由纯粹的自私本能驱使，任何社区都无法存续；集体生活的纽带将迅速瓦解。

我们所设计的解决方案在不同文化和世纪之间惊人地相似。我们制定规则，然后任命监护者来执行规则，违者受罚。但这种方法存在一个问题——罗马诗人 Juvenal（尤维纳利斯）在近两千年前就指出了这一点：Quis custodiet ipsos custodes？谁来监护监护者本身？

渔业部门任命监督员以防止过度捕捞——但如果监督员收受贿赂、睁一只眼闭一只眼呢？警察存在的目的是保护每个人的财产和安全——但谁来确保他们不滥用权力？政府收税用于公共服务——但我们如何阻止官员将公款转入自己的账户？

每一个制度都面临同样的根本悖论。制度通过奖励良好行为和惩罚违规者来促进合作。然而制度自身的运转又依赖于成员的合作。我们并没有解决合作问题——只是把它向后退了一步。那么制度为何能发挥作用？要理解这个谜题，我们需要先弄清楚，人类的合作在自然界中究竟有何非凡之处。

合作在自然界中无处不在。漫步于任何森林，凝视任何潮汐水洼，观察任何一片草甸，你都会目睹无数似乎违背自然选择残酷逻辑的合作关系。这些联盟并非神秘莫测，而是遵循进化生物学家已经充分理解的、可预测的模式。少数几个基本机制就能解释从蚁群到珊瑚礁的合作现象：亲缘选择、互惠利他和声誉。

想象一个繁忙的蚁群世界。数千只工蚁不知疲倦地劳作，有的照料幼虫，有的外出觅食，还有的守卫巢穴抵御入侵者。有些蚂蚁的合作行为达到了惊人的极端：某些工蚁的头部异常膨大且扁平，几乎无法移动，它们终生充当活的门板，用圆盘状的颅骨封堵巢穴入口。更加戏剧性的是，当一种爆炸蚁的工蚁受到威胁时，它们会真正撕裂自己的身体，向攻击者喷射有毒的粘性化合物。为什么工蚁会为一个自己永远无法统领的蚁群牺牲一切？这种非凡的利他行为之所以可能，是因为工蚁通常是姐妹，彼此之间共享相当大比例的基因。当它们为蚁群牺牲时，它们实际上在帮助自己基因的复本存活下去。进化生物学家将此称为亲缘选择（kin selection）——亲属之间的利他行为在遗传学上是合理的，因为它促进了共有基因的存活。

再看看吸血蝙蝠（Desmodus rotundus，普通吸血蝠）。当一只蝙蝠在夜间狩猎中未能找到血液时，它面临饥饿的威胁。但成功进食的同栖伙伴会反刍出宝贵的血液来拯救挨饿的邻居——不仅是亲属，还包括无血缘关系的伙伴。为什么要帮助一只与你基因共享程度不比随便一只蝙蝠多的陌生者？因为对方有望回报这份恩惠。蝙蝠实行互惠分享，优先帮助那些之前分享过食物的伙伴。互惠机制如同社会保险：今晚分享血液的蝙蝠，在未来自己需要时可以期待获得帮助。

还有散布在珊瑚礁上的清洁站，小型清洁鱼（Labroides dimidiatus，裂唇鱼）在那里为较大的客户鱼清除寄生虫。清洁鱼完全可以咬下美味的粘液而不是吃无聊的寄生虫，但它们通常会抵制这种诱惑。为什么要与一种根本没有能力回报的异种鱼类合作？因为其他客户鱼在旁观。当观察者看到清洁鱼咬食粘液时，它们会避开作弊者，转而寻找合作的清洁鱼。声誉决定了未来的生意。

亲缘选择、互惠利他和声誉——这三种力量驱动着整个自然界的合作。但每一种都受到显著约束。亲缘选择仅在亲属之间有效。互惠利他需要相同个体之间反复互动。声誉则只在小型群体中才能发挥作用，在那里你的行为容易被观察，关于过去行为的信息能够可靠传播。

人类遵循同样的进化规则，但我们却不知怎地将合作推展到了远远超越亲缘、互惠和声誉自然边界的地方。每一天，我们信任无数陌生人——与我们没有血缘关系的人，我们永远不会再见的人，以及在没有旁观者的情况下可以轻易背叛这种信任的人。我们走进车流，假定司机们会停下来。我们吃着我们永远不会看到其制作过程的人所准备的食物。我们把积蓄托付给面目模糊的银行，把孩子交给我们几乎不认识的人经营的学校。我们登上将我们送上天空的金属管道，相信机械师维护了引擎，飞行员是合格的，空中交通管制员已经为我们清除了航路。

制度有助于弥合这一鸿沟。交通法规和警察维持街道秩序；食品检查员确保餐饮安全；法院执行合同；监管机构监督银行和航空公司。通过奖励合作和制裁滥用行为，制度使信任得以在陌生人之间流动。

但这只是转移了问题：如果制度保证了合作，那么谁来保证制度自身的正常运转？谁来监护监护者？

答案是声誉。社区本身通过维系陌生人之间合作的同样社会力量来确保制度的完整性。被监护者监护着监护者。

看看日本德川时代各地山村如何管理他们的共有森林。这些共享林地提供建筑用木材、屋顶用茅草、马匹用饲料、肥料，以及冬季生存必不可少的柴火和木炭。他们发展出精细的分配规则：每户按年度轮换被分配特定的区域，并规定了精心安排的开山日，居民可以在那天采伐特定的资源。

关键是，他们雇佣了被称为"侦探"的专业监督员，骑马巡逻公共土地，监视未经授权的使用者。侦探可以向违规者索要现金和清酒，村庄则施加逐级加重的惩罚——极端情况下包括驱逐出境。没有这些监督员，所有精心设计的规则都很容易被规避。在广袤的森林中，分散的住户忙于各自的事务，谁能观察到是否有人违反封山令或在非采伐季节盗伐？

这个解决方案看起来似乎只是将问题向后推了一步——谁来确保侦探本身不会腐败？但这种表面的位移实际上是一个重大进步。侦探人数不多，他们的行为是可见的，他们的使命是明确的。在广袤地形上监测数十名村民几乎不可能，而确保少数几位高度可见的监督员的诚实则简单得多。换言之，社区并非仅仅增加了一条规则——它设计了一种真正的社会技术。通过引入侦探这一角色，它将一个不可能的问题（防止森林过度使用）转化为了一个更简单的问题（确保少数几位可见的侦探的诚实）。而这个更简单的问题可以通过一种众所周知的机制来解决：声誉。

我和同事开发了一个数学模型来将这一想法形式化。我们从博弈论者所说的重复信任博弈开始。在一个大型社区中，人们经常遇到不太了解的人，必须决定是否信任他们。被信任的人随后面临自己的选择：以付出代价的方式回报这份信任，或者欺骗。这些决策中只有一部分会被观察到，从而形成声誉：关于过去行为的不完美记录。

这捕捉了基于声誉合作的基本逻辑。被信任的人面临权衡：现在欺骗以获取即时利益，还是付出回报的代价来建立一个能确保未来获得信任的声誉。而其他人则依据这些声誉来决定是否给予信任。

但这个简单的设定也展示了合作为何经常崩溃。声誉只有在作弊诱惑不太强烈且行为足够可观察的情况下才有效。一旦观察的可能性低于某个阈值，声誉就无法再维持回报行为，合作随之瓦解。这正是日本村民面临的那个不可能的问题。他们可以宣布封山，但在广袤的森林中，住户分散各地，没人能看到谁在违规季节偷伐木材。

这就是我和同事引入制度的环节。在模型中，个体现在可以向一个制度贡献资源，该制度汇集并放大这些贡献，用它们来监测信任博弈并惩罚作弊者。这改变了原始信任博弈的规则，使行为更容易被观察，作弊的代价更高。要做到这一点，必须满足两个条件。第一，贡献的成本必须足够低且可见，使声誉仅凭自身就能维持它们。第二，制度必须高效地放大这些贡献，产生足够强的激励，使原始信任博弈中的合作成为可能。

在这个意义上，制度是一种社会技术。杠杆不会凭空创造力量——它放大你施加的力量。一个设计良好的制度不会废除合作问题；它重新设计这个问题。在我们的模型中，制度必须产生足够强的激励来解锁信任博弈中的合作，同时依赖另一种形式的合作：贡献。当制度高效时，它能从成本足够低（使声誉得以维持）的贡献中产生这些激励。这正是化解 Juvenal 悖论的关键所在。不存在无限倒退，因为那个更简单的问题可以仅凭声誉来解决。

日本村民设计了这一原则的一个版本。他们绝非孤例。在《公共事务的治理之道》（Governing the Commons, 1990）中，诺贝尔奖得主 Elinor Ostrom（埃莉诺·奥斯特罗姆）追踪了世界各地社区如何成功地使共享的森林、渔场和灌溉系统繁荣数百年，并提炼出成功背后的设计原则——包括使用可问责的监督员。从日本的山村到瑞士阿尔卑斯山的合作社再到菲律宾的灌溉协会，长寿的公共资源管理体系都需要任命其警惕性可由社区评判和奖赏的人。正如 Ostrom 所指出的，发现违规者的人会因作为公共资源的优秀守护者而获得地位和声望，而那些偷懒懈怠的人一旦被发现就会被轻易解雇。通过任命可问责的监督员，这些社区将不可见的多数人的合作转化为了可见的少数人的合作。

现代社会通过嵌套的问责层级来扩展这一原则。以航空业为例：机械师维护飞机，主管检查他们的工作，航空公司安全部门监督主管，国家主管部门审计航空公司，民选官员最终为这些主管部门负责。在每一个层级，需要被监督的人数在减少，而他们的行为变得更加可见。我们将生命托付给这道问责的瀑布——从数千名看不见的机械师到少数几位失职会成为头条新闻的公共官员。同样的架构在现代生活的方方面面不断重复，将庞大的潜在不信任网络转化为可管理的监督链条。

当制度设计良好时，它们可以极大地扩展合作的规模。但正如经济学家 Deirdre McCloskey（迪尔德丽·麦克洛斯基）所指出的，制度并非灵丹妙药。她在《资产阶级平等》（Bourgeois Equality, 2016）一书中写道：你可以设立英式法庭，甚至给律师们配上假发，但如果法官贪污受贿、律师毫无职业操守，而公众又对两者都嗤之以鼻，那么引入这样一个听起来不错的制度根本无法改善法治。

制度无法逃脱合作问题——它只能重新设计它。使我们的飞机安全的那层层叠叠的监督机制，如果缺少了在乎主管评价的机械师、关注公众评判的官员以及愿意追究他们责任的公民，就无法运转。同样，司法系统在每个层面都需要正直。没有任何制度能凭空变出合作。它必须已经存在。

想想历史上最受推崇的制度设计之一：美国宪法。James Madison（詹姆斯·麦迪逊）和其他制宪者设计了一套制衡体系以防止暴政，将权力分属三个独立的政府分支。在《联邦党人文集》第51篇中，Madison 解释了其中的逻辑：野心必须用来对抗野心。每个分支都将拥有捍卫自身权威的手段和动机，从而使权力制约权力。

然而，再精妙的宪法也无法预见每一个偶然事件，任何规则都可能被按照有利于自己的方式解读。正如政治学家 Steven Levitsky（史蒂文·莱维茨基）和 Daniel Ziblatt（丹尼尔·齐布拉特）在《民主是如何死亡的》（How Democracies Die, 2018）一书中所观察到的，美国的民主依赖于不成文的规范，这些规范防止普通的政治竞争沦为全面的战争。政治对手必须通过相互宽容来合作——接受对手为合法的竞争者而非生存威胁——以及制度克制，选择不去利用每一个法律上的优势。

历史表明，这些规范远非自然而然形成的。在建国初期，John Adams（约翰·亚当斯）的联邦党与 Thomas Jefferson（托马斯·杰斐逊）的民主共和党将彼此视为生存威胁。联邦党人攻击 Jefferson 是一个不信上帝的雅各宾派，会释放法国大革命式的恐怖，而共和党人则谴责联邦党人是密谋恢复英国暴政的君主主义者。1798年，联邦党控制的国会通过了《外侨及煽动叛乱法案》，将批评政府定为犯罪。在这些法律下，联邦党检察官逮捕了报纸编辑，甚至逮捕了一位反对总统 Adams 的共和党国会议员。

1800年 Adams 与 Jefferson 之间的选举将国家推向了悬崖边缘。联邦党人控制的众议院利用原始选举制度中的一个技术性漏洞（后来由第十二修正案修复），试图阻止 Jefferson 获胜。武装冲突似乎迫在眉睫：共和党州长准备了民兵，而一家联邦党报纸扬言任何起义都将被马萨诸塞州的军队镇压。然而 Adams 选择和平离任，Jefferson 接任总统。Jefferson 后来将这一决定描述为一场可与1776年革命相媲美的革命。民主存活了下来——险之又险——不是因为宪法要求如此，而是因为领导人选择了克制。

政党之间的合作还仅仅是开始。民主制度在每一个层面都需要合作：公正的法官、诚实的官僚、克制的立法者，以及足够重视正直品质并愿意追究领导人责任的公民。公民内在诚实度更高的国家——即使没有制度激励也愿意合作的国家——始终拥有腐败更少的制度。制度恰恰在最不需要它们的社会中运作得最好，也就是那些拥有强有力合作规范的社会。

因此，制度并非魔法。它们依赖于其所治理的人，因为它们只能放大已有的合作倾向。然而有些社会比其他社会更具合作性。是什么造成了这些差异？

必要的要素是耐心。制度通过将一个困难的合作问题转化为一个声誉可以解决的更简单的问题来发挥作用。但人们仍然必须愿意参与基于声誉的合作，而这建立在一种当下与未来的权衡之上。

在我们的模型中，个体必须真正具备耐心。他们必须将未来伙伴的信任看得比贡献的即时成本更重要。同样，各地的制度代理人必须抵制眼前的诱惑——收受贿赂、挪用资金或利用职务之便谋取私利——以维护自己的长期声誉。

回想一下珊瑚礁上的清洁鱼。这些鱼不断面临咬食粘液而非吃寄生虫的诱惑，为此放弃即时的营养利益以维护声誉并留住未来的客户。因此，清洁鱼表现出策略性的冲动控制，当客户在旁观且更可能寻找替代清洁鱼时，它们更能抵制诱惑。

人类的合作认知要精妙得多，但建立在相同的适应性原则之上。更有耐心的人在实验室实验中给予伙伴更多，也向慈善机构捐赠更多。我们的信任直觉追踪着耐心与合作之间的这种关联：当人们展现出自制力时，我们会评判他们为更有道德、更值得信赖。这或许可以解释为什么各个历史时期的社会都将日常诱惑——如食物、性、酒精和懒惰——道德化。近期的实验表明，更容易屈服于这些诱惑的人被视为更缺乏合作精神，因为人们认为这些享乐会削弱自制力。清教徒式的道德规范，似乎源自评估合作伙伴的同一套认知系统。

因此，合作建立在一种当下与未来的权衡之上。但什么会使天平倾向长期声誉呢？有两个因素尤为关键：物质安全和社会资本。

1970年，意大利创建了新的地区政府，拥有相同的权力、程序和预算。政治学家 Robert Putnam（罗伯特·帕特南）及其同事看到了一个独特的机会：同一份制度蓝图在截然不同的社会背景中实施。他们利用二十年的数据，通过12项指标——从预算执行的及时性到医疗服务的提供再到官僚系统的响应能力——追踪了意大利各地区的政府表现。

结果记录在《让民主运转起来》（Making Democracy Work, 1993）一书中，令人震惊。像 Emilia-Romagna（艾米利亚-罗马涅）这样的北部地区运作效率极高：快速响应公民诉求、政策制定富有创新、腐败问题很少。而像 Calabria（卡拉布里亚）这样的南部地区在同样的制度框架下举步维艰：迟缓、无响应、效率低下。

政府表现最强的预测因子是什么？是个人参与合唱团、体育俱乐部和公民社团的程度。公民一起做志愿服务、一起歌唱、一起组织活动的地区，其制度明显更好。最引人注目的是，19世纪80年代参与这些基层活动的情况，能够预测1978至1985年的政府表现。Putnam 用社会资本（social capital）来解释这种关联——即从悠久的公民参与历史中涌现出来的信任、社会网络和互惠规范。

这种模式远远超出了20世纪80年代的意大利。选举问责有助于解释这一点。在社会资本较高的地方，选民更一致地惩罚腐败或缺勤的政客。而且这不仅限于民主治理。在世界各地的渔业社区中，更强的社会纽带预示着社区是保护鱼类资源还是任其枯竭。

为什么社会资本如此重要？用当期成本和未来收益的框架来理解会有帮助。在关系紧密的社区中，人们预期将共同分享未来，一个受损的声誉会伴随你数十年。社会资本使未来的分量变得更重。从渔业社区到整个地区乃至国家，社会资本增加了长期声誉的价值，从而促进了制度性合作。

反过来说，物质安全使当前成本的感受变得更轻。当基本需求得到保障时，人们有余裕去思考超越眼前生存的问题。当你挣扎着付房租或养家糊口时，眼前的需求如此迫切，以至于较长期的考量便退隐到背景之中。一个勉强度日的渔民可能为了今晚养家而冒过度捕捞的风险；而一个富裕的渔民则有条件为下一季保存鱼获，并投资于与其他渔民的关系。

这解释了为什么制度表现与经济发展水平相关。虽然腐败的制度当然会损害经济增长，但经济困难也会加剧眼前压力，使腐败成为一种更合理的策略。因此，贫困率较高的地区往往腐败率也较高。

因此，制度可以被理解为一种社会技术。我们不断地在工程化它们，往往甚至没有意识到这一点。当邻居们组织起来维护一个共享的花园或游乐场时，他们会推选一个小委员会来管理资金和决策。这种安排之所以有效，是因为它将协调数十名贡献者的困难问题转化为了信任少数几位可见之人的简单问题——这些人可以因勤勉而受到称赞，也可以因滥用而被追责。

正如任何工具一样，制度不能凭空创造原本不存在的东西；它们只能放大已有的合作能力。制度建立在使合作成为理性选择的条件之上：物质安全和社会资本。当这些条件具备时，声誉就可以大规模地发挥作用。一层问责支撑着下一层，直到合作远远超越了熟悉程度的限制。从维系吸血蝙蝠和珊瑚礁清洁鱼的同一种力量中，我们建造了城市、市场和国家。制度，就是将信任扩展到数百万陌生人之间的方式。
articles.content_en:
Every human society, from the smallest village to the largest nation, faces the same fundamental challenge: how to get people to act in the interests of the collective rather than their own. Fishermen must limit their catch so fish stocks don't collapse. People must respect others' property and safety. Citizens must pay taxes to fund roads, schools and hospitals. Left to pure self-interest, no community could endure; the bonds of collective life would quickly unravel.

The solutions we've devised are remarkably similar across cultures and centuries. We create rules. Then we appoint guardians to enforce them. Those who break the rules are punished. But there's a problem with this approach, one that the Roman poet Juvenal identified nearly 2,000 years ago: Quis custodiet ipsos custodes? Who will guard the guards themselves?

Fisheries appoint monitors to prevent overfishing -- but what if the monitors accept bribes to look the other way? Police officers exist to protect everyone's property and safety -- but who ensures that they don't abuse their power? Governments collect taxes for public services -- but how do we stop officials from diverting the funds to their own accounts?

Every institution faces the same fundamental paradox. Institutions foster cooperation by rewarding good behaviour and punishing rule-breakers. Yet they themselves depend on cooperative members to function. We haven't solved the cooperation problem -- we've simply moved it back one step. So why do institutions work at all? To understand this puzzle, we need to first ask what makes human cooperation so extraordinary in the natural world.

Cooperation is everywhere in nature. Walk through any forest, peer into any tide pool, observe any meadow, and you'll witness countless partnerships that seem to defy the brutal logic of natural selection. Far from being mysterious, these alliances follow predictable patterns that evolutionary biologists have come to understand well. A handful of basic mechanisms explain cooperation from ant colonies to coral reefs: kinship, reciprocity and reputation.

Consider the bustling world of an ant colony. Thousands of workers toil tirelessly, some tending larvae, others foraging for food, still others defending the nest from invaders. Some take cooperation to shocking extremes: certain ants have heads so enlarged and flattened they can barely move, spending their lives as living doors by using their disc-shaped skulls to seal nest entrances. Even more dramatically, when minor workers of an exploding ant species are threatened, they literally rupture their own bodies, spraying attackers with toxic, sticky compounds. Why would workers sacrifice everything for a colony they'll never lead? This extraordinary altruism is possible because the workers are typically sisters, often sharing substantial portions of their genes with each other. When they sacrifice for the colony, they're helping copies of their own genes survive. Evolutionary biologists call this kin selection -- altruistic behaviour among relatives makes genetic sense because it promotes the survival of shared genes.

Or take vampire bats (Desmodus rotundus). When a bat fails to find blood during its nightly hunt, it faces starvation. But roost-mates who fed successfully will regurgitate precious blood to save their starving neighbour -- not just family members, but unrelated partners. Why help a stranger who shares no more of your genes than any random bat? Because they can be expected to return the favour. Bats engage in reciprocal sharing, favouring those who have shared with them before. Reciprocity functions like social insurance: the bat who shared tonight can expect to be helped in the future, when they need it.

Then there are the cleaning stations scattered across coral reefs, where small cleaner fish (Labroides dimidiatus) remove parasites from larger client fish. The cleaners could easily bite off delicious mucus instead of eating boring parasites, but often they resist the temptation. Why cooperate with a fish from another species that is physically incapable of returning the favour? Because other clients are watching. When observers see a cleaner fish bite, they avoid the cheaters and seek out cooperative cleaners instead. Reputation determines future business.

Kin selection, reciprocity and reputation -- these three forces drive cooperation across the natural world. But each operates within significant constraints. Kin selection works only among relatives. Reciprocity requires repeated interactions between the same individuals. Reputation can function only in small groups, where your actions are easily observed and information about past behaviour spreads reliably.

Humans follow the same evolutionary rules, yet we've somehow pushed cooperation far beyond the natural reach of kinship, reciprocity and reputation. Every day, we trust countless strangers -- people who share no blood with us, whom we will never meet again, and who could easily betray that trust without anyone watching. We step into traffic assuming that drivers will stop. We eat food prepared by hands we'll never see. We entrust our savings to faceless banks, and our children to schools staffed by people we barely know. We board metal tubes that lift us into the sky, trusting that mechanics maintained the engines, that pilots are qualified, and that air-traffic controllers have cleared our path.
