Institutions help bridge this gap. Traffic laws and police keep streets orderly; food inspectors ensure meals are safe; courts enforce contracts; regulators watch over banks and airlines. By rewarding cooperation and sanctioning abuse, institutions allow trust to flow among strangers.

But this only shifts the question: if institutions guarantee cooperation, what guarantees their own proper functioning? Who guards the guardians?

The answer is reputation. The community itself ensures institutional integrity through the same social forces that sustain cooperation among strangers. The guarded guard the guardians.

Consider how villages across Japan's mountainous terrain managed their communal forests during the Tokugawa era. These shared woodlands provided timber for construction, thatch for roofing, fodder for horses, fertiliser, and the firewood and charcoal essential for winter survival. They developed elaborate allocation rules: each household was assigned specific zones in annual rotation, with carefully timed mountain opening days when residents could harvest particular resources.

Crucially, they hired specialised monitors called detectives who patrolled the commons on horseback, watching for unauthorised users. Detectives could demand cash and sake from violators, while the village imposed escalating penalties -- including banishment in extreme cases. Without these monitors, all the elaborate rules could be easily circumvented. In vast forests with scattered households busy with their own work, who could possibly observe whether someone was breaking the mountain closure or harvesting out of season?

This solution might seem to simply move the problem back one step -- who ensures the detectives themselves don't become corrupt? But this apparent displacement is actually a major advance. There were few detectives, their actions were visible, and their mission clear. Where it was nearly impossible to monitor dozens of villagers across vast terrain, it became much simpler to ensure the honesty of a few highly visible monitors. In other words, the community had not simply added a rule -- it had designed a genuine social technology. By introducing the role of detective, it transformed an impossible problem (preventing forest overuse) into a simpler one (ensuring the honesty of a few visible detectives). And this simpler problem could be solved through a well-known mechanism: reputation.

My colleagues and I developed a mathematical model to formalise this idea. We began with what game theorists call a repeated trust game. In a large community, people regularly encounter others they don't know well, and must decide whether to trust them. Those who are trusted then face their own choice: reciprocate that trust -- at a cost -- or cheat. Only some of these decisions are observed, creating reputations: imperfect track records of past behaviour.

This captures the basic logic of reputation-based cooperation. Those being trusted face a trade-off. Cheat now for a quick benefit, or pay the cost of reciprocation and build a reputation that secures future trust. Others, in turn, rely on those reputations when deciding whether to trust.

But this simple setup also shows why cooperation often breaks down. Reputation works only when the temptation to defect isn't too strong and behaviour is sufficiently observable. Once the likelihood of observation falls below a certain threshold, reputation can no longer sustain reciprocation, and cooperation collapses. This was the impossible problem facing the Japanese villagers. They could declare the mountains closed but, in vast forests with scattered households, no one could see who was sneaking timber out of season.

This is where my colleagues and I introduce an institution. In the model, individuals can now contribute resources to an institution that pools and amplifies these contributions, using them to monitor the trust game and punish cheaters. This changes the rules of the original trust game, making behaviour easier to observe and cheating more costly. Two conditions must hold for this to work. First, contributions must be cheap and visible enough that reputation alone can sustain them. Second, the institution must efficiently amplify these contributions, generating incentives strong enough to make cooperation possible in the original trust game.

Institutions, in this sense, are social technologies. A lever doesn't create force from nothing -- it amplifies the force you apply. A well-designed institution doesn't abolish the cooperation problem; it redesigns it. In our model, the institution must generate strong-enough incentives to unlock cooperation in the trust game, while resting on another form of cooperation: contribution. When the institution is efficient, it can produce these incentives from contributions cheap enough for reputation to sustain. This is what dissolves Juvenal's paradox. There's no infinite regress, because the easier problem can be solved by reputation alone.

The Japanese villagers engineered one version of this principle. They are far from alone. In Governing the Commons (1990), the Nobel Prize-winner Elinor Ostrom traced how communities around the world managed to keep shared forests, fisheries and irrigation systems thriving for centuries, drawing out the design principles behind their success -- including the use of accountable monitors. From Japanese mountain settlements to Swiss alpine cooperatives and Philippine irrigation societies, long-lived commons required appointing people whose vigilance the community could judge and reward. As Ostrom notes: 'The individual who finds a rule-infractor gains status and prestige for being a good protector of the commons,' while those who slack off 'can be fired easily if discovered'. By appointing accountable monitors, these communities transformed the cooperation of the invisible many into that of a visible few.

Modern societies scale this principle through nested layers of accountability. Consider aviation: mechanics maintain planes, supervisors check their work, airline safety departments oversee supervisors, national authorities audit airlines, and elected officials ultimately answer for those authorities. At each level, the number of people requiring oversight shrinks while their actions become more visible. We trust our lives to this cascade of accountability -- from thousands of unseen mechanics to a handful of public officials whose failures make headlines. The same architecture repeats across modern life, transforming vast networks of potential distrust into manageable chains of oversight.

Institutions can dramatically extend the scale of cooperation when they're well-designed. But as the economist Deirdre McCloskey observes, institutions are not a silver bullet. As she writes in Bourgeois Equality (2016):

You can set up British-style courts of law, and even provide the barristers with wigs, but if the judges are venal and the barristers have no professional pride and if the public disdains them both, then the introduction of such a nice-sounding institution will fail to improve the rule of law.

Institutions cannot escape the problem of cooperation -- they can only redesign it. The cascading levels of oversight that make our planes safe cannot function without mechanics who care about their supervisor's judgments, officials attentive to public judgment, and citizens willing to hold them accountable. Likewise, the justice system requires integrity at every level. No institution can conjure cooperation out of thin air. It has to already exist.

Consider one of history's most celebrated institutional designs: the Constitution of the United States. James Madison and the other Framers crafted a system of checks and balances to prevent tyranny, dividing power among three independent branches of government. In Federalist 51, Madison explained the logic: 'Ambition must be made to counteract ambition.' Each branch would have the means and the motive to defend its own authority, so that power would restrain power.

Yet no constitution, however ingenious, can specify every contingency, and any rule can be interpreted to one's advantage. As the political scientists Steven Levitsky and Daniel Ziblatt observe in How Democracies Die (2018), US democracy rests on unwritten norms that prevent ordinary political competition from descending into all-out warfare. Political rivals must cooperate through mutual toleration -- accepting opponents as legitimate competitors rather than existential threats -- and institutional forbearance, choosing not to exploit every legal advantage.

History shows that these norms are far from automatic. In the early Republic, John Adams's Federalist Party and Thomas Jefferson's Democratic-Republicans viewed each other as existential threats. Federalists attacked Jefferson as a godless Jacobin who would unleash French Revolution-style terror, while Republicans denounced Federalists as monarchists plotting to restore British tyranny. In 1798, the Federalist Congress passed the Alien and Sedition Acts, criminalising criticism of the government. Under these laws, Federalist prosecutors jailed newspaper editors and even a Republican congressman for opposing the president John Adams.

The 1800 election between Adams and Jefferson pushed the country to the brink. Exploiting a technicality in the original electoral system (fixed by the 12th Amendment), the House of Representatives, run by the Federalists, sought to block Jefferson's victory. Armed conflict seemed possible: Republican governors prepared militias, while a Federalist newspaper boasted that any uprising would be crushed with Massachusetts troops. Yet Adams chose to leave office peacefully and Jefferson took his place as president. Jefferson later described this decision as a revolution, comparable to that of 1776. Democracy survived -- barely -- not because the Constitution mandated it, but because leaders chose restraint.

And cooperation between political parties is just the beginning. Democratic institutions require cooperation at every level: impartial judges, honest bureaucrats, restrained legislators, and citizens who value integrity enough to hold leaders accountable. Countries where citizens show more intrinsic honesty -- the willingness to cooperate even without institutional incentives -- consistently have less corrupt institutions. Institutions work best precisely where they're needed least, in societies with strong cooperative norms.

Institutions, then, are not magic. They depend on the people they govern, because they can amplify only existing cooperative tendencies. Yet some societies are more cooperative than others. What accounts for these differences?

The necessary ingredient is patience. Institutions work by transforming a hard cooperation problem into an easier one that reputation can solve. But people still have to be willing to engage in reputation-based cooperation, which rests on a present-future trade-off.

In our model, individuals literally have to be patient. They have to value the trust of future partners more than the immediate costs of contribution. Likewise, institutional agents everywhere must resist immediate temptations -- accepting bribes, diverting funds, or exploiting their position for personal gain -- to preserve their long-term standing.

Consider again the coral reef cleaner wrasse. These fish face constant temptation to bite off mucus instead of eating parasites, forgoing immediate nutritional benefits to maintain their reputation and secure future clients. As a result, cleaner fish display strategic impulse control, resisting temptation more when clients are watching and more likely to find alternative cleaners.

Human cooperative cognition is much more sophisticated, but rests on the same adaptive principles. More patient people give more to partners in laboratory experiments, and give more to charity. Our trust intuitions track this connection between patience and cooperation: we judge people as more moral and trustworthy when they demonstrate self-control. This may explain why societies across history have moralised everyday temptations such as food, sex, alcohol, and idleness. Recent experiments show that people who give in more easily to such temptations are seen as less cooperative, because those pleasures are thought to sap self-control. Puritanical moral codes, it seems, emerge from the same cognitive systems that evaluate cooperative partners.

Cooperation, then, rests on a present-future trade-off. But what shifts the calculus towards long-term reputation? Two factors prove especially crucial: material security and social capital.

In 1970, Italy created new regional governments with identical powers, procedures and budgets. The political scientist Robert Putnam and his colleagues saw a unique opportunity: the same institutional blueprint implemented across vastly different social contexts. Drawing on two decades of data, they tracked the performance of Italy's regions using 12 indicators from budget promptness to healthcare delivery to bureaucratic responsiveness.

The results, described in Making Democracy Work (1993), were stark. Northern regions like Emilia-Romagna operated with remarkable efficiency: quick to respond to citizen requests, innovative in policy-making, largely free of corruption. Southern regions like Calabria struggled with the same institutional framework: slow, unresponsive, plagued by inefficiency.

The strongest predictor of government performance? Individual participation in choral societies, sports clubs, and civic associations. Regions where citizens volunteered together, sang together, and organised together had dramatically better institutions. Most remarkably, participation in these grassroots activities in the 1890s predicted government performance in 1978-1985. Putnam explained this association in terms of social capital -- the trust, social networks and norms of reciprocity that emerge from a long history of civic engagement.

This pattern extends well beyond 1980s Italy. Electoral accountability helps explain why. Where social capital is high, voters more consistently punish corrupt or absentee politicians. And it's not limited to democratic governance. In fishing communities worldwide, stronger social ties predict whether communities preserve their fish stocks or allow them to collapse.

Why does social capital matter so much? It helps to think in terms of present costs and future benefits. In tight-knit communities where people expect to share a future together, a damaged reputation follows you around for decades. Social capital makes the future loom larger. From fishing communities to entire regions and countries, social capital increases the value of long-term reputation, facilitating institutional cooperation.

Conversely, material security makes present costs matter less. When basic needs are secure, people can afford to think beyond immediate survival. When you're struggling to make rent or feed your family, immediate needs loom so large that longer-term considerations fade into the background. A struggling fisherman may risk overfishing to feed his family tonight; a prosperous one can afford to preserve stocks for next season and invest in relationships with other fishermen.

This explains why institutional performance correlates with economic development. While corrupt institutions certainly harm economic growth, economic hardship also heightens immediate pressures, making corruption a more sensible strategy. As a result, areas with higher poverty tend to have higher corruption rates.

Institutions can thus be understood as social technologies. We engineer them constantly, often without realising it. When neighbours organise to maintain a shared garden or playground, they appoint a small committee to manage funds and decisions. The arrangement works because it transforms the hard problem of coordinating dozens of contributors into the easier problem of trusting a few visible people who can be praised for diligence or blamed for misuse.

paragraphs.cn: 每一个人类社会，从最小的村庄到最大的国家，都面临同一个根本性挑战：如何让人们为了集体利益而非个人私利行动。渔民必须限制捕捞量，以免鱼类资源枯竭。人们必须尊重他人的财产和人身安全。公民必须纳税以修建道路、学校和医院。若任由纯粹的自私本能驱使，任何社区都无法存续；集体生活的纽带将迅速瓦解。

Fishermen must limit their catch so fish stocks don't collapse. People must respect others' property and safety. Citizens must pay taxes to fund roads, schools and hospitals. paragraphs.en: Fisheries appoint monitors to prevent overfishing -- but what if the monitors accept bribes to look the other way? Police officers exist to protect everyone's property and safety -- but who ensures that they don't abuse their power? Governments collect taxes for public services -- but how do we stop officials from diverting the funds to their own accounts?

paragraphs.cn: 每一个制度都面临同样的根本悖论。制度通过奖励良好行为和惩罚违规者来促进合作。然而制度自身的运转又依赖于成员的合作。我们并没有解决合作问题——只是把它向后退了一步。那么制度为何能发挥作用？要理解这个谜题，我们需要先弄清楚，人类的合作在自然界中究竟有何非凡之处。

Yet they themselves depend on cooperative members to function. We haven't solved the cooperation problem -- we've simply moved it back one step. So why do institutions work at all? To understand this puzzle, we need to first ask what makes human cooperation so extraordinary in the natural world.

paragraphs.cn: 合作在自然界中无处不在。漫步于任何森林，凝视任何潮汐水洼，观察任何一片草甸，你都会目睹无数似乎违背自然选择残酷逻辑的合作关系。这些联盟并非神秘莫测，而是遵循进化生物学家已经充分理解的、可预测的模式。少数几个基本机制就能解释从蚁群到珊瑚礁的合作现象：亲缘选择、互惠利他和声誉。
paragraphs.en: Cooperation is everywhere in nature. Walk through any forest, peer into any tide pool, observe any meadow, and you'll witness countless partnerships that seem to defy the brutal logic of natural selection. Far from being mysterious, these alliances follow predictable patterns that evolutionary biologists have come to understand well. A handful of basic mechanisms explain cooperation from ant colonies to coral reefs: kinship, reciprocity and reputation.

paragraphs.cn: 想象一个繁忙的蚁群世界。数千只工蚁不知疲倦地劳作，有的照料幼虫，有的外出觅食，还有的守卫巢穴抵御入侵者。有些蚂蚁的合作行为达到了惊人的极端：某些工蚁的头部异常膨大且扁平，几乎无法移动，它们终生充当活的门板，用圆盘状的颅骨封堵巢穴入口。更加戏剧性的是，当一种爆炸蚁的工蚁受到威胁时，它们会真正撕裂自己的身体，向攻击者喷射有毒的粘性化合物。为什么工蚁会为一个自己永远无法统领的蚁群牺牲一切？这种非凡的利他行为之所以可能，是因为工蚁通常是姐妹，彼此之间共享相当大比例的基因。当它们为蚁群牺牲时，它们实际上在帮助自己基因的复本存活下去。进化生物学家将此称为亲缘选择（kin selection）——亲属之间的利他行为在遗传学上是合理的，因为它促进了共有基因的存活。
paragraphs.en: Consider the bustling world of an ant colony. Thousands of workers toil tirelessly, some tending larvae, others foraging for food, still others defending the nest from invaders. Some take cooperation to shocking extremes: certain ants have heads so enlarged and flattened they can barely move, spending their lives as living doors by using their disc-shaped skulls to seal nest entrances. Even more dramatically, when minor workers of an exploding ant species are threatened, they literally rupture their own bodies, spraying attackers with toxic, sticky compounds. Why would workers sacrifice everything for a colony they'll never lead? This extraordinary altruism is possible because the workers are typically sisters, often sharing substantial portions of their genes with each other. When they sacrifice for the colony, they're helping copies of their own genes survive. Evolutionary biologists call this kin selection -- altruistic behaviour among relatives makes genetic sense because it promotes the survival of shared genes.

paragraphs.cn: 再看看吸血蝙蝠（Desmodus rotundus，普通吸血蝠）。当一只蝙蝠在夜间狩猎中未能找到血液时，它面临饥饿的威胁。但成功进食的同栖伙伴会反刍出宝贵的血液来拯救挨饿的邻居——不仅是亲属，还包括无血缘关系的伙伴。为什么要帮助一只与你基因共享程度不比随便一只蝙蝠多的陌生者？因为对方有望回报这份恩惠。蝙蝠实行互惠分享，优先帮助那些之前分享过食物的伙伴。互惠机制如同社会保险：今晚分享血液的蝙蝠，在未来自己需要时可以期待获得帮助。
paragraphs.en: Or take vampire bats (Desmodus rotundus). When a bat fails to find blood during its nightly hunt, it faces starvation. But roost-mates who fed successfully will regurgitate precious blood to save their starving neighbour -- not just family members, but unrelated partners. Why help a stranger who shares no more of your genes than any random bat? Because they can be expected to return the favour. Bats engage in reciprocal sharing, favouring those who have shared with them before. Reciprocity functions like social insurance: the bat who shared tonight can expect to be helped in the future, when they need it.

paragraphs.cn: 还有散布在珊瑚礁上的清洁站，小型清洁鱼（Labroides dimidiatus，裂唇鱼）在那里为较大的客户鱼清除寄生虫。清洁鱼完全可以咬下美味的粘液而不是吃无聊的寄生虫，但它们通常会抵制这种诱惑。为什么要与一种根本没有能力回报的异种鱼类合作？因为其他客户鱼在旁观。当观察者看到清洁鱼咬食粘液时，它们会避开作弊者，转而寻找合作的清洁鱼。声誉决定了未来的生意。
paragraphs.en: Then there are the cleaning stations scattered across coral reefs, where small cleaner fish (Labroides dimidiatus) remove parasites from larger client fish. The cleaners could easily bite off delicious mucus instead of eating boring parasites, but often they resist the temptation. Why cooperate with a fish from another species that is physically incapable of returning the favour? Because other clients are watching. When observers see a cleaner fish bite, they avoid the cheaters and seek out cooperative cleaners instead. Reputation determines future business.

paragraphs.cn: 亲缘选择、互惠利他和声誉——这三种力量驱动着整个自然界的合作。但每一种都受到显著约束。亲缘选择仅在亲属之间有效。互惠利他需要相同个体之间反复互动。声誉则只在小型群体中才能发挥作用，在那里你的行为容易被观察，关于过去行为的信息能够可靠传播。
paragraphs.en: Kin selection, reciprocity and reputation -- these three forces drive cooperation across the natural world. But each operates within significant constraints. Kin selection works only among relatives. Reciprocity requires repeated interactions between the same individuals. Reputation can function only in small groups, where your actions are easily observed and information about past behaviour spreads reliably.

paragraphs.cn: 人类遵循同样的进化规则，但我们却不知怎地将合作推展到了远远超越亲缘、互惠和声誉自然边界的地方。每一天，我们信任无数陌生人——与我们没有血缘关系的人，我们永远不会再见的人，以及在没有旁观者的情况下可以轻易背叛这种信任的人。我们走进车流，假定司机们会停下来。我们吃着我们永远不会看到其制作过程的人所准备的食物。我们把积蓄托付给面目模糊的银行，把孩子交给我们几乎不认识的人经营的学校。我们登上将我们送上天空的金属管道，相信机械师维护了引擎，飞行员是合格的，空中交通管制员已经为我们清除了航路。
paragraphs.en: Humans follow the same evolutionary rules, yet we've somehow pushed cooperation far beyond the natural reach of kinship, reciprocity and reputation. Every day, we trust countless strangers -- people who share no blood with us, whom we will never meet again, and who could easily betray that trust without anyone watching. We step into traffic assuming that drivers will stop. We eat food prepared by hands we'll never see. We entrust our savings to faceless banks, and our children to schools staffed by people we barely know. We board metal tubes that lift us into the sky, trusting that mechanics maintained the engines, that pilots are qualified, and that air-traffic controllers have cleared our path.

paragraphs.cn: 制度有助于弥合这一鸿沟。交通法规和警察维持街道秩序；食品检查员确保餐饮安全；法院执行合同；监管机构监督银行和航空公司。通过奖励合作和制裁滥用行为，制度使信任得以在陌生人之间流动。
paragraphs.en: Institutions help bridge this gap. Traffic laws and police keep streets orderly; food inspectors ensure meals are safe; courts enforce contracts; regulators watch over banks and airlines. By rewarding cooperation and sanctioning abuse, institutions allow trust to flow among strangers.

paragraphs.cn: 但这只是转移了问题：如果制度保证了合作，那么谁来保证制度自身的正常运转？谁来监护监护者？
paragraphs.en: But this only shifts the question: if institutions guarantee cooperation, what guarantees their own proper functioning? Who guards the guardians?

paragraphs.cn: 答案是声誉。社区本身通过维系陌生人之间合作的同样社会力量来确保制度的完整性。被监护者监护着监护者。
paragraphs.en: The answer is reputation. The community itself ensures institutional integrity through the same social forces that sustain cooperation among strangers. The guarded guard the guardians.

paragraphs.cn: 看看日本德川时代各地山村如何管理他们的共有森林。这些共享林地提供建筑用木材、屋顶用茅草、马匹用饲料、肥料，以及冬季生存必不可少的柴火和木炭。他们发展出精细的分配规则：每户按年度轮换被分配特定的区域，并规定了精心安排的开山日，居民可以在那天采伐特定的资源。
paragraphs.en: Consider how villages across Japan's mountainous terrain managed their communal forests during the Tokugawa era. These shared woodlands provided timber for construction, thatch for roofing, fodder for horses, fertiliser, and the firewood and charcoal essential for winter survival. They developed elaborate allocation rules: each household was assigned specific zones in annual rotation, with carefully timed mountain opening days when residents could harvest particular resources.

paragraphs.cn: 关键是，他们雇佣了被称为"侦探"的专业监督员，骑马巡逻公共土地，监视未经授权的使用者。侦探可以向违规者索要现金和清酒，村庄则施加逐级加重的惩罚——极端情况下包括驱逐出境。没有这些监督员，所有精心设计的规则都很容易被规避。在广袤的森林中，分散的住户忙于各自的事务，谁能观察到是否有人违反封山令或在非采伐季节盗伐？
paragraphs.en: Crucially, they hired specialised monitors called detectives who patrolled the commons on horseback, watching for unauthorised users. Detectives could demand cash and sake from violators, while the village imposed escalating penalties -- including banishment in extreme cases. Without these monitors, all the elaborate rules could be easily circumvented. In vast forests with scattered households busy with their own work, who could possibly observe whether someone was breaking the mountain closure or harvesting out of season?

paragraphs.cn: 这个解决方案看起来似乎只是将问题向后推了一步——谁来确保侦探本身不会腐败？但这种表面的位移实际上是一个重大进步。侦探人数不多，他们的行为是可见的，他们的使命是明确的。在广袤地形上监测数十名村民几乎不可能，而确保少数几位高度可见的监督员的诚实则简单得多。换言之，社区并非仅仅增加了一条规则——它设计了一种真正的社会技术。通过引入侦探这一角色，它将一个不可能的问题（防止森林过度使用）转化为了一个更简单的问题（确保少数几位可见的侦探的诚实）。而这个更简单的问题可以通过一种众所周知的机制来解决：声誉。
paragraphs.en: This solution might seem to simply move the problem back one step -- who ensures the detectives themselves don't become corrupt? But this apparent displacement is actually a major advance. There were few detectives, their actions were visible, and their mission clear. Where it was nearly impossible to monitor dozens of villagers across vast terrain, it became much simpler to ensure the honesty of a few highly visible monitors. In other words, the community had not simply added a rule -- it had designed a genuine social technology. By introducing the role of detective, it transformed an impossible problem (preventing forest overuse) into a simpler one (ensuring the honesty of a few visible detectives). And this simpler problem could be solved through a well-known mechanism: reputation.

paragraphs.cn: 我和同事开发了一个数学模型来将这一想法形式化。我们从博弈论者所说的重复信任博弈开始。在一个大型社区中，人们经常遇到不太了解的人，必须决定是否信任他们。被信任的人随后面临自己的选择：以付出代价的方式回报这份信任，或者欺骗。这些决策中只有一部分会被观察到，从而形成声誉：关于过去行为的不完美记录。
paragraphs.en: My colleagues and I developed a mathematical model to formalise this idea. We began with what game theorists call a repeated trust game. In a large community, people regularly encounter others they don't know well, and must decide whether to trust them. Those who are trusted then face their own choice: reciprocate that trust -- at a cost -- or cheat. Only some of these decisions are observed, creating reputations: imperfect track records of past behaviour.

paragraphs.cn: 这捕捉了基于声誉合作的基本逻辑。被信任的人面临权衡：现在欺骗以获取即时利益，还是付出回报的代价来建立一个能确保未来获得信任的声誉。而其他人则依据这些声誉来决定是否给予信任。
paragraphs.en: This captures the basic logic of reputation-based cooperation. Those being trusted face a trade-off. Cheat now for a quick benefit, or pay the cost of reciprocation and build a reputation that secures future trust. Others, in turn, rely on those reputations when deciding whether to trust.

paragraphs.cn: 但这个简单的设定也展示了合作为何经常崩溃。声誉只有在作弊诱惑不太强烈且行为足够可观察的情况下才有效。一旦观察的可能性低于某个阈值，声誉就无法再维持回报行为，合作随之瓦解。这正是日本村民面临的那个不可能的问题。他们可以宣布封山，但在广袤的森林中，住户分散各地，没人能看到谁在违规季节偷伐木材。
paragraphs.en: But this simple setup also shows why cooperation often breaks down. Reputation works only when the temptation to defect isn't too strong and behaviour is sufficiently observable. Once the likelihood of observation falls below a certain threshold, reputation can no longer sustain reciprocation, and cooperation collapses. This was the impossible problem facing the Japanese villagers. They could declare the mountains closed but, in vast forests with scattered households, no one could see who was sneaking timber out of season.

paragraphs.cn: 这就是我和同事引入制度的环节。在模型中，个体现在可以向一个制度贡献资源，该制度汇集并放大这些贡献，用它们来监测信任博弈并惩罚作弊者。这改变了原始信任博弈的规则，使行为更容易被观察，作弊的代价更高。要做到这一点，必须满足两个条件。第一，贡献的成本必须足够低且可见，使声誉仅凭自身就能维持它们。第二，制度必须高效地放大这些贡献，产生足够强的激励，使原始信任博弈中的合作成为可能。
paragraphs.en: This is where my colleagues and I introduce an institution. In the model, individuals can now contribute resources to an institution that pools and amplifies these contributions, using them to monitor the trust game and punish cheaters. This changes the rules of the original trust game, making behaviour easier to observe and cheating more costly. Two conditions must hold for this to work. First, contributions must be cheap and visible enough that reputation alone can sustain them. Second, the institution must efficiently amplify these contributions, generating incentives strong enough to make cooperation possible in the original trust game.

paragraphs.cn: 在这个意义上，制度是一种社会技术。杠杆不会凭空创造力量——它放大你施加的力量。一个设计良好的制度不会废除合作问题；它重新设计这个问题。在我们的模型中，制度必须产生足够强的激励来解锁信任博弈中的合作，同时依赖另一种形式的合作：贡献。当制度高效时，它能从成本足够低（使声誉得以维持）的贡献中产生这些激励。这正是化解 Juvenal 悖论的关键所在。不存在无限倒退，因为那个更简单的问题可以仅凭声誉来解决。
paragraphs.en: Institutions, in this sense, are social technologies. A lever doesn't create force from nothing -- it amplifies the force you apply. A well-designed institution doesn't abolish the cooperation problem; it redesigns it. In our model, the institution must generate strong-enough incentives to unlock cooperation in the trust game, while resting on another form of cooperation: contribution. When the institution is efficient, it can produce these incentives from contributions cheap enough for reputation to sustain. This is what dissolves Juvenal's paradox. There's no infinite regress, because the easier problem can be solved by reputation alone.

paragraphs.cn: 日本村民设计了这一原则的一个版本。他们绝非孤例。在《公共事务的治理之道》（Governing the Commons, 1990）中，诺贝尔奖得主 Elinor Ostrom（埃莉诺·奥斯特罗姆）追踪了世界各地社区如何成功地使共享的森林、渔场和灌溉系统繁荣数百年，并提炼出成功背后的设计原则——包括使用可问责的监督员。从日本的山村到瑞士阿尔卑斯山的合作社再到菲律宾的灌溉协会，长寿的公共资源管理体系都需要任命其警惕性可由社区评判和奖赏的人。正如 Ostrom 所指出的，发现违规者的人会因作为公共资源的优秀守护者而获得地位和声望，而那些偷懒懈怠的人一旦被发现就会被轻易解雇。通过任命可问责的监督员，这些社区将不可见的多数人的合作转化为了可见的少数人的合作。
paragraphs.en: The Japanese villagers engineered one version of this principle. They are far from alone. In Governing the Commons (1990), the Nobel Prize-winner Elinor Ostrom traced how communities around the world managed to keep shared forests, fisheries and irrigation systems thriving for centuries, drawing out the design principles behind their success -- including the use of accountable monitors. From Japanese mountain settlements to Swiss alpine cooperatives and Philippine irrigation societies, long-lived commons required appointing people whose vigilance the community could judge and reward. As Ostrom notes: 'The individual who finds a rule-infractor gains status and prestige for being a good protector of the commons,' while those who slack off 'can be fired easily if discovered'. By appointing accountable monitors, these communities transformed the cooperation of the invisible many into that of a visible few.

paragraphs.cn: 现代社会通过嵌套的问责层级来扩展这一原则。以航空业为例：机械师维护飞机，主管检查他们的工作，航空公司安全部门监督主管，国家主管部门审计航空公司，民选官员最终为这些主管部门负责。在每一个层级，需要被监督的人数在减少，而他们的行为变得更加可见。我们将生命托付给这道问责的瀑布——从数千名看不见的机械师到少数几位失职会成为头条新闻的公共官员。同样的架构在现代生活的方方面面不断重复，将庞大的潜在不信任网络转化为可管理的监督链条。
paragraphs.en: Modern societies scale this principle through nested layers of accountability. Consider aviation: mechanics maintain planes, supervisors check their work, airline safety departments oversee supervisors, national authorities audit airlines, and elected officials ultimately answer for those authorities. At each level, the number of people requiring oversight shrinks while their actions become more visible. We trust our lives to this cascade of accountability -- from thousands of unseen mechanics to a handful of public officials whose failures make headlines. The same architecture repeats across modern life, transforming vast networks of potential distrust into manageable chains of oversight.

paragraphs.cn: 当制度设计良好时，它们可以极大地扩展合作的规模。但正如经济学家 Deirdre McCloskey（迪尔德丽·麦克洛斯基）所指出的，制度并非灵丹妙药。她在《资产阶级平等》（Bourgeois Equality, 2016）一书中写道：你可以设立英式法庭，甚至给律师们配上假发，但如果法官贪污受贿、律师毫无职业操守，而公众又对两者都嗤之以鼻，那么引入这样一个听起来不错的制度根本无法改善法治。
paragraphs.en: Institutions can dramatically extend the scale of cooperation when they're well-designed. But as the economist Deirdre McCloskey observes, institutions are not a silver bullet. As she writes in Bourgeois Equality (2016):

paragraphs.cn: 制度无法逃脱合作问题——它只能重新设计它。使我们的飞机安全的那层层叠叠的监督机制，如果缺少了在乎主管评价的机械师、关注公众评判的官员以及愿意追究他们责任的公民，就无法运转。同样，司法系统在每个层面都需要正直。没有任何制度能凭空变出合作。它必须已经存在。
paragraphs.en: You can set up British-style courts of law, and even provide the barristers with wigs, but if the judges are venal and the barristers have no professional pride and if the public disdains them both, then the introduction of such a nice-sounding institution will fail to improve the rule of law.

paragraphs.cn: 想想历史上最受推崇的制度设计之一：美国宪法。James Madison（詹姆斯·麦迪逊）和其他制宪者设计了一套制衡体系以防止暴政，将权力分属三个独立的政府分支。在《联邦党人文集》第51篇中，Madison 解释了其中的逻辑：野心必须用来对抗野心。每个分支都将拥有捍卫自身权威的手段和动机，从而使权力制约权力。
paragraphs.en: Institutions cannot escape the problem of cooperation -- they can only redesign it. The cascading levels of oversight that make our planes safe cannot function without mechanics who care about their supervisor's judgments, officials attentive to public judgment, and citizens willing to hold them accountable. Likewise, the justice system requires integrity at every level. No institution can conjure cooperation out of thin air. It has to already exist.

paragraphs.cn: 然而，再精妙的宪法也无法预见每一个偶然事件，任何规则都可能被按照有利于自己的方式解读。正如政治学家 Steven Levitsky（史蒂文·莱维茨基）和 Daniel Ziblatt（丹尼尔·齐布拉特）在《民主是如何死亡的》（How Democracies Die, 2018）一书中所观察到的，美国的民主依赖于不成文的规范，这些规范防止普通的政治竞争沦为全面的战争。政治对手必须通过相互宽容来合作——接受对手为合法的竞争者而非生存威胁——以及制度克制，选择不去利用每一个法律上的优势。
paragraphs.en: Consider one of history's most celebrated institutional designs: the Constitution of the United States. James Madison and the other Framers crafted a system of checks and balances to prevent tyranny, dividing power among three independent branches of government. In Federalist 51, Madison explained the logic: 'Ambition must be made to counteract ambition.' Each branch would have the means and the motive to defend its own authority, so that power would restrain power.

paragraphs.cn: 历史表明，这些规范远非自然而然形成的。在建国初期，John Adams（约翰·亚当斯）的联邦党与 Thomas Jefferson（托马斯·杰斐逊）的民主共和党将彼此视为生存威胁。联邦党人攻击 Jefferson 是一个不信上帝的雅各宾派，会释放法国大革命式的恐怖，而共和党人则谴责联邦党人是密谋恢复英国暴政的君主主义者。1798年，联邦党控制的国会通过了《外侨及煽动叛乱法案》，将批评政府定为犯罪。在这些法律下，联邦党检察官逮捕了报纸编辑，甚至逮捕了一位反对总统 Adams 的共和党国会议员。
paragraphs.en: Yet no constitution, however ingenious, can specify every contingency, and any rule can be interpreted to one's advantage. As the political scientists Steven Levitsky and Daniel Ziblatt observe in How Democracies Die (2018), US democracy rests on unwritten norms that prevent ordinary political competition from descending into all-out warfare. Political rivals must cooperate through mutual toleration -- accepting opponents as legitimate competitors rather than existential threats -- and institutional forbearance, choosing not to exploit every legal advantage.

paragraphs.cn: 1800年 Adams 与 Jefferson 之间的选举将国家推向了悬崖边缘。联邦党人控制的众议院利用原始选举制度中的一个技术性漏洞（后来由第十二修正案修复），试图阻止 Jefferson 获胜。武装冲突似乎迫在眉睫：共和党州长准备了民兵，而一家联邦党报纸扬言任何起义都将被马萨诸塞州的军队镇压。然而 Adams 选择和平离任，Jefferson 接任总统。Jefferson 后来将这一决定描述为一场可与1776年革命相媲美的革命。民主存活了下来——险之又险——不是因为宪法要求如此，而是因为领导人选择了克制。
paragraphs.en: History shows that these norms are far from automatic. In the early Republic, John Adams's Federalist Party and Thomas Jefferson's Democratic-Republicans viewed each other as existential threats. Federalists attacked Jefferson as a godless Jacobin who would unleash French Revolution-style terror, while Republicans denounced Federalists as monarchists plotting to restore British tyranny. In 1798, the Federalist Congress passed the Alien and Sedition Acts, criminalising criticism of the government. Under these laws, Federalist prosecutors jailed newspaper editors and even a Republican congressman for opposing the president John Adams.

paragraphs.cn: 政党之间的合作还仅仅是开始。民主制度在每一个层面都需要合作：公正的法官、诚实的官僚、克制的立法者，以及足够重视正直品质并愿意追究领导人责任的公民。公民内在诚实度更高的国家——即使没有制度激励也愿意合作的国家——始终拥有腐败更少的制度。制度恰恰在最不需要它们的社会中运作得最好，也就是那些拥有强有力合作规范的社会。
paragraphs.en: The 1800 election between Adams and Jefferson pushed the country to the brink. Exploiting a technicality in the original electoral system (fixed by the 12th Amendment), the House of Representatives, run by the Federalists, sought to block Jefferson's victory. Armed conflict seemed possible: Republican governors prepared militias, while a Federalist newspaper boasted that any uprising would be crushed with Massachusetts troops. Yet Adams chose to leave office peacefully and Jefferson took his place as president. Jefferson later described this decision as a revolution, comparable to that of 1776. Democracy survived -- barely -- not because the Constitution mandated it, but because leaders chose restraint.

paragraphs.cn: 因此，制度并非魔法。它们依赖于其所治理的人，因为它们只能放大已有的合作倾向。然而有些社会比其他社会更具合作性。是什么造成了这些差异？
paragraphs.en: And cooperation between political parties is just the beginning. Democratic institutions require cooperation at every level: impartial judges, honest bureaucrats, restrained legislators, and citizens who value integrity enough to hold leaders accountable. Countries where citizens show more intrinsic honesty -- the willingness to cooperate even without institutional incentives -- consistently have less corrupt institutions. Institutions work best precisely where they're needed least, in societies with strong cooperative norms.

paragraphs.cn: 必要的要素是耐心。制度通过将一个困难的合作问题转化为一个声誉可以解决的更简单的问题来发挥作用。但人们仍然必须愿意参与基于声誉的合作，而这建立在一种当下与未来的权衡之上。
paragraphs.en: Institutions, then, are not magic. They depend on the people they govern, because they can amplify only existing cooperative tendencies. Yet some societies are more cooperative than others. What accounts for these differences?

paragraphs.cn: 在我们的模型中，个体必须真正具备耐心。他们必须将未来伙伴的信任看得比贡献的即时成本更重要。同样，各地的制度代理人必须抵制眼前的诱惑——收受贿赂、挪用资金或利用职务之便谋取私利——以维护自己的长期声誉。
paragraphs.en: The necessary ingredient is patience. Institutions work by transforming a hard cooperation problem into an easier one that reputation can solve. But people still have to be willing to engage in reputation-based cooperation, which rests on a present-future trade-off.

paragraphs.cn: 回想一下珊瑚礁上的清洁鱼。这些鱼不断面临咬食粘液而非吃寄生虫的诱惑，为此放弃即时的营养利益以维护声誉并留住未来的客户。因此，清洁鱼表现出策略性的冲动控制，当客户在旁观且更可能寻找替代清洁鱼时，它们更能抵制诱惑。
paragraphs.en: In our model, individuals literally have to be patient. They have to value the trust of future partners more than the immediate costs of contribution. Likewise, institutional agents everywhere must resist immediate temptations -- accepting bribes, diverting funds, or exploiting their position for personal gain -- to preserve their long-term standing.

paragraphs.cn: 人类的合作认知要精妙得多，但建立在相同的适应性原则之上。更有耐心的人在实验室实验中给予伙伴更多，也向慈善机构捐赠更多。我们的信任直觉追踪着耐心与合作之间的这种关联：当人们展现出自制力时，我们会评判他们为更有道德、更值得信赖。这或许可以解释为什么各个历史时期的社会都将日常诱惑——如食物、性、酒精和懒惰——道德化。近期的实验表明，更容易屈服于这些诱惑的人被视为更缺乏合作精神，因为人们认为这些享乐会削弱自制力。清教徒式的道德规范，似乎源自评估合作伙伴的同一套认知系统。
paragraphs.en: Consider again the coral reef cleaner wrasse. These fish face constant temptation to bite off mucus instead of eating parasites, forgoing immediate nutritional benefits to maintain their reputation and secure future clients. As a result, cleaner fish display strategic impulse control, resisting temptation more when clients are watching and more likely to find alternative cleaners.

paragraphs.cn: 因此，合作建立在一种当下与未来的权衡之上。但什么会使天平倾向长期声誉呢？有两个因素尤为关键：物质安全和社会资本。
paragraphs.en: Human cooperative cognition is much more sophisticated, but rests on the same adaptive principles. More patient people give more to partners in laboratory experiments, and give more to charity. Our trust intuitions track this connection between patience and cooperation: we judge people as more moral and trustworthy when they demonstrate self-control. This may explain why societies across history have moralised everyday temptations such as food, sex, alcohol, and idleness. Recent experiments show that people who give in more easily to such temptations are seen as less cooperative, because those pleasures are thought to sap self-control. Puritanical moral codes, it seems, emerge from the same cognitive systems that evaluate cooperative partners.

paragraphs.cn: 1970年，意大利创建了新的地区政府，拥有相同的权力、程序和预算。政治学家 Robert Putnam（罗伯特·帕特南）及其同事看到了一个独特的机会：同一份制度蓝图在截然不同的社会背景中实施。他们利用二十年的数据，通过12项指标——从预算执行的及时性到医疗服务的提供再到官僚系统的响应能力——追踪了意大利各地区的政府表现。
paragraphs.en: Cooperation, then, rests on a present-future trade-off. But what shifts the calculus towards long-term reputation? Two factors prove especially crucial: material security and social capital.

paragraphs.cn: 结果记录在《让民主运转起来》（Making Democracy Work, 1993）一书中，令人震惊。像 Emilia-Romagna（艾米利亚-罗马涅）这样的北部地区运作效率极高：快速响应公民诉求、政策制定富有创新、腐败问题很少。而像 Calabria（卡拉布里亚）这样的南部地区在同样的制度框架下举步维艰：迟缓、无响应、效率低下。
paragraphs.en: In 1970, Italy created new regional governments with identical powers, procedures and budgets. The political scientist Robert Putnam and his colleagues saw a unique opportunity: the same institutional blueprint implemented across vastly different social contexts. Drawing on two decades of data, they tracked the performance of Italy's regions using 12 indicators from budget promptness to healthcare delivery to bureaucratic responsiveness.

paragraphs.cn: 政府表现最强的预测因子是什么？是个人参与合唱团、体育俱乐部和公民社团的程度。公民一起做志愿服务、一起歌唱、一起组织活动的地区，其制度明显更好。最引人注目的是，19世纪80年代参与这些基层活动的情况，能够预测1978至1985年的政府表现。Putnam 用社会资本（social capital）来解释这种关联——即从悠久的公民参与历史中涌现出来的信任、社会网络和互惠规范。
paragraphs.en: The results, described in Making Democracy Work (1993), were stark. Northern regions like Emilia-Romagna operated with remarkable efficiency: quick to respond to citizen requests, innovative in policy-making, largely free of corruption. Southern regions like Calabria struggled with the same institutional framework: slow, unresponsive, plagued by inefficiency.

paragraphs.cn: 这种模式远远超出了20世纪80年代的意大利。选举问责有助于解释这一点。在社会资本较高的地方，选民更一致地惩罚腐败或缺勤的政客。而且这不仅限于民主治理。在世界各地的渔业社区中，更强的社会纽带预示着社区是保护鱼类资源还是任其枯竭。
paragraphs.en: The strongest predictor of government performance? Individual participation in choral societies, sports clubs, and civic associations. Regions where citizens volunteered together, sang together, and organised together had dramatically better institutions. Most remarkably, participation in these grassroots activities in the 1890s predicted government performance in 1978-1985. Putnam explained this association in terms of social capital -- the trust, social networks and norms of reciprocity that emerge from a long history of civic engagement.

paragraphs.cn: 为什么社会资本如此重要？用当期成本和未来收益的框架来理解会有帮助。在关系紧密的社区中，人们预期将共同分享未来，一个受损的声誉会伴随你数十年。社会资本使未来的分量变得更重。从渔业社区到整个地区乃至国家，社会资本增加了长期声誉的价值，从而促进了制度性合作。
paragraphs.en: This pattern extends well beyond 1980s Italy. Electoral accountability helps explain why. Where social capital is high, voters more consistently punish corrupt or absentee politicians. And it's not limited to democratic governance. In fishing communities worldwide, stronger social ties predict whether communities preserve their fish stocks or allow them to collapse.

paragraphs.cn: 反过来说，物质安全使当前成本的感受变得更轻。当基本需求得到保障时，人们有余裕去思考超越眼前生存的问题。当你挣扎着付房租或养家糊口时，眼前的需求如此迫切，以至于较长期的考量便退隐到背景之中。一个勉强度日的渔民可能为了今晚养家而冒过度捕捞的风险；而一个富裕的渔民则有条件为下一季保存鱼获，并投资于与其他渔民的关系。
paragraphs.en: Why does social capital matter so much? It helps to think in terms of present costs and future benefits. In tight-knit communities where people expect to share a future together, a damaged reputation follows you around for decades. Social capital makes the future loom larger. From fishing communities to entire regions and countries, social capital increases the value of long-term reputation, facilitating institutional cooperation.

paragraphs.cn: 这解释了为什么制度表现与经济发展水平相关。虽然腐败的制度当然会损害经济增长，但经济困难也会加剧眼前压力，使腐败成为一种更合理的策略。因此，贫困率较高的地区往往腐败率也较高。
paragraphs.en: Conversely, material security makes present costs matter less. When basic needs are secure, people can afford to think beyond immediate survival. When you're struggling to make rent or feed your family, immediate needs loom so large that longer-term considerations fade into the background. A struggling fisherman may risk overfishing to feed his family tonight; a prosperous one can afford to preserve stocks for next season and invest in relationships with other fishermen.

paragraphs.cn: 因此，制度可以被理解为一种社会技术。我们不断地在工程化它们，往往甚至没有意识到这一点。当邻居们组织起来维护一个共享的花园或游乐场时，他们会推选一个小委员会来管理资金和决策。这种安排之所以有效，是因为它将协调数十名贡献者的困难问题转化为了信任少数几位可见之人的简单问题——这些人可以因勤勉而受到称赞，也可以因滥用而被追责。
paragraphs.en: This explains why institutional performance correlates with economic development. While corrupt institutions certainly harm economic growth, economic hardship also heightens immediate pressures, making corruption a more sensible strategy. As a result, areas with higher poverty tend to have higher corruption rates.

paragraphs.cn: 正如任何工具一样，制度不能凭空创造原本不存在的东西；它们只能放大已有的合作能力。制度建立在使合作成为理性选择的条件之上：物质安全和社会资本。当这些条件具备时，声誉就可以大规模地发挥作用。一层问责支撑着下一层，直到合作远远超越了熟悉程度的限制。从维系吸血蝙蝠和珊瑚礁清洁鱼的同一种力量中，我们建造了城市、市场和国家。制度，就是将信任扩展到数百万陌生人之间的方式。
paragraphs.en: Institutions can thus be understood as social technologies. We engineer them constantly, often without realising it. When neighbours organise to maintain a shared garden or playground, they appoint a small committee to manage funds and decisions. The arrangement works because it transforms the hard problem of coordinating dozens of contributors into the easier problem of trusting a few visible people who can be praised for diligence or blamed for misuse.

articles.title: 工具化正在使一切沦为手段
articles.subtitle: 当健康、财富和幸福成为唯一目的，我们失去了什么
articles.author: Julian Baggini
articles.summary: 几十年来，米高梅（MGM）制片厂出品的电影都以咆哮的雄狮莱奥作为开场画面，它身旁环绕着一句箴言——Ars gratia artis：为艺术而艺术。鉴于米高梅本身是一个逐利的庞然大物，我们或许会怀疑这一高尚情怀的真诚程度。不过，除了颇具争议的"道德教化"目标之外，它确实表达了人们应当从事电影创作的少数...
articles.content_cn:
几十年来，米高梅（MGM）制片厂出品的电影都以咆哮的雄狮莱奥作为开场画面，它身旁环绕着一句箴言——Ars gratia artis：为艺术而艺术。鉴于米高梅本身是一个逐利的庞然大物，我们或许会怀疑这一高尚情怀的真诚程度。不过，除了颇具争议的"道德教化"目标之外，它确实表达了人们应当从事电影创作的少数正当理由之一。为了其他任何目的而创作的艺术——无论是利润、自我宣传还是政治宣传——都不算是真正的艺术，或者至少不是最纯粹意义上的艺术。

因此，当我最近看到一张英国"国家艺术通票"的广告时，颇感震惊。这张通票能让持有者免费或以折扣价进入英国各地的画廊和博物馆。广告标语"看得更多，活得更丰富"听起来恰到好处：艺术的确能丰富我们的人生。但仔细一看，这里的"更多"完全是数量意义上的，而非质量意义上的。主要口号赫然写着："用艺术为你的生命增添岁月。"紧随其后的是："在画廊和博物馆中度过时光有助于延长寿命。"艺术不再是为艺术而存在，而是为心脏而存在——而且是有血有肉的那颗心脏，而非精神层面的心灵。这种围绕艺术的话语已变得无处不在，英格兰艺术委员会（Arts Council England）也在大力推广这样的理念："参与创意和文化活动已被证明对个人和社区具有健康益处。"

我或许对那张海报感到震惊，但并不意外。长久以来，我一直在私下哀叹万物被工具化的趋势：似乎没有什么东西还具有自身的价值，一切都被视为服务于某种功利功能的有用工具。我最初察觉到这一令人悲哀的趋势是在2010年，当时我不幸要为格雷琴·鲁宾（Gretchen Rubin）的著作《幸福计划》（The Happiness Project，2009）撰写书评，该书记录了她一年间对幸福生活的不懈追求。其中一段话令我印象如此深刻，以至于时至今日我几乎还能逐字复述。她与丈夫的一天从一开始就不太顺利，但在道歉之后，鲁宾写道："我们拥抱了——至少六秒钟，我恰好从研究中了解到，这是促进催产素和血清素分泌所需的最低时长，这两种化学物质能提升情绪、促进亲密关系的建立。紧张的氛围随之消散。"

这一幕令人不寒而栗：一个女人拥抱她的丈夫，不仅是出于爱或情感，更是为了释放激素、减轻自身压力。这些句子揭示了她所谓的幸福计划如何驱使她做每一件事都以改善情绪为出发点。似乎没有什么比这更重要的了，甚至真相也不例外。在将自身当作一台幸福机器对待的一年实验结束之际，她回顾了哪些改变发生了、哪些没有。"也许我只是在看我想看到的东西，"她反思道，紧接着又说："也许是吧，但谁在乎呢？"管它是真是假，只要让你感觉更好就行。

在目睹"为幸福而拥抱"与"为长寿而创作"之间的这些年里，我看到了无数类似的例子：生活中一切美好的事物，在被人推崇时，都不是因其自身价值，而是因其所带来的物质利益。这种工具化已经如此隐蔽地常态化，以至于我们甚至不觉得它有什么奇怪之处，更不用说认识到它的错误了。我们似乎也没有意识到它渗透得有多深。然而它的影响是深远的，让我们一次又一次地迷失生活中真正有价值的东西。

在对症下药之前，我需要为这个看似夸张的论断辩护：万物正在被工具化。这绝非修辞上的夸大其词，我确实很难想出任何一件有价值的事物，不曾被人以其功利效益来宣扬——而且往往先于其内在价值被提及。以做礼拜为例。大多数信徒认为崇拜是一种虔诚的本分，而非进入天堂的实用手段。然而今天，即便基督徒也不时引用研究来证明其好处，例如黛博拉·詹金斯（Deborah Jenkins）在《首要基督教》（Premier Christianity）杂志上指出："成为教会社区的一部分可以延长寿命、减少抑郁并促进心理健康。"我今天随手翻阅的一本书则以身体健康为由提倡祈祷，书中引用了一项研究，该研究"发现每日礼拜（solat）祈祷对心血管系统、血液以及肌肉和骨骼具有显著的医疗益处"。当然，如果受到质疑，没有人会说这些是践行宗教的最佳理由。但这并不妨碍他们将之作为非常好的理由提出来。况且，这些理由比声称一位全爱的造物主真的在乎你如何度过周日早晨要可信得多，也更有科学依据。

更世俗化的是，我们甚至被赋予了关于性高潮的工具性理由。2015年《每日电讯报》（The Telegraph）的一个标题——"科学家称：每日一次高潮或可预防前列腺癌"——概括了一种如今广为流传的观念：男性性交或自慰的最佳理由不是快感、亲密或释放性张力，而是保护健康。

你可以玩一个很漫长的游戏，逐一提出人们本珍视的事物，试图找到一样尚未因其对健康、财富或幸福的好处而被赞颂的东西。你的搜索将是徒劳的。北方歌剧院（Opera North）网站列出了歌唱的十项好处，其中只有一项——"让你表达自我"——与艺术和创造力有关。其余的包括"让你感觉更好""增强肺功能""帮助你战胜压力和放松""有助于改善记忆""在生活艰难时能帮你渡过难关"以及"提升你的自信心"。

我们日益追问的不是一项活动本身好在哪里，而是它能为吾人带来什么好处

许多倡导重新亲近自然的人，所用的理由恰恰迎合了那种功利主义的、以自我为中心的快乐主义——而正是这种心态导致人类最初与地球疏离。国民信托（National Trust）宣扬"在自然中散步有助于提升幸福感"，而日益流行的"森林浴"则鼓励我们将林地当作一种名副其实的步行诊所。这些自命的自然的倡导者似乎忽略了一个讽刺：如果我们走进树林是因为树木能为我们做什么，那么我们所怀揣的与那些砍伐森林者的心态如出一辙——同样是剥削性的、榨取式的。

甚至连哲学——对智慧的超然追求——也未能幸免于工具化的诅咒。大学已不再满足于说它们的课程让你探索一些关于存在的最根本问题。如今的问题带有更明确的功利色彩：哲学如何帮你买房或积累养老金？哲学被惯常地包装为"可迁移思维能力"的训练场，而这些能力最明显的迁移目标就是职场。剑桥大学哲学系甚至专门设有一个网页，列出它所教授的五项适合写进简历的技能：思维能力、沟通能力、组织能力、人际交往能力和研究能力。

工具化在我们与他人共同从事或为他人而做的事情上危害最大。伊曼努尔·康德（Immanuel Kant）将"始终将人性——无论在你自身还是他人身上——同时视为目的，而绝不仅仅视为手段"视为"绝对命令"（categorical imperative），即道德的绝对要求。我们用来描述将他人工具化的词语——非人化、物化、剥削——正反映了我们对其腐蚀性的认知。正因如此，将社会联结工具化既是不道德的，也是自我挫败的。如果我们开始强调社会关系能为我们做什么，我们就会把关系中的他人仅仅当作自我提升的工具。

以上甚至远未穷尽那些已被常态化工具化的活动。我们还可以加上：园艺、体育运动、露营、游泳、社会运动、社区志愿服务、烘焙面包、手工艺、写日记、大笑、说"谢谢"。我们日益追问的不是它们本身好在哪里，而是它们能为吾人带来什么好处。而所谓的"好处"指的是健康、财富和世俗的成功。

那些因自然、艺术、学习、友谊等自身之故而热爱它们的人，或许会觉得宣扬其工具性好处令人反感，但这样做有何危害呢？毕竟，一个过着工具化生活的人和一个没有这样做的人可能在做完全相同的事情。这一质疑忽略了一个事实：好的生活不仅取决于我们做什么，还取决于我们如何去做。两个文化日程相同的人可能去看同样的展览、看同样的电影、听同样的音乐，但如果他们的动机根本不同，那么他们所处的世界也截然不同。

要理解原因，我们需要回到一个基本问题：为什么任何事物具有价值？在《尼各马可伦理学》（Nicomachean Ethics）中，亚里士多德（Aristotle）是最早但绝非最后一个观察到以下事实的人：我们做某些事情是作为达到目的的手段，而做另一些事情则是将其本身视为目的。只有后者具有内在价值，而作为手段的事物仅有外在价值。如果我们追问人生的终极价值何在，显然在于那些具有内在价值的事物之中。

这一智慧如此无争议，以至于可能被视为陈词滥调。但它值得我们跨越时代、跨越人生各个阶段反复重申，因为我们太容易被纯粹的工具性利益所牵引，偏离真正有价值之物。金钱当然是最明显的例子。金钱的重要性仅在于它能购买什么，它可以用来获取许多我们最珍视的东西。然而，试图积累越来越多的金钱、永远觉得不够，似乎是太普遍的人性弱点——它将我们从与所爱之人共度的时光和心爱的活动中引开。

追求外在利益而非内在利益已是一个足够常见的错误。但万物工具化则更进一步。它不仅分散我们对那些本身即善的事物的注意力，更剥夺了这些事物本身的内在价值，将它们降格为纯粹的手段。更糟糕的是，这些目的本身甚至不具有内在价值。

生活不是为未来所做的训练。它是一场已经开始的比赛

想一想工具化所服务的目标：健康、财富和心理健康。这些东西都如此显而易见地令人向往，以至于我们很容易忽略一个事实——它们之中没有一个具有内在价值。财富如此，这一点显而易见；但心理健康和身体健康同样如此。

先谈身体健康。我们常常把它说得仿佛是最重要的事物。正因如此，奥古斯丁·伯勒斯的格言"拥有健康，你就拥有一切"在互联网上广为流传。但我们并非为健康本身而珍视它。我们看重健康有两个原因。其一，失去健康通常意味着痛苦和折磨，而痛苦本身是坏的，理应避免。其二，有了健康，我们才更有能力去做那些赋予人生意义的事情。但一个健康却缺少爱、缺少有意义的活动或经历的人生，将是空洞的。事实上，许多慢性病患者在发现健康并非最重要的东西时，连自己都感到惊讶。在疾病中，他们更清楚地看到了什么才是真正重要的，并发现例如生病却被人爱，远比健康却被人憎恨要好。正如一位轮椅使用的研究参与者对护理学副教授伊丽莎白·林赛（Elizabeth Lindsey）所说："即使我没有任何身体能力，我仍然可以充实地生活，因为我所生活的生命来自内心。"身体健康之所以重要，仅仅作为一座基础，让我们更容易去珍视那些真正重要的事物。事实上，林赛提出了"疾病中的健康"的概念，认为最完整意义上的健康并非疾病的不存在。

即使是心理健康本身也不是重要的。心理疾病是内在的恶，因为它是无收获的痛苦。但良好的心理健康，如同良好的身体健康一样，仅仅是更根本的价值的赋能者。甚至某些心理痛苦也不是内在的恶。我们因失去而悲伤，这实际上是好的——它表明当坏事降临到我们所在乎的人身上时，我们的情感能正常运作。

即便是幸福——或许是工具化最常声称的好处——也不是一种内在的善。如果一个人因为看到他偏见中所仇恨的人遭受苦难而感到快乐，这种快乐不是好的。生活在化学药物营造的迷幻快感中，满足却与现实世界脱节，也不是好的。在伴侣背后不忠的情况下，快乐地生活在关系稳固的幻觉中，同样不是好的。无知的幸福有时可能比痛苦的真相更好，但这并不使它成为好的。

那么，如果不是健康、财富和心理健康，什么本身是好的呢？哲学家们屡屡犯下一个错误，试图将一样东西认定为至善（summum bonum），即人类最高的善。对亚里士多德而言，它是理智沉思；对佛教徒而言，它是消除苦难；对康德而言，它是善的意志；对功利主义者而言，它是幸福。但似乎没有理由将内在价值局限为某一种状态或活动。亚里士多德将繁荣（flourishing）视为人类的最高善，这一点更为切中要害——他的错误仅在于对繁荣的具体要求规定得过于僵化。当我们的生活致力于投入那些因其自身而非因其他任何原因而有价值的事物时，我们就在繁荣。繁荣的形式如同人类自身一样多样。弗里德里希·尼采（Friedrich Nietzsche）认为没有音乐的人生将是一个错误，但对音乐无动于衷的人来说并非如此。人生终极价值源自具有内在价值的事物——这一理念本质上是多元的。

人类的内在之善包括所有让生命值得度过、而无需任何进一步辩护的事物。对它们追问"有什么意义？"本身就是没有抓住要点。它们本身就是意义所在。我们无法用论证来证明它们为何有价值；我们只能描述是什么使它们有价值，并希望他人认同它们的价值。例如，我们可以说，在森林中度过的一天应当首先被珍视，因为它使我们意识到活着的奇迹，并对自然世界心怀敬畏。参与或观看体育运动，是亲历或目睹心灵与身体比日常生活中更紧密协作的挣扎与喜悦。学习一门外语是通向另一种文化的入口，让你能够与该文化的成员交流，接触其文学和媒体。所有这些都丰富了我们的生活、拓展了我们的经验，这种价值不以你的寿命是否增加一秒或痴呆是否推迟一天为转移。如果你将它们视为提升未来精神、情感或身体力量的手段——而那个未来未必如当下这般有意义——你就把注意力从此时此地的价值中移开了。生活不是为未来所做的训练。它是一场已经开始的比赛，而时间正在流逝。

内在之善与外在之善的区别在概念上是清晰的，但在现实世界中很快便不再泾渭分明。最明显的是，许多事物可以同时具有内在价值和外在价值，正如我所论证的那些被错误工具化的事物一样。工具化并非创造了外在价值，它只是将外在价值凌驾于内在价值之上。

内在价值也不总是优先于外在价值。人有现实的需求，赚钱、劈柴或觅食有时可能比读一本小说或陪孙辈玩耍更为紧迫。许多事情必须出于工具性目的而做，仅仅从事具有内在价值的活动将是一种非凡的特权、一种放纵，或者两者兼有。

此外，并非所有外在之善都是同等的。有些比其他的更接近终极价值。为了赚取花在真正有价值的事物上的钱而讨好上司以赢得青睐，这将我们远远带离生活中最重要的东西，且无法保证我们能回到那里。相比之下，学习伦理学在某种意义上是过好生活的手段，但这一目的如此切近，几乎本身就称得上是好的。

那些看似捷径的东西实际上是短路，反而破坏了它们试图达成的目标

正因如此，我认为关于"为艺术而艺术"与"艺术作为教化工具"的辩论多少有些误导。某些艺术，尤其是器乐和抽象绘画，确实可以也应当仅仅为其自身而被欣赏。但许多文学、电影和戏剧能够让我们洞察伦理、政治和人心。所有这些理解帮助我们更好地生活、更关注我们自己和他人生活中真正重要的事物。这样的艺术可以被视为道德教育的手段，但在优秀的艺术中，手段与目的如此紧密地交织在一起，以至于二者的区分似乎显得牵强。例如，任何关于安东·契诃夫（Anton Chekhov）何以成为伟大剧作家的论述，都无法将他的戏剧技艺与其所呈现的人性关怀分割开来。许多说教式艺术的问题不在于它包含教训，而在于这些教训传达得太过粗糙。这样的作品不仅是糟糕的艺术，也是拙劣的教学工具。

内在价值与外在价值之间的关系是复杂的，而工具化的问题之一就在于它试图将这种关系扁平化和简单化。它鼓励我们识别什么最有用，然后将其与具有终极价值的东西分离开来并置于其上。在这样做的时候，它往往削弱或摧毁了它所承诺最大化的那些益处。

以社会联结为例。我刚刚听说一项研究表明，做任何事情——即使是阅读——与他人一起做都比独自做对我们更有益。这一信息如今被广泛传播和接受，因此人们知道社交对身心健康很重要。但友谊和社区最宝贵的特征之一，恰恰在于它们让我们走出对自我的关切，使我们更加关注他人的需要。要从社交中获得最大的收获，我们需要以正确的精神去做——选择与他人相处是因为我们关心他们、他们也关心我们，因为我们觉得他们令人振奋，因为我们享受成为集体体验或共同事业的一部分。因此，如果我们仅仅出于个人健康的理由而去社交，我们很可能无法获得社交通常带来的那些益处。

工具化给人一种高效的幻觉，因为它倡导直接追求我们所有人都想要的实际事物。但这往往适得其反。在大多数情况下，如果获得某种好处成为你做一件事的首要动机，你反而很可能得不到它所宣称的益处。那些看似捷径的东西实际上是短路，反而破坏了它们试图达成的目标。

如果工具化是一个如此深刻的错误，我们为何会犯下它？毕竟，我们并非蓄意要剥离最有价值的活动的意义，或把朋友当作心灵提升的工具。工具化根植于西方现代性的几个相互关联的特征之中。

启蒙运动催生了一种关于主权个体的至高地位的理念，这一理念在古典和基督教思想中有着深厚的根基。几个世纪以来，这一理念已成为某种常识。每个人都被认定是自己命运的主人、自身人生故事的书写者。自我表达和自我决定被视为成为真实自我的必要条件。

启蒙思想家在一个权力由少数人行使而多数人被压制的时代提倡更大的个人自由，这是正确的。但人类也是社会性动物，永远不可能完全自主。现代性的错误在于忽视了这一点，将所有重心放在个人自由上，而对我们之间相互依存的关注严重不足。这导致了对自主性重要性的过度强调，将个人主义的推崇推向了极端。其结果是原子化：一个我们的彼此分离变得过度的世界。

这个原子化的世界有几个特征，它们都在助长工具化。首先，它助长了一种控制幻觉。被鼓励感到自主的我们，忽略了有很多事情是我们无能为力的。世界自行展开，随机地带来机遇或制造障碍。我们甚至不能完全掌控自己。我们的根本禀赋——性情、个性、天赋和局限——并非我们所能选择。我们无法直接触及思想和意志隐秘的源头，也不能随心所欲地选择自己的喜好或信念。

在大量工具化现象背后的是一种粗陋的还原主义，它忽视系统而只关注系统中的要素

但我们被预先设定为认为自己是自由且自主的，于是便想象自己可以操控世界来实现任何愿望。幸福、健康和成功都唾手可得，只要我们做出正确的选择。于是世界变成了一系列可以拉动的操纵杆和可以按下的按钮，一切都要屈服于我们的意志。简而言之，一切都可以且必须成为我们所选择的任何目的的手段，因为在我们看来，这就是自我决定所要求的。

在晚期资本主义时代，我们的自主能动性日益通过我们作为消费者的身份来表达。自由首先是选择如何花钱的自由，承诺是：我们所需的一切都可以用金钱换取。消费心态已影响了我们与一切事物——不仅仅是所购买之物——的关系。其结果是，世界在本质上变成了交易性的，意味着一切都是获取其他东西的工具。约会软件给人一种我们在选购伴侣的印象绝非偶然，因为我们甚至用消费框架来处理亲密关系。政治也变成了一种选票交易，选民和政客都信奉赢者通吃，如同拍卖中的最高出价者，至于支持了败选一方的人则活该倒霉。民主应当是一种管理相互竞争的需求的方式，而非让胜者获得他们想要的一切。投票应当是表达你的声音，而非满足你的要求。但在新的消费心态中，选票购买权力，不再授予责任。

工具化的另一个深层文化根源是从自然科学中悄然渗透到我们文化中的还原主义。还原主义是这样一种观念：理解事物运作方式的方法是将其拆解为组成部分。这一理念在自然科学中服务了数百年之久。但关于其局限性的线索来自它在社会科学中的相对失败。经济、社会和心理无法通过简单的机械过程来解释。我们已经认识到，即使在自然科学中，通过拆解事物所能解释的也有限，而观察系统作为一个整体如何运作同样重要——有时甚至更为重要。

在大量工具化现象背后的是一种粗陋的还原主义，它忽视系统而只关注系统中的要素。一段丰富的体验——例如置身户外——被简化为刺激血液循环或释放激素的手段。艺术，它搅动着多种往往相互冲突的情感，却仅因其唤起某些正面情绪的能力而被推崇。社会纽带，它带来痛苦和心碎也带来欢乐，却被简化为情感支持的来源。

将膨胀的个人自主信念、交易性的消费心态和还原主义的事物运作态度结合在一起，我们将世界视为可供掠夺以促进自身福祉的资源集合，这几乎是不可避免的。悲剧在于，当我们这样做的时候，我们忽视而非满足了最深层的需要。

如果我们逆转万物工具化的趋势，我们的文化会是什么样子？当然，我们仍然会把许多事当作达到目的的手段。我们也会乐于承认生活中许多美好的事物同时带来工具性的好处。但我们会将这些视为受欢迎的副产品，而非它们的目的。一个去工具化的世界将是一个我们更加关注此时此地有价值之物的世界。

以友谊为例。我们从他人那里获得的个人好处是真实的，但不应当成为与他们在一起的理由。关系之所以有价值，是因为我们珍视关系中的人，而不是因为与他们共度时光能在大脑中释放内啡肽。大卫·休谟（David Hume）在两个多世纪前就纠正了这一错误，他写道："我在行善于朋友时感到快乐，是因为我爱他；而非为了那份快乐才爱他。"拒绝工具化意味着理解：感觉良好常常伴随着好好生活而来，但它并不是好好生活本身所包含的东西。

为事物自身的价值而非它们可能带来的好处去欣赏它们，是一种解放。它将我们从一种内在压力中释放出来——那种总是要确保所做之事服务于某个进一步目的、以未来收益来为每一天辩护的压力。充分地生活意味着充分地欣赏生活所带来的一切，而非试图从中榨取可兑现的利益。它让我们认识到，好的生活是我们每天都可以过的——无论以大的方式还是小的方式。最重要的是，它告诉我们：我们所爱和所关心的人和事，其本身就已经足够，不需要再服务于任何进一步的功能来证明我们为之投入时间和关怀是值得的。置身于这个世界，认识到生活本身就是目的——这才是通达生命圆满的关键。
articles.content_en:
For decades, films out of the Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer Studios have opened with Leo the roaring lion, garlanded with the motto *Ars gratia artis*: art for art's sake. Given that MGM is a money-making behemoth, we might doubt the sincerity of this high-minded sentiment. Still, along with the contested goal of moral improvement, it certainly expresses one of the few legitimate reasons why people *should* make movies. Art for the sake of anything else -- profit, self-promotion, propaganda -- isn't really art at all, or at least not in its purest sense.

It therefore came as a bit of a shock to see a recent advert for the National Art Pass, which gives holders free or discounted entry to galleries and museums around the United Kingdom. The tag line 'See more. Live more' sounded right: art does indeed enrich our lives. But it turned out that the 'more' here was purely quantitative, not qualitative. 'Grow some years onto your life with art,' proclaimed the main slogan, followed by: 'Spending time in galleries and museums could help you live longer.' Art not for art's sake, but for your heart's sake, the fleshy not the spiritual one at that. This messaging around the arts has become ubiquitous, with Arts Council England promoting the idea that 'engaging in creative and cultural activities has proven health benefits for individuals and communities.'

I may have been shocked by the poster, but I was not surprised by it. For a long time, I have been privately lamenting the instrumentalisation of everything: how nothing seems to be of value in itself anymore but is only seen as useful in the service of some utilitarian function. I first got wind of this lamentable trend in 2010, when I had the misfortune to review Gretchen Rubin's book *The Happiness Project* (2009), an account of a year in relentless pursuit of the happy life. One passage struck me so hard I can almost recall it word for word today. A day with her husband gets off to a sticky start but, following an apology, Rubin writes: 'We hugged -- for at least six seconds, which, I happened to know from my research, is the minimum time necessary to promote the flow of oxytocin and serotonin, mood-boosting chemicals that promote bonding. The moment of tension passed.'

I was left with the chilling image of a woman holding her husband not only out of love or affection but in order to release hormones and reduce her stress. Those sentences highlighted how her happiness project had led her to do everything with the improvement of her mood in mind. Nothing else seemed to matter as much, even truth. At the end of her year-long experiment in treating herself as a felicific machine, she reflected on what had or had not changed. 'Maybe I was seeing what I wanted to see,' she wondered, only to add: 'Maybe, but who cares?' Whatever makes you feel better, true or not.

In the years between witnessing hugging for happiness and creativity for longevity, I have seen countless other examples of all that is good in life being promoted not for their own sake but for the material benefits they bring. This instrumentalisation has become normalised so insidiously that we don't even notice that it is odd, let alone wrong. Nor do we seem to be aware of quite how pervasive it is. Yet its effects are profound, leading us to lose sight again and again of what is truly of value in life.

Before offering a diagnosis for what has gone wrong and how to cure it, I need to defend the seemingly hyperbolic claim that *everything* is becoming instrumentalised. Far from being a rhetorical exaggeration, I genuinely find it difficult to think of anything worthwhile that at least some people have not been advocating for its utilitarian benefits before mentioning any of its intrinsic merits. Take churchgoing. Most believers hold that worship is a devotional duty rather than a pragmatic means of getting into heaven. Today, however, it is not uncommon to hear even Christians, such as Deborah Jenkins in *Premier Christianity* magazine, pointing to research that: 'Being part of a church community can lengthen life, reduce depression and promote positive mental health.' A book I flicked through today advocated prayer for physical health, pointing to a study that 'found significant medical benefits on the cardiovascular system, blood, as well as muscle and bone resulting from the *solat* daily prayer.' Of course, if challenged, none would say these are the *best* reasons to practise religion. But it doesn't stop them offering these reasons as very good ones. Furthermore, they are more credible and certainly more scientific than claims that an all-loving creator god really thinks it's important how you spend your Sunday mornings.

More profanely, we are even given instrumental reasons to orgasm. A headline from *The Telegraph* in 2015 -- 'An Orgasm A Day Could Keep Prostate Cancer Away, Scientists Claim' -- summed up a now-widely shared belief that one of the best reasons for a man to have sex or masturbate is not pleasure, intimacy or the release of sexual tension but to protect his health.

You could play a very long game of suggesting things people value in their own right in the hope of finding one that hasn't been praised for its health, wealth or wellbeing advantages. Your search would be in vain. The Opera North website lists 10 benefits of singing, and only one -- it lets you express yourself -- has anything to do with art and creativity. The others include 'makes you feel better', 'enhances lung function', 'helps you beat stress and relax', 'helps improve memory', 'can help when life gets tough' and 'boosts your confidence'.

We increasingly ask not what is good about an activity but what good it can do for us

Many people who advocate for reconnecting with nature do so with reasons that are designed to appeal to the very same utilitarian, self-centred hedonism that is to blame for humanity losing touch with the Earth in the first place. The National Trust talks of how 'walking in nature can help wellbeing' while the growing popularity of 'forest bathing' encourages us to use woodland as though it were a kind of literal walk-in clinic. These would-be advocates for nature seem to miss the irony that if we go out among the trees because of what they can do for us, we're going with the same exploitative, extractive mindset as those who log them.

Even philosophy, the disinterested pursuit of wisdom, has fallen prey to the curse of instrumentalisation. It is no longer enough for universities to say that their programmes allow you to explore some of the most fundamental questions of existence. Now the questions are of a decidedly more bottom-line sort: how will philosophy help you buy a house or build your pension pot? Philosophy is routinely sold as a trainer of 'transferable thinking skills', and it's clear where these most transfer to: the world of work. The Faculty of Philosophy at the University of Cambridge has a whole webpage devoted to five resumé-friendly skills it teaches: intellectual, communication, organisational, interpersonal and research.

Instrumentalisation is most pernicious when it applies to things we do with and for others. Immanuel Kant saw it as a 'categorical imperative' -- an absolute demand of morality -- to 'treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, always at the same time as an end, never merely as a means.' The words we use to describe the instrumentalisation of others echo how corrupting we think it is: dehumanisation, objectification, exploitation. That is why the instrumentalisation of social connection is immoral as well as self-defeating. If we start to foreground what social relationships do for us, we treat the other people involved as mere tools for self-advancement.

I haven't even begun to exhaust the list of activities that have become routinely instrumentalised. Among those we could add are gardening, playing sport, camping, swimming, campaigning, community volunteer work, baking bread, crafts, keeping a diary, laughing, saying 'thank you'. We increasingly ask not what is good about them but what good they can do for us. And by 'good' we mean health, wealth and worldly success.

Those who love nature, art, learning, friendship and so on for their own sake may find it distasteful to spotlight their instrumental benefits, but what is the harm in doing so? After all, someone living an instrumentalised life and someone who is not might be doing exactly the same things. This objection misses the fact that a good life does not only depend on *what* we do, but *how* we do it. Two people with the same cultural calendars may go to the same exhibitions, watch the same films and listen to the same music, but if their motivations are fundamentally different then so are the worlds they inhabit.

To understand why, we have to go back to the basic question of why anything has value. In the *Nicomachean Ethics*, Aristotle was one of the first but certainly not the last to observe that we do some things as means to ends and other things as ends in themselves. Only the latter have intrinsic value, while means to ends have mere extrinsic value. If we ask where the ultimate value in life lies, it is clearly in things with intrinsic value.

This piece of wisdom is so uncontroversial it could be dismissed as platitudinous. But it merits repeating across the ages and across all our life stages because it is so easy to be sucked away from what has real value by purely instrumental goods. Money is of course the clearest example. Money is important only for what it can buy and can be used to obtain many of the things we most value. Yet it seems all too human to try to accumulate more and more of it, never believing one has enough, diverting us from time with loved ones and cherished activities.

Pursuing extrinsic rather than intrinsic goods is a common-enough mistake. But the instrumentalisation of everything takes it one step further. It doesn't just distract us from all the things that are good in themselves; it strips these very things of their intrinsic value and turns them into mere means to ends. Worse, these ends are not even of value in themselves.

Life isn't a training for the future. It's a game that's already started

Think about what instrumentalisation serves: health, wealth and psychological wellbeing. These are all so obviously desirable that it's easy to miss the fact that none have intrinsic value. That is clearly true of wealth, but it is equally true of mental and physical health.

Take bodily health first. We often talk about it as if it were the most important thing of all. That's why Augusten Burroughs's quote 'When you have your health, you have everything' has a healthy life as an internet meme. But we don't prize health for its own sake. We value it for two reasons. One is that the alternative usually involves pain and suffering, which are bad in themselves and so to be avoided. The other is that, with health, we are more able to do the things that bring meaning to our lives. But a healthy life devoid of love, meaningful activity or experiences would be empty. Indeed, many people with chronic illness surprise themselves and others when they discover that, actually, their health is not the most important thing after all. In sickness, they see more clearly what matters and find that it is better to be, say, ill and loved than in good health and loathed. As one wheelchair-using study participant told Elizabeth Lindsey, an associate professor of nursing: 'I can live life to the fullest, even if I have no physical ability, I can still live life to the fullest because where I am living, life is from within.' Physical health is important only as a foundation to make it easier for us to appreciate the things that really matter. Indeed, Lindsey talks of 'health within illness', arguing that health in its fullest sense is not the absence of illness.

Even mental health is not important in itself. Mental illness is intrinsically bad, since it is suffering without gain. But being in good mental health, like being in good physical health, is just an enabler of what is more fundamentally valuable. Even some mental distress is not intrinsically bad. It is a good thing that we grieve as it shows that our emotions are functioning correctly when bad things happen to the people who matter to us.

Not even happiness, perhaps the most commonly claimed benefit of instrumentalisation, is an intrinsic good. It is not good if someone feels happy to see people they prejudicially hate suffer. It would not be good to live life in a chemical cloud of bliss, content but disengaged from the real world. It is not good to live happily in the illusion of a strong relationship when your partner is cheating behind your back. Blissful ignorance may sometimes be better than painful knowledge, but that does not make it good.

So what is good in itself, if not health, wealth and mental wellbeing? Philosophers have repeatedly made the mistake of trying to identify one thing as the *summum bonum*, the 'ultimate good' for humankind. For Aristotle, it was intellectual contemplation; for the Buddhists, elimination of suffering; for Kant, a good will; for utilitarians, happiness. But there seems no reason to try to narrow down what is intrinsically valuable to one state or activity. Aristotle was more on point when he identified flourishing as the highest good for humanity, erring only when he became too prescriptive about what flourishing demands. We flourish when our lives are ones of engagement with things that are valuable for their own sakes and not for any other. Flourishing takes as many forms as human beings do. Friedrich Nietzsche thought that life without music would be a mistake, but it wouldn't be for someone who is left cold by it. The idea that ultimate value in life comes from things with intrinsic value is a pluralistic one.

Intrinsic human goods include all the things that make life worth living without need of any further justification. To ask of them 'What's the point?' would be to miss the point. They are the point. We cannot give arguments for why they are valuable; we can only describe what makes them valuable and hope others recognise their worth. For example, we can say that a day spent in the forest should be appreciated first and foremost because it makes us recognise the wonder of being alive and marvel at the natural world. To play or watch a sport is to participate in or witness the struggle and delight of attempting to bring mind and body together more seamlessly than in the rest of life. Learning a foreign language is a gateway into another culture that allows you to communicate with members of it and access its literature and media. All these things enrich our lives and broaden our experience, which is valuable even if it doesn't add a second to your lifespan or delay dementia by a day. If you see them as a means to boost your mental, emotional or physical strength for future times that may or may not be as meaningful, you are taking your focus away from what is valuable here and now. Life isn't a training for the future. It's a game that's already started, and time is running out.

The distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic goods may be conceptually sharp, but in the real world it quickly becomes less clear-cut. Most obviously, many things can be both intrinsically and extrinsically valuable, as is the case for all the things that I argued have been wrongly instrumentalised. Instrumentalisation does not create extrinsic value, it merely elevates it above what is intrinsically valuable.

Nor is it always the case that intrinsic trumps extrinsic value. Human beings have practical needs, and it can be more important to earn money, chop wood or hunt food than to read a novel or play with your grandchildren. Many things have to be done for instrumental purposes, and to occupy yourself only with what is intrinsically worthwhile would be an exceptional privilege, an indulgence, or both.

Furthermore, not all extrinsic goods are created equal. Some serve ultimate value more closely than others. Flattering the boss to win favour to earn money to spend on things of true value takes us far from what is most important in life with no guarantee we will get back to it. Studying ethics, in contrast, is in a sense a means to an end of living well, but the end is so close to hand that it almost counts as good in itself.

What look like shortcuts turn out to be short circuits, undermining what they seek to achieve

This is why I think the debate over 'art for art's sake' versus 'art as a didactic tool' is somewhat misguided. Some art, especially instrumental music and abstract painting, can and should be appreciated only for its own sake. But much literature, film and drama can give us insight into ethics, politics and the human heart. All such understanding helps us to live better and attend more to what truly matters, in our lives and those of others. Such art can be seen as a means to the end of moral education, but in good art, means and ends are so closely tied that the distinction seems artificial. For instance, any account of why Anton Chekhov was such a great playwright could not separate his stagecraft and the humanity of what it represents. The problem with much didactic art is not that it contains lessons but that they are conveyed so crudely. Such works are not just bad art but poor pedagogical tools.

The relationship between intrinsic and extrinsic value is complex, and one of the problems of instrumentalisation is that it seeks to flatten and simplify it. It encourages us to identify what is most useful, and then separate it from, and prioritise it above, what is of ultimate value. In doing so, it often diminishes or destroys the very benefits it promises to maximise.

Take social connection. I have just heard of a study that says that doing anything -- even reading -- is better for us when we do it with others than alone. This message is now widely broadcast and understood, so people know that conviviality is important for their mental and physical health. But one of the most valuable features of friendship and community is how they take us out of concern for ourselves and make us more aware of the needs of others. To get the most out of socialising we need to do it in the right spirit, choosing to be with other people because we care for them and they for us, because we find them stimulating, because we enjoy being part of a collective experience or endeavour. So if we choose to mingle only for reasons of our personal wellbeing, we are probably not going to get the benefits that socialising usually brings.

Instrumentalisation has the illusion of efficiency because it promotes the direct pursuit of practical things that we all want. But often this turns out to be counterproductive. More often than not, you will fail to get the claimed benefits of an activity if getting them becomes your primary motivation. What look like shortcuts turn out to be short circuits, undermining what they seek to achieve.

If instrumentalisation is such a profound mistake, why have we made it? After all, we do not deliberately set out to strip meaning from our most valued activities or treat friends as psychic enhancers. Instrumentalisation has its roots in several connected features of Western modernity.

The Enlightenment brought to fruition an idea of the primacy of the sovereign, autonomous individual, one that had deep roots in classical and Christian thought. Over the centuries, this idea has become a kind of common sense. Each person is supposed to be the master of their own destiny, the author of their own life story. Self-expression and self-determination are seen as essential for being an authentic self.

Enlightenment thinkers were correct to promote greater individual freedom in an age when power was wielded by the few over a subjugated majority. But human beings are also social animals and can never be entirely autonomous. Modernity's mistake is to lose sight of this, placing all the emphasis on personal liberty and not enough on our interdependence. This has led to an exaggeration of the importance of autonomy that has pushed the prizing of individuality too far. The result is atomisation: a world in which our separateness from others has become excessive.

This atomised world has several features, all of which encourage instrumentalisation. First, it promotes an illusion of control. Encouraged to feel autonomous, we lose sight of the fact that there is much over which we have no power. The world unfolds, opening up opportunities and throwing spanners in the works in equally random measure. We are not even in full control of ourselves. We had no say in our fundamental constitutions: our dispositions, personalities, gifts and limitations. We have no direct access to the hidden springs of thought and volition and cannot just choose what we like or what we believe.

Behind much instrumentalisation is a crude reductionism that ignores systems and focuses on elements within it

But primed to think of ourselves as free and autonomous, we imagine that we can manipulate the world to achieve whatever we want. Happiness, health and success are all ours for the taking, just as long as we make the right choices. And so the world becomes a series of levers to be pulled and buttons to be pushed, all to yield to our wills. In short, everything can and must be a means to whatever ends we choose, because that is what we think self-determination requires.

In the era of late capitalism, our autonomous agency has increasingly been expressed through our status as consumers. Freedom is above all the choice of how to spend our money, with the promise that everything we need can be obtained in exchange for cash. The consumer mindset has affected how we relate to everything, not just the things we buy. The result is that the world has become essentially transactional, meaning that everything is an instrument for getting something else. It is no coincidence that dating apps give the impression that we are shopping for partners because we approach even relationships with the consumer framing. Politics has also become a trade for votes in which the electorate and politicians believe that the winner takes all, like the highest bidder in an auction, and damn those who backed the losing side. Democracy should be a way of managing competing demands, not giving the winners everything they want. Voting should be about having your say, not getting your way. But in the new consumer mindset, votes buy power, they no longer mandate responsibility.

Another deep cultural source of instrumentalisation is the reductionism that has surreptitiously seeped into our culture from natural science. Reductionism is the idea that the way to understand how things work is to break them down into their constitutive parts. It's an idea that served natural science well for centuries. But a clue as to its limitations comes in its relative failure in the social sciences. Economies, societies and psychologies cannot be explained by simple mechanistic processes. We have learned that, even in the natural sciences, you can explain only so much by taking things apart, and that it is equally -- sometimes more -- important to see how systems work as a whole.

Behind much instrumentalisation is a crude reductionism that ignores systems and focuses on elements within it. The richness of an experience, such as being in the outdoors, is reduced to a means to stimulate blood flow or release hormones. Art, which stirs a large variety of often conflicting emotions, is prized purely for its capacity to evoke certain good ones. Social bonds, which cause pain and heartache as well as joy, are reduced to sources of emotional support.

Combine an inflated belief in personal autonomy, a transactional consumer mentality and a reductionist attitude to how things work, and it is inevitable that we treat the world as a collection of resources we can plunder to promote our own wellbeing. The tragedy is that when we do so, we neglect rather than serve our deepest needs.

What would our culture look like if we were to reverse the instrumentalisation of everything? Of course, we would still do many things as means to ends. We would also be happy to agree that many of the good things in life bring us instrumental benefits too. But we would see these as welcome side-effects, not their purposes. A deinstrumentalised world would be one in which we would attend more to what is of value right here, right now.

Take friendship. The personal benefits we get from others are real, but they should not be the reason for being with them. Relationships are valuable because we value the people in them, not because spending time with them releases endorphins in our brains. David Hume corrected this error more than two centuries ago when he wrote: 'I feel a pleasure in doing good to my friend, because I love him; but do not love him for the sake of that pleasure.' To reject instrumentalisation is to understand that feeling good often follows from living well, but it is not what living well consists in.

To appreciate things for their own value instead of what they might bring us is liberating. It frees us from the internal pressure always to make sure that what we are doing serves some further purpose, to justify our days in terms of the future credits that we accrue from them. Living life to the full means fully appreciating what life brings, not trying to extract bankable benefits from it. It leaves us able to recognise that the good life is something we can live every day, in small ways as well as big. Most importantly, it tells us that the things and people we love are enough in and of themselves and don't need to serve any further function to justify devoting time and care on them. To be in this world realising that life is its own end is the key to attaining its fullness.
