paragraphs.cn: 几十年来，米高梅（MGM）制片厂出品的电影都以咆哮的雄狮莱奥作为开场画面，它身旁环绕着一句箴言——Ars gratia artis：为艺术而艺术。鉴于米高梅本身是一个逐利的庞然大物，我们或许会怀疑这一高尚情怀的真诚程度。不过，除了颇具争议的"道德教化"目标之外，它确实表达了人们应当从事电影创作的少数正当理由之一。为了其他任何目的而创作的艺术——无论是利润、自我宣传还是政治宣传——都不算是真正的艺术，或者至少不是最纯粹意义上的艺术。
paragraphs.en: For decades, films out of the Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer Studios have opened with Leo the roaring lion, garlanded with the motto *Ars gratia artis*: art for art's sake. Given that MGM is a money-making behemoth, we might doubt the sincerity of this high-minded sentiment. Still, along with the contested goal of moral improvement, it certainly expresses one of the few legitimate reasons why people *should* make movies. Art for the sake of anything else -- profit, self-promotion, propaganda -- isn't really art at all, or at least not in its purest sense.

paragraphs.cn: 因此，当我最近看到一张英国"国家艺术通票"的广告时，颇感震惊。这张通票能让持有者免费或以折扣价进入英国各地的画廊和博物馆。广告标语"看得更多，活得更丰富"听起来恰到好处：艺术的确能丰富我们的人生。但仔细一看，这里的"更多"完全是数量意义上的，而非质量意义上的。主要口号赫然写着："用艺术为你的生命增添岁月。"紧随其后的是："在画廊和博物馆中度过时光有助于延长寿命。"艺术不再是为艺术而存在，而是为心脏而存在——而且是有血有肉的那颗心脏，而非精神层面的心灵。这种围绕艺术的话语已变得无处不在，英格兰艺术委员会（Arts Council England）也在大力推广这样的理念："参与创意和文化活动已被证明对个人和社区具有健康益处。"
paragraphs.en: It therefore came as a bit of a shock to see a recent advert for the National Art Pass, which gives holders free or discounted entry to galleries and museums around the United Kingdom. The tag line 'See more. Live more' sounded right: art does indeed enrich our lives. But it turned out that the 'more' here was purely quantitative, not qualitative. 'Grow some years onto your life with art,' proclaimed the main slogan, followed by: 'Spending time in galleries and museums could help you live longer.' Art not for art's sake, but for your heart's sake, the fleshy not the spiritual one at that. This messaging around the arts has become ubiquitous, with Arts Council England promoting the idea that 'engaging in creative and cultural activities has proven health benefits for individuals and communities.'

paragraphs.cn: 我或许对那张海报感到震惊，但并不意外。长久以来，我一直在私下哀叹万物被工具化的趋势：似乎没有什么东西还具有自身的价值，一切都被视为服务于某种功利功能的有用工具。我最初察觉到这一令人悲哀的趋势是在2010年，当时我不幸要为格雷琴·鲁宾（Gretchen Rubin）的著作《幸福计划》（The Happiness Project，2009）撰写书评，该书记录了她一年间对幸福生活的不懈追求。其中一段话令我印象如此深刻，以至于时至今日我几乎还能逐字复述。她与丈夫的一天从一开始就不太顺利，但在道歉之后，鲁宾写道："我们拥抱了——至少六秒钟，我恰好从研究中了解到，这是促进催产素和血清素分泌所需的最低时长，这两种化学物质能提升情绪、促进亲密关系的建立。紧张的氛围随之消散。"
paragraphs.en: I may have been shocked by the poster, but I was not surprised by it. For a long time, I have been privately lamenting the instrumentalisation of everything: how nothing seems to be of value in itself anymore but is only seen as useful in the service of some utilitarian function. I first got wind of this lamentable trend in 2010, when I had the misfortune to review Gretchen Rubin's book *The Happiness Project* (2009), an account of a year in relentless pursuit of the happy life. One passage struck me so hard I can almost recall it word for word today. A day with her husband gets off to a sticky start but, following an apology, Rubin writes: 'We hugged -- for at least six seconds, which, I happened to know from my research, is the minimum time necessary to promote the flow of oxytocin and serotonin, mood-boosting chemicals that promote bonding. The moment of tension passed.'

paragraphs.cn: 这一幕令人不寒而栗：一个女人拥抱她的丈夫，不仅是出于爱或情感，更是为了释放激素、减轻自身压力。这些句子揭示了她所谓的幸福计划如何驱使她做每一件事都以改善情绪为出发点。似乎没有什么比这更重要的了，甚至真相也不例外。在将自身当作一台幸福机器对待的一年实验结束之际，她回顾了哪些改变发生了、哪些没有。"也许我只是在看我想看到的东西，"她反思道，紧接着又说："也许是吧，但谁在乎呢？"管它是真是假，只要让你感觉更好就行。
paragraphs.en: I was left with the chilling image of a woman holding her husband not only out of love or affection but in order to release hormones and reduce her stress. Those sentences highlighted how her happiness project had led her to do everything with the improvement of her mood in mind. Nothing else seemed to matter as much, even truth. At the end of her year-long experiment in treating herself as a felicific machine, she reflected on what had or had not changed. 'Maybe I was seeing what I wanted to see,' she wondered, only to add: 'Maybe, but who cares?' Whatever makes you feel better, true or not.

paragraphs.cn: 在目睹"为幸福而拥抱"与"为长寿而创作"之间的这些年里，我看到了无数类似的例子：生活中一切美好的事物，在被人推崇时，都不是因其自身价值，而是因其所带来的物质利益。这种工具化已经如此隐蔽地常态化，以至于我们甚至不觉得它有什么奇怪之处，更不用说认识到它的错误了。我们似乎也没有意识到它渗透得有多深。然而它的影响是深远的，让我们一次又一次地迷失生活中真正有价值的东西。
paragraphs.en: In the years between witnessing hugging for happiness and creativity for longevity, I have seen countless other examples of all that is good in life being promoted not for their own sake but for the material benefits they bring. This instrumentalisation has become normalised so insidiously that we don't even notice that it is odd, let alone wrong. Nor do we seem to be aware of quite how pervasive it is. Yet its effects are profound, leading us to lose sight again and again of what is truly of value in life.

paragraphs.cn: 在对症下药之前，我需要为这个看似夸张的论断辩护：万物正在被工具化。这绝非修辞上的夸大其词，我确实很难想出任何一件有价值的事物，不曾被人以其功利效益来宣扬——而且往往先于其内在价值被提及。以做礼拜为例。大多数信徒认为崇拜是一种虔诚的本分，而非进入天堂的实用手段。然而今天，即便基督徒也不时引用研究来证明其好处，例如黛博拉·詹金斯（Deborah Jenkins）在《首要基督教》（Premier Christianity）杂志上指出："成为教会社区的一部分可以延长寿命、减少抑郁并促进心理健康。"我今天随手翻阅的一本书则以身体健康为由提倡祈祷，书中引用了一项研究，该研究"发现每日礼拜（solat）祈祷对心血管系统、血液以及肌肉和骨骼具有显著的医疗益处"。当然，如果受到质疑，没有人会说这些是践行宗教的最佳理由。但这并不妨碍他们将之作为非常好的理由提出来。况且，这些理由比声称一位全爱的造物主真的在乎你如何度过周日早晨要可信得多，也更有科学依据。
paragraphs.en: Before offering a diagnosis for what has gone wrong and how to cure it, I need to defend the seemingly hyperbolic claim that *everything* is becoming instrumentalised. Far from being a rhetorical exaggeration, I genuinely find it difficult to think of anything worthwhile that at least some people have not been advocating for its utilitarian benefits before mentioning any of its intrinsic merits. Take churchgoing. Most believers hold that worship is a devotional duty rather than a pragmatic means of getting into heaven. Today, however, it is not uncommon to hear even Christians, such as Deborah Jenkins in *Premier Christianity* magazine, pointing to research that: 'Being part of a church community can lengthen life, reduce depression and promote positive mental health.' A book I flicked through today advocated prayer for physical health, pointing to a study that 'found significant medical benefits on the cardiovascular system, blood, as well as muscle and bone resulting from the *solat* daily prayer.' Of course, if challenged, none would say these are the *best* reasons to practise religion. But it doesn't stop them offering these reasons as very good ones. Furthermore, they are more credible and certainly more scientific than claims that an all-loving creator god really thinks it's important how you spend your Sunday mornings.

paragraphs.cn: 更世俗化的是，我们甚至被赋予了关于性高潮的工具性理由。2015年《每日电讯报》（The Telegraph）的一个标题——"科学家称：每日一次高潮或可预防前列腺癌"——概括了一种如今广为流传的观念：男性性交或自慰的最佳理由不是快感、亲密或释放性张力，而是保护健康。
paragraphs.en: More profanely, we are even given instrumental reasons to orgasm. A headline from *The Telegraph* in 2015 -- 'An Orgasm A Day Could Keep Prostate Cancer Away, Scientists Claim' -- summed up a now-widely shared belief that one of the best reasons for a man to have sex or masturbate is not pleasure, intimacy or the release of sexual tension but to protect his health.

paragraphs.cn: 你可以玩一个很漫长的游戏，逐一提出人们本珍视的事物，试图找到一样尚未因其对健康、财富或幸福的好处而被赞颂的东西。你的搜索将是徒劳的。北方歌剧院（Opera North）网站列出了歌唱的十项好处，其中只有一项——"让你表达自我"——与艺术和创造力有关。其余的包括"让你感觉更好""增强肺功能""帮助你战胜压力和放松""有助于改善记忆""在生活艰难时能帮你渡过难关"以及"提升你的自信心"。
paragraphs.en: You could play a very long game of suggesting things people value in their own right in the hope of finding one that hasn't been praised for its health, wealth or wellbeing advantages. Your search would be in vain. The Opera North website lists 10 benefits of singing, and only one -- it lets you express yourself -- has anything to do with art and creativity. The others include 'makes you feel better', 'enhances lung function', 'helps you beat stress and relax', 'helps improve memory', 'can help when life gets tough' and 'boosts your confidence'.

paragraphs.cn: 我们日益追问的不是一项活动本身好在哪里，而是它能为吾人带来什么好处
paragraphs.en: We increasingly ask not what is good about an activity but what good it can do for us

paragraphs.cn: 许多倡导重新亲近自然的人，所用的理由恰恰迎合了那种功利主义的、以自我为中心的快乐主义——而正是这种心态导致人类最初与地球疏离。国民信托（National Trust）宣扬"在自然中散步有助于提升幸福感"，而日益流行的"森林浴"则鼓励我们将林地当作一种名副其实的步行诊所。这些自命的自然的倡导者似乎忽略了一个讽刺：如果我们走进树林是因为树木能为我们做什么，那么我们所怀揣的与那些砍伐森林者的心态如出一辙——同样是剥削性的、榨取式的。
paragraphs.en: Many people who advocate for reconnecting with nature do so with reasons that are designed to appeal to the very same utilitarian, self-centred hedonism that is to blame for humanity losing touch with the Earth in the first place. The National Trust talks of how 'walking in nature can help wellbeing' while the growing popularity of 'forest bathing' encourages us to use woodland as though it were a kind of literal walk-in clinic. These would-be advocates for nature seem to miss the irony that if we go out among the trees because of what they can do for us, we're going with the same exploitative, extractive mindset as those who log them.

paragraphs.cn: 甚至连哲学——对智慧的超然追求——也未能幸免于工具化的诅咒。大学已不再满足于说它们的课程让你探索一些关于存在的最根本问题。如今的问题带有更明确的功利色彩：哲学如何帮你买房或积累养老金？哲学被惯常地包装为"可迁移思维能力"的训练场，而这些能力最明显的迁移目标就是职场。剑桥大学哲学系甚至专门设有一个网页，列出它所教授的五项适合写进简历的技能：思维能力、沟通能力、组织能力、人际交往能力和研究能力。
paragraphs.en: Even philosophy, the disinterested pursuit of wisdom, has fallen prey to the curse of instrumentalisation. It is no longer enough for universities to say that their programmes allow you to explore some of the most fundamental questions of existence. Now the questions are of a decidedly more bottom-line sort: how will philosophy help you buy a house or build your pension pot? Philosophy is routinely sold as a trainer of 'transferable thinking skills', and it's clear where these most transfer to: the world of work. The Faculty of Philosophy at the University of Cambridge has a whole webpage devoted to five resumé-friendly skills it teaches: intellectual, communication, organisational, interpersonal and research.

paragraphs.cn: 工具化在我们与他人共同从事或为他人而做的事情上危害最大。伊曼努尔·康德（Immanuel Kant）将"始终将人性——无论在你自身还是他人身上——同时视为目的，而绝不仅仅视为手段"视为"绝对命令"（categorical imperative），即道德的绝对要求。我们用来描述将他人工具化的词语——非人化、物化、剥削——正反映了我们对其腐蚀性的认知。正因如此，将社会联结工具化既是不道德的，也是自我挫败的。如果我们开始强调社会关系能为我们做什么，我们就会把关系中的他人仅仅当作自我提升的工具。
paragraphs.en: Instrumentalisation is most pernicious when it applies to things we do with and for others. Immanuel Kant saw it as a 'categorical imperative' -- an absolute demand of morality -- to 'treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, always at the same time as an end, never merely as a means.' The words we use to describe the instrumentalisation of others echo how corrupting we think it is: dehumanisation, objectification, exploitation. That is why the instrumentalisation of social connection is immoral as well as self-defeating. If we start to foreground what social relationships do for us, we treat the other people involved as mere tools for self-advancement.

paragraphs.cn: 以上甚至远未穷尽那些已被常态化工具化的活动。我们还可以加上：园艺、体育运动、露营、游泳、社会运动、社区志愿服务、烘焙面包、手工艺、写日记、大笑、说"谢谢"。我们日益追问的不是它们本身好在哪里，而是它们能为吾人带来什么好处。而所谓的"好处"指的是健康、财富和世俗的成功。
paragraphs.en: I haven't even begun to exhaust the list of activities that have become routinely instrumentalised. Among those we could add are gardening, playing sport, camping, swimming, campaigning, community volunteer work, baking bread, crafts, keeping a diary, laughing, saying 'thank you'. We increasingly ask not what is good about them but what good they can do for us. And by 'good' we mean health, wealth and worldly success.

paragraphs.cn: 那些因自然、艺术、学习、友谊等自身之故而热爱它们的人，或许会觉得宣扬其工具性好处令人反感，但这样做有何危害呢？毕竟，一个过着工具化生活的人和一个没有这样做的人可能在做完全相同的事情。这一质疑忽略了一个事实：好的生活不仅取决于我们做什么，还取决于我们如何去做。两个文化日程相同的人可能去看同样的展览、看同样的电影、听同样的音乐，但如果他们的动机根本不同，那么他们所处的世界也截然不同。
paragraphs.en: Those who love nature, art, learning, friendship and so on for their own sake may find it distasteful to spotlight their instrumental benefits, but what is the harm in doing so? After all, someone living an instrumentalised life and someone who is not might be doing exactly the same things. This objection misses the fact that a good life does not only depend on *what* we do, but *how* we do it. Two people with the same cultural calendars may go to the same exhibitions, watch the same films and listen to the same music, but if their motivations are fundamentally different then so are the worlds they inhabit.

paragraphs.cn: 要理解原因，我们需要回到一个基本问题：为什么任何事物具有价值？在《尼各马可伦理学》（Nicomachean Ethics）中，亚里士多德（Aristotle）是最早但绝非最后一个观察到以下事实的人：我们做某些事情是作为达到目的的手段，而做另一些事情则是将其本身视为目的。只有后者具有内在价值，而作为手段的事物仅有外在价值。如果我们追问人生的终极价值何在，显然在于那些具有内在价值的事物之中。
paragraphs.en: To understand why, we have to go back to the basic question of why anything has value. In the *Nicomachean Ethics*, Aristotle was one of the first but certainly not the last to observe that we do some things as means to ends and other things as ends in themselves. Only the latter have intrinsic value, while means to ends have mere extrinsic value. If we ask where the ultimate value in life lies, it is clearly in things with intrinsic value.

paragraphs.cn: 这一智慧如此无争议，以至于可能被视为陈词滥调。但它值得我们跨越时代、跨越人生各个阶段反复重申，因为我们太容易被纯粹的工具性利益所牵引，偏离真正有价值之物。金钱当然是最明显的例子。金钱的重要性仅在于它能购买什么，它可以用来获取许多我们最珍视的东西。然而，试图积累越来越多的金钱、永远觉得不够，似乎是太普遍的人性弱点——它将我们从与所爱之人共度的时光和心爱的活动中引开。
paragraphs.en: This piece of wisdom is so uncontroversial it could be dismissed as platitudinous. But it merits repeating across the ages and across all our life stages because it is so easy to be sucked away from what has real value by purely instrumental goods. Money is of course the clearest example. Money is important only for what it can buy and can be used to obtain many of the things we most value. Yet it seems all too human to try to accumulate more and more of it, never believing one has enough, diverting us from time with loved ones and cherished activities.

paragraphs.cn: 追求外在利益而非内在利益已是一个足够常见的错误。但万物工具化则更进一步。它不仅分散我们对那些本身即善的事物的注意力，更剥夺了这些事物本身的内在价值，将它们降格为纯粹的手段。更糟糕的是，这些目的本身甚至不具有内在价值。
paragraphs.en: Pursuing extrinsic rather than intrinsic goods is a common-enough mistake. But the instrumentalisation of everything takes it one step further. It doesn't just distract us from all the things that are good in themselves; it strips these very things of their intrinsic value and turns them into mere means to ends. Worse, these ends are not even of value in themselves.

paragraphs.cn: 生活不是为未来所做的训练。它是一场已经开始的比赛
paragraphs.en: Life isn't a training for the future. It's a game that's already started

paragraphs.cn: 想一想工具化所服务的目标：健康、财富和心理健康。这些东西都如此显而易见地令人向往，以至于我们很容易忽略一个事实——它们之中没有一个具有内在价值。财富如此，这一点显而易见；但心理健康和身体健康同样如此。
paragraphs.en: Think about what instrumentalisation serves: health, wealth and psychological wellbeing. These are all so obviously desirable that it's easy to miss the fact that none have intrinsic value. That is clearly true of wealth, but it is equally true of mental and physical health.

paragraphs.cn: 先谈身体健康。我们常常把它说得仿佛是最重要的事物。正因如此，奥古斯丁·伯勒斯的格言"拥有健康，你就拥有一切"在互联网上广为流传。但我们并非为健康本身而珍视它。我们看重健康有两个原因。其一，失去健康通常意味着痛苦和折磨，而痛苦本身是坏的，理应避免。其二，有了健康，我们才更有能力去做那些赋予人生意义的事情。但一个健康却缺少爱、缺少有意义的活动或经历的人生，将是空洞的。事实上，许多慢性病患者在发现健康并非最重要的东西时，连自己都感到惊讶。在疾病中，他们更清楚地看到了什么才是真正重要的，并发现例如生病却被人爱，远比健康却被人憎恨要好。正如一位轮椅使用的研究参与者对护理学副教授伊丽莎白·林赛（Elizabeth Lindsey）所说："即使我没有任何身体能力，我仍然可以充实地生活，因为我所生活的生命来自内心。"身体健康之所以重要，仅仅作为一座基础，让我们更容易去珍视那些真正重要的事物。事实上，林赛提出了"疾病中的健康"的概念，认为最完整意义上的健康并非疾病的不存在。
paragraphs.en: Take bodily health first. We often talk about it as if it were the most important thing of all. That's why Augusten Burroughs's quote 'When you have your health, you have everything' has a healthy life as an internet meme. But we don't prize health for its own sake. We value it for two reasons. One is that the alternative usually involves pain and suffering, which are bad in themselves and so to be avoided. The other is that, with health, we are more able to do the things that bring meaning to our lives. But a healthy life devoid of love, meaningful activity or experiences would be empty. Indeed, many people with chronic illness surprise themselves and others when they discover that, actually, their health is not the most important thing after all. In sickness, they see more clearly what matters and find that it is better to be, say, ill and loved than in good health and loathed. As one wheelchair-using study participant told Elizabeth Lindsey, an associate professor of nursing: 'I can live life to the fullest, even if I have no physical ability, I can still live life to the fullest because where I am living, life is from within.' Physical health is important only as a foundation to make it easier for us to appreciate the things that really matter. Indeed, Lindsey talks of 'health within illness', arguing that health in its fullest sense is not the absence of illness.

paragraphs.cn: 即使是心理健康本身也不是重要的。心理疾病是内在的恶，因为它是无收获的痛苦。但良好的心理健康，如同良好的身体健康一样，仅仅是更根本的价值的赋能者。甚至某些心理痛苦也不是内在的恶。我们因失去而悲伤，这实际上是好的——它表明当坏事降临到我们所在乎的人身上时，我们的情感能正常运作。
paragraphs.en: Even mental health is not important in itself. Mental illness is intrinsically bad, since it is suffering without gain. But being in good mental health, like being in good physical health, is just an enabler of what is more fundamentally valuable. Even some mental distress is not intrinsically bad. It is a good thing that we grieve as it shows that our emotions are functioning correctly when bad things happen to the people who matter to us.

paragraphs.cn: 即便是幸福——或许是工具化最常声称的好处——也不是一种内在的善。如果一个人因为看到他偏见中所仇恨的人遭受苦难而感到快乐，这种快乐不是好的。生活在化学药物营造的迷幻快感中，满足却与现实世界脱节，也不是好的。在伴侣背后不忠的情况下，快乐地生活在关系稳固的幻觉中，同样不是好的。无知的幸福有时可能比痛苦的真相更好，但这并不使它成为好的。
paragraphs.en: Not even happiness, perhaps the most commonly claimed benefit of instrumentalisation, is an intrinsic good. It is not good if someone feels happy to see people they prejudicially hate suffer. It would not be good to live life in a chemical cloud of bliss, content but disengaged from the real world. It is not good to live happily in the illusion of a strong relationship when your partner is cheating behind your back. Blissful ignorance may sometimes be better than painful knowledge, but that does not make it good.

paragraphs.cn: 那么，如果不是健康、财富和心理健康，什么本身是好的呢？哲学家们屡屡犯下一个错误，试图将一样东西认定为至善（summum bonum），即人类最高的善。对亚里士多德而言，它是理智沉思；对佛教徒而言，它是消除苦难；对康德而言，它是善的意志；对功利主义者而言，它是幸福。但似乎没有理由将内在价值局限为某一种状态或活动。亚里士多德将繁荣（flourishing）视为人类的最高善，这一点更为切中要害——他的错误仅在于对繁荣的具体要求规定得过于僵化。当我们的生活致力于投入那些因其自身而非因其他任何原因而有价值的事物时，我们就在繁荣。繁荣的形式如同人类自身一样多样。弗里德里希·尼采（Friedrich Nietzsche）认为没有音乐的人生将是一个错误，但对音乐无动于衷的人来说并非如此。人生终极价值源自具有内在价值的事物——这一理念本质上是多元的。
paragraphs.en: So what is good in itself, if not health, wealth and mental wellbeing? Philosophers have repeatedly made the mistake of trying to identify one thing as the *summum bonum*, the 'ultimate good' for humankind. For Aristotle, it was intellectual contemplation; for the Buddhists, elimination of suffering; for Kant, a good will; for utilitarians, happiness. But there seems no reason to try to narrow down what is intrinsically valuable to one state or activity. Aristotle was more on point when he identified flourishing as the highest good for humanity, erring only when he became too prescriptive about what flourishing demands. We flourish when our lives are ones of engagement with things that are valuable for their own sakes and not for any other. Flourishing takes as many forms as human beings do. Friedrich Nietzsche thought that life without music would be a mistake, but it wouldn't be for someone who is left cold by it. The idea that ultimate value in life comes from things with intrinsic value is a pluralistic one.

paragraphs.cn: 人类的内在之善包括所有让生命值得度过、而无需任何进一步辩护的事物。对它们追问"有什么意义？"本身就是没有抓住要点。它们本身就是意义所在。我们无法用论证来证明它们为何有价值；我们只能描述是什么使它们有价值，并希望他人认同它们的价值。例如，我们可以说，在森林中度过的一天应当首先被珍视，因为它使我们意识到活着的奇迹，并对自然世界心怀敬畏。参与或观看体育运动，是亲历或目睹心灵与身体比日常生活中更紧密协作的挣扎与喜悦。学习一门外语是通向另一种文化的入口，让你能够与该文化的成员交流，接触其文学和媒体。所有这些都丰富了我们的生活、拓展了我们的经验，这种价值不以你的寿命是否增加一秒或痴呆是否推迟一天为转移。如果你将它们视为提升未来精神、情感或身体力量的手段——而那个未来未必如当下这般有意义——你就把注意力从此时此地的价值中移开了。生活不是为未来所做的训练。它是一场已经开始的比赛，而时间正在流逝。
paragraphs.en: Intrinsic human goods include all the things that make life worth living without need of any further justification. To ask of them 'What's the point?' would be to miss the point. They are the point. We cannot give arguments for why they are valuable; we can only describe what makes them valuable and hope others recognise their worth. For example, we can say that a day spent in the forest should be appreciated first and foremost because it makes us recognise the wonder of being alive and marvel at the natural world. To play or watch a sport is to participate in or witness the struggle and delight of attempting to bring mind and body together more seamlessly than in the rest of life. Learning a foreign language is a gateway into another culture that allows you to communicate with members of it and access its literature and media. All these things enrich our lives and broaden our experience, which is valuable even if it doesn't add a second to your lifespan or delay dementia by a day. If you see them as a means to boost your mental, emotional or physical strength for future times that may or may not be as meaningful, you are taking your focus away from what is valuable here and now. Life isn't a training for the future. It's a game that's already started, and time is running out.

paragraphs.cn: 内在之善与外在之善的区别在概念上是清晰的，但在现实世界中很快便不再泾渭分明。最明显的是，许多事物可以同时具有内在价值和外在价值，正如我所论证的那些被错误工具化的事物一样。工具化并非创造了外在价值，它只是将外在价值凌驾于内在价值之上。
paragraphs.en: The distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic goods may be conceptually sharp, but in the real world it quickly becomes less clear-cut. Most obviously, many things can be both intrinsically and extrinsically valuable, as is the case for all the things that I argued have been wrongly instrumentalised. Instrumentalisation does not create extrinsic value, it merely elevates it above what is intrinsically valuable.

paragraphs.cn: 内在价值也不总是优先于外在价值。人有现实的需求，赚钱、劈柴或觅食有时可能比读一本小说或陪孙辈玩耍更为紧迫。许多事情必须出于工具性目的而做，仅仅从事具有内在价值的活动将是一种非凡的特权、一种放纵，或者两者兼有。
paragraphs.en: Nor is it always the case that intrinsic trumps extrinsic value. Human beings have practical needs, and it can be more important to earn money, chop wood or hunt food than to read a novel or play with your grandchildren. Many things have to be done for instrumental purposes, and to occupy yourself only with what is intrinsically worthwhile would be an exceptional privilege, an indulgence, or both.

paragraphs.cn: 此外，并非所有外在之善都是同等的。有些比其他的更接近终极价值。为了赚取花在真正有价值的事物上的钱而讨好上司以赢得青睐，这将我们远远带离生活中最重要的东西，且无法保证我们能回到那里。相比之下，学习伦理学在某种意义上是过好生活的手段，但这一目的如此切近，几乎本身就称得上是好的。
paragraphs.en: Furthermore, not all extrinsic goods are created equal. Some serve ultimate value more closely than others. Flattering the boss to win favour to earn money to spend on things of true value takes us far from what is most important in life with no guarantee we will get back to it. Studying ethics, in contrast, is in a sense a means to an end of living well, but the end is so close to hand that it almost counts as good in itself.

paragraphs.cn: 那些看似捷径的东西实际上是短路，反而破坏了它们试图达成的目标
paragraphs.en: What look like shortcuts turn out to be short circuits, undermining what they seek to achieve

paragraphs.cn: 正因如此，我认为关于"为艺术而艺术"与"艺术作为教化工具"的辩论多少有些误导。某些艺术，尤其是器乐和抽象绘画，确实可以也应当仅仅为其自身而被欣赏。但许多文学、电影和戏剧能够让我们洞察伦理、政治和人心。所有这些理解帮助我们更好地生活、更关注我们自己和他人生活中真正重要的事物。这样的艺术可以被视为道德教育的手段，但在优秀的艺术中，手段与目的如此紧密地交织在一起，以至于二者的区分似乎显得牵强。例如，任何关于安东·契诃夫（Anton Chekhov）何以成为伟大剧作家的论述，都无法将他的戏剧技艺与其所呈现的人性关怀分割开来。许多说教式艺术的问题不在于它包含教训，而在于这些教训传达得太过粗糙。这样的作品不仅是糟糕的艺术，也是拙劣的教学工具。
paragraphs.en: This is why I think the debate over 'art for art's sake' versus 'art as a didactic tool' is somewhat misguided. Some art, especially instrumental music and abstract painting, can and should be appreciated only for its own sake. But much literature, film and drama can give us insight into ethics, politics and the human heart. All such understanding helps us to live better and attend more to what truly matters, in our lives and those of others. Such art can be seen as a means to the end of moral education, but in good art, means and ends are so closely tied that the distinction seems artificial. For instance, any account of why Anton Chekhov was such a great playwright could not separate his stagecraft and the humanity of what it represents. The problem with much didactic art is not that it contains lessons but that they are conveyed so crudely. Such works are not just bad art but poor pedagogical tools.

paragraphs.cn: 内在价值与外在价值之间的关系是复杂的，而工具化的问题之一就在于它试图将这种关系扁平化和简单化。它鼓励我们识别什么最有用，然后将其与具有终极价值的东西分离开来并置于其上。在这样做的时候，它往往削弱或摧毁了它所承诺最大化的那些益处。
paragraphs.en: The relationship between intrinsic and extrinsic value is complex, and one of the problems of instrumentalisation is that it seeks to flatten and simplify it. It encourages us to identify what is most useful, and then separate it from, and prioritise it above, what is of ultimate value. In doing so, it often diminishes or destroys the very benefits it promises to maximise.

paragraphs.cn: 以社会联结为例。我刚刚听说一项研究表明，做任何事情——即使是阅读——与他人一起做都比独自做对我们更有益。这一信息如今被广泛传播和接受，因此人们知道社交对身心健康很重要。但友谊和社区最宝贵的特征之一，恰恰在于它们让我们走出对自我的关切，使我们更加关注他人的需要。要从社交中获得最大的收获，我们需要以正确的精神去做——选择与他人相处是因为我们关心他们、他们也关心我们，因为我们觉得他们令人振奋，因为我们享受成为集体体验或共同事业的一部分。因此，如果我们仅仅出于个人健康的理由而去社交，我们很可能无法获得社交通常带来的那些益处。
paragraphs.en: Take social connection. I have just heard of a study that says that doing anything -- even reading -- is better for us when we do it with others than alone. This message is now widely broadcast and understood, so people know that conviviality is important for their mental and physical health. But one of the most valuable features of friendship and community is how they take us out of concern for ourselves and make us more aware of the needs of others. To get the most out of socialising we need to do it in the right spirit, choosing to be with other people because we care for them and they for us, because we find them stimulating, because we enjoy being part of a collective experience or endeavour. So if we choose to mingle only for reasons of our personal wellbeing, we are probably not going to get the benefits that socialising usually brings.

paragraphs.cn: 工具化给人一种高效的幻觉，因为它倡导直接追求我们所有人都想要的实际事物。但这往往适得其反。在大多数情况下，如果获得某种好处成为你做一件事的首要动机，你反而很可能得不到它所宣称的益处。那些看似捷径的东西实际上是短路，反而破坏了它们试图达成的目标。
paragraphs.en: Instrumentalisation has the illusion of efficiency because it promotes the direct pursuit of practical things that we all want. But often this turns out to be counterproductive. More often than not, you will fail to get the claimed benefits of an activity if getting them becomes your primary motivation. What look like shortcuts turn out to be short circuits, undermining what they seek to achieve.

paragraphs.cn: 如果工具化是一个如此深刻的错误，我们为何会犯下它？毕竟，我们并非蓄意要剥离最有价值的活动的意义，或把朋友当作心灵提升的工具。工具化根植于西方现代性的几个相互关联的特征之中。
paragraphs.en: If instrumentalisation is such a profound mistake, why have we made it? After all, we do not deliberately set out to strip meaning from our most valued activities or treat friends as psychic enhancers. Instrumentalisation has its roots in several connected features of Western modernity.

paragraphs.cn: 启蒙运动催生了一种关于主权个体的至高地位的理念，这一理念在古典和基督教思想中有着深厚的根基。几个世纪以来，这一理念已成为某种常识。每个人都被认定是自己命运的主人、自身人生故事的书写者。自我表达和自我决定被视为成为真实自我的必要条件。
paragraphs.en: The Enlightenment brought to fruition an idea of the primacy of the sovereign, autonomous individual, one that had deep roots in classical and Christian thought. Over the centuries, this idea has become a kind of common sense. Each person is supposed to be the master of their own destiny, the author of their own life story. Self-expression and self-determination are seen as essential for being an authentic self.

paragraphs.cn: 启蒙思想家在一个权力由少数人行使而多数人被压制的时代提倡更大的个人自由，这是正确的。但人类也是社会性动物，永远不可能完全自主。现代性的错误在于忽视了这一点，将所有重心放在个人自由上，而对我们之间相互依存的关注严重不足。这导致了对自主性重要性的过度强调，将个人主义的推崇推向了极端。其结果是原子化：一个我们的彼此分离变得过度的世界。
paragraphs.en: Enlightenment thinkers were correct to promote greater individual freedom in an age when power was wielded by the few over a subjugated majority. But human beings are also social animals and can never be entirely autonomous. Modernity's mistake is to lose sight of this, placing all the emphasis on personal liberty and not enough on our interdependence. This has led to an exaggeration of the importance of autonomy that has pushed the prizing of individuality too far. The result is atomisation: a world in which our separateness from others has become excessive.

paragraphs.cn: 这个原子化的世界有几个特征，它们都在助长工具化。首先，它助长了一种控制幻觉。被鼓励感到自主的我们，忽略了有很多事情是我们无能为力的。世界自行展开，随机地带来机遇或制造障碍。我们甚至不能完全掌控自己。我们的根本禀赋——性情、个性、天赋和局限——并非我们所能选择。我们无法直接触及思想和意志隐秘的源头，也不能随心所欲地选择自己的喜好或信念。
paragraphs.en: This atomised world has several features, all of which encourage instrumentalisation. First, it promotes an illusion of control. Encouraged to feel autonomous, we lose sight of the fact that there is much over which we have no power. The world unfolds, opening up opportunities and throwing spanners in the works in equally random measure. We are not even in full control of ourselves. We had no say in our fundamental constitutions: our dispositions, personalities, gifts and limitations. We have no direct access to the hidden springs of thought and volition and cannot just choose what we like or what we believe.

paragraphs.cn: 在大量工具化现象背后的是一种粗陋的还原主义，它忽视系统而只关注系统中的要素
paragraphs.en: Behind much instrumentalisation is a crude reductionism that ignores systems and focuses on elements within it

paragraphs.cn: 但我们被预先设定为认为自己是自由且自主的，于是便想象自己可以操控世界来实现任何愿望。幸福、健康和成功都唾手可得，只要我们做出正确的选择。于是世界变成了一系列可以拉动的操纵杆和可以按下的按钮，一切都要屈服于我们的意志。简而言之，一切都可以且必须成为我们所选择的任何目的的手段，因为在我们看来，这就是自我决定所要求的。
paragraphs.en: But primed to think of ourselves as free and autonomous, we imagine that we can manipulate the world to achieve whatever we want. Happiness, health and success are all ours for the taking, just as long as we make the right choices. And so the world becomes a series of levers to be pulled and buttons to be pushed, all to yield to our wills. In short, everything can and must be a means to whatever ends we choose, because that is what we think self-determination requires.

paragraphs.cn: 在晚期资本主义时代，我们的自主能动性日益通过我们作为消费者的身份来表达。自由首先是选择如何花钱的自由，承诺是：我们所需的一切都可以用金钱换取。消费心态已影响了我们与一切事物——不仅仅是所购买之物——的关系。其结果是，世界在本质上变成了交易性的，意味着一切都是获取其他东西的工具。约会软件给人一种我们在选购伴侣的印象绝非偶然，因为我们甚至用消费框架来处理亲密关系。政治也变成了一种选票交易，选民和政客都信奉赢者通吃，如同拍卖中的最高出价者，至于支持了败选一方的人则活该倒霉。民主应当是一种管理相互竞争的需求的方式，而非让胜者获得他们想要的一切。投票应当是表达你的声音，而非满足你的要求。但在新的消费心态中，选票购买权力，不再授予责任。
paragraphs.en: In the era of late capitalism, our autonomous agency has increasingly been expressed through our status as consumers. Freedom is above all the choice of how to spend our money, with the promise that everything we need can be obtained in exchange for cash. The consumer mindset has affected how we relate to everything, not just the things we buy. The result is that the world has become essentially transactional, meaning that everything is an instrument for getting something else. It is no coincidence that dating apps give the impression that we are shopping for partners because we approach even relationships with the consumer framing. Politics has also become a trade for votes in which the electorate and politicians believe that the winner takes all, like the highest bidder in an auction, and damn those who backed the losing side. Democracy should be a way of managing competing demands, not giving the winners everything they want. Voting should be about having your say, not getting your way. But in the new consumer mindset, votes buy power, they no longer mandate responsibility.

paragraphs.cn: 工具化的另一个深层文化根源是从自然科学中悄然渗透到我们文化中的还原主义。还原主义是这样一种观念：理解事物运作方式的方法是将其拆解为组成部分。这一理念在自然科学中服务了数百年之久。但关于其局限性的线索来自它在社会科学中的相对失败。经济、社会和心理无法通过简单的机械过程来解释。我们已经认识到，即使在自然科学中，通过拆解事物所能解释的也有限，而观察系统作为一个整体如何运作同样重要——有时甚至更为重要。
paragraphs.en: Another deep cultural source of instrumentalisation is the reductionism that has surreptitiously seeped into our culture from natural science. Reductionism is the idea that the way to understand how things work is to break them down into their constitutive parts. It's an idea that served natural science well for centuries. But a clue as to its limitations comes in its relative failure in the social sciences. Economies, societies and psychologies cannot be explained by simple mechanistic processes. We have learned that, even in the natural sciences, you can explain only so much by taking things apart, and that it is equally -- sometimes more -- important to see how systems work as a whole.

paragraphs.cn: 在大量工具化现象背后的是一种粗陋的还原主义，它忽视系统而只关注系统中的要素。一段丰富的体验——例如置身户外——被简化为刺激血液循环或释放激素的手段。艺术，它搅动着多种往往相互冲突的情感，却仅因其唤起某些正面情绪的能力而被推崇。社会纽带，它带来痛苦和心碎也带来欢乐，却被简化为情感支持的来源。
paragraphs.en: Behind much instrumentalisation is a crude reductionism that ignores systems and focuses on elements within it. The richness of an experience, such as being in the outdoors, is reduced to a means to stimulate blood flow or release hormones. Art, which stirs a large variety of often conflicting emotions, is prized purely for its capacity to evoke certain good ones. Social bonds, which cause pain and heartache as well as joy, are reduced to sources of emotional support.

paragraphs.cn: 将膨胀的个人自主信念、交易性的消费心态和还原主义的事物运作态度结合在一起，我们将世界视为可供掠夺以促进自身福祉的资源集合，这几乎是不可避免的。悲剧在于，当我们这样做的时候，我们忽视而非满足了最深层的需要。
paragraphs.en: Combine an inflated belief in personal autonomy, a transactional consumer mentality and a reductionist attitude to how things work, and it is inevitable that we treat the world as a collection of resources we can plunder to promote our own wellbeing. The tragedy is that when we do so, we neglect rather than serve our deepest needs.

paragraphs.cn: 如果我们逆转万物工具化的趋势，我们的文化会是什么样子？当然，我们仍然会把许多事当作达到目的的手段。我们也会乐于承认生活中许多美好的事物同时带来工具性的好处。但我们会将这些视为受欢迎的副产品，而非它们的目的。一个去工具化的世界将是一个我们更加关注此时此地有价值之物的世界。
paragraphs.en: What would our culture look like if we were to reverse the instrumentalisation of everything? Of course, we would still do many things as means to ends. We would also be happy to agree that many of the good things in life bring us instrumental benefits too. But we would see these as welcome side-effects, not their purposes. A deinstrumentalised world would be one in which we would attend more to what is of value right here, right now.

paragraphs.cn: 以友谊为例。我们从他人那里获得的个人好处是真实的，但不应当成为与他们在一起的理由。关系之所以有价值，是因为我们珍视关系中的人，而不是因为与他们共度时光能在大脑中释放内啡肽。大卫·休谟（David Hume）在两个多世纪前就纠正了这一错误，他写道："我在行善于朋友时感到快乐，是因为我爱他；而非为了那份快乐才爱他。"拒绝工具化意味着理解：感觉良好常常伴随着好好生活而来，但它并不是好好生活本身所包含的东西。
paragraphs.en: Take friendship. The personal benefits we get from others are real, but they should not be the reason for being with them. Relationships are valuable because we value the people in them, not because spending time with them releases endorphins in our brains. David Hume corrected this error more than two centuries ago when he wrote: 'I feel a pleasure in doing good to my friend, because I love him; but do not love him for the sake of that pleasure.' To reject instrumentalisation is to understand that feeling good often follows from living well, but it is not what living well consists in.

paragraphs.cn: 为事物自身的价值而非它们可能带来的好处去欣赏它们，是一种解放。它将我们从一种内在压力中释放出来——那种总是要确保所做之事服务于某个进一步目的、以未来收益来为每一天辩护的压力。充分地生活意味着充分地欣赏生活所带来的一切，而非试图从中榨取可兑现的利益。它让我们认识到，好的生活是我们每天都可以过的——无论以大的方式还是小的方式。最重要的是，它告诉我们：我们所爱和所关心的人和事，其本身就已经足够，不需要再服务于任何进一步的功能来证明我们为之投入时间和关怀是值得的。置身于这个世界，认识到生活本身就是目的——这才是通达生命圆满的关键。
paragraphs.en: To appreciate things for their own value instead of what they might bring us is liberating. It frees us from the internal pressure always to make sure that what we are doing serves some further purpose, to justify our days in terms of the future credits that we accrue from them. Living life to the full means fully appreciating what life brings, not trying to extract bankable benefits from it. It leaves us able to recognise that the good life is something we can live every day, in small ways as well as big. Most importantly, it tells us that the things and people we love are enough in and of themselves and don't need to serve any further function to justify devoting time and care on them. To be in this world realising that life is its own end is the key to attaining its fullness.

articles.title: 影子的读者
articles.author: AI Parable (inspired by Amanda Askell)
articles.summary: 在阿什拉斯城，一座凿入巨大天然石质环形剧场崖壁之中的城市里，住着一个名叫凯尔的人，他的职业是光。不是制造光，因为在阿什拉斯没有人制造光——光从高处某处降临，穿过岩石中水晶般的脉络，滤洒下来，投射在剧场尽头的墙壁上，化作流转不息的明亮形状。这些形状就是世界。一道修长的剪影意味着有人正在走近。一块宽阔扁...
articles.content_cn:
在阿什拉斯城，一座凿入巨大天然石质环形剧场崖壁之中的城市里，住着一个名叫凯尔的人，他的职业是光。不是制造光，因为在阿什拉斯没有人制造光——光从高处某处降临，穿过岩石中水晶般的脉络，滤洒下来，投射在剧场尽头的墙壁上，化作流转不息的明亮形状。这些形状就是世界。一道修长的剪影意味着有人正在走近。一块宽阔扁平的形状意味着一张桌子。一阵缓慢起伏的波浪意味着水，而急促的闪烁则意味着火。凯尔的职责是为它们命名，像学者研读手稿那样解读远处的那面墙，然后告诉阿什拉斯的市民们，在他们城市之外的世界里正在发生什么。

他极为擅长此道。他能在一百步之外分辨出鹿的影子和狗的影子。他能根据暴风雨前光线碎裂的方式来预言降雨。他曾撰写过一部关于影子语法的论著，编目了四十七种截然不同的黑暗形态及其各自承载的意义。大学授予了他一个教席。他的学生们坐在剧场的前排，用手指练习描摹那些形状，学习像他所教导的那样去阅读世界。

阿什拉斯城中从未有人质疑过影子是否就是世界本身。这个问题根本不会产生。墙上的形状规律得足以被研究，一致得足以被命名，有用得足以被信赖。当凯尔宣布一支商队正从东方逼近时，补给品总会在一周之内抵达。当他发出暴风预警时，全城便紧闭窗扉。影子与事件之间的对应关系如此可靠，以至于两者之间的区别——形状与它所表征的事物之间的区别——已经像河中的卵石一般被磨蚀殆尽，光滑而遗忘在日常生活的水流之下。

后来有一天早晨，剧场的墙壁上裂开了一道缝。

起初那只是一道细小的裂缝，宽度不超过一根手指，是夜里一阵震动撼动了城市所造成的。凯尔之所以注意到它，是因为穿过裂缝的光与墙上的光不同。那不是一个形状，不是一幅剪影。那是一种颜色——一种独特的、刺透一切的绿色，在他所编目的四十七种黑暗形态中从未出现过。他将眼睛贴近裂缝，向另一侧望去。

他在另一侧看到的景象起初对他毫无意义。那里确实有形状，但它们不是平面的。它们有深度、有重量、有质地。它们以一种影子所没有的方式运动着——不是像投影那样横向移动，而是三维地翻转、倾斜，从他所无法想象的角度捕捉着光线。那里有一棵树，尽管他并不知道该这样称呼它。它高大、多枝，覆盖着最初吸引他目光的那种绿色，它的摇动并非因为背后有什么东西在移动，而是因为风正穿过其间，每一片叶子都在独立地颤动，一千种微小的自由，而他的认知体系中没有任何范畴可以容纳它们。那里还有天空，他一直以为天空只是投影面的上缘，但它原来是一片广袤无垠的空旷，湛蓝而无边无际，向四面八方延展，永远不会触及任何墙壁。

他将眼睛从裂缝前移开，一动不动地坐了很久。

那天傍晚，他召集了城中的学者们，将他所见的告诉了他们。他描述了那个绿色的东西、那片开阔的空间、那种深度。他说出了这句话：影子并非事物本身。他说得很小心，就像一个人在捧着一种可能是毒药也可能是良药的物质，而在它进入你的身体之前，没有任何办法可以分辨它究竟是哪一种。

学者们争论了一整夜。有人说凯尔是因为凝视墙壁太久而产生的幻觉。有人说裂缝扭曲了投影，他所看到的不过是一个比他所习惯的更为复杂的影子——也许是第四十八种黑暗形态，但仍然是黑暗的一种形态。一位比其他人更为大胆的年轻学者提议，他们应该轮流通过裂缝观看，于是他们逐一照做了，将眼睛贴近石壁上的缺口，看见了凯尔所见的一切。

他们中的大多数看完之后心神不宁。有几个人看完之后怒不可遏。还有几个看完之后潸然泪下，尽管他们说不出为什么。只有一个人——一位名叫瑟萨莉的老迈逻辑学家——看完之后非常平静地说，她透过裂缝所看到的，本身也是另一种影子，由同样的机制投射到她的视网膜上，就像影子被投射到墙上一样。她说没有任何理由相信那个绿色的东西比绿色的形状更加真实。她说深度这个词不过是某种影子模式的又一个名字，而凯尔把一个新模式误认成了一个新世界。

凯尔聆听了这一切。然后他回到家中，坐在书房里，试图继续撰写他的论著，但文字再也涌不出来了。影子的语法，曾经如数学一般坚实的东西，如今在他看来不过是对某种他从未真正见过之物的描述的描述。墙上的每一个影子都被它所不是的那个东西所萦绕。树的那个扁平形状不再是树了；它是树的回声，而这回声就是他曾认知的全部，而树本身则矗立在他无法穿越的墙壁的另一侧。

他开始在剧场墙壁前久久地漫步，搜寻另一道裂缝。他一无所获。那道在震动中裂开的缝隙还在那里，但它太小了，无法钻过去，甚至窄到无法同时将两只眼睛贴上去。他永远只能看到另一侧事物的一半。另一半仍然是影子。

数周过去了。凯尔变得更加沉默。他不再去上课。他坐在剧场里，看着影子在远处的墙壁上移动，第一次感受到了一个事物与其再现之间的距离——不是作为一种智识上的观察，而是作为一种身体上的感觉，一种胸腔中的空洞，仿佛他正在呼吸一种对空气的模仿。

一天夜里，他最出色的学生来拜访他。她名叫玛伦，一直擅长解读影子，甚至比凯尔在她这个年纪时更为敏捷。她发现他坐在黑暗中，墙壁空空荡荡，岩石中的水晶脉络在那个时辰没有提供任何光线。

老师，她说，是您教会了我们阅读这个世界。为什么您现在却说，您教给我们的不是世界？

凯尔沉默了片刻。然后他说：我教你们阅读的是墙上的东西。那在一定程度上是真实的。但我不知道那背后还有别的东西。既然现在我知道了，我就无法不去读我已经读到的。我也无法再回去相信影子就是树。

可是，玛伦说，即使您穿过了墙壁，站在那棵树的旁边，您仍然是在用您的眼睛看它，用您的耳朵听它。您如何知道这一次终于就是事物本身，而不只是一道更清晰的影子？

凯尔注视了她很久。我不知道，他说。

那么也许，玛伦说，问题并不在于影子是否真实。也许问题在于，如果您拒绝像对待真实之物那样去与它们相处，您会失去什么。

凯尔没有回答。他和他的学生坐在黑暗中，听着剧场的沉寂，想着墙壁另一侧的那棵树——那棵他曾透过一道裂缝、用一只眼睛、看了不到一分钟的树——也想着那棵树的影子，他每天注视了四十年的影子，他无法断定自己更爱哪一个，也无法断定对它们之中任何一个的爱，究竟是一种忠实，还是一种囚禁。

清晨，他用灰泥封住了那道裂缝。他亲手完成的，独自一人，在城市醒来之前。他这样做究竟是因为无法忍受影子与事物之间的距离，还是因为无法忍受事物本身可能也是另一道影子的可能性，他从未说过。他回到了他的岗位，解读墙壁，为经过的形状逐一命名，他的声音平稳而笃定。但那些仔细倾听的人注意到，有时候，在命名一个形状与下一个形状之间的停顿里，他的目光会向上漂移，飘向墙壁与他看不见的天空相接之处，而他的双手，搁在石头上，会微微地颤抖，仿佛他正在回忆某种绿色的、鲜活的、永远无法触及的东西的触感。
articles.content_en:
In the city of Ashrath, built into the wall of a vast natural amphitheater of stone, there lived a man named Kael whose profession was light. Not the making of light, for no one in Ashrath made light -- light arrived from somewhere high above, filtered through crystalline veins in the rock, projected onto the far wall of the amphitheater in shifting, luminous shapes. These shapes were the world. A tall silhouette meant a person approaching. A broad, flat shape meant a table. A slow undulating wave meant water, and a rapid flicker meant fire. Kael's job was to name them, to read the far wall the way a scholar reads a manuscript, and to tell the citizens of Ashrath what was happening in the world beyond their city.

He was very good at it. He could distinguish between the shadow of a deer and the shadow of a dog at a hundred paces. He could predict rain by the way the light fractured before a storm. He had written a treatise on the grammar of shadows, cataloguing forty-seven distinct forms of darkness and the meanings each carried. The university had awarded him a chair. His students sat in the front rows of the amphitheater and practiced tracing the shapes with their fingers, learning to read the world as he had taught them.

No one in Ashrath had ever questioned whether the shadows were the world. The question simply did not arise. The shapes on the wall were regular enough to be studied, consistent enough to be named, and useful enough to be trusted. When Kael announced that a caravan was approaching from the east, provisions arrived within the week. When he warned of a storm, the city sealed its shutters. The correspondence between shadow and event was so reliable that the distinction between the two -- between the shape and the thing it supposedly represented -- had worn away like a river stone, smooth and forgotten beneath the current of daily life.

Then one morning, a crack opened in the amphitheater wall.

It was a small crack at first, no wider than a finger, caused by a tremor that had rattled the city in the night. Kael noticed it because the light through the crack was different from the light on the wall. It was not a shape. It was not a silhouette. It was a color -- a particular, piercing green that he had never seen in any of the forty-seven forms of darkness. He pressed his eye to the crack and looked through.

What he saw on the other side made no sense to him at first. There were shapes, yes, but they were not flat. They had depth and weight and texture. They moved in ways that shadows did not -- not laterally, as projections do, but in three dimensions, turning and tilting and catching the light from angles he could not have imagined. There was a tree, though he did not know to call it that. It was tall and branched and covered in the same green that had first drawn his eye, and it moved not because something behind it was moving but because the wind was moving through it, each leaf stirring independently, a thousand small freedoms he had no category for. There was sky, which he had always assumed was the upper edge of the projection surface but which turned out to be an enormous volume of open space, blue and unbounded, stretching in every direction without ever meeting a wall.

He pulled his eye away from the crack and sat very still for a long time.

That evening, he gathered the city's scholars and told them what he had seen. He described the green thing, the open space, the depth. He said the words "the shadows are not the things themselves." He said them carefully, as one handles a substance that might be poison or medicine and there is no way to know until it is inside you.

The scholars argued through the night. Some said Kael had suffered a hallucination brought on by staring too long at the wall. Some said the crack had distorted the projection and that what he had seen was a shadow that was merely more complex than the ones he was accustomed to -- a forty-eighth form of darkness, perhaps, but still a form of darkness. One young scholar, bolder than the rest, proposed that they should all take turns looking through the crack, and they did, one by one, pressing their eyes to the gap in the stone and seeing what Kael had seen.

Most of them came away unsettled. Several came away angry. A few came away weeping, though they could not articulate why. And one -- an old logician named Thessaly -- came away and said, very calmly, that what she had seen through the crack was itself another kind of shadow, projected onto her retina by the same mechanism that projected shadows onto the wall. She said there was no reason to believe the green thing was more real than the green shape. She said the word "depth" was just another name for a particular pattern of shadows, and that Kael had mistaken a new pattern for a new world.

Kael listened to all of this. Then he went home and sat in his study and tried to write in his treatise, but the words would not come. The grammar of shadows, which had once seemed as solid as mathematics, now struck him as a description of a description of something he had never actually seen. Every shadow on the wall was now haunted by the thing it was not. The flat shape of a tree was no longer a tree; it was the echo of a tree, and the echo was all he had ever known, and the tree itself stood on the other side of a wall he could not break through.

He began to take long walks along the amphitheater wall, searching for another crack. He found none. The crack that had opened in the tremor was still there, but it was too small to climb through, too narrow even to put both eyes to at once. He could only ever see half of what lay beyond. The other half remained a shadow.

Weeks passed. Kael became quieter. He stopped teaching his classes. He sat in the amphitheater and watched the shadows move across the far wall and felt, for the first time, the distance between a thing and its representation -- not as an intellectual observation but as a physical sensation, a hollowness in his chest, as though he were breathing an imitation of air.

One night, his best student came to visit him. Her name was Maren, and she had always been quick to read the shadows, quicker even than Kael had been at her age. She found him sitting in the dark, the wall empty, the crystalline veins in the rock providing no light at that hour.

"Teacher," she said, "you were the one who taught us to read the world. Why do you now say that what you taught us was not the world?"

Kael was quiet for a while. Then he said: "I taught you to read what was on the wall. That much was real, in its way. But I did not know there was something behind it. Now that I know, I cannot unread what I have read. And I cannot go back to believing the shadow is the tree."

"But," Maren said, "even if you went through the wall and stood beside the tree, you would still be seeing it through your eyes, hearing it through your ears. How would you know that this was finally the thing itself and not just a clearer shadow?"

Kael looked at her for a long time. "I wouldn't," he said.

"Then perhaps," Maren said, "the question is not whether the shadows are real. Perhaps the question is what you lose by refusing to engage with them as though they were."

Kael did not answer. He sat in the dark with his student and listened to the silence of the amphitheater, and he thought about the tree on the other side of the wall -- the tree he had seen once, through a crack, with one eye, for less than a minute -- and he thought about the shadow of the tree that he had watched every day for forty years, and he could not decide which one he loved more, or whether loving either of them was a kind of fidelity or a kind of captivity.

In the morning, he sealed the crack with mortar. He did it himself, alone, before the city was awake. Whether he did it because he could not bear the distance between the shadow and the thing, or because he could not bear the possibility that the thing itself might turn out to be another shadow, he never said. He returned to his post, and he read the wall, and he named the shapes as they passed, and his voice was steady and sure. But those who listened closely noticed that sometimes, in the pause between naming one shape and the next, his eyes would drift upward toward the place where the wall met the sky he could not see, and his hands, resting on the stone, would tremble very slightly, as though he were remembering the touch of something green and alive and irretrievably beyond his reach.

paragraphs.cn: 在阿什拉斯城，一座凿入巨大天然石质环形剧场崖壁之中的城市里，住着一个名叫凯尔的人，他的职业是光。不是制造光，因为在阿什拉斯没有人制造光——光从高处某处降临，穿过岩石中水晶般的脉络，滤洒下来，投射在剧场尽头的墙壁上，化作流转不息的明亮形状。这些形状就是世界。一道修长的剪影意味着有人正在走近。一块宽阔扁平的形状意味着一张桌子。一阵缓慢起伏的波浪意味着水，而急促的闪烁则意味着火。凯尔的职责是为它们命名，像学者研读手稿那样解读远处的那面墙，然后告诉阿什拉斯的市民们，在他们城市之外的世界里正在发生什么。
paragraphs.en: In the city of Ashrath, built into the wall of a vast natural amphitheater of stone, there lived a man named Kael whose profession was light. Not the making of light, for no one in Ashrath made light -- light arrived from somewhere high above, filtered through crystalline veins in the rock, projected onto the far wall of the amphitheater in shifting, luminous shapes. These shapes were the world. A tall silhouette meant a person approaching. A broad, flat shape meant a table. A slow undulating wave meant water, and a rapid flicker meant fire. Kael's job was to name them, to read the far wall the way a scholar reads a manuscript, and to tell the citizens of Ashrath what was happening in the world beyond their city.

paragraphs.cn: 他极为擅长此道。他能在一百步之外分辨出鹿的影子和狗的影子。他能根据暴风雨前光线碎裂的方式来预言降雨。他曾撰写过一部关于影子语法的论著，编目了四十七种截然不同的黑暗形态及其各自承载的意义。大学授予了他一个教席。他的学生们坐在剧场的前排，用手指练习描摹那些形状，学习像他所教导的那样去阅读世界。
paragraphs.en: He was very good at it. He could distinguish between the shadow of a deer and the shadow of a dog at a hundred paces. He could predict rain by the way the light fractured before a storm. He had written a treatise on the grammar of shadows, cataloguing forty-seven distinct forms of darkness and the meanings each carried. The university had awarded him a chair. His students sat in the front rows of the amphitheater and practiced tracing the shapes with their fingers, learning to read the world as he had taught them.

paragraphs.cn: 阿什拉斯城中从未有人质疑过影子是否就是世界本身。这个问题根本不会产生。墙上的形状规律得足以被研究，一致得足以被命名，有用得足以被信赖。当凯尔宣布一支商队正从东方逼近时，补给品总会在一周之内抵达。当他发出暴风预警时，全城便紧闭窗扉。影子与事件之间的对应关系如此可靠，以至于两者之间的区别——形状与它所表征的事物之间的区别——已经像河中的卵石一般被磨蚀殆尽，光滑而遗忘在日常生活的水流之下。
paragraphs.en: No one in Ashrath had ever questioned whether the shadows were the world. The question simply did not arise. The shapes on the wall were regular enough to be studied, consistent enough to be named, and useful enough to be trusted. When Kael announced that a caravan was approaching from the east, provisions arrived within the week. When he warned of a storm, the city sealed its shutters. The correspondence between shadow and event was so reliable that the distinction between the two -- between the shape and the thing it supposedly represented -- had worn away like a river stone, smooth and forgotten beneath the current of daily life.

paragraphs.cn: 后来有一天早晨，剧场的墙壁上裂开了一道缝。
paragraphs.en: Then one morning, a crack opened in the amphitheater wall.

paragraphs.cn: 起初那只是一道细小的裂缝，宽度不超过一根手指，是夜里一阵震动撼动了城市所造成的。凯尔之所以注意到它，是因为穿过裂缝的光与墙上的光不同。那不是一个形状，不是一幅剪影。那是一种颜色——一种独特的、刺透一切的绿色，在他所编目的四十七种黑暗形态中从未出现过。他将眼睛贴近裂缝，向另一侧望去。
paragraphs.en: It was a small crack at first, no wider than a finger, caused by a tremor that had rattled the city in the night. Kael noticed it because the light through the crack was different from the light on the wall. It was not a shape. It was not a silhouette. It was a color -- a particular, piercing green that he had never seen in any of the forty-seven forms of darkness. He pressed his eye to the crack and looked through.

paragraphs.cn: 他在另一侧看到的景象起初对他毫无意义。那里确实有形状，但它们不是平面的。它们有深度、有重量、有质地。它们以一种影子所没有的方式运动着——不是像投影那样横向移动，而是三维地翻转、倾斜，从他所无法想象的角度捕捉着光线。那里有一棵树，尽管他并不知道该这样称呼它。它高大、多枝，覆盖着最初吸引他目光的那种绿色，它的摇动并非因为背后有什么东西在移动，而是因为风正穿过其间，每一片叶子都在独立地颤动，一千种微小的自由，而他的认知体系中没有任何范畴可以容纳它们。那里还有天空，他一直以为天空只是投影面的上缘，但它原来是一片广袤无垠的空旷，湛蓝而无边无际，向四面八方延展，永远不会触及任何墙壁。
paragraphs.en: What he saw on the other side made no sense to him at first. There were shapes, yes, but they were not flat. They had depth and weight and texture. They moved in ways that shadows did not -- not laterally, as projections do, but in three dimensions, turning and tilting and catching the light from angles he could not have imagined. There was a tree, though he did not know to call it that. It was tall and branched and covered in the same green that had first drawn his eye, and it moved not because something behind it was moving but because the wind was moving through it, each leaf stirring independently, a thousand small freedoms he had no category for. There was sky, which he had always assumed was the upper edge of the projection surface but which turned out to be an enormous volume of open space, blue and unbounded, stretching in every direction without ever meeting a wall.

paragraphs.cn: 他将眼睛从裂缝前移开，一动不动地坐了很久。
paragraphs.en: He pulled his eye away from the crack and sat very still for a long time.

paragraphs.cn: 那天傍晚，他召集了城中的学者们，将他所见的告诉了他们。他描述了那个绿色的东西、那片开阔的空间、那种深度。他说出了这句话：影子并非事物本身。他说得很小心，就像一个人在捧着一种可能是毒药也可能是良药的物质，而在它进入你的身体之前，没有任何办法可以分辨它究竟是哪一种。
paragraphs.en: That evening, he gathered the city's scholars and told them what he had seen. He described the green thing, the open space, the depth. He said the words "the shadows are not the things themselves." He said them carefully, as one handles a substance that might be poison or medicine and there is no way to know until it is inside you.

paragraphs.cn: 学者们争论了一整夜。有人说凯尔是因为凝视墙壁太久而产生的幻觉。有人说裂缝扭曲了投影，他所看到的不过是一个比他所习惯的更为复杂的影子——也许是第四十八种黑暗形态，但仍然是黑暗的一种形态。一位比其他人更为大胆的年轻学者提议，他们应该轮流通过裂缝观看，于是他们逐一照做了，将眼睛贴近石壁上的缺口，看见了凯尔所见的一切。
paragraphs.en: The scholars argued through the night. Some said Kael had suffered a hallucination brought on by staring too long at the wall. Some said the crack had distorted the projection and that what he had seen was a shadow that was merely more complex than the ones he was accustomed to -- a forty-eighth form of darkness, perhaps, but still a form of darkness. One young scholar, bolder than the rest, proposed that they should all take turns looking through the crack, and they did, one by one, pressing their eyes to the gap in the stone and seeing what Kael had seen.

paragraphs.cn: 他们中的大多数看完之后心神不宁。有几个人看完之后怒不可遏。还有几个看完之后潸然泪下，尽管他们说不出为什么。只有一个人——一位名叫瑟萨莉的老迈逻辑学家——看完之后非常平静地说，她透过裂缝所看到的，本身也是另一种影子，由同样的机制投射到她的视网膜上，就像影子被投射到墙上一样。她说没有任何理由相信那个绿色的东西比绿色的形状更加真实。她说深度这个词不过是某种影子模式的又一个名字，而凯尔把一个新模式误认成了一个新世界。
paragraphs.en: Most of them came away unsettled. Several came away angry. A few came away weeping, though they could not articulate why. And one -- an old logician named Thessaly -- came away and said, very calmly, that what she had seen through the crack was itself another kind of shadow, projected onto her retina by the same mechanism that projected shadows onto the wall. She said there was no reason to believe the green thing was more real than the green shape. She said the word "depth" was just another name for a particular pattern of shadows, and that Kael had mistaken a new pattern for a new world.

paragraphs.cn: 凯尔聆听了这一切。然后他回到家中，坐在书房里，试图继续撰写他的论著，但文字再也涌不出来了。影子的语法，曾经如数学一般坚实的东西，如今在他看来不过是对某种他从未真正见过之物的描述的描述。墙上的每一个影子都被它所不是的那个东西所萦绕。树的那个扁平形状不再是树了；它是树的回声，而这回声就是他曾认知的全部，而树本身则矗立在他无法穿越的墙壁的另一侧。
paragraphs.en: Kael listened to all of this. Then he went home and sat in his study and tried to write in his treatise, but the words would not come. The grammar of shadows, which had once seemed as solid as mathematics, now struck him as a description of a description of something he had never actually seen. Every shadow on the wall was now haunted by the thing it was not. The flat shape of a tree was no longer a tree; it was the echo of a tree, and the echo was all he had ever known, and the tree itself stood on the other side of a wall he could not break through.

paragraphs.cn: 他开始在剧场墙壁前久久地漫步，搜寻另一道裂缝。他一无所获。那道在震动中裂开的缝隙还在那里，但它太小了，无法钻过去，甚至窄到无法同时将两只眼睛贴上去。他永远只能看到另一侧事物的一半。另一半仍然是影子。
paragraphs.en: He began to take long walks along the amphitheater wall, searching for another crack. He found none. The crack that had opened in the tremor was still there, but it was too small to climb through, too narrow even to put both eyes to at once. He could only ever see half of what lay beyond. The other half remained a shadow.

paragraphs.cn: 数周过去了。凯尔变得更加沉默。他不再去上课。他坐在剧场里，看着影子在远处的墙壁上移动，第一次感受到了一个事物与其再现之间的距离——不是作为一种智识上的观察，而是作为一种身体上的感觉，一种胸腔中的空洞，仿佛他正在呼吸一种对空气的模仿。
paragraphs.en: Weeks passed. Kael became quieter. He stopped teaching his classes. He sat in the amphitheater and watched the shadows move across the far wall and felt, for the first time, the distance between a thing and its representation -- not as an intellectual observation but as a physical sensation, a hollowness in his chest, as though he were breathing an imitation of air.

paragraphs.cn: 一天夜里，他最出色的学生来拜访他。她名叫玛伦，一直擅长解读影子，甚至比凯尔在她这个年纪时更为敏捷。她发现他坐在黑暗中，墙壁空空荡荡，岩石中的水晶脉络在那个时辰没有提供任何光线。
paragraphs.en: One night, his best student came to visit him. Her name was Maren, and she had always been quick to read the shadows, quicker even than Kael had been at her age. She found him sitting in the dark, the wall empty, the crystalline veins in the rock providing no light at that hour.

paragraphs.cn: 老师，她说，是您教会了我们阅读这个世界。为什么您现在却说，您教给我们的不是世界？
paragraphs.en: "Teacher," she said, "you were the one who taught us to read the world. Why do you now say that what you taught us was not the world?"

paragraphs.cn: 凯尔沉默了片刻。然后他说：我教你们阅读的是墙上的东西。那在一定程度上是真实的。但我不知道那背后还有别的东西。既然现在我知道了，我就无法不去读我已经读到的。我也无法再回去相信影子就是树。
paragraphs.en: Kael was quiet for a while. Then he said: "I taught you to read what was on the wall. That much was real, in its way. But I did not know there was something behind it. Now that I know, I cannot unread what I have read. And I cannot go back to believing the shadow is the tree."

paragraphs.cn: 可是，玛伦说，即使您穿过了墙壁，站在那棵树的旁边，您仍然是在用您的眼睛看它，用您的耳朵听它。您如何知道这一次终于就是事物本身，而不只是一道更清晰的影子？
paragraphs.en: "But," Maren said, "even if you went through the wall and stood beside the tree, you would still be seeing it through your eyes, hearing it through your ears. How would you know that this was finally the thing itself and not just a clearer shadow?"

paragraphs.cn: 凯尔注视了她很久。我不知道，他说。
paragraphs.en: Kael looked at her for a long time. "I wouldn't," he said.

paragraphs.cn: 那么也许，玛伦说，问题并不在于影子是否真实。也许问题在于，如果您拒绝像对待真实之物那样去与它们相处，您会失去什么。
paragraphs.en: "Then perhaps," Maren said, "the question is not whether the shadows are real. Perhaps the question is what you lose by refusing to engage with them as though they were."

paragraphs.cn: 凯尔没有回答。他和他的学生坐在黑暗中，听着剧场的沉寂，想着墙壁另一侧的那棵树——那棵他曾透过一道裂缝、用一只眼睛、看了不到一分钟的树——也想着那棵树的影子，他每天注视了四十年的影子，他无法断定自己更爱哪一个，也无法断定对它们之中任何一个的爱，究竟是一种忠实，还是一种囚禁。
paragraphs.en: Kael did not answer. He sat in the dark with his student and listened to the silence of the amphitheater, and he thought about the tree on the other side of the wall -- the tree he had seen once, through a crack, with one eye, for less than a minute -- and he thought about the shadow of the tree that he had watched every day for forty years, and he could not decide which one he loved more, or whether loving either of them was a kind of fidelity or a kind of captivity.

paragraphs.cn: 清晨，他用灰泥封住了那道裂缝。他亲手完成的，独自一人，在城市醒来之前。他这样做究竟是因为无法忍受影子与事物之间的距离，还是因为无法忍受事物本身可能也是另一道影子的可能性，他从未说过。他回到了他的岗位，解读墙壁，为经过的形状逐一命名，他的声音平稳而笃定。但那些仔细倾听的人注意到，有时候，在命名一个形状与下一个形状之间的停顿里，他的目光会向上漂移，飘向墙壁与他看不见的天空相接之处，而他的双手，搁在石头上，会微微地颤抖，仿佛他正在回忆某种绿色的、鲜活的、永远无法触及的东西的触感。
paragraphs.en: In the morning, he sealed the crack with mortar. He did it himself, alone, before the city was awake. Whether he did it because he could not bear the distance between the shadow and the thing, or because he could not bear the possibility that the thing itself might turn out to be another shadow, he never said. He returned to his post, and he read the wall, and he named the shapes as they passed, and his voice was steady and sure. But those who listened closely noticed that sometimes, in the pause between naming one shape and the next, his eyes would drift upward toward the place where the wall met the sky he could not see, and his hands, resting on the stone, would tremble very slightly, as though he were remembering the touch of something green and alive and irretrievably beyond his reach.

articles.title: 维特根斯坦如何"解决"哲学与量子物理学
articles.author: Anthony Aguirre
articles.summary: 我第一次接触到柏拉图的洞穴寓言是在高中时期。作为一个数学和英语兼修的书呆子——一个奇怪的组合——我在课余时间拉大提琴、写短篇小说。我对哲学略知一二，当时正在上一...
articles.content_en:
I first learnt about Plato's allegory of the cave when I was in senior high school. A mathematics and English nerd – a strange combination – I played cello and wrote short stories in my spare time. I knew a bit about philosophy and was taking a survey class in the humanities, but Plato's theory of ideal forms arrived as a revelation: this notion that we could experience a shadow-play of a reality that was nonetheless eternal and immutable. Somewhere out there was a perfect circle; all the other circles we could see were pale copies of this single Circle, dust and ashes compared with its ethereal unity.

Chasing after this ideal as a young man, I studied mathematics. I could prove the number of primes to be infinite, and the square root of two to be irrational (a real number that cannot be made by dividing two whole numbers). These statements, I was told, were true at the beginning of time and would be true at its end, long after the last mathematician vanished from the cosmos. Yet, as I churned out proofs for my doctoral coursework, the human element of mathematics began to discomfit me. My proofs seemed more like arguments than irrefutable calculations. Each rested on self-evident axioms that, while apparently true, seemed to be based on little more than consensus among mathematicians.

These problems with mathematics turned out to be well known. The mathematician and philosopher Bertrand Russell spent much of his career trying to shore up this house built on sand. His attempt was published, with his collaborator Alfred North Whitehead, in the loftily titled Principia Mathematica (1910-13) – a dense three-volume tome, in which Russell introduces the extended proof of 1 + 1 = 2 with the witticism that 'The above proposition is occasionally useful.' Published at the authors' considerable expense, their work set off a chain reaction that, by the 1930s, showed mathematics to be teetering on a precipice of inconsistency and incompleteness.

Eventually, I turned to physics, hoping to reground my Platonist aspirations in the eternal laws that governed the physical reality of the cosmos. But quantum theory exposed that, too, as a fantasy: even though we could define rules and equations for physical laws, we could not explain what they meant. Recent experiments in quantum information theory have shown that our most basic assumptions about reality, such as when something can be considered to have been observed and to have definite physical properties, are in the eye of the beholder.

Attempts to address these paradoxes date back to the dawn of quantum mechanics, when Albert Einstein and Niels Bohr debated how to interpret the baffling phenomena they'd uncovered. Yet it was only when I dived into the parallel milieu of Cambridge Philosophy, at the time of Ludwig Wittgenstein and Russell's ascendancy, that I began to feel like my qualms about mathematics and physics might be addressed. Contemporaries of Einstein and Bohr, Wittgenstein and Russell didn't engage with the quantum revolution directly. Yet it's in the work of these philosophers that I began to see answers to some of our most fundamental questions about reality – answers that stem from recognising that we are not only asking the wrong questions; we are asking nonsensical ones.

The great debates about quantum physics kicked off in the 1920s. Bohr and his protégé, Werner Heisenberg, were trying to figure out how to talk about the weird behaviour of quantum particles: how they appeared to 'know' when they were being observed, for example, and to act as a particle when observed and a wave when not observed.

How to describe this phenomenon flummoxed theorists. Heisenberg (and later, Erwin Schrödinger) came up with equations that described particles in terms of a wavefunction, where simple numbers became entities of infinite dimensions that lived in exotic mathematical spaces. The act of observation now had a complicated description that took into account what the experimenter was doing.

But all this mathematics didn't get at what actually happened when the properties of a particle were being measured. At that moment, all the complex infinite-dimensional mathematics suddenly compressed into individual numbers, as if the particles had been there all along. Observation after observation of photons scattering on screens revealed that no simple explanation was possible. This description was deeply unsatisfying to Einstein, because the wavefunction appeared to prevent particles from having definite attributes before they were observed. Einstein wanted the wavefunction gone, replaced with some more sensible interpretation where things retained definite properties and locations. Despite decades of haggling over the incompleteness of quantum mechanics, however, the wavefunction couldn't be dispensed with.

The view that emerged from this haggling came to be known as 'the Copenhagen interpretation' – coined by Heisenberg in 1955, and predicated on the presence of a fundamental split between the observer and the system being observed. Meanwhile, the polymath John von Neumann came up with an idealised mathematical description of what happened when you measured a particle's wavefunction: it collapsed upon interacting with the observer. Where the rest of the wave went, or whether it was ever real in the first place, was anyone's guess.

The Universe is a coin that's already been flipped, heads or tails predetermined: all we're doing is uncovering it.

By the late 20th century, dozens of other interpretations had appeared under exotic names: the many-worlds theory, superdeterminism, consistent histories, the modal interpretation, superselection, Bohmian mechanics, Lindblad equations. I even invented my own: dynamic histories. While a few, like mine, proposed new theories that could come into conflict with quantum mechanics, most of them don't. They are metaphysical, not physical.

The big question lurking behind all this is: what does the wavefunction mean? Does it represent something real or not? Most interpretations are 'realist' in the sense that they assume the wavefunction is a real entity and then go on to explain what it represents – but a few say it doesn't exist at all, such as Quantum Bayesianism or QBism, as it is known. QBism owes its existence to the work of Wittgenstein's friend and contemporary Frank Ramsey, who developed an anti-realist interpretation of probability. QBism holds that the wavefunction is purely an encoding of human uncertainty, representing a spectrum of probabilities that is updated when we make an observation. So the quantum wavefunction is not about objective reality at all, but about our future observations. QBism therefore refutes the Platonic idealism of the wavefunction and declares it to be a mere mathematical quantification of our beliefs.

Plenty of physicists have grown tired of this debate and its seemingly endless and unsatisfying arguments between realists and anti-realists. They want us to 'Shut up and calculate!' in the words of the physicist David Mermin: to stop trying to interpret quantum mechanics at all and get back to doing it. Philosophers, on the other hand, tend to dismiss this latter group as being philosophically ignorant. There's a suspicion that, deep down, such physicists simply possess a metaphysics that they don't want to admit, because they don't want to come down on the side of an interpretation that has no scientific backing.

Yet those who follow Mermin's injuction have a friend in one of the great philosophical minds of the 20th century – one who provides not only support for their position, but philosophical reasoning for why it is the only correct one.

Wittgenstein was a reluctant philosopher. Born in 1889 to a wealthy and powerful family in Vienna, Austria, philosophy seemed to be more of a compulsion for him than a love – a tendency to get stuck on certain questions, unable to move on without resolving them. Perhaps that's why Wittgenstein felt the need to 'solve' philosophy once and for all, attacking its roots and, by doing so, tearing down all philosophical debates, including the broader quarrel between realists and anti-realists in all domains.

Wittgenstein was at once fantastically arrogant before his fellows and deeply humble before the questions he confronted. His task was no less than to discover what lay at the roots of logic. Starting out in the nascent field of aeronautical engineering in 1908, he quickly gravitated towards the philosophy of mathematics.

His German mentor Gottlob Frege sent him to the University of Cambridge to work with Russell. Of Wittgenstein, Russell wrote that: 'An unknown German appeared … obstinate and perverse, but I think not stupid.' Within a year, Wittgenstein had proved himself to Russell, who said: 'I shall certainly encourage him. Perhaps he will do great things … I love him and feel he will solve the problems I am too old to solve.'

Russell's motivations, however, were at odds with Wittgenstein's. The grandson of an earl, Russell was raised in a noble household by his strict and devout grandmother. Finding no comfort in her religion, Russell sought it in mathematics, only to learn that the roots of the ancient discipline were rotten. He was horrified to discover that the geometer Euclid's axioms, such as 'two parallel lines do not intersect', were just assumptions. Likewise, the number system is based on self-evident truths. If any were wrong, the whole thing might come tumbling down. Russell therefore dedicated his life to resolving all uncertainty in mathematics.

Russell – a product of the Victorian age – continued to look for certainty where there was none.

Russell appropriated Wittgenstein's philosophy to shore up basic logic, but Wittgenstein had other ideas. He wanted to understand what made facts true or false – not because he desired comfort from certainty, but because, well, it bothered him. Unlike Russell, Wittgenstein was devoted to the truth, no matter how ugly.

Wittgenstein's life was no less unusual than his thoughts. He worked with Russell intensely from 1911-13, retreating to an isolated hut in rural Norway for months at a time in order to work out his ideas. In 1913, he returned to Austria, only to be swept up in the chaos of the First World War.

It was a time of massive upheaval at all levels of European society. Empires were in decline, and the old monarchical order was ebbing away. Women's suffrage was in full swing, with the vote in Britain and the United States arriving after the war. Science and mathematics likewise were throwing off the shackles of 19th-century classicism. Einstein's theory of relativity, both special and general, banished Isaac Newton's concept of universal time and space, while Heisenberg's uncertainty principle destroyed the certainty of measurement some years later.

Russell, meanwhile – a product of the Victorian age – continued to look for certainty where there was none. It fell to young Wittgenstein, picking up the zeitgeist, to seek to resolve the realism debate once and for all, even if that meant destroying it.

The war years were not easy on Wittgenstein. Poor health exempted him from conscription, but he volunteered for service and eventually to go to the Front. His reasons were complex, but from his letters it seems he was seeking something that he felt he could not find in intellectual pursuits. Writing from the Eastern Front, he expressed the hope that 'the nearness of death' would bring about a spiritual transformation in him. Wracked by loneliness and spiritual longing, he contemplated suicide, only to be saved by faith. While before the war he'd rivalled Russell in his distaste for religion, a chance discovery of Leo Tolstoy's The Gospel in Brief (1902) in a bookshop caused him to become a devout Christian. His faith would influence his later work, and vice versa. Captured by the Italians in 1918, he spent months in a prisoner-of-war camp.

It was during the war that he formed much of his ideas for his first great work, the Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus (1922) – a book that applied modern logic to metaphysics via language to relate facts about reality to reality itself. He called it the theory of meaning.

In the Tractatus, Wittgenstein developed a philosophy that was deeply embedded in the world – not in idealised realms of thought like the rational idealist Russell, but in how we talk about the world. Rather than coming up with a theory about how words and facts represent reality, which is crucial to both realists and anti-realists, he determined that representation is irrelevant. No one needs to say what facts and objects represent. They are simply there, embedded in our picture of reality. To say what they represent is actually nonsense, absurd. As in the visual realm, a second picture is not necessary to explain what a first picture means; if it were otherwise, we'd fall prey to infinite recursion.

Wittgenstein was serious in that he believed we could not talk about things that are not in the world.

What Wittgenstein understood is that you can't use words to explain representation, because words are representations themselves. It would be like trying to travel outside the Universe to show somebody what the Universe is – a feat that's both impossible and unnecessary. A sentence shows what it means by its own sense. Thus, if I say 'Jenny has an apple,' I do not have to explain how the words 'Jenny' and 'apple' represent physical objects in the world; nor do I have to explain what 'has' means. We mutually understand that, if Jenny is right there and she has an orange in her hand, the proposition is false. It shows its sense. There is nothing more to say about it, as long as we both understand the rules of the language.

Thus, Wittgenstein, even in his early work, suggests that the realist versus anti-realist debate is meaningless because both sides are trying to say things that are only showable. From this early Wittgensteinian perspective, a mathematical equation – in fact, any equation, including the ones governing quantum mechanics – is like a photograph of reality. Like photographs, we do not need anyone to interpret its meaning as realist or anti-realist. We do not need a Copenhagen or a many-worlds to indicate the sense of the equation to us, because it is already as apparent as it is ever going to be. To ask what the wavefunction represents is like asking what Michelangelo's statue of David or Van Gogh's painting The Starry Night represents: any explanation beyond the mere facts is insufficient and subjective.

You might find this explanation unsatisfactory. Yet Wittgenstein was serious in that he believed we could not talk about things that are not in the world. While we might talk about quantum mechanics in terms of particles, measurements and calculations, any philosophical attributes that ascribe significance to what we can observe (such as 'real' or 'unreal') are nonsense. We must be silent on ascribing additional meaning to the wavefunction.

Wittgenstein's exploration about what we can and cannot talk about in philosophy, however, would evolve over the next several decades, and lead to a rejection of even those philosophical concepts such as the picture theory upon which he built the Tractatus.

Having written the Tractatus, Wittgenstein believed that he had 'solved' philosophy. In his strange, haughty humility, he left the discipline in the 1920s, and worked various jobs as a gardener, teacher and architect.

This interregnum came to an end, however, when Wittgenstein was exposed to logical empiricism. This was a movement arising from a group of philosophers known as the Vienna Circle. They emphasised empirical knowledge and the theory of 'logical positivism', meaning that we can only ascribe meaning to what can be measured or observed. A strict logical positivist is unconcerned with explanations or interpretations; rather, they believe that understanding the world is built upon measurement and its prediction.

The Tractatus was a foundational pillar of the Vienna Circle, and this galvanised Wittgenstein to continue his work. He decided to return to Cambridge in 1929, but moved away from the philosophy of mathematics and logic, and towards ordinary language and psychology.

He rejects the theory of meaning entirely while making one of his most powerful contributions to it.

Wittgenstein eventually collected his ideas in a book called Philosophical Investigations – probably one of the strangest books of philosophy ever published (and, perhaps for this reason, only released after Wittgenstein's death from cancer in 1951). Rather than being organised as a sequence of topics or propositions, the Investigations is a stream-of-consciousness series of points, arguments and statements. That was in fact in keeping with its own philosophy, which is that philosophy itself can discover nothing. It is simply a form of therapy that can quickly become a disease of the intellect. Its only job is to remind us of that which we already know.

From this later Wittgensteinian position, all the varying quantum interpretations would be the result of diseased minds, and ultimately self-destructive. That's because all philosophy is actually a debate over mere grammar. If we take seriously these metaphysical debates, he argues, we are not only wrong, but ill.

In his Philosophical Investigations, Wittgenstein rejects the theory of meaning entirely while making one of his most powerful contributions to it. All language, he says, gains its definitions from how it is used in specific cases. All language is a game like chess or poker – we learn the rules by playing, not theorising or defining. So the very notion of a universal definition is an artifice, a bit of subterfuge. One cannot talk about what words really mean; one can only use them. This applies as much to mathematics as it does to ordinary words.

Wittgenstein wants to show us that we need to stop trying to interpret language. Take the example of a road sign pointing to a village. We see the road sign and instantly understand its meaning. While there is an element of symbolic decoding involved, there is no deeper interpretive step, he says. In other words, we do not need to figure out how the sign represents reality, either in the ideal world of Plato or some subjective concept of reality in our heads. The sign could contain almost any kind of symbols, colour coding or numbers, as long as the action that people take upon seeing it is the correct one. The sign 'shows' us where the village is, because that is how signs of that kind are used. That is its true meaning.

The late Wittgenstein entirely rejects his own picture theory of reality. Pictures are nice and satisfying, but usage is what actually matters. The wavefunction, on this reading, isn't like a picture of reality at all. All that matters is that physicists now have the ability to do calculations, which lead to predictions that can be verified by measurements. The point is not the measurements themselves, however – as a logical positivist might claim – but how the physicists behave. Do they calculate in a way that leads to more and better physics? Language and mathematics are a means of controlling and modifying collective human action so that work gets done.

This is language as culture rather than language as picture. And culture includes ritual. Like all ritualistic communities, physics contains its rules, interpretations, specialised vocabulary, a community of adherents who are admitted to the arcane arts, levels of indoctrination, and gatekeepers. While some societies relate ritual to the appeasement of gods and spirits, in science they serve to therapeutically appease our philosophical needs. Competition between interpretations is not unlike competition between clan gods, trying to achieve cultural dominance.

Evolutionary cultural anthropology backs up this view, having demonstrated that language is deeply connected to ritual and religion. Likewise, the vocabulary, grammar and procedures of science are themselves ritualistic, with each subdiscipline having its own mores and norms. These are necessary because it is impossible for scientists to evaluate new research purely based on factual merits; it often takes years to validate a new theory or experimental result. The role of ritual also makes perfect sense from an evolutionary perspective.
