Humans have spent hundreds of thousands of years navigating a hostile planet by encoding information crucial for survival into ritual, which can then be transmitted across generations. When we invented the scientific method only a few hundred years ago, we had to graft it onto that part of our nature in order to pass it down the generations, hijacking an ancient and effective cultural mechanism for a new purpose.

The activity of science, rather than its interpretations, defines what words and symbols mean.

Hence, quantum interpretation is not really an investigation into reality, and it tells us nothing new about the world. Rather, it is a grammatical investigation or, in anthropological terms, a cultural one. It is a competition between differing philosophical therapies, satisfying different emotional-cultural needs.

Crucially, it is in the activity of science, whether via experiment or calculation, that all its useful information it generates exists. Wittgenstein explains, for example, how the act of determining the length of an object is not a case for learning theories and definitions, but an activity: "What 'determining the length' means is not learned by learning what length and determining are; the meaning of the word 'length' is learnt by learning, among other things, what it is to determine length."

This description indicates that to learn what quantum physics means is to learn to calculate it – and vice versa.

Wittgenstein suggests that even mathematics is potentially a shared language and activity. He asks: "what would this mean: 'Even though everybody believed that twice two was five it would still be four'?—For what would it be like for everybody to believe that?—Well, I could imagine, for instance, that people had a different calculus, or a technique which we should not call 'calculating'. But would it be wrong?"

He suggests that 'odd' would be a better word for it, but we would have no common frame of reference to call it wrong. He goes on to suggest that mathematics is very much an activity, like a game, and we all know the same rules that form a system. Hence, we all come to the same conclusions and never argue about what is proved. Yet, some alien species could come up with different rules for their mathematical game that are no less valid because they are following their rules.

If Wittgenstein were alive today, he might have couched his arguments in the vocabulary of cultural anthropology. For this shared grammar and these language games, in his view, form part of much larger ritualistic mechanisms that connect human activity with human knowledge, as deeply as DNA connects to human biology. It is also a perfect example of how evolution works by using pre-existing mechanisms to generate new behaviours.

The conclusion from all of this is that interpretation and representation in language and mathematics are little different than the supernatural explanations of ancient religions. Trying to resolve the debate between Bohr and Einstein is like trying to answer the Zen kōan about whether the tree falling in the forest makes a sound if no one can hear it. One cannot say definitely yes or no, because all human language must connect to human activity. And all human language and activity are ritual, signifying meaning by their interconnectedness. To ask what the wavefunction means without specifying an activity – and experiment – to extract that meaning is, therefore, as sensible as asking about the sound of the falling tree. It is nonsense.

I have come to think of the world not as filled with sharply defined truths but as a place of myriad possibilities.

As a scientist and mathematician, Wittgenstein has challenged my own tendency to seek out interpretations of phenomena that have no scientific value – and to see such explanations as nothing more than narratives. He taught that all that philosophy can do is remind us of what is evidently true. It's evidently true that the wavefunction has a multiverse interpretation, but one must assume the multiverse first, since it cannot be measured. So the interpretation is a tautology, not a discovery.

I have humbled myself to the fact that we can't justify clinging to one interpretation of reality over another. In place of my early enthusiastic Platonism, I have come to think of the world not as one filled with sharply defined truths, but rather as a place containing myriad possibilities – each of which, like the possibilities within the wavefunction itself, can be simultaneously true. Likewise, mathematics and its surrounding language don't represent reality so much as serve as a trusty tool for helping people to navigate the world. They are of human origin and for human purposes.

To shut up and calculate, then, recognises that there are limits to our pathways for understanding. Our only option as scientists is to look, predict and test. This might not be as glamorous an offering as the interpretations we can construct in our minds, but it is the royal road to real knowledge.

paragraphs.cn: 我第一次接触到柏拉图的洞穴寓言是在高中时期。作为一个数学和英语兼修的书呆子——一个奇怪的组合——我在课余时间拉大提琴、写短篇小说。我对哲学略知一二，当时正在上一门人文学科概论课，但柏拉图的理念论给了我一种启示：这个观念认为，我们所经验到的不过是永恒不变的实在的一场皮影戏。在某个地方存在着一个完美的圆；我们所见的所有其他圆都不过是这个唯一的"圆"的苍白复制品，与它那空灵的统一性相比不过是尘土与灰烬。
paragraphs.en: I first learnt about Plato's allegory of the cave when I was in senior high school. A mathematics and English nerd – a strange combination – I played cello and wrote short stories in my spare time. I knew a bit about philosophy and was taking a survey class in the humanities, but Plato's theory of ideal forms arrived as a revelation: this notion that we could experience a shadow-play of a reality that was nonetheless eternal and immutable. Somewhere out there was a perfect circle; all the other circles we could see were pale copies of this single Circle, dust and ashes compared with its ethereal unity.

paragraphs.cn: 为了追寻这一理想，年轻时的我选择了学习数学。我能够证明素数有无穷多个，证明根号二是无理数（即无法用两个整数的比值来表示的实数）。别人告诉我，这些命题在时间之初就是真的，在时间之末也将是真的，即便最后一位数学家早已从宇宙中消亡。然而，当我在博士课程中不断产出证明时，数学中的人为因素开始令我感到不安。我的证明看起来更像是论证而非不可辩驳的计算。每一个证明都建立在自明的公理之上，这些公理虽然表面上正确，但似乎不过是基于数学家之间的共识而已。
paragraphs.en: Chasing after this ideal as a young man, I studied mathematics. I could prove the number of primes to be infinite, and the square root of two to be irrational (a real number that cannot be made by dividing two whole numbers). These statements, I was told, were true at the beginning of time and would be true at its end, long after the last mathematician vanished from the cosmos. Yet, as I churned out proofs for my doctoral coursework, the human element of mathematics began to discomfit me. My proofs seemed more like arguments than irrefutable calculations. Each rested on self-evident axioms that, while apparently true, seemed to be based on little more than consensus among mathematicians.

paragraphs.cn: 数学中的这些问题其实早已为人所知。数学家兼哲学家伯特兰·罗素花了大半个职业生涯试图修补这座建立在沙滩上的大厦。他与合作者阿尔弗雷德·诺斯·怀特海共同发表的成果，就是那部冠以崇高名称的《数学原理》（1910-1913年）——一部艰深的三卷本巨著，罗素在书中引出对1+1=2的详尽证明时打趣道：'上述命题偶尔是有用的。'这部著作由作者自费出版，却引发了一场连锁反应，到了1930年代，人们发现数学正摇摇欲坠于不一致性和不完备性的悬崖边缘。
paragraphs.en: These problems with mathematics turned out to be well known. The mathematician and philosopher Bertrand Russell spent much of his career trying to shore up this house built on sand. His attempt was published, with his collaborator Alfred North Whitehead, in the loftily titled Principia Mathematica (1910-13) – a dense three-volume tome, in which Russell introduces the extended proof of 1 + 1 = 2 with the witticism that 'The above proposition is occasionally useful.' Published at the authors' considerable expense, their work set off a chain reaction that, by the 1930s, showed mathematics to be teetering on a precipice of inconsistency and incompleteness.

paragraphs.cn: 最终，我转向了物理学，希望能将我的柏拉图式抱负重新锚定在支配宇宙物理实在的永恒定律之上。但量子理论戳穿了这也不过是一种幻想：虽然我们可以为物理定律定义规则和方程，却无法解释它们意味着什么。量子信息理论的最新实验表明，我们对实在最基本的假设——比如何时可以认为某物已被观测并具有确定的物理性质——其实取决于观测者的视角。
paragraphs.en: Eventually, I turned to physics, hoping to reground my Platonist aspirations in the eternal laws that governed the physical reality of the cosmos. But quantum theory exposed that, too, as a fantasy: even though we could define rules and equations for physical laws, we could not explain what they meant. Recent experiments in quantum information theory have shown that our most basic assumptions about reality, such as when something can be considered to have been observed and to have definite physical properties, are in the eye of the beholder.

paragraphs.cn: 试图回应这些悖论的努力可以追溯到量子力学诞生之初，当时阿尔伯特·爱因斯坦和尼尔斯·玻尔就如何解释他们所发现的令人困惑的现象展开了争论。然而，直到我深入探究了剑桥哲学这一并行的思想场域——路德维希·维特根斯坦和罗素正值声望鼎盛的时期——我才开始觉得我对数学和物理学的疑虑或许可以得到解答。维特根斯坦和罗素是爱因斯坦和玻尔的同代人，他们并未直接介入量子革命。然而，正是在这些哲学家的著作中，我开始看到一些关于实在的最根本问题的答案——这些答案源于一个认识：我们不仅在提出错误的问题；我们提出的是毫无意义的问题。
paragraphs.en: Attempts to address these paradoxes date back to the dawn of quantum mechanics, when Albert Einstein and Niels Bohr debated how to interpret the baffling phenomena they'd uncovered. Yet it was only when I dived into the parallel milieu of Cambridge Philosophy, at the time of Ludwig Wittgenstein and Russell's ascendancy, that I began to feel like my qualms about mathematics and physics might be addressed. Contemporaries of Einstein and Bohr, Wittgenstein and Russell didn't engage with the quantum revolution directly. Yet it's in the work of these philosophers that I began to see answers to some of our most fundamental questions about reality – answers that stem from recognising that we are not only asking the wrong questions; we are asking nonsensical ones.

paragraphs.cn: 关于量子物理学的伟大争论始于1920年代。玻尔和他的门生维尔纳·海森堡试图弄清楚如何描述量子粒子的怪异行为：例如，它们似乎'知道'自己何时正在被观测，在观测时表现为粒子，未被观测时则表现为波。
paragraphs.en: The great debates about quantum physics kicked off in the 1920s. Bohr and his protégé, Werner Heisenberg, were trying to figure out how to talk about the weird behaviour of quantum particles: how they appeared to 'know' when they were being observed, for example, and to act as a particle when observed and a wave when not observed.

paragraphs.cn: 如何描述这一现象让理论家们一筹莫展。海森堡（以及后来的埃尔温·薛定谔）提出了用波函数来描述粒子的方程，其中简单的数字变成了生活在奇异数学空间中的无穷维实体。观测行为现在有了一个复杂的描述，需要考虑实验者正在做什么。
paragraphs.en: How to describe this phenomenon flummoxed theorists. Heisenberg (and later, Erwin Schrödinger) came up with equations that described particles in terms of a wavefunction, where simple numbers became entities of infinite dimensions that lived in exotic mathematical spaces. The act of observation now had a complicated description that took into account what the experimenter was doing.

paragraphs.cn: 但所有这些数学都无法触及测量粒子性质时到底发生了什么。在那一刻，所有复杂的无穷维数学突然压缩为一个个别数字，仿佛粒子一直就在那里一样。一次又一次对光子在屏幕上散射的观测表明，不可能有任何简单的解释。这一描述令爱因斯坦深感不满，因为波函数似乎阻止粒子在被观测之前具有确定的属性。爱因斯坦希望摒弃波函数，代之以某种更合理的解释——事物在其中保持确定的性质和位置的解释。然而，尽管数十年来关于量子力学不完备性的争论不绝于耳，波函数却无法被抛弃。
paragraphs.en: But all this mathematics didn't get at what actually happened when the properties of a particle were being measured. At that moment, all the complex infinite-dimensional mathematics suddenly compressed into individual numbers, as if the particles had been there all along. Observation after observation of photons scattering on screens revealed that no simple explanation was possible. This description was deeply unsatisfying to Einstein, because the wavefunction appeared to prevent particles from having definite attributes before they were observed. Einstein wanted the wavefunction gone, replaced with some more sensible interpretation where things retained definite properties and locations. Despite decades of haggling over the incompleteness of quantum mechanics, however, the wavefunction couldn't be dispensed with.

paragraphs.cn: 从这些争论中浮现出来的观点后来被称为'哥本哈根诠释'——由海森堡于1955年命名，其前提是观测者与被观测系统之间存在根本性的分割。与此同时，博学家约翰·冯·诺伊曼对测量粒子波函数时发生的事情提出了一种理想化的数学描述：波函数在与观测者相互作用时发生坍缩。至于波的其余部分去了哪里，或者它最初是否真的存在过，谁也说不清楚。
paragraphs.en: The view that emerged from this haggling came to be known as 'the Copenhagen interpretation' – coined by Heisenberg in 1955, and predicated on the presence of a fundamental split between the observer and the system being observed. Meanwhile, the polymath John von Neumann came up with an idealised mathematical description of what happened when you measured a particle's wavefunction: it collapsed upon interacting with the observer. Where the rest of the wave went, or whether it was ever real in the first place, was anyone's guess.

paragraphs.cn: 宇宙是一枚已经抛出的硬币，正面或反面早已注定：我们所做的不过是将它揭示出来
paragraphs.en: The Universe is a coin that's already been flipped, heads or tails predetermined: all we're doing is uncovering it.

paragraphs.cn: 到了20世纪末，数十种其他诠释以各种奇异的名字出现：多世界理论、超决定论、一致性历史、模态诠释、超选择、玻姆力学、林德布拉德方程。我甚至发明了自己的诠释：动态历史。虽然其中少数几个——比如我的——提出了可能与量子力学相冲突的新理论，但大多数并非如此。它们是形而上学的，而非物理学的。
paragraphs.en: By the late 20th century, dozens of other interpretations had appeared under exotic names: the many-worlds theory, superdeterminism, consistent histories, the modal interpretation, superselection, Bohmian mechanics, Lindblad equations. I even invented my own: dynamic histories. While a few, like mine, proposed new theories that could come into conflict with quantum mechanics, most of them don't. They are metaphysical, not physical.

paragraphs.cn: 隐藏在这一切背后的大问题是：波函数意味着什么？它是否代表某种真实的东西？大多数诠释是'实在论'的，因为它们假设波函数是一个真实的实体，然后解释它代表什么——但也有少数认为它根本不存在，比如量子贝叶斯主义，或称QBism。QBism得益于维特根斯坦的朋友和同时代人弗兰克·拉姆齐的工作，后者发展了一种反实在论的概率解释。QBism认为波函数纯粹是对人类不确定性的编码，代表一个在我们进行观测时会更新的概率谱。因此，量子波函数根本不是关于客观实在的，而是关于我们未来的观测。QBism由此否定了波函数的柏拉图式唯心论，宣称它不过是对我们信念的数学量化而已。
paragraphs.en: The big question lurking behind all this is: what does the wavefunction mean? Does it represent something real or not? Most interpretations are 'realist' in the sense that they assume the wavefunction is a real entity and then go on to explain what it represents – but a few say it doesn't exist at all, such as Quantum Bayesianism or QBism, as it is known. QBism owes its existence to the work of Wittgenstein's friend and contemporary Frank Ramsey, who developed an anti-realist interpretation of probability. QBism holds that the wavefunction is purely an encoding of human uncertainty, representing a spectrum of probabilities that is updated when we make an observation. So the quantum wavefunction is not about objective reality at all, but about our future observations. QBism therefore refutes the Platonic idealism of the wavefunction and declares it to be a mere mathematical quantification of our beliefs.

paragraphs.cn: 许多物理学家已经厌倦了这场辩论，厌倦了实在论者和反实在论者之间似乎无穷无尽且令人不满的争论。他们希望我们'闭嘴，去算！'——用物理学家戴维·默明的话来说：停止试图诠释量子力学，回去做物理。另一方面，哲学家们倾向于将这后一群人斥为哲学上的无知者。人们怀疑，在内心深处，这些物理学家不过是拥有一种他们不愿承认的形而上学，因为他们不想站在没有任何科学支撑的诠释那一边。
paragraphs.en: Plenty of physicists have grown tired of this debate and its seemingly endless and unsatisfying arguments between realists and anti-realists. They want us to 'Shut up and calculate!' in the words of the physicist David Mermin: to stop trying to interpret quantum mechanics at all and get back to doing it. Philosophers, on the other hand, tend to dismiss this latter group as being philosophically ignorant. There's a suspicion that, deep down, such physicists simply possess a metaphysics that they don't want to admit, because they don't want to come down on the side of an interpretation that has no scientific backing.

paragraphs.cn: 然而，那些遵循默明忠告的人，在20世纪最伟大的哲学头脑之一那里找到了盟友——这位哲学家不仅为他们的立场提供了支持，还提供了哲学上的论证来说明为什么这是唯一正确的立场。
paragraphs.en: Yet those who follow Mermin's injuction have a friend in one of the great philosophical minds of the 20th century – one who provides not only support for their position, but philosophical reasoning for why it is the only correct one.

paragraphs.cn: 维特根斯坦是一个勉为其难的哲学家。1889年，他出生于奥地利维也纳一个富有且权势显赫的家族。哲学对他来说更像是一种强迫而非热爱——一种被某些问题困住、不解决就无法继续前进的倾向。也许这就是为什么维特根斯坦觉得有必要一劳永逸地'解决'哲学，攻击它的根基，并以此拆毁所有哲学争论，包括各个领域中实在论者与反实在论者之间更广泛的纷争。
paragraphs.en: Wittgenstein was a reluctant philosopher. Born in 1889 to a wealthy and powerful family in Vienna, Austria, philosophy seemed to be more of a compulsion for him than a love – a tendency to get stuck on certain questions, unable to move on without resolving them. Perhaps that's why Wittgenstein felt the need to 'solve' philosophy once and for all, attacking its roots and, by doing so, tearing down all philosophical debates, including the broader quarrel between realists and anti-realists in all domains.

paragraphs.cn: 维特根斯坦在同行面前傲慢得惊人，在面对他所追问的问题时却谦卑至深。他的任务不亚于发现逻辑根基之所在。1908年，他从新兴的航空工程领域起步，但很快便转向了数学哲学。
paragraphs.en: Wittgenstein was at once fantastically arrogant before his fellows and deeply humble before the questions he confronted. His task was no less than to discover what lay at the roots of logic. Starting out in the nascent field of aeronautical engineering in 1908, he quickly gravitated towards the philosophy of mathematics.

paragraphs.cn: 他的德国导师戈特洛布·弗雷格将他送到剑桥大学跟随罗素工作。关于维特根斯坦，罗素写道：'一个不知名的德国人出现了……固执而乖僻，但我认为并不愚蠢。'不到一年，维特根斯坦就向罗素证明了自己的能力，罗素说：'我当然要鼓励他。也许他会做出伟大的事业……我爱他，觉得他将解决我年事已高无法解决的问题。'
paragraphs.en: His German mentor Gottlob Frege sent him to the University of Cambridge to work with Russell. Of Wittgenstein, Russell wrote that: 'An unknown German appeared … obstinate and perverse, but I think not stupid.' Within a year, Wittgenstein had proved himself to Russell, who said: 'I shall certainly encourage him. Perhaps he will do great things … I love him and feel he will solve the problems I am too old to solve.'

paragraphs.cn: 然而，罗素的动机与维特根斯坦的并不一致。作为一位伯爵的孙子，罗素在一个贵族家庭中由他严厉而虔诚的祖母抚养长大。在祖母的宗教中找不到慰藉的罗素转向了数学，却发现这门古老学科的根基已经腐朽。他震惊地发现，几何学家欧几里得的公理——比如'两条平行线永不相交'——不过是假设而已。同样，数系也建立在自明之理之上。如果其中任何一个是错的，整个大厦就可能轰然倒塌。因此，罗素将他的一生献给了消除数学中一切不确定性的事业。
paragraphs.en: Russell's motivations, however, were at odds with Wittgenstein's. The grandson of an earl, Russell was raised in a noble household by his strict and devout grandmother. Finding no comfort in her religion, Russell sought it in mathematics, only to learn that the roots of the ancient discipline were rotten. He was horrified to discover that the geometer Euclid's axioms, such as 'two parallel lines do not intersect', were just assumptions. Likewise, the number system is based on self-evident truths. If any were wrong, the whole thing might come tumbling down. Russell therefore dedicated his life to resolving all uncertainty in mathematics.

paragraphs.cn: 罗素——维多利亚时代的产物——在没有确定性的地方继续寻找确定性
paragraphs.en: Russell – a product of the Victorian age – continued to look for certainty where there was none.

paragraphs.cn: 罗素挪用了维特根斯坦的哲学来加固基本逻辑，但维特根斯坦另有想法。他想理解是什么使事实成其为真或假——不是因为他渴望从确定性中获得慰藉，而是因为，嗯，这困扰着他。与罗素不同，维特根斯坦忠于真理，无论它多么丑陋。
paragraphs.en: Russell appropriated Wittgenstein's philosophy to shore up basic logic, but Wittgenstein had other ideas. He wanted to understand what made facts true or false – not because he desired comfort from certainty, but because, well, it bothered him. Unlike Russell, Wittgenstein was devoted to the truth, no matter how ugly.

paragraphs.cn: 维特根斯坦的人生和他的思想一样不同寻常。1911至1913年间，他与罗素密切合作，不时退居到挪威乡间的一座孤屋中长达数月，以理清自己的想法。1913年，他回到奥地利，旋即被卷入第一次世界大战的混乱之中。
paragraphs.en: Wittgenstein's life was no less unusual than his thoughts. He worked with Russell intensely from 1911-13, retreating to an isolated hut in rural Norway for months at a time in order to work out his ideas. In 1913, he returned to Austria, only to be swept up in the chaos of the First World War.

paragraphs.cn: 那是一个欧洲社会各层面都发生剧变的时代。帝国走向衰落，旧的君主秩序正在退潮。妇女选举权运动如火如荼，英美两国在战后赋予了女性投票权。科学和数学同样在挣脱19世纪古典主义的桎梏。爱因斯坦的相对论——狭义和广义——驱逐了艾萨克·牛顿的绝对时空概念，而数年后海森堡的不确定性原理则摧毁了测量的确定性。
paragraphs.en: It was a time of massive upheaval at all levels of European society. Empires were in decline, and the old monarchical order was ebbing away. Women's suffrage was in full swing, with the vote in Britain and the United States arriving after the war. Science and mathematics likewise were throwing off the shackles of 19th-century classicism. Einstein's theory of relativity, both special and general, banished Isaac Newton's concept of universal time and space, while Heisenberg's uncertainty principle destroyed the certainty of measurement some years later.

paragraphs.cn: 与此同时，罗素——维多利亚时代的产物——在没有确定性的地方继续寻找确定性。捕捉时代精神的年轻维特根斯坦承担起一劳永逸地解决实在论之争的使命，即使那意味着摧毁这场争论本身。
paragraphs.en: Russell, meanwhile – a product of the Victorian age – continued to look for certainty where there was none. It fell to young Wittgenstein, picking up the zeitgeist, to seek to resolve the realism debate once and for all, even if that meant destroying it.

paragraphs.cn: 战争岁月对维特根斯坦来说并不好过。体弱多病的他本可免于征兵，但他自愿入伍，并最终主动请缨奔赴前线。他的动机是复杂的，但从他的信件来看，他似乎在寻找一种他觉得在智识追求中找不到的东西。他从东线战场写信表达了这样的希望：'死亡的临近'将带来他精神上的转变。被孤独和精神上的渴望所折磨，他曾考虑自杀，最终被信仰所拯救。虽然战前他对宗教的厌恶不亚于罗素，但在一家书店中偶然发现列夫·托尔斯泰的《福音书简编》（1902年）使他成为一名虔诚的基督徒。他的信仰会影响他后来的工作，反之亦然。1918年，他被意大利人俘虏，在战俘营中度过了数月。
paragraphs.en: The war years were not easy on Wittgenstein. Poor health exempted him from conscription, but he volunteered for service and eventually to go to the Front. His reasons were complex, but from his letters it seems he was seeking something that he felt he could not find in intellectual pursuits. Writing from the Eastern Front, he expressed the hope that 'the nearness of death' would bring about a spiritual transformation in him. Wracked by loneliness and spiritual longing, he contemplated suicide, only to be saved by faith. While before the war he'd rivalled Russell in his distaste for religion, a chance discovery of Leo Tolstoy's The Gospel in Brief (1902) in a bookshop caused him to become a devout Christian. His faith would influence his later work, and vice versa. Captured by the Italians in 1918, he spent months in a prisoner-of-war camp.

paragraphs.cn: 正是在战争期间，他形成了其第一部伟大著作《逻辑哲学论》（1922年）中的许多思想——这本书通过语言将现代逻辑应用于形而上学，将关于实在的事实与实在本身联系起来。他称之为意义理论。
paragraphs.en: It was during the war that he formed much of his ideas for his first great work, the Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus (1922) – a book that applied modern logic to metaphysics via language to relate facts about reality to reality itself. He called it the theory of meaning.

paragraphs.cn: 在《逻辑哲学论》中，维特根斯坦发展了一种深深嵌入世界的哲学——不像理性唯心主义者罗素那样嵌入理想化的思想领域，而是嵌入我们谈论世界的方式。他没有提出一个关于词语和事实如何表征实在的理论——这对于实在论者和反实在论者都至关重要——而是认为表征本身是无关紧要的。没有人需要说出事实和对象表征了什么。它们就那样存在着，嵌入在我们关于实在的图像之中。说它们表征了什么其实是无意义的、荒谬的。正如在视觉领域，不需要第二幅图像来解释第一幅图像的含义；否则，我们将陷入无穷递归。
paragraphs.en: In the Tractatus, Wittgenstein developed a philosophy that was deeply embedded in the world – not in idealised realms of thought like the rational idealist Russell, but in how we talk about the world. Rather than coming up with a theory about how words and facts represent reality, which is crucial to both realists and anti-realists, he determined that representation is irrelevant. No one needs to say what facts and objects represent. They are simply there, embedded in our picture of reality. To say what they represent is actually nonsense, absurd. As in the visual realm, a second picture is not necessary to explain what a first picture means; if it were otherwise, we'd fall prey to infinite recursion.

paragraphs.cn: 维特根斯坦是认真的——他相信我们无法谈论不在世界之中的事物
paragraphs.en: Wittgenstein was serious in that he believed we could not talk about things that are not in the world.

paragraphs.cn: 维特根斯坦所理解的是，你不能用词语来解释表征，因为词语本身就是表征。这就像试图走到宇宙之外去向别人展示宇宙是什么一样——这既不可能也不必要。一个句子通过其自身的意义来显示它的含义。因此，如果我说'珍妮有一个苹果'，我不必解释'珍妮'和'苹果'这两个词如何表征世界中的物理对象；我也不必解释'有'是什么意思。我们彼此都理解，如果珍妮就在那里，而她手里拿的是一个橘子，那么这个命题就是假的。它展示了自身的意义。只要我们都理解语言规则，就没有什么更多可说的了。
paragraphs.en: What Wittgenstein understood is that you can't use words to explain representation, because words are representations themselves. It would be like trying to travel outside the Universe to show somebody what the Universe is – a feat that's both impossible and unnecessary. A sentence shows what it means by its own sense. Thus, if I say 'Jenny has an apple,' I do not have to explain how the words 'Jenny' and 'apple' represent physical objects in the world; nor do I have to explain what 'has' means. We mutually understand that, if Jenny is right there and she has an orange in her hand, the proposition is false. It shows its sense. There is nothing more to say about it, as long as we both understand the rules of the language.

paragraphs.cn: 因此，维特根斯坦甚至在其早期著作中就暗示，实在论者与反实在论者之间的争论是无意义的，因为双方都在试图说出那些只能被显示的东西。从这一早期维特根斯坦主义的视角来看，一个数学方程——事实上，任何方程，包括支配量子力学的那些方程——就像是一张实在的照片。与照片一样，我们不需要任何人对它的意义做出实在论或反实在论的诠释。我们不需要哥本哈根诠释或多世界诠释来向我们指示方程的含义，因为它已经如它所将是的那样显而易见了。追问波函数表征了什么，就像追问米开朗基罗的大卫雕像或梵高的《星月夜》表征了什么：任何超出纯粹事实的解释都是不够的、主观的。
paragraphs.en: Thus, Wittgenstein, even in his early work, suggests that the realist versus anti-realist debate is meaningless because both sides are trying to say things that are only showable. From this early Wittgensteinian perspective, a mathematical equation – in fact, any equation, including the ones governing quantum mechanics – is like a photograph of reality. Like photographs, we do not need anyone to interpret its meaning as realist or anti-realist. We do not need a Copenhagen or a many-worlds to indicate the sense of the equation to us, because it is already as apparent as it is ever going to be. To ask what the wavefunction represents is like asking what Michelangelo's statue of David or Van Gogh's painting The Starry Night represents: any explanation beyond the mere facts is insufficient and subjective.

paragraphs.cn: 你可能觉得这个解释令人不满意。然而维特根斯坦是认真的——他相信我们无法谈论不在世界之中的事物。虽然我们可以用粒子、测量和计算来谈论量子力学，但任何将意义赋予我们所能观测到的东西的哲学属性（比如'真实的'或'不真实的'）都是无意义的。对于赋予波函数额外含义这件事，我们必须保持沉默。
paragraphs.en: You might find this explanation unsatisfactory. Yet Wittgenstein was serious in that he believed we could not talk about things that are not in the world. While we might talk about quantum mechanics in terms of particles, measurements and calculations, any philosophical attributes that ascribe significance to what we can observe (such as 'real' or 'unreal') are nonsense. We must be silent on ascribing additional meaning to the wavefunction.

paragraphs.cn: 然而，维特根斯坦关于哲学中什么能谈、什么不能谈的探索，在此后数十年间不断演进，并最终导致了他对那些哲学概念的拒斥——包括他建构《逻辑哲学论》时所依赖的图像理论。
paragraphs.en: Wittgenstein's exploration about what we can and cannot talk about in philosophy, however, would evolve over the next several decades, and lead to a rejection of even those philosophical concepts such as the picture theory upon which he built the Tractatus.

paragraphs.cn: 写完《逻辑哲学论》之后，维特根斯坦相信他已经'解决'了哲学。以一种奇特而傲慢的谦逊，他在1920年代离开了这一学科，先后从事过园丁、教师和建筑师等多种工作。
paragraphs.en: Having written the Tractatus, Wittgenstein believed that he had 'solved' philosophy. In his strange, haughty humility, he left the discipline in the 1920s, and worked various jobs as a gardener, teacher and architect.

paragraphs.cn: 然而，这一间歇期在维特根斯坦接触到逻辑经验主义之后宣告终结。这是一个由被称为维也纳学圈的哲学家群体所发起的运动。他们强调经验知识和'逻辑实证主义'理论，即我们只能对可以被测量或观测到的事物赋予意义。一个严格的逻辑实证主义者不关心解释或诠释；相反，他们认为理解世界建立在测量及其预测之上。
paragraphs.en: This interregnum came to an end, however, when Wittgenstein was exposed to logical empiricism. This was a movement arising from a group of philosophers known as the Vienna Circle. They emphasised empirical knowledge and the theory of 'logical positivism', meaning that we can only ascribe meaning to what can be measured or observed. A strict logical positivist is unconcerned with explanations or interpretations; rather, they believe that understanding the world is built upon measurement and its prediction.

paragraphs.cn: 《逻辑哲学论》是维也纳学圈的奠基支柱之一，这激励了维特根斯坦继续他的工作。他决定于1929年回到剑桥，但从此不再专注于数学和逻辑哲学，而是转向了日常语言和心理学。
paragraphs.en: The Tractatus was a foundational pillar of the Vienna Circle, and this galvanised Wittgenstein to continue his work. He decided to return to Cambridge in 1929, but moved away from the philosophy of mathematics and logic, and towards ordinary language and psychology.

paragraphs.cn: 他彻底否定了意义理论，同时又对它做出了最有力的贡献之一
paragraphs.en: He rejects the theory of meaning entirely while making one of his most powerful contributions to it.

paragraphs.cn: 维特根斯坦最终将自己的思想汇集为一本名为《哲学研究》的著作——这大概是哲学史上最奇特的书之一（或许正因如此，它直到维特根斯坦1951年因癌症去世后才得以出版）。这本书并非按照主题或命题的顺序组织，而是由一系列意识流式的观点、论证和陈述构成。这其实与其自身的哲学主张相吻合：哲学本身无法发现任何东西。它不过是一种疗法，而这种疗法很容易变成智识的疾病。它唯一的工作就是提醒我们那些我们已经知道的东西。
paragraphs.en: Wittgenstein eventually collected his ideas in a book called Philosophical Investigations – probably one of the strangest books of philosophy ever published (and, perhaps for this reason, only released after Wittgenstein's death from cancer in 1951). Rather than being organised as a sequence of topics or propositions, the Investigations is a stream-of-consciousness series of points, arguments and statements. That was in fact in keeping with its own philosophy, which is that philosophy itself can discover nothing. It is simply a form of therapy that can quickly become a disease of the intellect. Its only job is to remind us of that which we already know.

paragraphs.cn: 从这一后期维特根斯坦主义的立场来看，所有那些不同的量子诠释都是病态心灵的产物，最终是自我毁灭的。这是因为所有哲学实际上不过是对语法的争论。他认为，如果我们认真对待这些形而上学争论，我们不仅是错误的，而且是病态的。
paragraphs.en: From this later Wittgensteinian position, all the varying quantum interpretations would be the result of diseased minds, and ultimately self-destructive. That's because all philosophy is actually a debate over mere grammar. If we take seriously these metaphysical debates, he argues, we are not only wrong, but ill.

paragraphs.cn: 在《哲学研究》中，维特根斯坦彻底否定了意义理论，同时又对它做出了最有力的贡献之一。他说，一切语言的意义都来自它在具体情形中的使用方式。一切语言都是像国际象棋或扑克牌一样的游戏——我们通过玩耍来学习规则，而非通过理论化或下定义。因此，普遍定义的概念本身就是一种人为的建构，一种障眼法。一个人无法谈论词语'真正'意味着什么；他只能使用它们。这既适用于数学，也适用于日常语言。
paragraphs.en: In his Philosophical Investigations, Wittgenstein rejects the theory of meaning entirely while making one of his most powerful contributions to it. All language, he says, gains its definitions from how it is used in specific cases. All language is a game like chess or poker – we learn the rules by playing, not theorising or defining. So the very notion of a universal definition is an artifice, a bit of subterfuge. One cannot talk about what words really mean; one can only use them. This applies as much to mathematics as it does to ordinary words.

paragraphs.cn: 维特根斯坦想向我们展示，我们需要停止试图诠释语言。以指向村庄的路标为例。我们看到路标就立刻理解了它的含义。虽然其中涉及符号解码的成分，但他说不存在更深层的诠释步骤。换言之，我们不需要弄清楚路标如何表征实在——无论是在柏拉图的理想世界中还是在我们头脑中某种主观的实在概念中。这个路标几乎可以包含任何种类的符号、颜色编码或数字，只要人们看到它之后采取的行动是正确的就行。路标向我们'显示'了村庄在哪里，因为那就是那一类路标的使用方式。那就是它的真正含义。
paragraphs.en: Wittgenstein wants to show us that we need to stop trying to interpret language. Take the example of a road sign pointing to a village. We see the road sign and instantly understand its meaning. While there is an element of symbolic decoding involved, there is no deeper interpretive step, he says. In other words, we do not need to figure out how the sign represents reality, either in the ideal world of Plato or some subjective concept of reality in our heads. The sign could contain almost any kind of symbols, colour coding or numbers, as long as the action that people take upon seeing it is the correct one. The sign 'shows' us where the village is, because that is how signs of that kind are used. That is its true meaning.

paragraphs.cn: 后期的维特根斯坦完全否定了他自己的实在图像论。图像固然美好而令人满足，但真正重要的是使用。在这一解读下，波函数根本不像一幅实在的图像。重要的是物理学家现在有能力进行计算，从而得出可以通过测量来验证的预测。然而，关键并不在于测量本身——正如逻辑实证主义者可能声称的那样——而在于物理学家如何行事。他们的计算方式是否产生了更多、更好的物理学？语言和数学是一种控制和协调集体人类行动以完成工作的手段。
paragraphs.en: The late Wittgenstein entirely rejects his own picture theory of reality. Pictures are nice and satisfying, but usage is what actually matters. The wavefunction, on this reading, isn't like a picture of reality at all. All that matters is that physicists now have the ability to do calculations, which lead to predictions that can be verified by measurements. The point is not the measurements themselves, however – as a logical positivist might claim – but how the physicists behave. Do they calculate in a way that leads to more and better physics? Language and mathematics are a means of controlling and modifying collective human action so that work gets done.

paragraphs.cn: 这是作为文化的语言，而非作为图像的语言。而文化包含了仪式。与所有仪式性社群一样，物理学包含其规则、诠释、专业术语、被准入奥秘的追随者群体、不同等级的教化，以及守门人。虽然某些社会将仪式与安抚神灵联系起来，但在科学中，仪式的作用是治疗性地安抚我们的哲学需求。不同诠释之间的竞争与部族神灵之间的竞争并无不同，都在试图取得文化上的优势。
paragraphs.en: This is language as culture rather than language as picture. And culture includes ritual. Like all ritualistic communities, physics contains its rules, interpretations, specialised vocabulary, a community of adherents who are admitted to the arcane arts, levels of indoctrination, and gatekeepers. While some societies relate ritual to the appeasement of gods and spirits, in science they serve to therapeutically appease our philosophical needs. Competition between interpretations is not unlike competition between clan gods, trying to achieve cultural dominance.

paragraphs.cn: 进化文化人类学支持了这一观点，该学科已经证明语言与仪式和宗教有着深刻的联系。同样，科学的词汇、语法和程序本身也是仪式性的，每个分支学科都有自己的习俗和规范。这些之所以必要，是因为科学家不可能纯粹基于事实本身来评估新研究；验证一个新理论或实验结果往往需要数年时间。仪式的作用从进化角度来看也完全合理。人类在数十万年间通过将关乎生存的信息编码到仪式中来在充满敌意的星球上导航，然后将其代代相传。当我们在仅仅几百年前发明了科学方法时，我们必须将它嫁接到我们的这一本性之上以便传承下去，为新的目的劫持了一种古老而有效的文化机制。
paragraphs.en: Evolutionary cultural anthropology backs up this view, having demonstrated that language is deeply connected to ritual and religion. Likewise, the vocabulary, grammar and procedures of science are themselves ritualistic, with each subdiscipline having its own mores and norms. These are necessary because it is impossible for scientists to evaluate new research purely based on factual merits; it often takes years to validate a new theory or experimental result. The role of ritual also makes perfect sense from an evolutionary perspective. Humans have spent hundreds of thousands of years navigating a hostile planet by encoding information crucial for survival into ritual, which can then be transmitted across generations. When we invented the scientific method only a few hundred years ago, we had to graft it onto that part of our nature in order to pass it down the generations, hijacking an ancient and effective cultural mechanism for a new purpose.

paragraphs.cn: 科学的活动，而非其诠释，定义了词语和符号的含义
paragraphs.en: The activity of science, rather than its interpretations, defines what words and symbols mean.

paragraphs.cn: 因此，量子诠释并非真正对实在的探究，它也没有告诉我们关于世界的任何新东西。相反，它是一种语法探究，或者用人类学的术语来说，一种文化探究。它是不同哲学疗法之间的竞争，满足着不同的情感-文化需求。
paragraphs.en: Hence, quantum interpretation is not really an investigation into reality, and it tells us nothing new about the world. Rather, it is a grammatical investigation or, in anthropological terms, a cultural one. It is a competition between differing philosophical therapies, satisfying different emotional-cultural needs.

paragraphs.cn: 关键在于，正是在科学活动中——无论是通过实验还是计算——科学所产生的所有有用信息才得以存在。维特根斯坦举例解释了如何确定一个物体长度这一行为并非学习理论和定义的过程，而是一种活动："'确定长度'意味着什么，不是通过学习什么是长度和什么是确定来学会的；'长度'这个词的含义是通过学习——在诸多事情中——确定长度是怎么一回事来学会的。"
paragraphs.en: Crucially, it is in the activity of science, whether via experiment or calculation, that all its useful information it generates exists. Wittgenstein explains, for example, how the act of determining the length of an object is not a case for learning theories and definitions, but an activity: "What 'determining the length' means is not learned by learning what length and determining are; the meaning of the word 'length' is learnt by learning, among other things, what it is to determine length."

paragraphs.cn: 这一描述表明，学会量子物理学意味着什么就是学会计算它——反之亦然。
paragraphs.en: This description indicates that to learn what quantum physics means is to learn to calculate it – and vice versa.

paragraphs.cn: 维特根斯坦暗示，甚至数学也是一种共享的语言和活动。他问道：'即使每个人都相信二乘二等于五，它仍然是四——这意味着什么？因为每个人都相信二乘二等于五会是什么样子呢？嗯，我可以想象，比如，人们有一种不同的演算，或者一种我们不会称之为"计算"的技术。但它是错误的吗？'
paragraphs.en: Wittgenstein suggests that even mathematics is potentially a shared language and activity. He asks: "what would this mean: 'Even though everybody believed that twice two was five it would still be four'?—For what would it be like for everybody to believe that?—Well, I could imagine, for instance, that people had a different calculus, or a technique which we should not call 'calculating'. But would it be wrong?"

paragraphs.cn: 他暗示'奇怪'是一个更好的词，但我们没有共同的参照框架来称其为错误。他进一步暗示数学在很大程度上是一种活动，像一场游戏，我们都知道构成一个系统的相同规则。因此，我们得出相同的结论，从不争论证明了什么。然而，某种外星物种可能为其数学游戏想出不同的规则，这些规则同样有效，因为他们遵循的是他们的规则。
paragraphs.en: He suggests that 'odd' would be a better word for it, but we would have no common frame of reference to call it wrong. He goes on to suggest that mathematics is very much an activity, like a game, and we all know the same rules that form a system. Hence, we all come to the same conclusions and never argue about what is proved. Yet, some alien species could come up with different rules for their mathematical game that are no less valid because they are following their rules.

paragraphs.cn: 如果维特根斯坦今天还活着，他可能会用文化人类学的词汇来表述他的论证。因为在他看来，这些共享的语法和语言游戏构成了更大的仪式性机制的一部分，将人类活动与人类知识连接起来，其深度如同DNA之于人类生物学。这也是进化如何利用已有机制来产生新行为的一个完美例证。
paragraphs.en: If Wittgenstein were alive today, he might have couched his arguments in the vocabulary of cultural anthropology. For this shared grammar and these language games, in his view, form part of much larger ritualistic mechanisms that connect human activity with human knowledge, as deeply as DNA connects to human biology. It is also a perfect example of how evolution works by using pre-existing mechanisms to generate new behaviours.

paragraphs.cn: 从这一切中得出的结论是，语言和数学中的诠释与表征与古代宗教的超自然解释并无二致。试图解决玻尔与爱因斯坦之间的争论，就像试图回答那个禅宗公案：如果森林里一棵树倒下而没有人听到，它是否发出了声音。你无法给出确定的肯定或否定回答，因为所有人类语言都必须与人类活动相联系。而所有人类语言和活动都是仪式，通过其相互关联来表示意义。因此，在不指明某种活动——某个实验——来提取意义的情况下追问波函数意味着什么，就像追问那棵倒下的树的声音一样。这是无意义的。
paragraphs.en: The conclusion from all of this is that interpretation and representation in language and mathematics are little different than the supernatural explanations of ancient religions. Trying to resolve the debate between Bohr and Einstein is like trying to answer the Zen kōan about whether the tree falling in the forest makes a sound if no one can hear it. One cannot say definitely yes or no, because all human language must connect to human activity. And all human language and activity are ritual, signifying meaning by their interconnectedness. To ask what the wavefunction means without specifying an activity – and experiment – to extract that meaning is, therefore, as sensible as asking about the sound of the falling tree. It is nonsense.

paragraphs.cn: 我已不再认为世界充满了截然分明的真理，而是一个充满无数可能性的所在
paragraphs.en: I have come to think of the world not as filled with sharply defined truths but as a place of myriad possibilities.

paragraphs.cn: 作为一名科学家和数学家，维特根斯坦挑战了我自己对那些没有科学价值的现象寻求诠释的倾向——并让我认识到这类解释不过是叙事而已。他教导说，哲学所能做的只是提醒我们那些显而易见为真的事物。波函数有一个多世界诠释，这是显而易见为真的——但你必须首先假设多重宇宙的存在，而它是无法被测量的。所以这种诠释是一个同义反复，而非一个发现。
paragraphs.en: As a scientist and mathematician, Wittgenstein has challenged my own tendency to seek out interpretations of phenomena that have no scientific value – and to see such explanations as nothing more than narratives. He taught that all that philosophy can do is remind us of what is evidently true. It's evidently true that the wavefunction has a multiverse interpretation, but one must assume the multiverse first, since it cannot be measured. So the interpretation is a tautology, not a discovery.

paragraphs.cn: 我已经谦卑地认识到，我们无法证明坚持某一种实在诠释而放弃另一种是合理的。取代早年热忱的柏拉图主义，我已不再认为世界充满了截然分明的真理，而是一个充满无数可能性的所在——每一个可能性，正如波函数本身中的可能性一样，可以同时为真。同样，数学及其周围的语言与其说是表征实在，不如说是帮助人们在世界中导航的可靠工具。它们源自人类，为人类的目的而存在。
paragraphs.en: I have humbled myself to the fact that we can't justify clinging to one interpretation of reality over another. In place of my early enthusiastic Platonism, I have come to think of the world not as one filled with sharply defined truths, but rather as a place containing myriad possibilities – each of which, like the possibilities within the wavefunction itself, can be simultaneously true. Likewise, mathematics and its surrounding language don't represent reality so much as serve as a trusty tool for helping people to navigate the world. They are of human origin and for human purposes.

paragraphs.cn: 因此，闭嘴去算，意味着承认我们理解世界的途径是有限的。作为科学家，我们唯一的选择就是观察、预测和检验。这可能不如我们在脑海中构建的各种诠释那般迷人，但它通向真正知识的王道。
paragraphs.en: To shut up and calculate, then, recognises that there are limits to our pathways for understanding. Our only option as scientists is to look, predict and test. This might not be as glamorous an offering as the interpretations we can construct in our minds, but it is the royal road to real knowledge.

articles.title: 尼采的道德思想如何被语文学塑造
articles.author: Matthew Meyer
articles.summary: 在《人性的，太人性的》（1878）中，弗里德里希·尼采写道：'缺乏历史感是所有哲学家的原罪。'在指责哲学缺乏历史感时，尼采呼应了19世纪思想的更广泛趋势。与'哲...
articles.content_en:
In Human, All Too Human (1878), Friedrich Nietzsche wrote that 'A lack of historical sense is the original failing of all philosophers.' In accusing philosophy of lacking historical sense, Nietzsche was echoing broader trends in 19th-century thought. In comparison with the 'philosophical' 18th century, the 19th century is sometimes described as the 'historical' century, one in which investigation into more universal features of human reason gave way to increased focus on how particular historical trajectories influence language, culture and moral assumptions.

PARA 2:
The 19th century is also what one might call the 'philological century'. Philology is the critical study of written sources, including their linguistic features, history of reception and cultural context. Today, the term sounds outmoded, evoking dusty, learned tomes of fastidious source criticism. However, philology was a leading intellectual discipline in 19th-century Germany due to a flurry of methodological developments that revolutionised our understanding of ancient and sacred texts. New rigorous techniques of verifying sources were developed, merely speculative hypotheses were discouraged, more detailed comparative studies of language were conducted. While such methods were scholarly, sometimes bordering on scholastic, their application had significant cultural impact, spilling out of scholarly journals into broader public consciousness.

PARA 3:
Nietzsche imbibed these trends as a young man. An all-round talented student (mathematics was a notable exception), he gained admittance as a 14-year-old to Schulpforta, one of the most prestigious humanistic schools in Germany. A training ground for scholars and teachers, the school's specialty was classical antiquity, and Nietzsche received a rigorous education in Greek and Latin, read historical works by Voltaire and Cicero, and wrote philological treatises on topics such as the saga of Ermanarich and the Greek poet Theognis.

PARA 4:
This philological education informed not only his early works that deal directly with Greek antiquity, such as The Birth of Tragedy (1872), but also his later books on morality and moral psychology. Appreciating this philological influence is crucial for understanding the significance of his philosophically most important work, On the Genealogy of Morality (1887), and the 'genealogical' method of philosophy it has inspired.

PARA 5:
On the Genealogy of Morality is a puzzling book. It deals with many classic topics of moral philosophy, such as the concept of good, free will, moral responsibility and guilt. However, it does not investigate them in a typical philosophical fashion – for instance, by asking 'What is good?' or 'Do we have free will?' Rather, it takes a historical approach, asking where our ideas about the good, or free will, or guilt, have come from.

PARA 6:
Nietzsche's answers to these historical questions were and continue to be, to put it mildly, controversial. Not for nothing did he describe himself as 'dynamite'. For instance, he argued that contemporary Western egalitarian and altruistic assumptions were a form of 'slave morality' that emerged from the frustrations and ressentiment (roughly, resentment) of a priestly caste. This slave morality was a reaction against what Nietzsche calls 'master morality', an archaic ethics that emphasises virtues of excellence, health and respect for social hierarchy. Master morality valorises strength and denigrates the meekness and bookish intellectualism of the religious leaders of the priestly caste. In response, according to Nietzsche, the religious leaders invented a new evaluative framework in which they come out on top – one in which aggressiveness is classified as 'evil' and meekness and altruism as 'good' – a revaluation of existing values. These moral assumptions are then taken up by the lower slave caste, as they valorised their lowly status and enabled them to reinterpret their impotence as principled choice. In sum, on Nietzsche's view, many of our fundamental moral assumptions derive from archaic status competition.

PARA 7:
What has puzzled many readers of Nietzsche's genealogy is how these historical claims are supposed to be relevant to philosophical questions about morality. Nietzsche states that his ultimate aim is to assess 'the value of our values', but it is not clear how his historical claims could or should contribute to this assessment. In turning to history, it might seem that Nietzsche is simply shifting the subject of moral philosophy, following the adage that if you don't like what is being said, then you should change the conversation. After all, the question of where our values come from just seems like a different question from philosophical questions about their nature, value and authority. If Nietzsche is trying to use this history to answer these philosophical questions, then it might seem he is committing a fallacy, the 'genetic fallacy'.

PARA 8:
Showing that something has a bad origin isn't sufficient to show that it is bad

PARA 9:
The genetic fallacy is the purported mistake of evaluating something on the basis of its origin or past characteristics. For instance, imagine a friend tells you that you should not wear a wedding ring because wedding rings originally symbolised ankle chains worn by women to prevent them from running away from their husbands (the example is from Attacking Faulty Reasoning (7th ed, 2012) by T Edward Damer). Even if wedding rings have such a dubious history, this history does not show that it is bad or objectionable to wear them now. When Nietzsche suggests that he should reevaluate Christian morality on the basis of this origin story, we might suspect he is employing a similar type of fallacious reasoning.

PARA 10:
Concerns about falling prey to the genetic fallacy are part of what explain moral philosophy's lack of a historical sense. Showing that something has a bad origin isn't sufficient to show that it is bad, as the previous examples indicate. Neither does examining the origins of our values seem necessary for evaluating them. To evaluate our moral beliefs and practices, it seems that we need only look at the reasons for and against them, not the causes of our beliefs in them. For instance, to criticise egalitarianism, we should consider potential objections to egalitarian attitudes, such as that they recommend levelling down, prevent the achievement of human excellence, or fail to honour supposed differences in moral desert. Whatever one thinks of these criticisms, it seems that making and assessing them does not require looking at the history of our values. If these points are correct, we can see why moral philosophy would proceed ahistorically: if moral philosophy aims to critically scrutinise the value of our values, and origin stories are neither necessary nor sufficient for this purpose, then origin stories are irrelevant to moral philosophy.

PARA 11:
Nietzsche thought this reasoning mistaken, although exactly why he thought so is a matter of scholarly dispute. His historical critique is typically understood in one of two ways: either he held that historical information was directly relevant to the authority or justification of moral norms, or he held it was indirectly relevant by providing evidence that moral attitudes are motivated by ressentiment and/or hinder great human achievements.

PARA 12:
To grasp how history might be directly relevant to the status of our values, we might begin by acknowledging that the value of many human artefacts depends on their history – Picasso paintings are more valuable than perfect replicas because they come from Picasso and not from a copyist, and your family heirlooms hold special value because of their historical connection to your family. Something similar may be true of our values.

PARA 13:
For instance, if our values include or imply commands, then their history might be relevant to their authority, our obligation to obey them. Many commands depend for their authority on the authority of the commander, and vengeful clergy with status anxiety do not seem like authoritative commanders. More specifically, one might think that the authority of moral commands depends on their coming from God or pure reason, so insofar as historical investigation reveals that they derive from a secular 'human, all too human' source, then history might undermine their authority. If Christian values turn out to have been born from resentment, then that, on this sort of argument, undermines their value to us today. In a similar way, philological work that traces the Bible back to secular origins might undermine the authority of the moral teachings contained within it by undermining the claim that those teachings come from God or Jesus.

PARA 14:
Another way that the status of our values may depend directly on their history is if they include or imply beliefs about value claims, as historical information can affect whether our beliefs are justified. I might destabilise a belief by coming to know that the process by which I acquired that belief is unreliable. For instance, say I believe a rumour that my grandfather used to pilfer money from the church collection jar, and I acquired this belief from my father. However, I then learn that my father heard this rumour from his brother, who is a notorious liar and resentful of his strict religious upbringing. Given that my belief traces back to an unreliable source, I might reasonably think that my belief is no longer justified.

PARA 15:
If moral norms were originally motivated by ressentiment, then this directly 'taints' them in some fashion

PARA 16:
Similarly, it might be that we acquired our moral beliefs from our parents (and broader culture), who acquired them from their parents (and broader culture), all the way back to the vengeful clergy. But if vengeful clergy are not a reliable guide to the moral truth, then I should distrust my parents' teachings and therefore my moral beliefs. Nietzsche's historical story might therefore help emancipate us from illusions by exposing information that challenges our faith in cherished but unjustified assumptions.

PARA 17:
These points about authority and justification are points that philosophers sometimes make about the relevance of history for moral philosophy, and it is possible that Nietzsche was making points of this kind. However, this interpretation does not capture what many commentators and lay readers typically take to be the main insights of Nietzsche's text. For instance, when undergraduates read the Genealogy, they usually take with them the idea that Nietzsche's history shows that much avowed concern with virtue, rightness and justice is less noble than it purports to be – it is motivated by some concoction of petty vengefulness, self-valorisation and resentment against powerful and high-status others. Here, it is Nietzsche's claims about the particular interests and motives of the priests, warriors and slaves that is crucial, not merely the fact that none of these figures is divine or reliable.

PARA 18:
How might these interests and motives play a role in Nietzsche's critique of morality? Perhaps Nietzsche is assuming that, if moral norms were originally motivated by ressentiment, then this directly renders them objectionable by 'tainting' them in some fashion, even if those who conform to them nowadays are not motivated in the same manner. However, Nietzsche need not make this assumption. It is more likely that Nietzsche sees his historical story as relevant in a more indirect manner, as evidence of similar psychological dynamics in contemporary society. On such a view, genealogy is relevant to a critique of morality because it provides evidence that contemporary morality has objectionable features, such as bad motives, that are not themselves historical.

PARA 19:
The idea that many avowed moral views have dubious motives is not a hard sell, particularly given modern internet culture. So one might wonder why one need turn to history to make this observation. There are two reasons. First, Nietzsche's history helps us see these dynamics more clearly by telling a historical story in which they are present in a simple and unmasked form. While contemporary morality is overlaid with complexities and rationalisations, if we look to the past, we can better see psychological dynamics that have now been obscured. History, on this interpretation, is used to unmask the present.

PARA 20:
Second, because we are less personally and emotionally invested in viewing past situations in a particular manner, we are better able to take a less credulous, more realistic perspective of it. While we might bristle at thinking that our political convictions are motivated by ressentiment, we are more likely to recognise this dynamic in historically distant others, which then enables us to recognise it more easily in ourselves. Historical perspective prevents us from giving in to wishful thinking.

PARA 21:
These points form an important strand of Nietzsche's thinking. However, there are challenges to reading Nietzsche as using history in this indirect, evidential manner. Such interpretations treat the past as merely a simplified version of the present, differing from contemporary society only in minor ways. Consequently, this approach overlooks the kinds of significant historical change and contingency that are central to historicist perspectives on our social practices. If this account of Nietzsche's genealogy were the whole story, it would seem that Nietzsche himself would lack a historical sense. If we want to understand how Nietzsche's Genealogy might honour such historicist assumptions, we should turn to the history of Nietzsche's genealogical method itself. That is, we should return to philology.

PARA 22:
Much philological research in Nietzsche's time was interested in investigating the way in which ancient and sacred texts are complex composites, with diverging elements stitched together from conflicting sources. For instance, Julius Wellhausen argued that the Pentateuch, the Hebrew Bible, was a human artefact that could be decomposed into four independent sources, each of which originated significantly after Moses (who was previously thought to be the sole author, a kind of ghostwriter for the divine). This source criticism enables us to see the Hebrew Bible less as a unified work and more as an amalgam of distinct elements with differing histories. This information discourages looking to the Bible for a unified theology, and therefore encouraged taking a less theological approach to the Bible generally.

PARA 23:
As a teenager at Schulpforta, Nietzsche learned this broad philological approach to ancient texts. For instance, his teacher Friedrich August Koberstein (himself a revered scholar and historian) suggested that he research a poem on the saga of the 4th-century Ostrogoth King Ermanarich. The poem is puzzling because some parts describe Ermanarich as a noble hero while others describe him as a coward and someone who murdered his wife. It is not obvious how readers are supposed to evaluate Ermanarich. Adopting a historical perspective, young Nietzsche argued that these conflicts arise from the fact that the poem does not have a single author but rather derives from a variety of sources – it is a layered construction with some parts from the Near East, others from Germany, as well as some from Denmark and Britain.

PARA 24:
Nietzsche traces back different aspects of our moral framework to distinct sources

PARA 25:
This broad approach to texts informs Nietzsche's thinking about contemporary morality, which he describes in the Genealogy's preface as a 'long, hard-to-decipher hieroglyphic script'. When we look at our contemporary values, there are some ostensible tensions. For instance, many believe that excellent human achievements deserve special admiration and rewards, but also that it is only fair to receive special rewards for things that one has earned. But excellent human achievements are often due, at least partially, to innate talents that are not earned, so these views seem in tension. Much moral philosophy involves teasing out such intuitive judgments, finding tensions among them, and considering ways of resolving these tensions, much like a theological approach to the Bible involves identifying explicit and implicit theological claims in the text, finding tensions among them, and considering ways of resolving them.

PARA 26:
However, Nietzsche's philological approach is different. Like Wellhausen's historical analysis of the Hebrew Bible, Nietzsche traces back different aspects of our moral framework to distinct sources. The perfectionist elements come from the warrior caste, the egalitarian ones from the priestly caste and slave caste, and other aspects of our moral framework – such as respect for ancestors – have still other sources. Rather than simply different strands coming together, we have a 'document' that has been rewritten and reinterpreted over time, often by those with complex and conflicting motivations. A philological approach to morality understands it as a complex amalgam rather than a unified evaluative framework.

PARA 27:
This historical approach differs from more typical forms of moral philosophy because it does not assume that there is a unified answer to questions like 'What is the good?', at least if that answer is supposed to fit with many of our intuitive judgments. Rather, it brings out the competing strands of our moral thinking by providing an explanation of their presence that suggests that there won't be a way to reconcile them. That is, just as a scientific philology of the Bible might undermine the presuppositions of Christian theology – that one will be able to construct a unified theology that fits sacred texts – so too a philology of morality undermines a presupposition of a certain strand of moral philosophy, that there is a unified moral theory that will make sense of our moral assumptions and practices. When one takes the historical perspective, much moral philosophy looks like it is engaged in a hopeless apologetic project of trying to reconcile what cannot be reconciled. There is no higher compromise between masters and slaves.

PARA 28:
Now, if Nietzsche's philological approach were relevant only to philosophical methodology, it might seem of little interest outside academic debate. However, this approach to morality does not just highlight a problem for contemporary moral philosophy. It also highlights a problem for us. For the fact that our modern moral views are fragmented and disunified itself poses problems. It means that it is difficult to act effectively and consistently, as there is no overarching framework for reconciling competing considerations in practical decision-making. It means that we might be committed to assessing ourselves according to conflicting criteria, ensuring that we are never able to measure up to the standards we set for ourselves. It sets us against ourselves. Nietzsche considered this kind of fragmentation and tension in our views a kind of illness, one of the ways in which modern society is sick.

PARA 29:
Once one sees how the tools of philology can be applied to morality, it is easy to see how they can be extended to other parts of contemporary culture. If we examine the history of the norms that structure various social identities, we are often able to make salient forms of internal conflict that are latent in everyday life. For example, by examining the history of gender norms, we may more clearly discern tensions within contemporary conceptions of gender – eg, if you're a woman, you are supposed to be both an angel of the house and a girlboss, and if you're a man, you need to be an alpha while avoiding being toxically masculine. These competing norms often create conflicts within those who are invested in these identities, and clarifying the conflict may help avoid fruitless and frustrating attempts to reconcile these competing elements.

PARA 30:
Of course, fragmentation and tension need not always be negative – there can be productive tensions, as Nietzsche recognised. Later theorists who have wanted to apply this philological approach to the cultural sphere have often aimed to emphasise its emancipatory potential. For example, understanding national histories as multifaceted genealogies, yielding political communities that are complex composites rather than unified cultures, enables one to appreciate the dynamism of cultural richness and challenge simplistic views of cultural homogeneity. 'Unity' is not a virtue in every domain.

PARA 31:
Stepping back, a central puzzle about Nietzsche is why a self-styled 'philosopher of the future' should be so interested in the past. By examining the history of Nietzsche's historicism, we can discern an answer. If employed critically, philology is not merely a tool for exploring the past but one for actively shaping the future.

paragraphs.cn: 在《人性的，太人性的》（1878）中，弗里德里希·尼采写道：'缺乏历史感是所有哲学家的原罪。'在指责哲学缺乏历史感时，尼采呼应了19世纪思想的更广泛趋势。与'哲学的'18世纪相比，19世纪有时被称为'历史的'世纪——对人类理性更普遍特征的探究让位于对特定历史轨迹如何影响语言、文化和道德假设的更多关注。
paragraphs.en: In Human, All Too Human (1878), Friedrich Nietzsche wrote that 'A lack of historical sense is the original failing of all philosophers.' In accusing philosophy of lacking historical sense, Nietzsche was echoing broader trends in 19th-century thought. In comparison with the 'philosophical' 18th century, the 19th century is sometimes described as the 'historical' century, one in which investigation into more universal features of human reason gave way to increased focus on how particular historical trajectories influence language, culture and moral assumptions.

paragraphs.cn: 19世纪也是人们所说的'语文学的世纪'。语文学是对文献典籍的批判性研究，包括其语言学特征、接受史和文化语境。如今，这个术语听起来已经过时，让人联想到满是灰尘的博学典籍和一丝不苟的文献考证。然而，由于一系列方法论革新彻底改变了我们对古代和宗教典籍的理解，语文学曾是19世纪德国最具影响力的知识学科之一。新的严格文献考证方法被发展出来，纯粹的思辨性假说受到抑制，更细致的语言比较研究得以开展。这些方法虽然带有浓厚的学术色彩，有时甚至近乎经院哲学，但其应用却产生了深远的文化影响，从学术期刊溢出到更广泛的公众意识之中。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 2:
The 19th century is also what one might call the 'philological century'. Philology is the critical study of written sources, including their linguistic features, history of reception and cultural context. Today, the term sounds outmoded, evoking dusty, learned tomes of fastidious source criticism. However, philology was a leading intellectual discipline in 19th-century Germany due to a flurry of methodological developments that revolutionised our understanding of ancient and sacred texts. New rigorous techniques of verifying sources were developed, merely speculative hypotheses were discouraged, more detailed comparative studies of language were conducted. While such methods were scholarly, sometimes bordering on scholastic, their application had significant cultural impact, spilling out of scholarly journals into broader public consciousness.

paragraphs.cn: 尼采在年轻时便深受这些思潮的影响。作为一名全面发展的优等生（数学是一个显著的例外），他在十四岁时被舒尔普福尔塔学校录取——这是德国最负盛名的人文主义学校之一。作为学者和教师的摇篮，该校的专长是古典时代研究。尼采在那里接受了严格的希腊语和拉丁语教育，阅读了伏尔泰和西塞罗的历史著作，并撰写了关于厄尔马纳里克传说和希腊诗人忒奥格尼斯等主题的语文学论文。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 3:
Nietzsche imbibed these trends as a young man. An all-round talented student (mathematics was a notable exception), he gained admittance as a 14-year-old to Schulpforta, one of the most prestigious humanistic schools in Germany. A training ground for scholars and teachers, the school's specialty was classical antiquity, and Nietzsche received a rigorous education in Greek and Latin, read historical works by Voltaire and Cicero, and wrote philological treatises on topics such as the saga of Ermanarich and the Greek poet Theognis.

paragraphs.cn: 这种语文学训练不仅影响了他直接涉及古希腊文化的早期著作（如1872年的《悲剧的诞生》），也影响了他后来关于道德和道德心理学的著作。认识到这种语文学影响，对于理解他哲学上最重要的著作——1887年的《论道德的谱系》——以及该书所启发的'谱系学'哲学方法的意义至关重要。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 4:
This philological education informed not only his early works that deal directly with Greek antiquity, such as The Birth of Tragedy (1872), but also his later books on morality and moral psychology. Appreciating this philological influence is crucial for understanding the significance of his philosophically most important work, On the Genealogy of Morality (1887), and the 'genealogical' method of philosophy it has inspired.

paragraphs.cn: 《论道德的谱系》是一本令人困惑的书。它涉及道德哲学的许多经典议题，如善的概念、自由意志、道德责任和罪感。然而，它并非以典型的哲学方式探讨这些问题——例如，追问'什么是善？'或'我们是否拥有自由意志？'相反，它采取了一种历史的进路，追问我们关于善、自由意志或罪感的观念从何而来。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 5:
On the Genealogy of Morality is a puzzling book. It deals with many classic topics of moral philosophy, such as the concept of good, free will, moral responsibility and guilt. However, it does not investigate them in a typical philosophical fashion – for instance, by asking 'What is good?' or 'Do we have free will?' Rather, it takes a historical approach, asking where our ideas about the good, or free will, or guilt, have come from.

paragraphs.cn: 尼采对这些历史问题的回答，委婉地说，过去和现在都备受争议。他自称为'炸药'绝非没有道理。例如，他认为当代西方的平等主义和利他主义假设是一种'奴隶道德'，源自一个祭司阶层的挫折感和怨恨（ressentiment，大致意为怨恨）。这种奴隶道德是对尼采所谓的'主人道德'的一种反动——后者是一种强调卓越、健康和尊重社会等级等美德的古老伦理。主人道德推崇力量，贬低祭司阶层宗教领袖的温顺和书生气。尼采认为，作为回应，宗教领袖发明了一套新的价值评判框架，在其中他们反居上位——在这一框架中，攻击性被归为'恶'，温顺和利他主义被归为'善'——一种对既有价值的重估。这些道德假设随后被下层奴隶阶层所采纳，因为这些假设赋予他们卑微的地位以价值，使他们能够将自身的无力重新诠释为有原则的选择。总之，在尼采看来，我们的许多基本道德假设源自古老的地位竞争。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 6:
Nietzsche's answers to these historical questions were and continue to be, to put it mildly, controversial. Not for nothing did he describe himself as 'dynamite'. For instance, he argued that contemporary Western egalitarian and altruistic assumptions were a form of 'slave morality' that emerged from the frustrations and ressentiment (roughly, resentment) of a priestly caste. This slave morality was a reaction against what Nietzsche calls 'master morality', an archaic ethics that emphasises virtues of excellence, health and respect for social hierarchy. Master morality valorises strength and denigrates the meekness and bookish intellectualism of the religious leaders of the priestly caste. In response, according to Nietzsche, the religious leaders invented a new evaluative framework in which they come out on top – one in which aggressiveness is classified as 'evil' and meekness and altruism as 'good' – a revaluation of existing values. These moral assumptions are then taken up by the lower slave caste, as they valorised their lowly status and enabled them to reinterpret their impotence as principled choice. In sum, on Nietzsche's view, many of our fundamental moral assumptions derive from archaic status competition.

paragraphs.cn: 令尼采谱系学的许多读者困惑的是，这些历史主张如何与关于道德的哲学问题相关联。尼采声明他的最终目的是评估'我们价值的价值'，但他的历史主张如何或为何能对这种评估有所贡献，并不清楚。转向历史，似乎尼采只是在转换道德哲学的主题，遵循这样的格言：如果你不喜欢正在谈论的话题，那就改变话题。毕竟，我们的价值从何而来的问题，似乎与关于其本性、价值和权威的哲学问题是不同的问题。如果尼采试图用这段历史来回答这些哲学问题，那么他似乎犯了一种谬误，即'发生学谬误'。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 7:
What has puzzled many readers of Nietzsche's genealogy is how these historical claims are supposed to be relevant to philosophical questions about morality. Nietzsche states that his ultimate aim is to assess 'the value of our values', but it is not clear how his historical claims could or should contribute to this assessment. In turning to history, it might seem that Nietzsche is simply shifting the subject of moral philosophy, following the adage that if you don't like what is being said, then you should change the conversation. After all, the question of where our values come from just seems like a different question from philosophical questions about their nature, value and authority. If Nietzsche is trying to use this history to answer these philosophical questions, then it might seem he is committing a fallacy, the 'genetic fallacy'.

paragraphs.cn: 证明某事物有糟糕的起源，并不足以证明它是坏的
paragraphs.en:
PARA 8:
Showing that something has a bad origin isn't sufficient to show that it is bad

paragraphs.cn: 发生学谬误是指根据某事物的起源或过去特征来评价它的一种被认为的错误。例如，设想一位朋友告诉你不应该戴婚戒，因为婚戒最初象征着女性佩戴的脚链，用以防止她们逃离丈夫（该例出自T·爱德华·达默的《攻击错误推理》第7版，2012年）。即使婚戒确实有这样一段可疑的历史，这一历史并不能证明现在佩戴婚戒是坏的或值得反对的。当尼采建议我们应该根据这段起源故事来重新评估基督教道德时，我们可能会怀疑他在运用类似的谬误推理。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 9:
The genetic fallacy is the purported mistake of evaluating something on the basis of its origin or past characteristics. For instance, imagine a friend tells you that you should not wear a wedding ring because wedding rings originally symbolised ankle chains worn by women to prevent them from running away from their husbands (the example is from Attacking Faulty Reasoning (7th ed, 2012) by T Edward Damer). Even if wedding rings have such a dubious history, this history does not show that it is bad or objectionable to wear them now. When Nietzsche suggests that he should reevaluate Christian morality on the basis of this origin story, we might suspect he is employing a similar type of fallacious reasoning.

paragraphs.cn: 对陷入发生学谬误的担忧，部分解释了道德哲学为何缺乏历史感。如前例所示，证明某事物有糟糕的起源并不足以证明它是坏的。同样，考察我们价值的起源似乎也不是评估它们的必要条件。要评估我们的道德信念和实践，我们似乎只需审视支持和反对它们的理由，而非我们持有这些信念的原因。例如，要批评平等主义，我们应当考虑对平等主义态度的潜在反对意见，比如它们主张向下拉平、阻碍人类卓越的实现，或者未能尊重所谓的道德应得差异。无论人们对这些批评有何看法，提出和评估它们似乎并不需要考察我们价值的历史。如果这些观点成立，我们就能理解道德哲学为何以非历史的方式推进：如果道德哲学的目的是批判性地审视我们价值的价值，而起源故事对此既非必要也非充分，那么起源故事就与道德哲学无关。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 10:
Concerns about falling prey to the genetic fallacy are part of what explain moral philosophy's lack of a historical sense. Showing that something has a bad origin isn't sufficient to show that it is bad, as the previous examples indicate. Neither does examining the origins of our values seem necessary for evaluating them. To evaluate our moral beliefs and practices, it seems that we need only look at the reasons for and against them, not the causes of our beliefs in them. For instance, to criticise egalitarianism, we should consider potential objections to egalitarian attitudes, such as that they recommend levelling down, prevent the achievement of human excellence, or fail to honour supposed differences in moral desert. Whatever one thinks of these criticisms, it seems that making and assessing them does not require looking at the history of our values. If these points are correct, we can see why moral philosophy would proceed ahistorically: if moral philosophy aims to critically scrutinise the value of our values, and origin stories are neither necessary nor sufficient for this purpose, then origin stories are irrelevant to moral philosophy.

paragraphs.cn: 尼采认为这种推理是错误的，尽管他为何如此认为在学界仍有争议。他的历史批判通常被理解为两种方式之一：要么他认为历史信息与道德规范的权威性或正当性直接相关，要么他认为历史信息通过提供证据表明道德态度是出于怨恨而激发的和/或阻碍了人类的伟大成就，从而间接相关。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 11:
Nietzsche thought this reasoning mistaken, although exactly why he thought so is a matter of scholarly dispute. His historical critique is typically understood in one of two ways: either he held that historical information was directly relevant to the authority or justification of moral norms, or he held it was indirectly relevant by providing evidence that moral attitudes are motivated by ressentiment and/or hinder great human achievements.

paragraphs.cn: 要理解历史如何可能与我们的价值地位直接相关，我们可以先承认许多人类造物的价值取决于其历史——毕加索的画作比完美的复制品更有价值，因为它们出自毕加索而非临摹者之手；你的家族传家宝之所以具有特殊价值，是因为它们与你家族的历史联系。我们的价值可能也是如此。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 12:
To grasp how history might be directly relevant to the status of our values, we might begin by acknowledging that the value of many human artefacts depends on their history – Picasso paintings are more valuable than perfect replicas because they come from Picasso and not from a copyist, and your family heirlooms hold special value because of their historical connection to your family. Something similar may be true of our values.

paragraphs.cn: 例如，如果我们的价值包含或隐含着命令，那么它们的历史可能与其权威性——即我们服从它们的义务——相关。许多命令的权威性取决于发出命令者的权威，而满怀报复心理、饱受地位焦虑的祭司似乎并非权威的命令者。更具体地说，人们可能认为道德命令的权威性取决于它们是否来自上帝或纯粹理性，因此，如果历史研究揭示它们源自一种世俗的'人性的，太人性的'来源，那么历史就可能在削弱其权威性。如果基督教价值观原来诞生于怨恨，那么根据这种论证，这就削弱了它们对我们今天的价值。类似地，将《圣经》追溯至世俗起源的语文学研究，可能通过动摇这些教诲来自上帝或耶稣的说法，从而削弱其中所含道德教诲的权威性。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 13:
For instance, if our values include or imply commands, then their history might be relevant to their authority, our obligation to obey them. Many commands depend for their authority on the authority of the commander, and vengeful clergy with status anxiety do not seem like authoritative commanders. More specifically, one might think that the authority of moral commands depends on their coming from God or pure reason, so insofar as historical investigation reveals that they derive from a secular 'human, all too human' source, then history might undermine their authority. If Christian values turn out to have been born from resentment, then that, on this sort of argument, undermines their value to us today. In a similar way, philological work that traces the Bible back to secular origins might undermine the authority of the moral teachings contained within it by undermining the claim that those teachings come from God or Jesus.

paragraphs.cn: 我们的价值的地位可能直接取决于其历史的另一种方式是，如果它们包含或隐含着关于价值主张的信念，因为历史信息可以影响我们的信念是否具有正当性。我可能因为了解到自己获取某一信念的过程不可靠，从而动摇该信念。例如，假设我相信一个关于我祖父过去常从教堂奉献罐中偷钱的传言，而我是从父亲那里获得这一信念的。然而，我后来得知，父亲是从他兄弟那里听到这个传言的，而这位兄弟是出了名的说谎者，且对其严格的宗教教养心怀怨恨。既然我的信念可以追溯到一个不可靠的来源，我可能有理由认为我的信念不再具有正当性。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 14:
Another way that the status of our values may depend directly on their history is if they include or imply beliefs about value claims, as historical information can affect whether our beliefs are justified. I might destabilise a belief by coming to know that the process by which I acquired that belief is unreliable. For instance, say I believe a rumour that my grandfather used to pilfer money from the church collection jar, and I acquired this belief from my father. However, I then learn that my father heard this rumour from his brother, who is a notorious liar and resentful of his strict religious upbringing. Given that my belief traces back to an unreliable source, I might reasonably think that my belief is no longer justified.

paragraphs.cn: 如果道德规范最初是由怨恨所驱动的，那么这就直接以某种方式'玷污'了它们
paragraphs.en:
PARA 15:
If moral norms were originally motivated by ressentiment, then this directly 'taints' them in some fashion

paragraphs.cn: 类似地，我们可能是从父母（以及更广泛的文化）那里获得了道德信念，而父母又是从他们的父母（以及更广泛的文化）那里获得的，如此追溯下去，源头是那些满怀报复心的祭司。但如果满怀报复心的祭司并非道德真理的可靠向导，那么我就应当怀疑父母的教导，进而怀疑我自己的道德信念。因此，尼采的历史叙事可能有助于通过揭示那些挑战我们对珍视但缺乏正当性的假设的信仰的信息，来将我们从幻觉中解放出来。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 16:
Similarly, it might be that we acquired our moral beliefs from our parents (and broader culture), who acquired them from their parents (and broader culture), all the way back to the vengeful clergy. But if vengeful clergy are not a reliable guide to the moral truth, then I should distrust my parents' teachings and therefore my moral beliefs. Nietzsche's historical story might therefore help emancipate us from illusions by exposing information that challenges our faith in cherished but unjustified assumptions.

paragraphs.cn: 这些关于权威性和正当性的观点，是哲学家有时会提出的关于历史与道德哲学相关性的论点，尼采可能也在提出类似的论点。然而，这种解读并未捕捉到许多评论者和普通读者通常认为的尼采文本的核心洞见。例如，当本科生阅读《论道德的谱系》时，他们通常会形成这样的印象：尼采的历史表明，许多自诩的对美德、正确性和正义的关切并不像其所宣称的那样高尚——其动机是某种由琐碎的报复心、自我赋值和对强势高地位者的怨恨混合而成的心理。在这里，关键的是尼采关于祭司、武士和奴隶的特定利益和动机的主张，而不仅仅是这些人物中没有一个是神圣的或可靠的这一事实。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 17:
These points about authority and justification are points that philosophers sometimes make about the relevance of history for moral philosophy, and it is possible that Nietzsche was making points of this kind. However, this interpretation does not capture what many commentators and lay readers typically take to be the main insights of Nietzsche's text. For instance, when undergraduates read the Genealogy, they usually take with them the idea that Nietzsche's history shows that much avowed concern with virtue, rightness and justice is less noble than it purports to be – it is motivated by some concoction of petty vengefulness, self-valorisation and resentment against powerful and high-status others. Here, it is Nietzsche's claims about the particular interests and motives of the priests, warriors and slaves that is crucial, not merely the fact that none of these figures is divine or reliable.

paragraphs.cn: 这些利益和动机如何在尼采对道德的批判中发挥作用？也许尼采假设，如果道德规范最初是由怨恨所驱动的，那么这就直接以某种方式'玷污'了它们，使其变得不可取，即使当今遵从这些规范的人并非出于同样的动机。然而，尼采无需做出这一假设。更有可能的是，尼采以一种更间接的方式看待其历史叙事的相关性——将其作为当代社会存在类似心理动力的证据。按照这种观点，谱系学之所以与道德批判相关，是因为它提供了证据表明当代道德具有不可取的特征——如不良动机——而这些特征本身并非历史性的。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 18:
How might these interests and motives play a role in Nietzsche's critique of morality? Perhaps Nietzsche is assuming that, if moral norms were originally motivated by ressentiment, then this directly renders them objectionable by 'tainting' them in some fashion, even if those who conform to them nowadays are not motivated in the same manner. However, Nietzsche need not make this assumption. It is more likely that Nietzsche sees his historical story as relevant in a more indirect manner, as evidence of similar psychological dynamics in contemporary society. On such a view, genealogy is relevant to a critique of morality because it provides evidence that contemporary morality has objectionable features, such as bad motives, that are not themselves historical.

paragraphs.cn: 认为许多自诩的道德观点背后有可疑动机这一观点并不难以接受，尤其是在现代互联网文化中。因此，人们可能要问，为什么需要转向历史来做出这一观察。有两个原因。第一，尼采的历史帮助我们更清晰地看到这些动力机制，它讲述了一个这些动力以简单而毫无伪装的形式呈现的历史故事。虽然当代道德被层层复杂性和合理化所覆盖，但如果我们回望过去，就能更好地看到如今已被遮蔽的心理动力。在这种解读下，历史被用来揭开当下的面纱。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 19:
The idea that many avowed moral views have dubious motives is not a hard sell, particularly given modern internet culture. So one might wonder why one need turn to history to make this observation. There are two reasons. First, Nietzsche's history helps us see these dynamics more clearly by telling a historical story in which they are present in a simple and unmasked form. While contemporary morality is overlaid with complexities and rationalisations, if we look to the past, we can better see psychological dynamics that have now been obscured. History, on this interpretation, is used to unmask the present.

paragraphs.cn: 第二，因为我们在以特定方式看待过去的情境时投入的个人情感较少，所以我们更有可能对其采取一种不那么轻信、更现实的视角。虽然我们可能对'自己的政治信念是出于怨恨'这种说法感到不快，但我们更容易在历史上遥远的他人身上识别出这种动力，进而使我们更容易在自己身上识别出它。历史的视角使我们避免陷入一厢情愿的幻想。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 20:
Second, because we are less personally and emotionally invested in viewing past situations in a particular manner, we are better able to take a less credulous, more realistic perspective of it. While we might bristle at thinking that our political convictions are motivated by ressentiment, we are more likely to recognise this dynamic in historically distant others, which then enables us to recognise it more easily in ourselves. Historical perspective prevents us from giving in to wishful thinking.

paragraphs.cn: 这些观点构成了尼采思想的一条重要线索。然而，将尼采解读为以这种间接的、证据性的方式使用历史也面临挑战。这类解读将过去仅仅视为当下的简化版本，认为它只在与当代社会的细微方面有所不同。因此，这种方法忽视了对我们的社会实践采取历史主义视角所关注的重大历史变迁和偶然性。如果关于尼采谱系学的这种叙述就是全部故事，那么尼采本人似乎也缺乏历史感。如果我们想理解尼采的谱系学如何可能尊重这样的历史主义假设，我们就应该转向尼采谱系方法本身的历史。也就是说，我们应该回到语文学。
paragraphs.en:
PARA 21:
These points form an important strand of Nietzsche's thinking. However, there are challenges to reading Nietzsche as using history in this indirect, evidential manner. Such interpretations treat the past as merely a simplified version of the present, differing from contemporary society only in minor ways. Consequently, this approach overlooks the kinds of significant historical change and contingency that are central to historicist perspectives on our social practices. If this account of Nietzsche's genealogy were the whole story, it would seem that Nietzsche himself would lack a historical sense. If we want to understand how Nietzsche's Genealogy might honour such historicist assumptions, we should turn to the history of Nietzsche's genealogical method itself. That is, we should return to philology.
