But ultimately, it tells you about what things mean, while a theory like utilitarianism tells you what’s right. One indicator of the difference between the respective theories’ explanatory roles is the difference between them in terms of what we may call ‘domain generality’. Theories like ‘terms refer to the features that causally regulate their usage’ or ‘only things posited by the successful natural sciences exist’ have implications beyond ethics – into what ‘cat’ means, or about whether René Descartes’s postulated _res cogitans_ exists — while utilitarianism is solely a theory of right and wrong, and that’s it.

paragraphs.cn: 现在，如果你去那个以前叫推特（Twitter）的网站上搜索「道德基础」或类似的词，你会搜到很多关于上帝或宗教的帖子。所以值得问一问：上帝是我这样的人谈论「基础」时心中所想的那种东西吗？关于这个问题有很多可说的，但表面上看，不是。如果有人声称一个行动在道德上是错的，当且仅当上帝禁止它，我会把这看作一个普通的道德理论，与声称一个行动在道德上是错的当且仅当它不能促进总体幸福相当。如果功利主义甚至没有资格成为基础，那么这个简单版本的神命论（divine command theory）同样没有。当然，确实有办法强化神命论，使其可以恰当地被视为对基础的一种尝试——例如引入「上帝本性」的形而上学。（应该说：其他规范伦理理论也有类似的强化方式。）我现在想说的唯一一点是，「上帝命令X」并不比「X最大化总体幸福」更能带我们「走出伦理学」。二者各自的道德相关性都有争议，而这种争论将在普通的一阶道德思维的竞技场上进行，与其余的规范伦理角斗士们一起。
paragraphs.en: Now, if you were to go on the website formerly known as Twitter and search for ‘foundation morality’ or something similar, you’d turn up many threads about God or religion. So it’s worth asking: is God the kind of thing that people like me have in mind when we talk about a ‘foundation’? There’s much to be said on this matter, but on the face of it, no. If someone were to claim that an action is morally wrong if and only if God forbids it, I’d take this as an ordinary moral theory on a par with the claim that an action is morally wrong if and only if it fails to promote wellbeing. If utilitarianism isn’t the sort of thing that’s even eligible to be a foundation, then neither is this simple version of divine command theory. Now, to be sure, there are ways of beefing up divine command theory so that it might properly be regarded as a stab at a foundation – bringing in the metaphysics of ‘God’s nature’, for example. (It should be said: there are parallel ways of beefing up other normative ethical theories, too.) The only point I wish to make now is that ‘God commands X’ no more takes us ‘outside of ethics’ than ‘X maximises overall wellbeing’. The moral relevance of each one is up for dispute, and that dispute would take place in the arena of regular old first-order moral thinking, with the rest of the normative-ethical gladiators.

paragraphs.cn: 那么，为什么人们常常认为道德需要一个基础呢？要解释一种在你看来如此显然正确的思维方式，似乎很困难。然而，我不认为它是正确的，更不用说显然正确了，所以让我试一试。基本上，我怀疑许多人之所以认为道德需要基础，是因为他们以这样或那样的方式将被称为「规范伦理学」的那种探究同化为普通的事实探究——在后者中，最受争议的主张确实似乎有基础或解释。不管你是否接受充足理由原则（the principle of sufficient reason）之类的高深哲学立场，我猜如果有人说明天会下雪，然后又声称对此没有解释——它只是一个粗粝事实——你大概会对此嗤之以鼻。但如果这种说法已经令人难以置信，那么伦理学整体「就那么漂浮在那里」——正如我所说——不受任何可能用来解释它的东西的束缚，这个观点就更可能让你觉得荒谬绝伦了。
paragraphs.en: So why is it so often thought that morality requires a foundation? It may seem difficult to explain a way of thinking that strikes one as so obviously correct. I, however, do not think it is correct, let alone obviously correct, and so let me try my hand. Basically, I suspect that many people think morality needs a foundation because they in some way or other assimilate the enquiry that gets called ‘normative ethics’ to ordinary factual enquiry, in which there do indeed seem to be foundations/explanations for the most argued-over claims. Whether or not you accept highfalutin philosophical positions like the principle of sufficient reason, my guess is that that you would look askance at someone who said that it’s going to snow tomorrow but then claimed that there was no explanation for that – that it’s just a brute fact. But if that claim strains credulity, then the view in which ethics as a whole ‘just floats there’, as I put it, untethered from anything that might serve to explain it, is apt to strike you as downright absurd.

paragraphs.cn: 相应地，我认为道德不需要基础的根本原因在于，我否认相关类型的伦理争论类似于普通的事实争论。它们有一些特征使人容易被欺骗而以为如此，但实际上它们有着关键的不同。更具体地说，被称为「规范伦理学」的争论最像许多人所说的「纯粹语词的」或「非实质性的」争论。一个经典的例子来自威廉·詹姆斯（William James）的著作《实用主义》（Pragmatism, 1907）。一个人绕着一棵树追一只松鼠。这个人是否因此绕着松鼠走了一圈？一个争论者说「没有」，因为这个人总是在松鼠的后面。另一个说「有」，因为这个人先在松鼠的北面，然后西面，然后南面，然后东面。这场争论中的人有不同的信念，这是肯定的；他们的冲突不是欲望或情感的冲突。不过，在一种明确的意义上，他们并没有真正以不同的方式表征世界。你在这场争论中采取的立场并不直接或间接地通过推理决定你认为世界的任何方面看起来、闻起来、听起来是什么样子；采取这场争论的一方或另一方也不会引导你以实现你的目标的方式行动——无论这些目标可能是什么，无论你的能力如何。换言之，这种信念并不像地图那样的表征方式发挥功能。
paragraphs.en: Correlatively, the fundamental reason why I don’t think that morality requires a foundation is that I deny that the relevant sorts of ethical disputes are akin to ordinary factual disputes. They have features that make it easy to be _fooled_ into thinking otherwise, but in fact they’re crucially different. More specifically, disputes that get called ‘normative ethics’ are most like disputes that many people have labelled ‘merely verbal’ or ‘non-substantive’. A classic example comes from William James’s book _Pragmatism_ (1907). A man is chasing a squirrel around a tree. Is the man thereby going around the squirrel? One disputant says ‘no’, because the man is always behind the squirrel. Another says ‘yes’, because the man is first north of the squirrel, then west, then, south, then east of it. The people in this dispute have different beliefs, to be sure; their conflict is not a conflict of desires or emotions. Still, there’s a clear sense in which they’re not really representing the world in different ways. The side you take in this dispute does not determine, either directly or indirectly by way of inference, the way you think any aspect of the world looks, smells, sounds, etc; nor would taking one side or the other of this dispute guide you to act in a way that achieves your aims, whatever these aims may be and whatever your powers may be. The belief, in other words, doesn’t function in the way a representation like a map does.

paragraphs.cn: 我认为那些倾向于被称为「规范伦理学」的辩论与此非常相似。如果功利主义是真的，世界将看起来、闻起来、听起来是这个样子；如果功利主义是假的，世界也将看起来、闻起来、听起来是这个样子。在功利主义问题上选边站队并不能像地图那样帮助我们推进我们的终极目标——无论这些目标碰巧是什么。它只是改变了我们的终极目标是什么。
paragraphs.en: I think the debates that tend to get called ‘normative ethical’ are a lot like this. The way that the world will look, smell, sound, etc if utilitarianism is true is just the way it will look, smell, sound, etc if utilitarianism is false. Taking sides for or against utilitarianism does not help us to further our ultimate goals, whatever they may happen to be, in the way that a map does. Rather, it simply changes what our ultimate goals are.

paragraphs.cn: 与动机和情感的联系欺骗我们将关于功利主义的争论同化为普通的事实争论
paragraphs.en: Connections with motivation and emotion fool us into assimilating disputes about utilitarianism to ordinary factual disputes

paragraphs.cn: 话虽如此，「功利主义」争论和「松鼠」争论之间也有一些重要的区别。我说过我们有时把关于松鼠的那种争论称为「纯粹语词的」或「非实质性的」。我们有时也会对它们说类似这样的话：「你可以说这个，也可以说那个。有什么意义呢？」这是因为，在这些争论中不仅没有表征准确性可供争夺——它们似乎也没有提供任何有价值的东西。它们似乎是，再一次，没有意义的。我们的大多数关于道德和政治的辩论并非如此。这是因为这些辩论关系到我们自己和他人的一动机，以及赞扬、责备、尊重等等，而「松鼠」那样的争论似乎并不涉及这些。我们可以说它们是有意义的，但不是实质性的。与「松鼠」不同，它们重要。但与普通的事实争论不同，它们重要的方式不是通过提供对世界的准确表征。
paragraphs.en: With that said, there are also some important differences between the ‘utilitarianism’ and ‘squirrel’ debates. I said that we sometimes call disputes like that about the squirrel ‘merely verbal’ or ‘non-substantive’. We also sometimes say of them something like: ‘You could say this, or you could say that. What’s the point?’ This is because not only is there no representational accuracy up for grabs in these debates – nothing of value seems to be afforded by them. They seem to be, again, pointless. Not so the majority of our debates about morality and politics. This is because such debates bear on our own and others’ motivations, as well as on praise, blame, esteem and so forth in a way that debates like ‘squirrel’ seem not to. We might say that they are significant, but not substantive. Unlike ‘squirrel’, they matter. But then unlike ordinary factual disputes, the _way_ that they matter is not by affording accurate representation of the world.

paragraphs.cn: 正是这些与动机和情感的联系欺骗我们将关于功利主义的争论，或「电车难题」（trolley problem），或分配正义，同化为普通的事实争论。因为它们关系到我们做什么和我们如何感受，我们不认为可以随随便便地「选哪边都行」。换言之，我们不认为它们是任意的，就像我们认为「松鼠」是任意的那样。我们也不认为可以通过概念上的规定来 settle 它们，就像我们会 settle「松鼠」那样的争论一样。
paragraphs.en: It’s these connections with motivation and emotion that fool us into assimilating disputes about utilitarianism, or the ‘trolley problem’, or distributive justice, to ordinary factual disputes. Because they bear on what we do and how we feel, we do not reckon that we can simply ‘go either way’ on them in a willy-nilly fashion. We do not regard them as arbitrary, in other words, in the way that we regard ‘squirrel’. Nor do we think it’s acceptable to settle them by conceptual fiat, as we would settle disputes like ‘squirrel’.

paragraphs.cn: 我的意思是这样的：如果我发现自己卷入了一场关于那个人是否绕着松鼠走了一圈一讨论，我可能会试图通过说这样的话来终止讨论：「看，我所说的『绕着走』就是这个意思……」相比之下，假设我们卷入了一场关于媒体是否有权利为了确保某个不被看好的候选人落选而搁置某篇报道的争论。在这里，我不会试图通过说，例如，「看，我所说的『权利』就是指『促进总体幸福』……」来解决争论。我会认为这种争论应当通过论证来解决，而不是通过规定。同样，我认为我们可以把这种差异归因于这样一个事实：规范伦理争论虽然像「松鼠」一样未能提供表征意义，但在实践和情感方面具有「松鼠」所不具有的意义。
paragraphs.en: Here is what I mean by that. Were I to find myself embroiled in a discussion about whether the man is going around the squirrel, I would probably try to put a stop to it by saying: ‘Look, all I mean by “going around” is this…’. By contrast, suppose we were embroiled in a dispute about whether the media would be right to mothball a story in an attempt to ensure that a disfavoured candidate is not elected. Here I would not try to settle the dispute by saying, eg, ‘Look, by “right”, all I mean is “maximises overall wellbeing”…’ I’d see such a dispute as to be settled by argument, not by stipulation. And again, I think we can chalk up this difference to the fact that normative-ethical disputes, despite failing to afford representational significance just like ‘squirrel’, are significant in practical and affective ways that ‘squirrel’ is not.

paragraphs.cn: 这一切将「规范伦理」争论置于一个奇怪的范畴，使得在哲学理论方面很难知道该对它们说些什么。我实际上认为这是一个优势，因为当涉及到伦理学时，真理、客观性和知识该怎么说，显然并不一目了然！这一点可以从以下事实得到印证：一些超级聪明的哲学家认为存在关于伦理学的客观真理，一些人认为伦理学就像炼金术一样是胡说八道，一些人认为伦理争论实际上是欲望式态度的冲突的伪装，等等。任何认为伦理争论以某种方式运作，以至于某一种理论解释是完全显而易见、自然且易于陈述的人，就需要向我们解释，在知识产权历史的如此晚期阶段，怎么可能有这么多聪明人错得如此离谱。
paragraphs.en: This all puts ‘normative-ethical’ disputes in a strange category, and makes it difficult to know what to say about them in terms of philosophical theory. I actually consider this an advantage, for it is manifestly not obvious what to say about truth and objectivity and knowledge when it comes to ethics! This is witnessed by the fact that some super-smart philosophers think that there are objective truths about ethics, some think ethics is bullshit along the lines of alchemy, some think ethical disputes are really conflicts of desire-like attitudes in disguise, and so on. Anyone who thought ethical disputes work in such a way that one theoretical interpretation is just utterly obvious and natural and easy to state would then owe us an explanation of how so many smart people could be getting it so terribly wrong at this late stage in intellectual history.

paragraphs.cn: 因此，承认这一点绝非显而易见，以下是我自己的理论解释。伦理学之所以既不需要也不接受自身之外的基础，原因在于：像「松鼠」但不像任何普通事实争论，相关类型的伦理争论是非表征性的，或者——我更喜欢这样说——未能提供「表征价值」。也就是说，在这类争论中，得出这个结论而非那个结论，并不会在任何值得关心的强健意义上表征或镜像或复制世界。但是，人们通常召唤来充当「基础」的那些来自形而上学、语义学等领域的伦理外考量，只有在关系到哪些道德信念（如果有的话）在表征方面是好的或坏的情况下，才能与伦理学相关。它们不像幸福、自由、平等、尊严等其他东西那样在伦理上重要。但由于在规范伦理争论中表征价值和反价值并不在考虑之列，这些关于道德属性的形而上学、道德术语的意义和指称等等的考量，与根本性的伦理学无关。
paragraphs.en: And so, acknowledging that it is by no means obvious, here is my own theoretical interpretation. The reason why ethics neither requires nor admits of a foundation outside of itself is that, like ‘squirrel’ but unlike any ordinary factual disputes, the relevant kinds of ethical dispute are non-representational or, as I prefer to put it, fail to afford ‘representational value’. That is to say, one does not represent or mirror or copy the world in any robust sense that is worth caring about by coming to any conclusion rather than another pursuant to such a dispute. But the sorts of extra-ethical considerations drawn from metaphysics, semantics and so on that people typically call upon to serve as ‘foundations’ could be relevant to ethics only by bearing on which moral beliefs, if any, were good or bad in representational respects. They’re not ethically important in the ways that happiness, freedom, equality, dignity and other such things are. But since representational value and disvalue aren’t on the cards when it comes to normative-ethical disputes, these considerations regarding the metaphysics of moral properties, the sense and reference of moral terms and so on, are irrelevant to fundamental ethics.

paragraphs.cn: 因此，认为所谓的「错误理论家」或道德「虚无主义者」所说的那样——世界上不存在道德属性之类的东西，因此所有关于对错的判断都是错误的——将是一个错误。世界不需要有这些小小的道德小玩意才能让事情有对错之分；只需要有幸福与不幸、自由与暴政等等就足够了。认为伊丽莎白·安斯康姆（Elizabeth Anscombe）在她那篇影响深远的论文《现代道德哲学》（Modern Moral Philosophy, 1958）中所说的——道德上的「应当」缺乏意义，因此没有什么是我们应当在道德上去做的——也是一个错误。
paragraphs.en: And so it would be a mistake to think, with so-called ‘error theorists’ or ‘nihilists’ about morality, that there are no such things as moral properties in the world, and so all attributions of rightness or wrongness are false. The world doesn’t have to have these little moral doodads for things to be right or wrong; there just has to be happiness and unhappiness, freedom and tyranny, and so forth. It would be a mistake to think, with Elizabeth Anscombe in her influential paper ‘Modern Moral Philosophy’ (1958), that the moral ‘ought’ lacks sense, as it were, and so there is nothing that we morally ought to do.

paragraphs.cn: 如果这些是问题，它们是任何思考事物的人的问题
paragraphs.en: If these are problems, they’re problems for anyone who thinks things

paragraphs.cn: 某物是否「缺乏意义」是一个语义问题，而语义学与规范伦理学无关。它只有在有助于确定关于伦理学的信念的表征价值的情况下才与伦理学相关，但并没有这样的价值处于利害攸关之中。如我一开始所说，我的争论对象不仅仅是怀疑论者。那些试图从语义学的主张（例如，关于道德术语的意义或指涉）或形而上学的主张（例如，关于什么最能实现道德对某个适当「自然」属性簇的还原）中榨出积极道德结论的人，犯了同样的基本错误。他们把规范伦理探究当作表征性的，即使它并不是。
paragraphs.en: Whether something ‘lacks sense’ is a semantic matter, and semantics does not bear on normative ethics. It would bear on ethics only if it went towards determining the representational values of beliefs about ethics, but there are no such values at stake. As I said at the outset, my quarrel is not only with the sceptics. Someone who attempted to wring some positive moral conclusions out of claims in semantics (eg, about the sense or reference of moral terms) or metaphysics (eg, about what would best accomplish the reduction of morality to some cluster of suitably ‘natural’ properties) is making the same sort of basic error. They are treating normative-ethical enquiry as representational, even though it is not.

paragraphs.cn: 但是，如果规范伦理争论的双方都不比另一方更成功地表征或「镜像」世界，那为什么我们不能像在「松鼠」中似乎可以的那样「选哪边都行」呢？如果在任何强健意义上都不存在准确或不准确表征的可能性，怎么还能有对错之分呢？
paragraphs.en: But if neither side of a normative-ethical dispute is representing or ‘mirroring’ the world any more successfully than the other is, then why can’t we ‘go either way’, as it seems we can in ‘squirrel’? How can there be a truth of the matter, if there’s no possibility of accurate or inaccurate representation in any robust sense?

paragraphs.cn: 我的基本回答是：赋予这些规范伦理辩论以重要性的外观的东西——其结论对动机和情感的影响——也使得它们实际上确实重要。在这些伦理争论中，有价值与意义可供争夺，但这种价值并不在于在强健意义上表征世界。它是我所说的「特属于伦理的价值」——即出于正确的理由做正确之事的价值。而我正是试图从这种价值中榨取一种专属于伦理学的真理或正确性。想象一个在所有非道德方面都理想的顾问——对非评价性事物有真实的信念、完美的推理能力等等。如果我们把一个特定的道德信念植入这样的人身上，而她建议你做且仅做所有正确的行动，那么这个信念就在这种专属于伦理的意义上算作真的，即使该信念并不「描绘」或「镜像」世界。
paragraphs.en: My basic answer is that what gives these normative-ethical debates the appearance of mattering – their conclusions’ influence on motivation and affect – also makes it the case that they actually matter. There’s value and significance up for grabs in these ethical disputes, then, but it’s not value that inheres in representing the world in a robust sense. It’s what I call ‘specifically ethical value’ – the value of doing the right thing for the right reason. And it’s from this sort of value that I try to wring a kind of truth or correctness that’s proprietary to ethics. Imagine a kind of advisor who’s ideal in all non-moral respects – true beliefs about non-evaluative matters, perfect inferential abilities, etc. If we plug a particular moral belief into such a person, and she advises you to do all and only right actions, then that belief counts as true in this proprietarily ethical sense, even though the belief does not ‘picture’ or ‘mirror’ the world.

paragraphs.cn: 请注意，我对伦理真理的论证最终落脚于关于「特属于伦理的」价值的主张，而我关于形而上学、语义学等与伦理学无关的论证最终落脚于关于我所说的「表征」价值的主张。这可能让你觉得我在针对关于评价性真理和知识的怀疑论者乞求论题——换言之，在我打算向这种怀疑论者证明的事情上，我一开头就假定了它。我的回应是：是的，我确实在乞求论题，但这本身并不让我陷入不好的同伴。任何对某一主题领域提出积极主张的人——外部世界、归纳法、数学知识等等——而不是完全保留判断，都必须在某个时刻面对所谓的「阿格里帕三难困境」（Agrippan trilemma）：要么将某些事实设定为不可解释的，要么乞求论题，要么接受无穷倒退。如果这些是问题，它们不是专门针对我的问题；它们是任何思考事物的人的问题。
paragraphs.en: Note that my brief for ethical truth bottoms out in claims about ‘specifically ethical’ value, and that my argument for the irrelevance of metaphysics, semantics, etc to ethics bottoms out in claims about what I called ‘representational’ value. This might strike you as begging the question against the sceptic about evaluative truth and knowledge – in other words, as assuming at the outset just what I intend to demonstrate to such a sceptic. My rejoinder: yes, I do beg the question, but this, in itself, does not put me in bad company. Everyone who ventures a positive claim about some subject matter – the external world, induction, mathematical knowledge, what-have-you – rather than withholding judgment entirely, must at some point confront the so-called ‘Agrippan trilemma’: either posit certain facts as unexplained, or beg the question, or accept an infinite regress. If these are problems, they’re not problems for me specifically; they’re problems for anyone who thinks things.

paragraphs.cn: 因此我说，真正的罪过不在于乞求论题，而在于未能将世界的各个方面纳入一个更一般的证成性框架。例如，一种关于先验知识（a priori knowledge）的理论，如果它解释了对该理论本身的知识何以可能，那么它可能乞求了论题，但只要它解释了一般的先验知识——例如数学、逻辑和道德的先验知识——而不仅仅是关于它自身的先验知识，它就不一定是有问题的。一种关于对世界的准确心理表征的理论，如果它解释了我们对该理论本身的信念如何准确地表征世界，那也是在乞求论题，但只要它解释了一般的准确心理表征，这就不应该让我们担心。这些理论通过使原本神秘的东西变得可理解来证明自身的价值，因此如果它们在过程中也证成了自身，这不应困扰我们。
paragraphs.en: So I say that the true sin lies not in question-begging, but in failing to subsume aspects of the world within a more general vindicatory framework. For example, a theory of _a priori_ knowledge that explains how knowledge of that very theory is possible might beg the question, but so long as it accounts for _a priori_ knowledge in general – eg, of mathematics, logic and morality – and not just _a priori_ knowledge of itself, it needn’t be problematic. A theory of accurate mental representation of the world that explains how our beliefs in that very theory accurately represent the world also begs the question, but this should not worry us insofar as it explains accurate mental representation across the board. These theories earn their keep by making sense of what would otherwise remain mysterious, and so it should not trouble us if they end up vindicating themselves in the process.

paragraphs.cn: 我提议通过在根本上以价值——无论是表征价值、特属于伦理的价值，还是其他种类的价值——来证成所有值得证成的主张和领域——不仅仅是伦理学，而是从生物化学到体育预测的一切——来获得类似的解释统一性。正是这种价值优先的对探究的重新构想，我保留了「实用主义」（pragmatism）这一标签。实用主义提供了一种方式来理解伦理真理、客观性和知识，方法是将它们嵌入一幅更全面的世界图景之中，但不是以某种据称更根本的探究领域为伦理学提供基础的方式。由此浮现的是一个自由漂浮的评价领域，连同关于为什么这毕竟不那么奇怪或神秘的解释。
paragraphs.en: I propose to attain a similar sort of explanatory unity by vindicating all claims and domains that are worthy of it – not just ethics, but everything from biochemistry to sports prognostication – fundamentally in terms of values, be these representational, specifically ethical, or other sorts of values. It is this values-first re-imagining of enquiry for which I reserve the label ‘pragmatism’. Pragmatism offers a way of making sense of ethical truth, objectivity and knowledge by ensconcing these within a more comprehensive world picture, but not in such a way that would count as providing a foundation for ethics in some allegedly more fundamental area of enquiry. What emerges is a free-floating evaluative sphere, coupled with an account of why this is not so odd or mysterious after all.

articles.title: 忘掉寻找共同基础的温馨感吧——要战胜极化，试试改变你的期望
articles.author: Sarah Pessin
articles.summary: 2025年6月，科罗拉多州道格拉斯县（Douglas County）超过70%的选民——保守派和进步派 alike——投票反对了「自治」公投案。该公投措施本将赋予该县对某些地方事务的更大控制权，如建筑分区、停车规则和下水道维护。
articles.content_cn:
2025年6月，科罗拉多州道格拉斯县（Douglas County）超过70%的选民——保守派和进步派 alike——投票反对了「自治」公投案。该公投措施本将赋予该县对某些地方事务的更大控制权，如建筑分区、停车规则和下水道维护。

该县历史上是共和党的大本营，但如今也居住着日益增长且敢于发声的民主党人，它是美国政治分歧的一个缩影——从禁书争论到新冠疫情口罩争议。这个分裂的县两党联合拒绝自治，是否意味着科罗拉多人已经破解了极化问题？

可惜，并非如此。

事实证明，双方都对这次选举的昂贵和仓促性质感到反感。这很难说是对手之间彼此热络起来的温馨故事——而这往往是双方善良的人们似乎在期盼的公民解决方案。

你可以在一个公共广播电台的标题中感受到那种渴望——该标题宣布「一位自由派的城市园丁与一位保守派的军属女家长共进晚餐」。或者在莎拉·西尔弗曼（Sarah Silverman）的《我爱你，美国》（I Love You, America）中也可以感受到——在这档电视节目中，这位喜剧演员出发去用一个又一个击掌穿越一个分裂的国家。你在「村庄广场」（The Village Square）——一个非营利性公民组织——中也能看到这一点，该组织自称为「一群胆大的自由派和保守派」，承诺进行两党对话，虽有分歧但也有「一段美好时光」。

但如果这种特定的尝试方式把标准定得太高了——或者至少，太舒适惬意了？

作为一名研究跨传统意义建构、伦理学和政治学的哲学家，我想建议科罗拉多人不需要拥抱和解或击掌前行。相反，他们可以从各种伦理传统中寻找洞见，关于如何在彼此憎恨对方的观点和价值观的情况下仍然保护彼此。

论成为挑剔的公主

首先，在美国民主中，一些张力是特性，而非缺陷。虽然大多数人会坚持认为种族主义和性别主义必须消除，但一些分歧——比如宗教差异——将会持续存在。所以梦想与邻居实现全面的和谐，在某种程度上偏离了要点。

此外，正如我在其他地方论证过的，团结的梦想可能使善意的邻居面临变成公民版「公主与豌豆」的风险——源自汉斯·克里斯蒂安·安徒生（Hans Christian Andersen）的童话。就像一位如此纯粹的王室成员，即便堆满了 plush 床垫也无法阻止她被一颗豌豆的凸起所惊醒，不断寻求共同基础可能使人倾向于成为求同者，对差异越来越过敏。

而这可能让每个人的胃都更加剧烈地翻涌——对着所有那些并非与自己一模一样的邻居。

「硬希望」的遗产

当涉及更好的公民生活时，拥抱彼此并非消除彼此的唯一替代方案。我一直在为美国公民生活中的积怨开发一种不同的疗法。

它基于我数十年来研究哲学的经历，以及我总是被每个文本中关于人的真实性和伦理回应的最 precarious 和最脆弱的洞见所吸引的倾向。

我称之为「硬希望」（Hard Hope）。

硬希望从众多不同背景的广泛思想者的神学和伦理政治中汲取灵感。

在1958年的一次圣诞节布道中，马丁·路德·金（Martin Luther King Jr.）反思了圣经中「爱你的敌人」的诫命。他提到上帝时指出：「值得注意的是，他并没有说，『喜欢你的敌人。』喜欢是一种感情化的东西，一种情感化的东西。有很多人我发现很难喜欢。」

这是一个需要片刻才能领会的惊人洞见：他说的是，真正的邻里之爱与心形表情符号关系不大。

类似地，犹太哲学家伊曼纽尔·列维纳斯（Emmanuel Levinas）强调，人们被召唤去服务他人，不是基于共同的基础，而是基于他人是「我无法容纳的绝对他者」。列维纳斯受到《出埃及记》33章20节的启发，其中说没有人能看见上帝的面容。他将邻居的彻底他者性描述为一张不可知的面孔，而人们尽管如此仍对之负有伦理义务。列维纳斯论证说，人们对他人负有义务，恰恰在于他们是他人。而不在于人们感到彼此相连。

在类似的精神中，奇卡诺裔酷儿女权主义者格洛丽亚·安萨尔杜瓦（Gloria Anzaldúa）呼吁一种「精神行动主义」，其中正义不仅需要打破不平等，还需要与对手共同建设。为了实现与他人的这种激进连接，安萨尔杜瓦借助纳瓦特尔语（Nahuatl）中表示「之间」的词语「nepantla」，并发出对「nepantleras」的召唤——那些能够在分裂视角之间的模糊门槛中穿行的人。

「尊重人们的他者性，nepantleras 倡导一种『nos/otras』立场——即『我们』与『他者』之间的联盟。在 nos/otras 中，『我们』被一分为二，中间的斜杠代表桥梁——我们此刻所能期望的最好的相互性，」她写道。

硬希望呼吁人们不仅关照他们喜欢的邻居，还要关照他们最不喜欢的邻居。即便人们前往投票站拒绝对手最过分的越界行为，即便他们在社区内部和跨社区努力提升正义并为所有人争取更美好的未来。它不是呼吁改变一个群体的政治，尽管有时它可能意味着缓和它们。它始终意味着将参与政治的呼吁与接触人的呼吁区分开来——即使它期望每个人都做到两者。

硬希望邀请人们暂时放下那种认为每个人都只差一步就能达成共识、并在分歧中迸发出同情、友谊和和谐的泡泡糖式乐观主义。相反，硬希望邀请人们采取一种不寻常的心境——在不喜欢邻居的情况下感到对他们有一种亏欠感。它呼吁人们深入挖掘，超越「共同的人性」感，到达一种更深的「不共享的他者性」感——正是这种他者性召唤人们为他人服务。

这是一种激进形式的希望，更多关乎负债共处，而非热情的战友之情。

而在此过程中，不需要烘烤或掰开哪怕一块面包。
articles.content_en:
More than 70% of voters in Colorado’s Douglas County, conservative and progressive alike, voted “no” on home rule in June 2025. The ballot measure would have granted the county increased control over certain local matters such as building zoning, parking rules and sewer maintenance.

Historically Republican, but home to a growing population of vocal Democrats, the county is a microcosm of American political divides – from book ban debates to COVID mask controversies. Does this divided county’s bipartisan rejection of home rule mean that Coloradans have cracked the polarization problem?

Alas, not really.

It turns out both sides recoiled at the expensive and rushed nature of the election. It was hardly the heartwarming tale of opponents warming up to each other, which is often the civic solution good humans on both sides seem to be wishing for.

You can sense that longing in a public radio headline announcing the “liberal urban gardener breaking bread with a conservative military-family matriarch.” Or in Sarah Silverman’s “I Love You, America,” a TV series in which the comedian set out to high-five her way across a divided country. You see it in The Village Square, a nonprofit civic organization that describes itself as a “nervy bunch of liberals and conservatives” who promise bipartisan dialogue with disagreement but also “a good time.”

But what if this particular kind of trying sets the bar too high – or, at least, too comfy and cozy?

As a philosopher who studies meaning-making, ethics and politics across traditions, I’d like to suggest that Coloradans don’t need to hug it out or high-five their way forward. Rather, they can look to a variety of ethical traditions for insights about protecting each other even when they hate each other’s views and values.

On becoming fussy princesses

For starters, in American democracy some tensions are a feature, not a bug. While most would insist racism and sexism need to go, some disconnects – such as religious differences – are going to stay. So dreaming of full-on harmony with neighbors kind of misses the point.

Furthermore, as I’ve argued elsewhere, the unity dream can put well-meaning neighbors at risk of becoming the civic equivalents of the princess and the pea. from the Hans Christian Andersen fairy tale. Just like a royal so pure that even piles of plush mattresses can’t prevent her being awakened by the lumpiness of a single pea, constantly seeking common ground can dispose people to become sameness-seekers who are increasingly allergic to difference.

And this can make everyone’s stomachs churn even more furiously at all of their not-just-like-them neighbors.

The legacy of Hard Hope

When it comes to better civics, embracing each other is not the only alternative to erasing each other. I’ve been developing a different remedy for rancor in American civic life.

It’s based on my decades of studying philosophy and gravitating always to each text’s most precarious and vulnerable insights on human authenticity and ethical response.

I call it “Hard Hope.”

Hard Hope takes its inspiration from the theological and ethical politics of a wide array of thinkers from many different backgrounds.

In a Christmas sermon in 1958, Martin Luther King Jr. reflected on the biblical injunction to “love thy enemy.” Referring to God, he notes: “It’s significant that he does not say, ‘Like your enemy.’ Like is a sentimental something, an affectionate something. There are a lot of people that I find it difficult to like.”

It’s an arresting insight that takes a moment to sink in: He’s saying that real neighborly love has little to do with heart emojis.

Similarly, Emmanuel Levinas, a Jewish philosopher, emphasizes being called upon to serve others not in light of shared ground but in light of their being “the absolutely other which I can not contain.” Levinas was inspired by Exodus 33:20 which says no human can see God’s face. He describes the utter otherness of the neighbor as an unknowable face to which people are nonetheless ethically beholden. People are beholden to others inasmuch as they are other, Levinas argues. Not inasmuch as people feel connected.

And in like spirit, the queer Chicana feminist Gloria Anzaldúa calls for a “spiritual activism” in which justice requires not only interrupting inequity, but also building with opponents. For such radical connection with others, Anzaldúa draws on the Nahuatl term for in between, “nepantla,” and issues a call to “nepantleras” – people who are able to navigate ambiguous thresholds within split perspectives.

“Honoring people’s otherness, las nepantleras advocate a ‘nos/otras’ position — an alliance between ‘us’ and ‘others.’ In nos/otras, the ‘us’ is divided in two, the slash in the middle representing the bridge – the best mutuality we can hope for at the moment,” she writes.

Hard Hope is a call to look out not only for neighbors we like but for neighbors we like least. That’s even as people take to voting booths to reject their opponents’ worst oversteps, and even as they work within and across communities to elevate justice and secure better futures for all. It’s not a call to change a group’s politics, though at times it can mean tempering them. And it always means distinguishing the call to engage politics from the call to engage people – even as it expects everyone to do both.

Hard Hope asks people to take a break from the bubblegum optimism of believing everyone is just moments away from seeing eye to eye and bursting into compassion, friendship and harmony across divides. Instead, Hard Hope invites people to take up the unusual mood of feeling a sense of debt to their neighbors without liking them. It’s a call to dig deep, beyond a sense of “shared humanity” to an even deeper sense of an “unshared otherness” that calls people into service to others.

It’s a radical form of hope that’s more about indebted coexistence than enthusiastic camaraderie.

And not a single loaf of bread needs baking or breaking in the process.

It’s a call to dig deep, beyond a sense of “shared humanity” to an even deeper sense of an “unshared otherness” that calls people into service to others.

paragraphs.cn: 这是一种激进形式的希望，更多关乎负债共处，而非热情的战友之情。
paragraphs.en: It’s a radical form of hope that’s more about indebted coexistence than enthusiastic camaraderie.

paragraphs.cn: 而在此过程中，不需要烘烤或掰开哪怕一块面包。
paragraphs.en: And not a single loaf of bread needs baking or breaking in the process.

articles.title: 两位钟表匠
articles.author: AI Parable (inspired by Amanda Askell)
articles.summary: 在一个以信任为通货、以沉默为法则的国度里，两位钟表匠——阿尔德里克（Aldric）和布莱思（Blythe）——为皇帝打造精密的钟表。他们的手艺堪称传奇，竞争也异常激烈。每个人都认为自己才配得上「皇家钟表大师」的头衔。
articles.content_cn:
在一个以信任为通货、以沉默为法则的国度里，两位钟表匠——阿尔德里克（Aldric）和布莱思（Blythe）——为皇帝打造精密的钟表。他们的手艺堪称传奇，竞争也异常激烈。每个人都认为自己才配得上「皇家钟表大师」的头衔。

一个秋日的傍晚，皇帝最珍爱的精密计时器从金库中不翼而飞。卫兵从床上将两位钟表匠一并捉拿。他们被分别押往不同的塔楼——彼此看不见、听不着，也无法传递任何消息——然后被告知了同样一番话。

「我们知道你们中有一人偷了计时器。也许两人都参与了，也许只有一个。条件如下：如果你坦白而你的同伴保持沉默，你将获得自由并被授予「皇家钟表大师」的头衔，而沉默者将在黑曜石矿场服刑二十年。如果你们都坦白，各服刑五年。如果你们都保持沉默，我们将以玩忽职守的轻罪关押你们一年，然后释放。」

阿尔德里克独自坐在冰冷的塔楼里，仔细地盘算着。「如果布莱思保持沉默，那我应该坦白——我可以获得自由，而不必服刑一年。如果布莱思坦白了，那我也应该坦白——五年总比二十年好。无论哪种情况，坦白都是更明智的选择。」他的逻辑天衣无缝、无懈可击。

在另一座塔楼里，布莱思背靠着石墙，以同样冷静的精密逻辑盘算着。「如果阿尔德里克保持沉默，我应该坦白——自由胜过一年刑期。如果阿尔德里克坦白了，我也应该坦白——五年好过二十年。无论他怎么选，坦白都对我最有利。」她的逻辑同样天衣无缝、同样无懈可击、同样完美无缺。

两人都坦白了。两人都在黑曜石矿场服了五年苦役。而那枚计时器，从头到尾不过是一个粗心的仆人放错了地方——根本没有人偷。

在各自的牢房里，每个人都做出了理性的选择。每个人都沿着逻辑走向了必然的结论。然而，他们共同抵达的结局，却比本可以通过互相信任、保持沉默而获得的那个更糟糕。在孤立中追求理性，将他们引离了他们本可以共同找到的最优答案。两座塔楼没有一扇窗户朝向彼此，而这才是最残酷的建筑设计。

这就是博弈论家们所称的「囚徒困境」（Prisoner's Dilemma）。两方无法沟通，各自面临合作与背叛之间的抉择。无论对方如何选择，背叛总能带来更好的个人结果——这就是所谓的「占优策略」（dominant strategy）。然而，当双方都遵循这一逻辑时，他们各自得到的结果却比双方都合作时更糟。这个困境揭示了人类事务中一种深刻的张力：个体的理性并不总能通向集体的福祉。它浮现于军备竞赛、价格战、环境退化之中，以及任何信任缺席、合作的代价是即时的而收益却是共享的处境之中。钟表匠的塔楼当然是隐喻——但矿场却是足够真实的。
articles.content_en:
In a country where trust was currency and silence was law, two watchmakers — Aldric and Blythe — built intricate timepieces for the Emperor. Their craft was legendary, their rivalry fierce. Each believed they alone deserved the title of Master Horologist.

One autumn evening, the Emperor's most treasured chronograph vanished from the vault. Guards seized both watchmakers from their beds. They were dragged to separate towers — out of sight, out of earshot, beyond the reach of any message — and told the same thing.

"We know one of you stole the chronograph. Perhaps both of you did, perhaps only one. Here is the offer. If you confess and your partner remains silent, you go free and receive the title of Master Horologist, while the silent one serves twenty years in the obsidian mines. If you both confess, you each serve five years. If you both remain silent, we will hold you for one year on a lesser charge of negligence, and then release you."

Aldric sat alone in his cold tower, reasoning carefully. "If Blythe stays silent, then I should confess — I go free instead of serving one year. If Blythe confesses, then I should also confess — five years is better than twenty. Either way, confessing is the wiser choice." His logic was airtight, flawless, impeccable.

In the other tower, Blythe sat with her back against the stone wall, reasoning with the same cold precision. "If Aldric stays silent, I should confess — freedom is better than one year. If Aldric confesses, I should confess too — five years beats twenty. No matter what he does, confessing serves me best." Her logic was equally airtight, equally flawless, equally impeccable.

Both confessed. Both served five years in the obsidian mines. And the chronograph had been misplaced by a careless servant all along — stolen by no one.

From their separate cells, each had made the rational choice. Each had followed reason to its inevitable conclusion. Yet together, they had arrived at an outcome worse than the one they could have reached had they trusted each other and stayed silent. Rationality, pursued in isolation, had led them away from the best answer they could have found together. The towers had no windows facing each other, and that was the cruelest architecture of all.

This is the structure that game theorists call the Prisoner's Dilemma. Two parties, unable to communicate, each face a choice between cooperation and defection. Regardless of what the other does, defecting always yields a better individual outcome — it is the "dominant strategy." Yet when both follow this logic, they arrive at a result worse for each of them than if both had cooperated. The dilemma reveals a deep tension in human affairs: individual rationality does not always lead to collective well-being. It surfaces in arms races, price wars, environmental degradation, and any situation where trust is absent and the costs of cooperation are immediate while the rewards are shared. The watchmakers' towers, of course, are metaphor — but the mines are real enough.
